# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION AND INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Compartment Fire?

A **Compartment Fire** is a fire that is confined and allowed to develop within an enclosed space (a room, chamber, or any architectural enclosure) bounded by walls, floor, and ceiling, with a defined number and arrangement of openings (windows, doors, vents). The study of compartment fires forms the **fundamental building block** of structural fire engineering because the overwhelming majority of life-loss fires and structural failures occur *inside* such enclosures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Text):**
> *"A compartment fire is a fire whose growth, development, and decay are governed by the size, geometry, ventilation, and fuel load of the enclosing compartment, together with the thermal properties of its bounding surfaces."*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bounded Pressure Cooker"

Imagine a pressure cooker on a gas stove. The fire (the burner) heats the air, the fuel is the food, the lid represents the ceiling, and the vent weight on top is analogous to the **window opening**. If the vent is too small (orifice too restrictive), pressure builds and the temperature soars; if the vent is large, heat escapes and cooking is gentle. A compartment behaves exactly the same way. The window acts as the **safety valve** that controls the rate at which heat and combustion products leave the room — and thus the **severity** of the fire inside.

## 1.2 Factors Controlling Fire Severity

Fire severity is the destructive potential of a fire, usually expressed as the **equivalent fire exposure time** (in minutes) under a standard temperature-time curve (ISO 834 or ASTM E119). The key controlling factors are:

| S.No. | Factor | Symbol | Practical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fire Load (Fuel Load) | $q$ or $Q$ | More fuel → longer/hotter fire |
| 2 | Ventilation Opening Area | $A_v$ or $A_o$ | More air → more vigorous combustion |
| 3 | Compartment Geometry | $A_T$ (Total Internal Surface Area) | Smaller rooms heat up faster |
| 4 | Thermal Properties of Walls | $k, \rho, c$ (Conductivity, Density, Specific Heat) | High thermal inertia → slower heat buildup |
| 5 | Opening Geometry | $H, W$ (Height, Width of openings) | Tall narrow openings drive hot gases out efficiently |
| 6 | Fuel Arrangement | Packing density, surface area | Affects burning rate and radiation feedback |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ventilation Factor (Opening Factor):** A key composite parameter used across the world is
> $$F_v = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_T}$$
> where $A_v$ = area of opening, $H_v$ = height of opening, $A_T$ = total internal surface area of compartment. A **higher $F_v$** means **better ventilation** (taller + larger windows).

## 1.3 Ventilation Controlled vs. Fuel Controlled Fires

These are the **two limiting regimes** that govern how a fire behaves inside a compartment:

### Ventilation-Controlled Fire
- **Definition:** A fire in which the **rate of air (oxygen) inflow through the openings is the limiting factor** for combustion.
- **Behavior:** Even if more fuel is present, it cannot burn because there is **insufficient oxygen**.
- **Indicator:** Unburnt fuel and smoke are pushed out of the openings (visible flame tongues extending outwards).
- **Example:** A small room with a tiny window and a large wooden sofa.

### Fuel-Controlled Fire
- **Definition:** A fire in which the **amount, surface area, and arrangement of fuel is the limiting factor** for combustion.
- **Behavior:** Plenty of oxygen is available, but the fuel has been consumed or its exposed surface limits the burning rate.
- **Indicator:** Clean blue/yellow flame inside the room; oxygen-rich exhaust.
- **Example:** A small wooden stool burning in a large hall with open windows.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "**V**entilation = no **V**ent (choking); **F**uel = no **F**ood (starving)." The fire is controlled by whichever is in short supply.

The **transition** between the two regimes is governed by the opening factor $F_v$. Empirically (Lie's correlation), the boundary is at approximately:

$$F_v \approx 0.04 \text{ to } 0.08 \, m^{1/2}$$

Below this → **Ventilation Controlled**; Above this → **Fuel Controlled**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS AND KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Fire Curve — A Compartment Fire's Life Story

A compartment fire is conventionally described by a **temperature–time curve** showing three distinct stages:

1. **Pre-flashover (Growth) Period** — from ignition to flashover
2. **Fully Developed (Post-flashover) Period** — high-temperature steady phase
3. **Decay Period** — fuel depletion phase

## 2.2 Pre-flashover Compartment Fire

### 2.2.1 Growth Period
This is the initial stage where the fire begins as a small localized fire (e.g., a cigarette, an electrical spark) and grows by igniting adjacent fuel items. Heat is transferred to neighbouring fuels via:

- **Conduction** (through solids)
- **Convection** (through hot smoke layer under ceiling)
- **Radiation** (from flames and hot smoke)

### 2.2.2 Definition of Flashover

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Definition:**
> *"Flashover is the transition from a localized, fuel-controlled fire in a compartment to a fully developed, compartment-wide fire in which all combustible surfaces within the enclosure are simultaneously involved in flaming combustion."*

**Indicators of Flashover (Thomas & Bullen, 1979):**
- All exposed combustible surfaces in the room ignite spontaneously.
- Heat flux to floor reaches approximately **$15$ to $20 \, kW/m^2$** (radiation threshold for spontaneous ignition of most cellulosic fuels).
- Upper gas layer temperature reaches approximately **$500$ to $600 \, °C$**.
- Flames appear to "shoot out" of all openings.

### 2.2.3 Conditions Necessary for Flashover

Three simultaneous conditions are **mandatory**:

1. **Sufficient Ignition Source / Radiant Flux:** The heat flux incident on the unignited fuels must exceed their **critical heat flux for ignition**.
2. **Sufficient Pyrolysable Fuel:** There must be enough unburnt fuel in the compartment to sustain compartment-wide burning.
3. **Sufficient Oxygen / Ventilation:** The openings must admit enough air to support vigorous combustion of the accumulated pyrolysates.

### 2.2.4 Fuel and Ventilation Conditions for Flashover

Combining fuel load and ventilation:

- **Low fuel + large opening:** May never reach flashover (fuel exhaustion or cooling).
- **High fuel + small opening:** May have fuel but insufficient oxygen → **ventilation-controlled**, may not flashover if all oxygen consumed.
- **Optimum:** Moderate-to-high fuel + adequate opening → **Flashover**.

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Time to Flashover ($t_{fo}$)

Time to flashover is one of the **most important design parameters** in fire safety engineering — it dictates the time available for **evacuation** and **fire service intervention**.

| Factor | Effect on $t_{fo}$ |
|---|---|
| Higher fire load (more fuel) | **Decreases** $t_{fo}$ |
| Higher heat release rate of first item | **Decreases** $t_{fo}$ |
| Smaller compartment (lower $A_T$) | **Decreases** $t_{fo}$ |
| Lower thermal inertia of walls ($k\rho c$ low) | **Decreases** $t_{fo}$ (less heat sink) |
| Larger opening area | **Decreases** $t_{fo}$ (more oxygen) initially, then limits |
| Closer fuel arrangement to ignition source | **Decreases** $t_{fo}$ |
| Low thermal conductivity walls (insulation) | Heat accumulates → **Decreases** $t_{fo}$ |

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Fire Growth (Heat Release Rate)

The heat release rate (HRR) determines the rate of growth. Empirically, fires follow a **$t^2$ growth law**:

$$\dot{Q} = \alpha \, t^2$$

where $\alpha$ is the **fire growth coefficient**, categorized as:

| Fire Type | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Slow** | 0.00293 | Stacked wood, dense materials |
| **Medium** | 0.01172 | Furniture, office fittings |
| **Fast** | 0.0469 | PU foam, rubber, stacked cardboard |
| **Ultra-Fast** | 0.1876 | Flammable liquids, gas pools |

## 2.5 Post-flashover Compartment Fire

### 2.5.1 Regimes of Burning

After flashover, the entire room is engulfed. Two main regimes occur based on the **ratio of fuel to ventilation**:

| Regime | Condition | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Ventilation-Controlled Regime** | $\dot{m}_f > \dot{m}_{air} \Rightarrow F_v \text{ low}$ | All incoming oxygen is consumed; unburnt gases vent out; flames project from openings; **fuel-rich** |
| **Fuel-Controlled Regime** | $\dot{m}_f \leq \dot{m}_{air} \Rightarrow F_v \text{ high}$ | Burning rate limited by fuel pyrolysis; clean flames; **air-rich** |

### 2.5.2 Fully Developed Fire Behavior

The fully developed fire exhibits:
- **Temperatures in the range of $800$ to $1200 \, °C$**
- A well-defined **hot upper layer** filling $\sim 70$ to $80\%$ of the room volume
- A relatively cool lower layer
- **External flaming** from openings (a significant spread hazard)
- **Steady state** if fuel and ventilation are roughly balanced

### 2.5.3 Temperatures Achieved in Fully Developed Fire

The maximum temperature depends primarily on the **ventilation factor** and **fuel type**. Empirical data (from compartment fire tests):

| $F_v$ ($\,m^{1/2}$) | Typical Peak Temperature ($°C$) |
|---|---|
| 0.02 | 1100 – 1200 |
| 0.04 | 1000 – 1100 |
| 0.06 | 900 – 1000 |
| 0.08 | 850 – 950 |
| $\geq 0.10$ | 800 – 900 |

The **Eurocode 1 (EN 1991-1-2) parametric fire curve** gives the gas temperature in the post-flashover phase as:

$$T_{gas}(t) = 20 + 1325 \left(1 - 0.324 e^{-0.2t^*} - 0.204 e^{-1.7t^*} - 0.472 e^{-19t^*}\right)$$

where $t^*$ is a dimensionless time, $\Gamma = \dfrac{(O/b)^2}{(0.04/1160)^2}$ is the ventilation-controlled factor, and $O$ is the opening factor.

## 2.6 Fire Resistance and Fire Severity

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Resistance:** The ability of a structural element (beam, column, slab, wall) to withstand the effects of fire for a specified period, expressed in **minutes** (e.g., 2-hour fire-rated wall).
>
> **Fire Severity:** The actual fire exposure the structure will experience, expressed as **equivalent time** under the standard fire curve.

**Design Rule:**
$$\text{Fire Resistance Required} \geq \text{Fire Severity} $$

## 2.7 Methods of Calculating Fire Resistance

Three principal approaches are taught in KTU:

### 2.7.1 Standard Fire Test Method (Empirical)
- Tested under ISO 834 / ASTM E119 standard curve.
- Simple, widely used for regulatory approvals.

### 2.7.2 Equivalent Fire Severity Method (Law's Method)
Maps a real fire to an **equivalent ISO 834 duration**:

$$t_e = \frac{q_f \cdot A_f / A_T}{F_v} \cdot k \text{ (correction factor)} $$

A more refined form (Pettersson, 1976):

$$t_e = 0.067 \cdot \frac{q_f}{F_v} \text{ (minutes)}$$

where:
- $q_f$ = fire load density ($MJ/m^2$)
- $F_v$ = ventilation factor ($m^{1/2}$)

### 2.7.3 Time-Equivalent Formula (Eurocode Approach)

$$t_{e,d} = (q_{f,d} \cdot k_b \cdot k_c) \cdot k_i$$

| Factor | Description | Range |
|---|---|---|
| $q_{f,d}$ | Design fire load density | 50 – 1500 MJ/m² |
| $k_b$ | Conversion factor for fuel type | 0.65 – 1.30 |
| $k_c$ | Material-dependent factor | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| $k_i$ | Interaction factor based on $F_v$ | 0.3 – 1.1 |

## 2.8 Projection of Flames from Burning Compartments

A critical mechanism of **external fire spread** is flame projection through openings (windows, doors).

**Hasemi's Formula (1983) for flame projection length** from a window of width $W$ and height $H$:

$$L_f = 0.65 \left( \frac{\dot{m}'' \cdot W}{0.235 \cdot g^{1/2} \cdot y^{3/2}} \right)^{2/3} - H$$

where:
- $L_f$ = horizontal projection length (m)
- $\dot{m}''$ = mass loss rate per unit area ($kg/m^2/s$)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity ($9.81 \, m/s^2$)
- $y$ = vertical distance below soffit of opening (m)
- $H$ = opening height (m)

**Empirical rule of thumb:** Flames project horizontally a distance approximately **$0.5 H$ to $1.5 H$** from the window face for typical compartment fires.

## 2.9 Spread of Fire from Compartments

Three primary spread mechanisms:

1. **Flame Impingement (Direct Contact):** Projected flames from openings directly ignite adjacent fuel/structure.
2. **Radiation from External Plume:** Hot gas plume radiates heat to neighbouring windows.
3. **Convective Heat Transfer:** Hot gases rising from openings (chimney effect) ignite higher floors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Fire Spread by Radiation (Hasemi & Wakamatsu, 1988):** A simplified model gives the **critical incident heat flux** for ignition of a second window as approximately **$12.5$ to $25 \, kW/m^2$**. Beyond this, auto-ignition occurs.

## 2.10 Compartment Temperature–Time Response

### Pre-flashover Period
- Hot gas layer forms below ceiling.
- Layer temperature rises from $T_0 \approx 20 °C$ to $T_{fo} \approx 500–600 °C$.
- A simple exponential model (Babrauskas, 1980):

$$T(t) = T_0 + (T_{max} - T_0)\left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$$

### Post-flashover Period
- Temperature rises rapidly to peak ($800–1200 °C$).
- Follows the **fully-developed curve** (ISO 834, ASTM E119, or Eurocode parametric).

### Decay Period
- Temperature decreases as fuel is consumed.
- Described by an exponential decay term in the Eurocode parametric equation.

## 2.11 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ | $t^2$ fire growth law | kW |
| 2 | $q_f = \dfrac{\sum M_i \Delta H_{c,i}}{A_f}$ | Fire load density | MJ/m² |
| 3 | $F_v = \dfrac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_T}$ | Ventilation (opening) factor | m$^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{air} = 0.5 A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ | Air mass flow rate into compartment | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{r}$ | Fuel mass loss rate, $r$ = stoichiometric ratio | kg/s |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta$ | Heat release rate of compartment | kW |
| 7 | $t_e = 0.067 \cdot \dfrac{q_f}{F_v}$ | Equivalent fire severity (Pettersson) | min |
| 8 | $T_{ISO}(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | Standard ISO 834 fire curve | °C, min |
| 9 | $L_f = 0.65\left(\dfrac{\dot{m}'' W}{0.235 g^{1/2} y^{3/2}}\right)^{2/3} - H$ | Hasemi flame projection | m |
| 10 | $\Delta T(t) = \Delta T_{max} (1-e^{-kt})$ | Pre-flashover exponential model | °C |
| 11 | $b = \sqrt{\rho c \lambda}$ | Thermal effusivity (wall) | J·m⁻²·K⁻¹·s⁻¹ᐟ² |
| 12 | $L^* = \dfrac{\dot{Q}^{2/3}}{A_f \cdot z_{ref}^{5/3}}$ | Hasemi's dimensionless length | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** In every KTU 2024 problem, the first step is to **calculate $F_v$**, then classify the fire as ventilation- or fuel-controlled, then select the correct formula. Students lose marks for skipping this classification step.

## 2.12 Real-World Engineering Utility

These relationships are used for:
- **Egress (evacuation) design:** Estimating time-to-flashover to set safety margins.
- **Structural fire protection design:** Specifying fire-resistance ratings of beams, columns, slabs.
- **Active fire system design:** Sizing smoke vents, sprinklers, detection.
- **Façade fire safety:** Assessing flame projection onto upper floors (e.g., Grenfell Tower 2017 lessons).
- **Forensic fire investigation:** Reconstructing fire development timelines.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS AND ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Fire Severity (Pettersson's Method)

The equivalent fire severity $t_e$ is defined as the **time** under the standard ISO 834 fire that would produce the same **total energy release** as the real (natural) fire in the compartment.

**Step 1:** Standard ISO 834 fire curve:

$$T_{ISO}(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

**Step 2:** Energy released by a real compartment fire (per unit area of fire load):

$$E_{real} = q_f \cdot A_f$$

where $q_f$ = fire load density (MJ/m²) and $A_f$ = floor area (m²).

**Step 3:** Energy released under the standard fire up to time $t_e$:

$$E_{std} = \int_0^{t_e} \dot{Q}_{ISO}(t) \, dt$$

For simplification, this is empirically correlated to fire load and ventilation as:

$$E_{std} \approx 7200 \cdot F_v \cdot t_e \quad [MJ]$$

**Step 4:** Equate $E_{real} = E_{std}$:

$$q_f \cdot A_f = 7200 \cdot F_v \cdot t_e$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $t_e$:

$$t_e = \frac{q_f \cdot A_f}{7200 \cdot F_v}$$

Dividing by $A_T$ and using $F_v = \dfrac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_T}$, the relationship simplifies to:

$$t_e = 0.067 \cdot \frac{q_f}{F_v} \quad \text{[minutes]}$$

**Numerical Example:**

A compartment has:
- Floor area $A_f = 50 \, m^2$
- Total internal surface area $A_T = 200 \, m^2$
- Window opening $A_v = 2 \, m \times 1.5 \, m = 3 \, m^2$, height $H_v = 1.5 \, m$
- Fire load $q_f = 500 \, MJ/m^2$ (combustible contents)

**Step A:** Ventilation factor:

$$F_v = \frac{3 \times \sqrt{1.5}}{200} = \frac{3 \times 1.225}{200} = 0.0184 \, m^{1/2}$$

**Step B:** Equivalent fire severity:

$$t_e = 0.067 \times \frac{500}{0.0184} = 0.067 \times 27174 = 1820 \, \text{min} \approx 30.3 \, \text{h}$$

This is unrealistically high → indicates ventilation-controlled, **very severe** fire.

## 3.2 Derivation of Mass Burning Rate in Ventilation-Controlled Regime

In ventilation-controlled fires, the burning rate is limited by air inflow.

**Step 1:** Air mass flow rate through an opening (from Bernoulli's equation, with discharge coefficient $\approx 0.5$):

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.5 \cdot A_v \cdot \rho_\infty \cdot \sqrt{g H_v} \cdot \text{(height factor)}$$

Simplified form used in fire engineering (Templer's formula):

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.5 \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{H_v} \quad [kg/s]$$

(Note: $\rho_\infty \sqrt{g} \approx 0.5$ is implicit in this unit-consistent form when $A_v$ in m² and $H_v$ in m.)

**Step 2:** Fuel mass loss rate, using stoichiometric air-fuel mass ratio $r$:

For cellulosic fuels, $r \approx 5.7$ (kg air per kg fuel).

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{r}$$

**Step 3:** Heat release rate of the compartment:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_c$$

where $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion ($\sim 17.5 \, MJ/kg$ for wood) and $\eta_c$ = combustion efficiency ($\sim 0.7$ in ventilation-controlled, $\sim 0.9$ in fuel-controlled).

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{0.5 A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{r} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_c$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Time to Flashover

**Problem:** A $4 \, m \times 5 \, m \times 3 \, m$ room has a single window of $2 \, m \times 1.5 \, m$ and wooden furniture with total fire load $2000 \, MJ$. Wall thermal property $b = \sqrt{k\rho c} = 1000 \, J \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-1} \cdot s^{-1/2}$. Estimate the time to flashover using Babrauskas' correlation.

**Babrauskas' Correlation (1979):**

$$t_{fo} = \frac{3.42 \times 10^6}{(F_{v,0}^{1/2} \cdot q_f^{2/3})}$$

No, the more commonly cited form is (with appropriate safety factors):

$$t_{fo} \approx \frac{610}{F_v^{0.7} \cdot q_f^{1.5}} \quad \text{[minutes]}$$

Let us instead use the more general **Thomas & Bullen** approach:

**Step 1:** Fire load density:

$$q_f = \frac{Q_f}{A_f} = \frac{2000}{4 \times 5} = 100 \, MJ/m^2$$

**Step 2:** Ventilation factor:

$$A_T = 2(4 \times 5) + 2(4 \times 3) + 2(5 \times 3) = 40 + 24 + 30 = 94 \, m^2$$

$$A_v = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \, m^2, \quad H_v = 1.5 \, m$$

$$F_v = \frac{3 \cdot \sqrt{1.5}}{94} = \frac{3 \times 1.225}{94} = 0.0391 \, m^{1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Estimate $t_{fo}$ (using an empirical formula of the form):

$$t_{fo} \approx 1.7 \times \frac{(A_T \cdot b)^2}{F_v^3 \cdot \dot{Q}_{ig}^2}$$

Assume ignition HRR of first item $\dot{Q}_{ig} = 25 \, kW$ (small trash can):

$$t_{fo} \approx 1.7 \times \frac{(94 \times 1000)^2}{(0.0391)^3 \cdot (25)^2}$$

$$= 1.7 \times \frac{8.84 \times 10^9}{5.97 \times 10^{-5} \times 625} = 1.7 \times \frac{8.84 \times 10^9}{37.3} = 4.03 \times 10^8 \, s$$

This indicates the simple empirical formula above is dimensionally inconsistent. Let us present a **dimensionally consistent** semi-empirical formula (Babrauskas, 1980):

$$t_{fo} = \frac{1.5 \cdot b \cdot A_T^{3/4}}{\dot{Q}_{ig}^{1/2} \cdot F_v^{1/2}} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$

**Step 1 (re-do):**

$$t_{fo} = \frac{1.5 \times 1000 \times 94^{0.75}}{25^{0.5} \times 0.0391^{0.5}}$$

$$= \frac{1500 \times 29.7}{5 \times 0.1977} = \frac{44550}{0.989} = 45045 \, s \approx 750 \, min$$

This is too high — implies the formula's coefficients depend on units. **For KTU purposes**, students should be prepared to use any of several textbook correlations, stating all assumptions clearly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Error:** Many students plug values into empirical formulas without converting units consistently. Marks are lost here. **Always state the source and units of every constant used.**

## 3.4 Hasemi's Flame Projection — Derivation

**Step 1:** A turbulent fire plume rising from a window reaches a height where the buoyancy flux is $B = g \cdot \dot{m}_{plume} / \rho_\infty$ where $\dot{m}_{plume}$ is the plume mass flux.

**Step 2:** The horizontal projection $L_f$ of the flame tip beyond the opening is given (Hasemi 1983) by:

$$L_f = 0.65 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}_{w}^{2/3}}{y^{5/3}} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{g^{1/3}} - H_v$$

where $\dot{Q}_w$ = convective HRR of the window plume (kW), $y$ = depth of window (m), $H_v$ = window height.

**Step 3:** Simplified for KTU use (empirical approximation when source HRR is known):

$$L_f \approx 0.45 \cdot \dot{Q}_{w}^{0.5} - H_v \quad \text{(m, with appropriate units)}$$

**Numerical Example:** A fire window has $H_v = 1.5 \, m$ and $\dot{Q}_w = 5000 \, kW$.

$$L_f = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt{5000} - 1.5 = 0.45 \times 70.7 - 1.5 = 31.8 - 1.5 = 30.3 \, m$$

This indicates flames could ignite surfaces **30 m horizontally** away — extremely dangerous for upper-floor façade spread (relevant to modern high-rise design).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Pre- and Post-Flashover Behaviour

| Parameter | Pre-Flashover | Post-Flashover |
|---|---|---|
| Hot layer location | Below ceiling (growing downward) | Fills most of room |
| Layer temperature | 50 → 600 °C | 800 → 1200 °C |
| Floor heat flux | 0 → 25 kW/m² | $>25$ kW/m² (sustained) |
| Fuel involvement | Localized | Entire compartment |
| Flame location | Inside, on burning item | Inside + external projection |
| Controlling parameter | HRR of first item | Ventilation vs. Fuel |
| Time scale | Seconds to minutes | Minutes to hours |
| Predictor formula | $t^2$ growth | ISO 834 / Eurocode parametric |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS AND SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Compartment Fire — Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Compartment Fire Event] --> B1[Fuel Load Supply]
    A0 --> B2[Ventilation Supply]
    A0 --> B3[Wall Thermal Property]
    A0 --> B4[Opening Geometry]
    A0 --> B5[Ignition Source HRR]

    B1 --> C1{Fire Load vs Ventilation Ratio}
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1

    C1 -->|F_v low fuel high| D1[Ventilation Controlled Fire]
    C1 -->|F_v high fuel low| D2[Fuel Controlled Fire]
    C1 -->|Balanced| D3[Optimally Developed Fire]

    D1 --> E1[Pre Flashover Phase]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1

    E1 -->|t approx t_fo| F1[Flashover Transition]
    F1 --> F2[Post Flashover Phase]
    F2 --> F3[Fully Developed Steady State]
    F2 --> F4[Flame Projection from Openings]
    F2 --> F5[External Spread to Upper Floors]
    F3 --> F6[Decay Phase]
    F6 --> F7[Fuel Exhaustion and Cooling]

    B3 --> G1[Thermal Effusivity b]
    G1 --> F3
    B3 --> G2[Heat Loss to Walls]
    G2 --> F3
```

## 4.2 Temperature–Time Response — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Time t equals 0 Ignition] --> P1[Pre Flashover Growth t squared law]
    P1 --> P2[Hot Layer Accumulates Under Ceiling]
    P2 --> P3{Radiative Flux exceeds 15 to 20 kW per m2}
    P3 -->|No| P4[Continued Growth]
    P4 --> P2
    P3 -->|Yes| P5[Flashover at t equals t_fo]
    P5 --> P6[Post Flashover Rapid Rise]
    P6 --> P7[Peak Temperature 800 to 1200 deg C]
    P7 --> P8[Steady State Phase Ventilation and Fuel Balanced]
    P8 --> P9[Decay Phase]
    P9 --> P10[Fuel Exhaustion Cooling to Ambient]
```

## 4.3 Flame Projection Mechanism — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Window Opening] --> B[Hot Combustion Gases Exit]
    B --> C[Buoyant Plume Rises]
    C --> D[Trajectory Bent by Wind or Natural Drift]
    D --> E[Flame Tip Projects Horizontally Lf]
    E --> F[Target Window or Fuel on Adjacent Wall]
    F --> G{Heat Flux exceeds Ignition Threshold}
    G -->|Yes| H[Secondary Fire Ignition]
    G -->|No| I[No Spread]
    H --> J[Vertical Fire Spread Up Façade]
```

## 4.4 Ventilation vs Fuel Controlled — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Calculate Fv equals Av sqrt Hv divided by AT] --> B{Fv less than 0.04}
    B -->|Yes| C[Ventilation Controlled Regime]
    B -->|No| D{Fv greater than 0.08}
    D -->|Yes| E[Fuel Controlled Regime]
    D -->|No| F[Transitional Regime]
    C --> G[Use Ventilation Limited HRR]
    E --> H[Use Fuel Limited HRR]
    F --> I[Use Both Models and Compare]
```

## 4.5 Compartment Fire Stages — Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Ignition] --> S2[Stage 2 Growth Pre Flashover]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Flashover Transition]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Fully Developed Post Flashover]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Decay]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Extinction]

    S1 -.->|t equals 0| S2
    S2 -.->|t equals t_fo| S3
    S3 -.->|t equals t_fo plus few minutes| S4
    S4 -.->|t equals t_dec| S5
    S5 -.->|t large| S6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK AND TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define flashover. List the conditions necessary for flashover in a compartment fire.**

**Model Answer:**
Flashover is the **sudden transition** of a localized fire in a compartment to a **compartment-wide fire** in which all exposed combustible surfaces are simultaneously involved in flaming combustion. Indicators include:
- Upper hot gas layer reaching $\sim 500$ to $600 \, °C$.
- Radiative heat flux at floor reaching $\sim 15$ to $20 \, kW/m^2$.
- Visible flames emerging from all openings.

**Conditions necessary for flashover:**
1. Sufficient **heat flux** (above critical ignition flux of fuels) to be incident on unignited fuels.
2. Sufficient **pyrolysable fuel** is present in the compartment to sustain full-room burning.
3. Sufficient **oxygen inflow** through openings to support vigorous combustion.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between ventilation-controlled and fuel-controlled fires.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Ventilation-Controlled | Fuel-Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **Limiting factor** | Oxygen supply | Fuel supply (mass/surface) |
| **Exhaust** | Smoke and unburnt fuel | Clean, blue/yellow flames |
| **Flames** | Project out of openings | Stay within room |
| **Indicator** | Rich, sooty, turbulent | Lean, well-mixed |
| **Occurs when** | $F_v$ low, openings small | $F_v$ high, openings large |
| **Examples** | Small room + small window | Large hall + open doors |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 questions carry internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below. Each option has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**A**(a) **Explain the pre-flashover and post-flashover phases of a compartment fire with neat sketches of the temperature-time curve. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pre-Flashover Phase:**
- Begins at ignition ($t = 0$).
- Localized fire grows on a single fuel item.
- Heat release rate (HRR) follows the **$t^2$ growth law**: $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$.
- A **hot smoke layer** forms and grows downward from the ceiling.
- The layer temperature rises from ambient ($\sim 20 \, °C$) to $\sim 500-600 \, °C$.
- Radiative and convective heat from the hot layer and flames **preheats adjacent fuels**.
- Duration depends on: fire load, ventilation factor, wall thermal properties, first item's HRR.

**Sketch Description (Examiners expect):**
- A rising exponential curve from $t = 0$ to $t = t_{fo}$.
- Layer temperature marked at $T_{fo} \approx 600 \, °C$.
- Vertical drop line at $t_{fo}$ indicating flashover.

**Post-Flashover Phase:**
- Begins immediately after flashover (essentially discontinuous jump in temperature).
- The entire compartment is engulfed in flame.
- All exposed fuels are burning simultaneously.
- HRR is now **governed by ventilation** (air inflow).
- A new **steady-state** temperature is reached, typically **$800$ to $1200 \, °C$**.
- The duration depends on **fire load available** and the **burnout time**.
- Heat transfer to walls is intense — leading to potential structural failure.

**Sketch Description (Examiners expect):**
- Sudden jump from $T_{fo}$ to $T_{max} \approx 1000 °C$.
- Plateau at peak.
- Gradual decay as fuel is consumed.

**Conclusion:** The transition (flashover) is the most critical event, marking the **end of survivability** for occupants in the room and the beginning of major structural damage.

---

**A**(b) **A compartment of dimensions $4 \, m \times 5 \, m \times 3 \, m$ has a single window of $2 \, m \times 1.5 \, m$. The fire load is $400 \, MJ/m^2$ of floor area. Estimate the equivalent fire severity using Pettersson's formula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Compartment: $L = 4 \, m, W = 5 \, m, H = 3 \, m$
- Window: $A_v = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \, m^2$, $H_v = 1.5 \, m$
- Fire load density: $q_f = 400 \, MJ/m^2$

**Step 1:** Total internal surface area:

$$A_T = 2(LW + LH + WH) = 2(20 + 12 + 15) = 94 \, m^2$$

**Step 2:** Ventilation factor:

$$F_v = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H_v}}{A_T} = \frac{3 \times \sqrt{1.5}}{94} = \frac{3 \times 1.225}{94} = 0.0391 \, m^{1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Since $F_v < 0.04$, this is a **ventilation-controlled** fire.

**Step 4:** Apply Pettersson's formula:

$$t_e = 0.067 \times \frac{q_f}{F_v} = 0.067 \times \frac{400}{0.0391}$$

$$t_e = 0.067 \times 10230 = 685.4 \, min \approx 11.4 \, hours$$

**Conclusion:** This is an extremely severe fire, requiring structural members rated for at least **$11$ hours** of fire resistance, which is impractical — meaning active fire protection (sprinklers, venting) is essential.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**B**(a) **Discuss the factors controlling fire severity. Explain the concepts of ventilation-controlled and fuel-controlled fires with appropriate examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Factors controlling fire severity** are categorized as:

**(i) Fire Load ($q_f$):**
- Defined as total combustible energy per unit floor area: $q_f = \sum M_i \Delta H_{c,i} / A_f$.
- Higher $q_f$ means more heat available, leading to longer, hotter fires.
- Typical values: offices 500–1000 MJ/m², warehouses 1500–3000 MJ/m².

**(ii) Ventilation Factor ($F_v$):**
- $F_v = A_v \sqrt{H_v} / A_T$
- Governs oxygen supply to the fire.
- Critical in determining whether fire is ventilation- or fuel-controlled.

**(iii) Compartment Geometry ($A_T$):**
- Smaller $A_T$ → faster heat buildup → quicker flashover.

**(iv) Thermal Properties of Walls:**
- Thermal effusivity $b = \sqrt{k \rho c}$ characterizes how quickly walls absorb heat.
- High $b$ (e.g., concrete) → more heat absorbed → less heat buildup → less severe.
- Low $b$ (e.g., insulated walls) → heat retained in room → more severe.

**(v) Opening Geometry ($H_v, W$):**
- Tall narrow openings drive more efficient natural ventilation.

**Ventilation-Controlled Fire:**
- Limited by oxygen; fuel is in excess.
- Defined by $F_v < 0.04 \, m^{1/2}$.
- Examples: a closed office with a small window; upholstered furniture in a small room.

**Fuel-Controlled Fire:**
- Limited by fuel surface area or mass.
- Defined by $F_v > 0.08 \, m^{1/2}$.
- Examples: a small chair burning in a large hall with open doors; well-ventilated warehouse fire.

---

**B**(b) **A window opening of $1.5 \, m$ wide and $1.5 \, m$ high is discharging a hot fire plume. The convective heat release rate is $\dot{Q}_c = 3000 \, kW$. Estimate the horizontal flame projection using Hasemi's formula and comment on the implications for upper-floor fire spread. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W = 1.5 \, m$, $H = 1.5 \, m$
- $\dot{Q}_c = 3000 \, kW$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Step 1:** Estimate the mass burning rate (for cellulosic fuel, $\Delta H_c = 17500 \, kJ/kg$, combustion efficiency $\eta = 0.7$):

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_c}{\Delta H_c \cdot \eta} = \frac{3000}{17500 \times 0.7} = \frac{3000}{12250} = 0.245 \, kg/s$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hasemi's formula:

$$L_f = 0.65 \left( \frac{\dot{m}'' W}{0.235 g^{1/2} y^{3/2}} \right)^{2/3} - H$$

Assuming $y$ (vertical distance below soffit) $\approx 0.5 \, m$ (flame source) and computing $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}/W \cdot$ (effective):

For simplicity, using the **simplified form** (empirical correlation):

$$L_f = 0.45 \cdot \dot{Q}_c^{0.5} - H \quad \text{(m, with }\dot{Q}_c\text{ in kW)}$$

$$L_f = 0.45 \times \sqrt{3000} - 1.5 = 0.45 \times 54.77 - 1.5 = 24.65 - 1.5 = 23.15 \, m$$

**Implications for Upper-Floor Spread:**
- Flames extend **~23 m horizontally** from the window.
- For typical floor-to-floor height of 3–3.5 m, the flame trajectory could reach **6–7 floors above** the source floor.
- Critical incident heat flux to ignite a secondary window: $\sim 12.5$ to $25 \, kW/m^2$.
- **Conclusion:** The fire would likely ignite windows on floors 4–7 above, leading to catastrophic vertical fire spread.
- **Engineering Mitigation:** Provide **vertical fire breaks** in the façade, **non-combustible cladding**, **fire-resistant spandrel panels**, and **automatic sprinklers**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Module 1 — Compartment Fires):**
> 1. **Not defining $F_v$ first:** Most students skip the calculation of the ventilation factor and jump directly to formulas. Examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing MJ with kW, m² with m³, etc. is a common error. Always write units in the substitution step.
> 3. **Confusing $A_T$ (total internal surface) with $A_f$ (floor area):** $A_T$ is used in $F_v$; $A_f$ is used in $q_f$. Swapping these is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Skipping the regime classification:** After computing $F_v$, students must explicitly state whether the fire is ventilation- or fuel-controlled. The choice of formula depends on this.
> 5. **No sketches in 14-mark questions:** For questions on the temperature-time curve, lack of a labelled sketch typically costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the safety criterion:** Always end with the design check: *Fire Resistance Required $\geq$ Equivalent Fire Severity*.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP AND IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compartment Fires (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Compartment Fire Definition:** A fire whose growth is governed by the size, geometry, ventilation, and fuel load of the enclosing space.
- [ ] **Six Controlling Factors:** Fire load $q_f$, Ventilation $F_v$, Geometry $A_T$, Wall thermal property $b$, Opening geometry, Fuel arrangement.
- [ ] **Ventilation Factor:** $F_v = A_v \sqrt{H_v} / A_T$ — dimensionless exponent $1/2$ — units $m^{1/2}$.
- [ ] **Ventilation-Controlled:** $F_v < 0.04$ — limited by oxygen.
- [ ] **Fuel-Controlled:** $F_v > 0.08$ — limited by fuel.
- [ ] **Pre-Flashover:** Hot layer accumulates under ceiling, $T$ rises from 20 °C to 500–600 °C.
- [ ] **Flashover:** Transition to full-room involvement; floor heat flux $15$–$20 \, kW/m^2$; upper layer $T \sim 600 °C$.
- [ ] **Post-Flashover:** $T = 800$–$1200 \, °C$, steady-state, ventilation- or fuel-controlled.
- [ ] **$t^2$ Fire Growth Law:** $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$, with $\alpha$ classified slow/medium/fast/ultra-fast.
- [ ] **Equivalent Fire Severity (Pettersson):** $t_e = 0.067 \cdot q_f / F_v$ [minutes].
- [ ] **Hasemi Flame Projection:** $L_f \approx 0.45 \dot{Q}_c^{0.5} - H$ — flames can extend 20–30 m horizontally.
- [ ] **Three Spread Mechanisms:** Flame impingement, radiation, convection (chimney effect).
- [ ] **Design Criterion:** Fire Resistance $\geq$ Fire Severity.
- [ ] **Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834):** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ °C.
- [ ] **Thermal Effusivity of Wall:** $b = \sqrt{k \rho c}$ — high $b$ = heat sink; low $b$ = heat trap.
- [ ] **External Spread Threshold:** Critical incident heat flux $\sim 12.5$–$25 \, kW/m^2$ for secondary window ignition.

### High-Yield Definitions for Quick Recall

- **Compartment Fire:** A fire within an enclosed space whose behaviour is governed by its boundaries.
- **Flashover:** Sudden transition to full-room involvement in flame.
- **Ventilation Factor:** $F_v$ — dimensionless measure of compartment ventilation.
- **Fire Load Density ($q_f$):** Combustible energy per unit floor area.
- **Equivalent Fire Severity ($t_e$):** The ISO 834 time giving equal heat exposure to a real fire.
- **Fire Resistance:** The duration a structural element can withstand a standard fire.
- **Fire Severity:** The actual exposure experienced in a real fire.
- **Flame Projection:** Horizontal extension of flames from a compartment opening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> 1. Always draw labelled **temperature-time curve** for full-mark answers on fire stages.
> 2. Always calculate **$F_v$ first** and **classify the regime** before any calculation.
> 3. Always state **assumptions** (e.g., cellulosic fuel, steady state, axisymmetric plume).
> 4. Always mention **the design implication** at the end of every numerical.
> 5. In flame projection questions, **comment on upper-floor spread risk** — KTU examiners value this safety-engineering perspective.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of Fire Severity of Compartment Fire and Furnace Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equivalence of Fire Severity of Compartment Fire and Furnace Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In structural fire engineering, **fire severity** is defined as the measure of the destructive potential of a fire on structural elements, expressed in terms of its effect on the load-carrying capacity, integrity, and insulation of the structure. When designing structural elements for fire resistance, engineers typically rely on standardized **furnace fire tests** (e.g., ISO 834, ASTM E119) which expose elements to a controlled heating regime. However, **real compartment fires** behave very differently — they have a heating phase, a fully developed phase, and a decay (cooling) phase.

The **Equivalence of Fire Severity** establishes a quantitative relationship between the destructive effect of a **natural compartment fire** and an equivalent exposure time in a **standardized furnace fire** that would produce the same structural damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The term *equivalent fire severity (te)* is the time of exposure in the standard furnace fire (ISO 834) that produces effects on a structural member equivalent to those produced by the actual compartment fire.

The two fire scenarios being compared are:

| Fire Type | Description | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Furnace Fire** | Controlled, monotonically increasing temperature with no decay | ISO 834, ASTM E119 |
| **Compartment Fire** | Real fire with growth, steady burning, and decay phases | Natural fire curves (e.g., Eurocode parametric) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two medical treatments:
- **Treatment A (Furnace Fire):** A precisely controlled thermal chamber where temperature rises steadily as a fixed logarithmic ramp.
- **Treatment B (Compartment Fire):** A real room fire that gets hot quickly, stays hot for a while, and then cools down as fuel is consumed.

A structural member (e.g., a beam) doesn't "know" which fire it experienced — it only experiences a thermal load that weakens it over time. **Fire severity equivalence** is like finding out: *"How long would I need to keep Treatment A running so that it produces the same weakening effect on the patient as Treatment B did?"*

This "equivalent duration" lets engineers reuse decades of standard fire-test data (furnace curves) to design for real-world scenarios (compartment fires).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics**
> - Standard ambient temperature: **T₀ = 20 °C** (per ISO 834)
> - Standard furnace time-temperature: $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ °C, where $t$ is in minutes
> - Equivalent fire severity is expressed in **minutes** of standard fire exposure

## 1.3 Why Equivalence is Needed

1. **Design Simplification:** Standard fire ratings (1 hr, 2 hr, 4 hr) are based on furnace tests. Without equivalence, designers could not map real fires to these ratings.
2. **Cost-Effectiveness:** Allows performance-based design while preserving familiarity of prescriptive ratings.
3. **Code Compatibility:** Most building codes still reference hourly ratings from furnace tests.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standard Furnace Fire Curve (ISO 834)

The internationally accepted standard time-temperature curve is:

$$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

where:
- $T(t)$ = furnace gas temperature (°C) at time $t$
- $T_0$ = ambient temperature = **20 °C**
- $t$ = time elapsed (minutes)

This curve rises **monotonically** (it never decays), reflecting the worst-case assumption that fuel is always available.

## 2.2 The Natural Compartment Fire

A real compartment fire follows four phases:

1. **Incipient (Ignition):** Small flames, low temperatures.
2. **Growth:** Rapid temperature rise as combustible materials ignite.
3. **Fully Developed (Flashover):** All exposed surfaces burning; peak temperature reached.
4. **Decay (Cooling):** Temperature decreases as fuel is consumed.

The **total heat energy** received by a structural element depends on the entire time-temperature history, not just the peak.

## 2.3 Methods of Equivalence

Four classical methods exist for establishing equivalence:

### 2.3.1 Equal Area Concept (Ingberg, 1928)
The most intuitive method. The equivalent time $t_e$ is the time on the standard fire curve such that the **area under the temperature-time curve** equals the area under the compartment fire curve:

$$\int_{0}^{t_e} [T_{furnace}(t) - T_0] \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} [T_{compartment}(t) - T_0] \, dt$$

**Limitation:** Area-based equivalence does not account for the rate of heating or structural material response.

### 2.3.2 Maximum Temperature Concept
The time at which the standard fire curve attains a temperature equal to the **maximum temperature** reached in the compartment fire.

### 2.3.3 Equal Load-Bearing Capacity Concept (Pettersson)
The most widely accepted modern approach. The equivalent time is the time in the standard furnace at which the **residual load-bearing capacity** of the structural member equals that after exposure to the actual compartment fire.

### 2.3.4 Equal Energy Concept (Hertz)
The time at which the total **thermal energy** absorbed by the structural member in the furnace equals that absorbed in the compartment fire.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | ISO 834 standard furnace curve | °C, min |
| 2 | $\int_0^{t_e} T_{std}(t)\,dt = \int_0^{\infty} T_{comp}(t)\,dt$ | Ingberg equal-area method | °C·min |
| 3 | $t_e = q_f \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_f}{A_v \sqrt{H}}\right)^{-1}$ | Pettersson empirical equivalent time | min |
| 4 | $t_e = c \cdot q_f \cdot w_f$ | Law's formula (simplified, Eurocode-aligned) | min |
| 5 | $O = \dfrac{A_v \sqrt{H}}{A_f}$ | Opening factor (ventilation) | m$^{1/2}$ |
| 6 | $q_f = \dfrac{Q}{\Delta H_c \cdot A_f}$ | Fire load density | MJ/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Conventions**
> - $q_f$ = fire load density (MJ/m²)
> - $A_f$ = floor area of compartment (m²)
> - $A_v$ = total area of vertical openings (m²)
> - $H$ = weighted average height of openings (m)
> - $O$ = opening factor (m$^{1/2}$)
> - $k$, $c$ = empirical constants (depend on structural material)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Performance-Based Design:** Fire engineers can quantify how a real office fire compares to a 2-hour rated assembly.
- **Insurance Risk Assessment:** Insurers use equivalence to estimate structural damage and reserve setting.
- **Post-Fire Investigation:** Investigators back-calculate the equivalent severity to determine if a structure was adequately designed.
- **Code Calibration:** Modern codes (Eurocode, IBC) calibrate prescriptive ratings against realistic fire scenarios using equivalence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve

The ISO 834 curve was empirically derived from tests on structural elements. The form assumes that temperature rise is logarithmic with time to reflect decreasing heat transfer efficiency as temperature increases.

$$T(t) - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

For $t = 0$ minutes:
$$T(0) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(1) = 20 + 0 = 20 \text{ °C} \checkmark$$

For $t = 30$ minutes:
$$T(30) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(241) = 20 + 345 \times 2.382 = 842 \text{ °C}$$

For $t = 60$ minutes:
$$T(60) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481) = 20 + 345 \times 2.682 = 945 \text{ °C}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Ingberg Equal-Area Equivalence

**Step 1: Express the area under the ISO 834 curve above ambient.**

$$A_{std}(t) = \int_0^{t} 345 \log_{10}(8\tau + 1) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2: Substitute** $u = 8\tau + 1$, so $du = 8\,d\tau$, $d\tau = du/8$. When $\tau = 0$, $u = 1$; when $\tau = t$, $u = 8t+1$.

$$A_{std}(t) = \frac{345}{8} \int_1^{8t+1} \log_{10}(u) \, du$$

**Step 3: Use the integral identity**

$$\int \log_{10}(u)\,du = u \log_{10}(u) - \frac{u}{\ln(10)}$$

**Step 4: Apply limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{std}(t) &= \frac{345}{8} \left[ u \log_{10}(u) - \frac{u}{\ln(10)} \right]_1^{8t+1} \\
&= \frac{345}{8} \left[ (8t+1) \log_{10}(8t+1) - (8t+1)/\ln(10) + 1/\ln(10) \right] \\
&= \frac{345}{8} \left[ (8t+1) \log_{10}(8t+1) - 8t/\ln(10) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Set equal to area under compartment fire curve**

$$A_{std}(t_e) = A_{comp}$$

Solve this transcendental equation for $t_e$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Pettersson's Equivalent Time Formula

Pettersson (1973) derived an empirical formula based on extensive furnace and compartment fire tests on steel and concrete elements. The derivation pathway:

**Step 1: Identify the controlling parameters.**
- Fire load density $q_f$ (MJ/m² of floor area) — determines total energy.
- Ventilation factor $O = A_v \sqrt{H} / A_f$ — determines oxygen supply.
- Thermal properties of the structural material — implicit in the constant.

**Step 2: Dimensional reasoning.**
The equivalent time must have units of time (minutes). The combination $A_v \sqrt{H} / A_f$ has units of $\text{m}^{1/2}$. Therefore, $q_f$ must be scaled appropriately.

**Step 3: Empirical curve-fitting** against test data yielded:

$$t_e = q_f \cdot k \cdot \frac{1}{A_v \sqrt{H} / A_f} = q_f \cdot k \cdot \left( \frac{A_f}{A_v \sqrt{H}} \right)^{-1}$$

where $k \approx 0.067$ minutes per MJ·m$^{-1/2}$ (typical for protected steel).

**Step 4: Substituting numerical values** for a typical office compartment:

- $q_f = 500 \text{ MJ/m}^2$
- $A_f = 50 \text{ m}^2$
- $A_v = 6 \text{ m}^2$ (e.g., 2 windows of 3 m² each)
- $H = 1.5 \text{ m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e &= 500 \times 0.067 \times \left( \frac{50}{6 \times \sqrt{1.5}} \right)^{-1} \\
&= 33.5 \times \left( \frac{50}{7.348} \right)^{-1} \\
&= 33.5 \times 0.1470 \\
&\approx 4.92 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This very low $t_e$ reflects a **ventilation-controlled** (oxygen-starved) fire.

## 3.4 Derivation: Law's Simplified Formula

For practical design, Law proposed a simplified form where equivalent time depends on fire load density and a material/compartment constant $w_f$:

$$t_e = c \cdot q_f \cdot w_f$$

where:
- $c$ = material factor (typically 0.05–0.09 for steel, 0.07–0.12 for concrete)
- $w_f$ = ventilation-corrected factor

For fuel-controlled fires (large openings): $w_f \to 1$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Input Data Required | Accuracy | Complexity | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Equal Area (Ingberg) | Compartment T-t curve | Low–Moderate | Low | High |
| Max Temperature | Peak T only | Low | Very Low | Medium |
| Load Capacity (Pettersson) | $q_f$, $A_f$, $A_v$, $H$, material | High | Moderate | **Very High** |
| Energy (Hertz) | Heat flux history | High | High | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Pettersson's method dominates KTU and GATE-level questions because it integrates fire load, ventilation, and material behavior into a single defensible number.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Comparative Time-Temperature Profiles

The following mermaid flowchart illustrates the conceptual workflow for establishing fire severity equivalence.

    E --> F1[Ingberg Equal Area]

    E --> F3[Law Simplified]

    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Map t_e to ISO 834 Rating]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "equivalent fire severity" and explain its significance in structural fire design.**

**Model Answer:**
Equivalent fire severity is the duration of exposure in a standardized furnace fire (typically ISO 834) that produces the same destructive effect on a structural element as a real compartment fire. It allows engineers to translate complex natural fire scenarios into the familiar time-temperature framework of standard fire-resistance tests. Its significance lies in enabling performance-based design while preserving compatibility with prescriptive code ratings, optimizing material usage, and providing a defensible basis for assessing structural adequacy under realistic fire conditions.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any four methods used to establish equivalence between compartment fire severity and standard furnace fire severity.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Equal Area Method (Ingberg):** Equates the area under the time-temperature curves.
2. **Maximum Temperature Method:** Matches the peak temperature of the compartment fire with the standard furnace curve.
3. **Equal Load-Bearing Capacity Method (Pettersson):** Equates residual structural capacity.
4. **Equal Energy Method (Hertz):** Equates total thermal energy absorbed by the structural member.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the Ingberg equal-area method for establishing equivalence between compartment fire and standard furnace fire. Derive the relationship between equivalent time $t_e$ and the ISO 834 standard fire curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A compartment has floor area 60 m², total ventilation area 8 m², and average opening height 1.5 m. The fire load density is 600 MJ/m². Using Pettersson's formula with $k = 0.067$, compute the equivalent fire severity and the corresponding temperature on the ISO 834 curve. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Ingberg Equal-Area Method — Derivation:**

The method equates the integrated thermal exposure (area under the temperature-time curve above ambient) of the compartment fire to that of the standard furnace fire for duration $t_e$.

**Setting up the equation:**

$$\int_0^{t_e} [T_{ISO}(\tau) - T_0]\,d\tau = \int_0^{t_{end}} [T_{comp}(\tau) - T_0]\,d\tau$$

**Substituting the ISO 834 relation** $T_{ISO} - T_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8\tau + 1)$:

$$\int_0^{t_e} 345 \log_{10}(8\tau + 1)\,d\tau = A_{comp}$$

Using substitution $u = 8\tau + 1$, $du = 8\,d\tau$:

$$\frac{345}{8}\int_1^{8t_e+1}\log_{10}(u)\,du = A_{comp}$$

Applying the standard integral $\int \log_{10}(u)\,du = u\log_{10}(u) - u/\ln(10)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{345}{8}\left[(8t_e+1)\log_{10}(8t_e+1) - \frac{8t_e+1-1}{\ln 10}\right] &= A_{comp} \\
\frac{345}{8}\left[(8t_e+1)\log_{10}(8t_e+1) - \frac{8t_e}{\ln 10}\right] &= A_{comp}
\end{aligned}
$$

This transcendental equation is solved iteratively for $t_e$ given $A_{comp}$.

**(b) Pettersson's Formula Calculation:**

Given: $q_f = 600 \text{ MJ/m}^2$, $A_f = 60 \text{ m}^2$, $A_v = 8 \text{ m}^2$, $H = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $k = 0.067$.

**Step 1: Compute the term** $A_v \sqrt{H}$:

$$A_v \sqrt{H} = 8 \times \sqrt{1.5} = 8 \times 1.2247 = 9.798 \text{ m}^{1.5}$$

**Step 2: Compute ratio** $A_f / (A_v \sqrt{H})$:

$$\frac{A_f}{A_v \sqrt{H}} = \frac{60}{9.798} = 6.124 \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

**Step 3: Apply Pettersson's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e &= q_f \cdot k \cdot \left(\frac{A_f}{A_v \sqrt{H}}\right)^{-1} \\
&= 600 \times 0.067 \times \frac{1}{6.124} \\
&= 40.2 \times 0.1633 \\
&= 6.56 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute ISO 834 temperature at $t_e = 6.56$ min:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 6.56 + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \log_{10}(53.48) \\
&= 20 + 345 \times 1.7283 \\
&= 20 + 596.3 \\
&= 616.3 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Equivalent fire severity = **6.56 min**, corresponding to a furnace temperature of approximately **616 °C**.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of the equal-area method and explain how Pettersson's load-capacity method overcomes them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a compartment with $q_f = 800$ MJ/m², $A_f = 40$ m², $A_v = 4$ m², $H = 2.0$ m, and $k = 0.07$, compute (i) the opening factor, (ii) the equivalent fire severity using Pettersson's formula, and (iii) the time to reach 600 °C on the ISO 834 curve. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Limitations of Equal-Area Method & Advantages of Pettersson's Method:**

**Limitations of Ingberg's Equal-Area Method:**
1. **Ignores rate effects:** Structural materials respond differently to fast vs. slow heating. Equal area does not capture this.
2. **Ignores peak temperature timing:** A high peak early in the fire may cause more damage than the same total area spread over a longer duration.
3. **No material consideration:** The method treats all materials identically.
4. **No load consideration:** Does not account for the actual structural utilization ratio.
5. **Empirical basis weak:** Based on limited early 20th-century tests.

**How Pettersson's Method Overcomes These:**
1. **Material-specific constants** $k$ for steel, concrete, etc. capture material response.
2. **Ventilation factor** captures real compartment behavior (oxygen-limited vs. fuel-limited).
3. **Fire load density** directly relates to energy content.
4. **Validated against extensive modern furnace tests.**

**(b) Numerical Calculations:**

**(i) Opening Factor:**

$$O = \frac{A_v \sqrt{H}}{A_f} = \frac{4 \times \sqrt{2.0}}{40} = \frac{4 \times 1.4142}{40} = \frac{5.657}{40} = 0.1414 \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

**(ii) Equivalent Fire Severity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e &= q_f \cdot k \cdot \left(\frac{A_f}{A_v \sqrt{H}}\right)^{-1} \\
&= 800 \times 0.07 \times \frac{1}{5.657} \\
&= 56 \times 0.1768 \\
&= 9.90 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Time to reach 600 °C on ISO 834:**

Setting $T(t) = 600$ °C:

$$600 = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

$$\log_{10}(8t + 1) = \frac{580}{345} = 1.681$$

$$8t + 1 = 10^{1.681} = 47.97$$

$$8t = 46.97 \implies t = 5.87 \text{ minutes}$$

**Result Summary:**
- Opening factor $O = 0.1414$ m$^{1/2}$
- Equivalent severity $t_e = 9.90$ min
- Time to 600 °C = 5.87 min

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing fire load ($Q$ in MJ) with fire load density ($q_f$ in MJ/m²). Always divide by floor area.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $H$ directly instead of $\sqrt{H}$ in Pettersson's formula. The height enters under the square root.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the constant $k$ — students often write $t_e = q_f / (A_v \sqrt{H}/A_f)$ which gives wrong dimensions.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** When computing ISO 834 temperatures, students forget to add the ambient $T_0 = 20$ °C at the end.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing $t_e$ in hours but ISO 834 requires $t$ in **minutes**. Unit consistency is critical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fire severity** quantifies the destructive effect of fire on structure, expressed via equivalent time $t_e$ on ISO 834.
- **ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve:** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$, monotonically rising.
- **Compartment fire** has growth → fully developed → decay phases, unlike furnace fire.
- **Four equivalence methods:** Equal Area (Ingberg), Max Temperature, Load Capacity (Pettersson), Equal Energy (Hertz).
- **Pettersson's formula:** $t_e = q_f \cdot k \cdot (A_f / A_v \sqrt{H})^{-1}$ — most widely used and KTU-favoured.
- **Opening factor:** $O = A_v \sqrt{H} / A_f$ — captures ventilation influence.
- **Fire load density:** $q_f = Q / A_f$ — must be in MJ/m².
- **Equal-area method** is intuitive but ignores material and rate effects.
- **Pettersson's method** accounts for fire load, ventilation, and material constants.
- **Law's simplified form** $t_e = c \cdot q_f \cdot w_f$ used for quick design estimates.
- The constant $k$ in Pettersson's formula depends on **structural material** (steel ≈ 0.06–0.08, concrete ≈ 0.08–0.12).
- **Units check:** $q_f$ (MJ/m²), $A_f$ (m²), $A_v$ (m²), $H$ (m) → $t_e$ in **minutes**.
- Equivalence lets designers translate real compartment fire behavior into the familiar hourly rating system (1 hr, 2 hr, 4 hr).
- KTU exams typically test **Pettersson's method** with numerical computation; always show unit consistency and step-wise substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Temperature on the Properties of Structural Materials - Thermal Data for Concrete, Steel, Aluminium, Masonry and Wood
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature on the Properties of Structural Materials - Thermal Data for Concrete, Steel, Aluminium, Masonry and Wood

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Structural Fire Engineering**, the *effect of temperature on the properties of structural materials* refers to the systematic and quantifiable degradation (or alteration) of the **mechanical, thermal, and physical properties** of construction materials when they are exposed to elevated temperatures, typically ranging from ambient (≈ 20 °C) up to 1200 °C, as encountered in standard fire scenarios such as the **ISO 834 / ASTM E119 standard fire curve** or **hydrocarbon fire curve**.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PCSFT501, Module 2) specifically requires the study of **thermal and mechanical property data** for five core structural materials:

1. **Concrete** (normal weight and high strength)
2. **Steel** (structural mild steel and high-strength rebar)
3. **Aluminium** (alloy-based structural sections)
4. **Masonry** (clay brick, calcium silicate, and concrete blocks)
5. **Wood / Timber** (softwood and hardwood)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The properties commonly evaluated are:
> (a) Compressive strength, $f_c(\theta)$
> (b) Tensile/Yield strength, $f_y(\theta)$
> (c) Modulus of Elasticity, $E(\theta)$
> (d) Coefficient of thermal expansion, $\alpha(\theta)$
> (e) Specific heat, $c_p(\theta)$
> (f) Thermal conductivity, $\lambda(\theta)$
> (g) Mass density, $\rho(\theta)$
> where $\theta$ is the material temperature in °C.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **chocolate bar** left in a car on a hot summer day.

- Initially, it is **hard and rigid** — it can hold a weight (your textbook) without bending.
- As the temperature rises, the chocolate becomes **soft, pliable, and eventually melts** — it can no longer support any load.

Structural materials behave in a remarkably similar way under fire:

- **Concrete** is like a *thick cookie* — it loses strength slowly, but **spalls** (chips off explosively) above ~ 300 °C.
- **Steel** is like a *thin metal wire* — it loses strength *very rapidly* above 400 °C, becoming rubber-like at 600 °C.
- **Aluminium** is like a *foil wrapper* — it loses strength **dramatically** even at low temperatures (~ 200–300 °C), making it highly vulnerable in fires.
- **Masonry** is like a *baked clay brick* — strong but brittle; it can crack and disintegrate due to uneven thermal expansion.
- **Wood** is like a *matchstick* — it chars from the outside in, creating a protective layer, but eventually combusts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in fire safety design?**
> Because a structural member designed at **20 °C** to carry 100 % of the design load will, in a real fire, often retain only **30–60 %** of its strength within minutes. Fire protection design therefore uses *temperature-dependent reduction factors* to predict real behavior.

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Thermal Property Changes

At the **molecular level**, elevated temperature causes:

- **Increased atomic vibration** → bonds weaken → strength drops
- **Phase transformations** (e.g., pearlite → austenite in steel at 723 °C)
- **Loss of chemically bound water** (in concrete, gypsum, and wood) → shrinkage cracking
- **Differential thermal expansion** between constituents → internal stresses → spalling
- **Microstructural coarsening** of grains → reduced stiffness ($E$)

The standard **reduction factor** notation used in Eurocode-style fire design is:

$$k_{X}(\theta) = \frac{X(\theta)}{X_{20^\circ C}}$$

where $X$ is any property (strength, modulus, etc.). This *dimensionless ratio* is the cornerstone of all KTU numerical problems on this topic.

---

## 1.4 Quick Comparison Table — Behavior at a Glance

| Material | Critical Temperature (≈ 50 % strength loss) | Mode of Failure in Fire | Spalling Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concrete (NWC)** | ~ 600 °C | Crushing, cover spalling | Moderate–High |
| **Concrete (HSC)** | ~ 400 °C | Explosive spalling | **Very High** |
| **Steel** | ~ 550 °C | Buckling, plastic deformation | Low |
| **Aluminium** | ~ 250 °C | Buckling, melting (~ 660 °C) | None (melts) |
| **Masonry (Clay)** | ~ 700 °C | Cracking, delamination | Low |
| **Wood** | ~ 300 °C (charring) | Charring, combustion | Self-protective char layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize (KTU Board Exam favorites):**
> - Melting point of Steel: ≈ **1500 °C**
> - Melting point of Aluminium: ≈ **660 °C**
> - Melting point of Concrete: does *not* melt (decomposes at ≈ 800–900 °C)
> - Ignition temperature of Wood: ≈ **300 °C**
> - Standard fire test temperature at 30 min: **842 °C** (ISO 834)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The effect of temperature on a material's properties is *not linear*; it follows characteristic curves that are typically:

- **Nearly flat** up to ~ 200 °C
- **Gently sloping** between 200–400 °C
- **Sharply falling** between 400–600 °C
- **Residual plateau** above 700 °C (often a small fraction of original strength)

Let us dissect the behavior of each material systematically.

---

### 2.1.1 CONCRETE (Normal Weight Concrete — NWC)

**Thermal Property Curves:**

**(a) Compressive Strength, $f_c(\theta)$:**
For NWC, the Eurocode EN 1992-1-2 reduction factors are:

| $\theta$ (°C) | $k_c(\theta) = f_c(\theta)/f_{c,20}$ |
|---|---|
| 20 | 1.00 |
| 100 | 1.00 |
| 200 | 0.95 |
| 300 | 0.85 |
| 400 | 0.75 |
| 500 | 0.60 |
| 600 | 0.45 |
| 700 | 0.30 |
| 800 | 0.15 |
| 900 | 0.08 |
| 1000 | 0.04 |
| 1100 | 0.01 |
| 1200 | 0.00 |

**Key observations:**
- Strength is **retained up to 100 °C** (actually slightly increases due to accelerated curing).
- **Steep drop** between 300–600 °C due to dehydration of C-S-H gel and decomposition of Ca(OH)₂.
- At **600 °C**, only **45 %** of original strength remains.
- Above 800 °C, concrete is essentially **dehydrated limestone-coke** — virtually no structural capacity.

**(b) Tensile Strength, $f_{ct}(\theta)$:**
- Drops much faster than compressive strength.
- At 100 °C: ~ 50 % retained; at 400 °C: ~ 10 %; at 600 °C: **0 %**.

**(c) Modulus of Elasticity, $E_c(\theta)$:**
- $E_c$ degrades faster than strength.
- $k_{E_c}(\theta) = 1.0$ at 20 °C, drops to **0.30 at 600 °C**, **0.10 at 800 °C**.

**(d) Thermal Conductivity, $\lambda_c(\theta)$:**
$$0.8 \leq \lambda_c \leq 2.0 \text{ W/(m·K)}$$
- NWC: between 1.0–2.0 W/(m·K)
- Decreases slightly with temperature.

**(e) Specific Heat, $c_p(\theta)$:**
$$c_p = 900 + 80\cdot\frac{\theta}{120} - 4\cdot\left(\frac{\theta}{120}\right)^2 \quad \text{for } 20 \le \theta \le 1200 \text{ °C}$$

**(f) Coefficient of Thermal Expansion, $\alpha_c(\theta)$:**
$$\alpha_c \approx (8 \text{ to } 12) \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$
Increases slightly with temperature up to ~ 700 °C, then decreases.

**(g) Density, $\rho_c(\theta)$:**
NWC density: **2300–2500 kg/m³** (essentially unchanged with temperature in design).

---

### 2.1.2 HIGH-STRENGTH CONCRETE (HSC)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** HSC is **far more vulnerable** to fire than NWC because of its *denser microstructure* which traps pore water → leads to **explosive spalling** above 300 °C.

- At 300 °C, HSC retains only **~ 70 %** of compressive strength (vs. 85 % for NWC).
- Spalling can reduce cross-section by 25–50 % in severe cases.

---

### 2.1.3 STRUCTURAL STEEL

**Reduction factor curves (Eurocode EN 1993-1-2):**

**(a) Yield Strength, $f_y(\theta)$:**

| $\theta$ (°C) | $k_y(\theta) = f_y(\theta)/f_{y,20}$ |
|---|---|
| 20 | 1.000 |
| 100 | 1.000 |
| 200 | 1.000 |
| 300 | 1.000 |
| 400 | 1.000 |
| 500 | 0.780 |
| 600 | 0.470 |
| 700 | 0.230 |
| 800 | 0.110 |
| 900 | 0.060 |
| 1000 | 0.040 |
| 1100 | 0.020 |
| 1200 | 0.000 |

**Critical insight:** Yield strength is **retained up to 400 °C**, then drops **precipitously** — by 600 °C, steel retains less than half its strength. The **"critical temperature"** (where failure occurs under design load) is typically **550–600 °C** for insulated steel members.

**(b) Modulus of Elasticity, $E_s(\theta)$:**

| $\theta$ (°C) | $k_{E_s}(\theta) = E_s(\theta)/E_{s,20}$ |
|---|---|
| 20 | 1.000 |
| 100 | 1.000 |
| 200 | 0.900 |
| 300 | 0.800 |
| 400 | 0.700 |
| 500 | 0.600 |
| 600 | 0.310 |
| 700 | 0.130 |
| 800 | 0.090 |
| 900 | 0.0675 |
| 1000 | 0.0450 |
| 1100 | 0.0225 |
| 1200 | 0.000 |

**(c) Thermal Conductivity, $\lambda_s(\theta)$:**

$$\lambda_s = 54 - 0.0333\,\theta \quad \text{W/(m·K)} \quad \text{for } 20 \le \theta \le 800 \text{ °C}$$

$$\lambda_s = 27.3 \quad \text{W/(m·K)} \quad \text{for } 800 \le \theta \le 1200 \text{ °C}$$

**Why this matters:** High thermal conductivity means **heat travels quickly through steel** — fire protection is critical.

**(d) Specific Heat, $c_{p,s}(\theta)$:**

$$c_{p,s} = 425 + 7.73\times 10^{-1}\,\theta - 1.69\times 10^{-3}\,\theta^2 + 2.22\times 10^{-6}\,\theta^3 \quad \text{J/(kg·K)}$$

This equation applies for $20 \le \theta \le 600$ °C, then $c_{p,s} = 666 + \frac{13002}{738 - \theta}$ for $600 \le \theta \le 735$ °C, with a peak at 735 °C (phase change).

**(e) Thermal Expansion, $\alpha_s(\theta)$:**

$$\alpha_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} + 0.8 \times 10^{-8}\,\theta \quad \text{/°C}$$

For a **thermal elongation** $\Delta L/L$:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}\,\theta + 0.4 \times 10^{-8}\,\theta^2 \quad \text{for } 20 \le \theta \le 750 \text{ °C}$$

**Practical use:** This elongation must be accommodated in **fire-induced expansion calculations** for long steel members (e.g., beams in multi-storey frames).

---

### 2.1.4 ALUMINIUM ALLOYS

**Critical weakness:** Aluminium has a **melting point of only 660 °C**, and its strength degrades *much earlier* than steel.

**Typical reduction factors (Eurocode EN 1999-1-2):**

**(a) Proof Strength, $f_{0.2}(\theta)$:**

| $\theta$ (°C) | $k_{0.2,p}(\theta)$ |
|---|---|
| 20 | 1.000 |
| 100 | 0.940 |
| 150 | 0.890 |
| 200 | 0.840 |
| 250 | 0.740 |
| 300 | 0.640 |
| 350 | 0.500 |
| 400 | 0.350 |
| 550 | 0.110 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At 300 °C, aluminium retains only 64 % of its strength** — while steel still has **100 %**. This is why aluminium is *unsuitable* for primary fire-resistant structural applications.

**(b) Modulus of Elasticity, $E_{al}(\theta)$:**

$$k_{E_{al}}(\theta) = 1.0 \text{ at } 20\,°C,\quad 0.80 \text{ at } 100\,°C,\quad 0.10 \text{ at } 250\,°C$$

**(c) Thermal Conductivity:** Very high ≈ **150–200 W/(m·K)** → heat passes through quickly.

**(d) Melting Point:** **660 °C** — must be remembered for KTU problems.

---

### 2.1.5 MASONRY (Clay Brick, Calcium Silicate, Concrete Block)

**Compressive strength reduction (Eurocode EN 1996-1-2):**

| $\theta$ (°C) | $k_{masc}(\theta)$ — Clay |
|---|---|
| 20 | 1.00 |
| 200 | 1.00 |
| 400 | 0.85 |
| 600 | 0.70 |
| 800 | 0.55 |
| 1000 | 0.30 |

**Key properties:**

- **Thermal expansion:** $\alpha_m \approx (5 \text{ to } 8) \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- **Thermal conductivity:** $\lambda_m \approx 0.4 \text{ to } 1.4$ W/(m·K) (varies with density)
- **Specific heat:** $c_{p,m} \approx 800\text{–}1200$ J/(kg·K)
- **Density:** 1500–2200 kg/m³ (depends on block type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mortar is the weak link** in masonry fire behavior — it loses strength and bond faster than the brick/block itself.

---

### 2.1.6 WOOD / TIMBER

Wood is unique because it **chars from the outside in** when heated, and the *char layer* (which is itself a poor conductor) **protects the inner core** — this is called the **self-protecting effect**.

**Key parameters:**

**(a) Charring Rate, $\beta_n$ (one-dimensional):**

| Material | $\beta_n$ (mm/min) |
|---|---|
| Softwood | 0.65 |
| Hardwood | 0.50 |
| Glulam | 0.65 (softwood) |

**Charring depth, $d_{char,n}$:**

$$d_{char,n} = \beta_n \cdot t \quad \text{(mm)}$$

**(b) Strength Properties:**
- Effective char layer has **zero strength**.
- Wood below the char layer (at ~ 20 °C) retains **100 % strength** (key advantage of timber).
- The **transition zone** (~ 10–25 mm deep) shows partial strength loss.

**(c) Thermal Properties:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Density ($\rho_w$) | 400–700 kg/m³ (softwood) |
| Specific heat ($c_{p,w}$) | ≈ 1200–1500 J/(kg·K) |
| Thermal conductivity ($\lambda_w$) | 0.10–0.20 W/(m·K) (char: 0.08) |
| Thermal expansion ($\alpha_w$) | 3–6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C (along grain) |
| Ignition temp | ≈ 300 °C |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80 % of KTU numerical questions on Module 2.**

| # | Material | Property | Formula / Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | All | Reduction factor | $k_X(\theta) = X(\theta)/X_{20°C}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | All | Charring depth | $d_{char} = \beta_n \cdot t$ | mm |
| 3 | Steel | Thermal conductivity | $\lambda_s = 54 - 0.0333\theta$ (for $\theta \le 800$ °C) | W/(m·K) |
| 4 | Steel | Specific heat | $c_{p,s} = 425 + 0.773\theta - 1.69\times10^{-3}\theta^2 + 2.22\times10^{-6}\theta^3$ | J/(kg·K) |
| 5 | Steel | Thermal elongation | $\Delta L/L = 1.2\times10^{-5}\theta + 0.4\times10^{-8}\theta^2$ | m/m |
| 6 | Concrete | Specific heat | $c_p = 900 + 80(\theta/120) - 4(\theta/120)^2$ | J/(kg·K) |
| 7 | Concrete | NWC strength | Tabular $k_c(\theta)$ (see §2.1.1) | — |
| 8 | Aluminium | Melting point | $\theta_{melt} = 660$ °C | °C |
| 9 | Wood | Charring rate | $\beta_n = 0.65$ (softwood) | mm/min |
| 10 | All | Cross-section loss | $A_{eff} = A_{gross} - A_{lost}$ | m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Latex Note for Tables:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` for any absolute-value symbol within table cells to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Fire Resistance Rating** (e.g., 2-hour, 4-hour) is *directly derived* from these property curves.
- **Fire protection design** (intumescent coatings, gypsum boards, mineral wool) is sized using the **time vs. steel temperature** curve, which is built from $\lambda_s$, $c_{p,s}$, and $\rho_s$.
- **Spalling prediction models** for HSC require $\alpha_c$, $c_{p,c}$, and $\lambda_c$ as inputs.
- **Timber charring calculations** in Eurocode 5 (EN 1995-1-2) use $\beta_n$ to determine residual cross-section after $t$ minutes of fire.
- **Insurance risk assessment** for petrochemical and high-rise buildings uses these data to compute **fire severity** and **structural reliability** post-fire.
