$$\boxed{\sigma_{c,core} = f_{c,300} = 18.75 \text{ N/mm}^{2}}$$

(Verification: applying the parabolic equation with $u = 1$ gives $\sigma_{c} = 3\varepsilon f_{c,\theta}/(\varepsilon_{c1,\theta}\cdot 3) = f_{c,\theta}$. ✓)

**Step 5 — Comparison and structural implication:**

| Location | $\theta$ (°C) | $f_{c,\theta}$ (N/mm²) | $\sigma$ (N/mm²) | Ratio to ambient |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cover | 500 | 12.50 | 10.52 | 0.42 |
| Core | 300 | 18.75 | 18.75 | 0.75 |

**Comment:** The cover concrete has lost 58 % of its ambient strength, while the core retains 75 %. Under load, the column experiences a **non-uniform stress distribution**: the cover is on the descending branch of its parabola (well past peak), while the core is exactly at peak strength. This creates a **stress redistribution** toward the core, which is the fundamental reason why large RC columns can survive 60–90 minutes of fire exposure despite severe cover spalling. However, if the fire persists, the core temperature will continue to rise past 400 °C and the column will eventually lose its last line of defence — the unspalled core concrete and the now-yielded reinforcement.

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **2-mark deduction** if the student forgets that concrete is **stronger in compression at the start of the parabola** and **weaker on the descending branch** — the curve is not symmetric.
> - **1-mark deduction** if the siliceous-aggregate table is used when the problem stated calcareous aggregate (different $k_{c,\theta}$ values).
> - **1-mark deduction** if the answer does not include the *comment* part — KTU 2024 scheme allocates marks for "design interpretation" even in numerical questions.
> - **1-mark deduction** if $u = \varepsilon/\varepsilon_{c1,\theta}$ is computed as a decimal but cubed incorrectly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Constitutive model** = $\sigma = f(\varepsilon, T)$ — temperature is a *first-class parameter* in fire design.
- [x] **Steel** uses **EN 1993-1-2**: 4-regime piecewise model (elastic → elliptical → plateau → hardening).
- [x] **Concrete** uses **EN 1992-1-2**: parabolic-rectangular, 2 regimes (parabolic up to $\varepsilon_{c1,\theta}$, constant plateau to $\varepsilon_{cu1,\theta}$).
- [x] **Strain decomposition** is mandatory: $\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{\sigma} + \varepsilon_{th}$.
- [x] **Reduction factors** for steel: $k_{E,\theta}$, $k_{p,\theta}$, $k_{y,\theta}$. **For concrete:** $k_{c,\theta}$.
- [x] **Limiting strains at fire** (per EN 1993-1-2): $\varepsilon_{y,\theta}=0.02$, $\varepsilon_{t,\theta}=0.15$, $\varepsilon_{u,\theta}=0.20$.
- [x] **Key ambient values:** $E_a = 200$ GPa, $\varepsilon_{c1} = 0.0025$.
- [x] **At 400 °C:** steel retains **70 %** stiffness, **42 %** proportional limit, **100 %** yield strength.
- [x] **At 600 °C:** concrete retains only **37.5 %** strength; steel retains **31 %** stiffness and **47 %** yield.
- [x] **Aggregate type matters** for concrete: siliceous vs. calcareous give different $k_{c,\theta}$ values.
- [x] **Always identify the regime first**, then choose the equation — never apply $E_{a,\theta} \cdot \varepsilon_{\sigma}$ beyond the proportional limit.
- [x] **Alternative models**: Ramberg-Osgood (steel), Popovics, Kent-Park, Mander (concrete) — for FE / research work.
- [x] **Engineering insight**: cover concrete spalls first → load redistributes to core + rebar → column may survive 60–90 min if the core stays below 400 °C.
- [x] **Common unit confusion:** strains are dimensionless but KTU 2024 prefers reporting as **0.012 (1.2 %)** to avoid decimal confusion.
- [x] **Examiner's golden rule:** State the regime-identification step explicitly in every answer — even if it's one line.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Protection of Structural Members Made of Steel, RCC and Wood, Fire Area - Calculation of Building Fire Area, Subdivision of Fire Areas in Industrial, Residential and Public Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Protection of Structural Members Made of Steel, RCC and Wood, Fire Area - Calculation of Building Fire Area, Subdivision of Fire Areas in Industrial, Residential and Public Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Protection of Structural Members — Fundamental Premise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**
> Fire protection of structural members refers to the engineering design and material treatment strategies employed to ensure that load-bearing elements of a building (beams, columns, slabs, trusses, walls) retain their **load-carrying capacity**, **integrity**, and **insulation** for a specified duration (usually expressed in hours, e.g., 1 hr, 2 hr, 4 hr) when exposed to a standard fire.

The performance of a structural member in fire is measured by three stability criteria defined in **IS 1641:1988** and **EN 1363-1**:

| Criterion | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Load-bearing capacity (Stability) | **R** | Member must not collapse under design load |
| Integrity | **E** | No cracks/openings through which flames/hot gases can pass |
| Insulation | **I** | Temperature on unexposed face must not rise > 140°C (avg) or 180°C (max single point) |

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)
Think of a structural member during a fire like an **athlete running a marathon while carrying a heavy backpack**. The marathon = fire exposure (heat), and the backpack = dead + live load. As the marathon continues (temperature rises), the athlete gets weaker. Fire protection is like giving the athlete:
- **Energy gels** (insulation boards, spray-on cementitious coatings) to slow exhaustion,
- **Cooling vests** (water-filled columns, intumescent paints) to dump heat,
- **A better running form** (concrete cover in RCC) to last longer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Temperatures (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Steel**: Loses ~50% strength at **550 °C**; structural failure around **600 °C**.
> - **Concrete (RCC)**: Calcareous aggregate starts degrading > **300 °C**; siliceous aggregate > **600 °C**.
> - **Wood**: Chars externally at **300 °C**, but inner core retains strength up to **260 °C** (char layer acts as insulator).

---

## 1.2 Fire Area — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Area Definition (NBC 2016 / IS 1641):**
> The **Fire Area** of a building (or a part of it) is the aggregate floor area (in m²) enclosed within the surrounding external walls and/or fire walls of the building (or part), measured at any horizontal plane, between fire-resistant walls, fire-rated floors, or other separations designed to resist fire spread.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a building as a **giant chocolate bar divided into squares**. Each square (fire compartment) is its own little world — if a fire starts in one square, it can only melt that square (and maybe neighbours if no wall is in place). The **fire area is the size of each square**. Bigger squares = more fire can grow before hitting a wall = more danger.

---

## 1.3 Need for Subdivision of Fire Areas

If a single fire area is too large, fire spreads catastrophically. To control fire, buildings are **subdivided into smaller fire compartments** by:
- **Fire walls** (≥ 2 hr rating),
- **Fire-rated floors** (≥ 1 hr),
- **Fire curtains / dampers** (for ducts),
- **Fire stoppers** (in cavities and penetrations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Subdivide?**
> - Limits fire to a manageable area (size of fire is proportional to √area).
> - Allows safe evacuation within the safe egress time.
> - Prevents domino-effect collapse of adjacent structures.
> - Minimizes property damage and business interruption.

![Foundation Block Diagram placeholder]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fire Protection of Steel Members

### 2.1.1 Why Steel Needs Protection

Steel is non-combustible but **thermo-mechanically vulnerable**. As temperature rises, yield strength drops:

$$f_y(T) = f_{y,20} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{T}{900 \ln(T/1750)}\right] \quad \text{(Eurocode 3 simplified)}$$

At T = 550 °C, structural steel retains only **60%** of its room-temperature yield strength. Since fire temperatures reach **1000 °C** in 5–10 minutes, unprotected steel fails within **10–15 minutes**.

### 2.1.2 Protection Methods for Steel

1. **Spray-Applied Cementitious Coatings** (vermiculite / gypsum-based)
   - Thickness: 10–50 mm depending on fire rating.
2. **Intumescent Paints**
   - Thin film (0.5–3 mm) that swells to 20–50× thickness at 200–300 °C.
3. **Gypsum Plaster / Lath**
   - Traditional method; 12–25 mm thickness.
4. **Concrete Encasement**
   - 50–100 mm cover; used for columns in industrial buildings.
5. **Mineral Wool / Ceramic Fibre Boards**
   - Encased box protection; common in high-rise commercial.
6. **Water-Filled Columns** (hollow sections only)
   - Active cooling; heat is absorbed by water circulation/evaporation.

### 2.1.3 Fire Resistance Rating of Protected Steel Columns

Using the **Steel Column Protection Equation (IS 1641 / EC3 simplified)**:

$$R = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{H_p}{W/D}\right)^{0.75}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Fire resistance rating in minutes
- $H_p$ = Thickness of protection material (mm)
- $W/D$ = Section factor (mass per unit length ÷ heated perimeter) (kg/m²)

---

## 2.2 Fire Protection of RCC Members

### 2.2.1 Inherent Advantages of Concrete
- Low thermal conductivity (~1.0–1.8 W/m·K vs 50 W/m·K for steel).
- Provides inherent fire resistance depending on **cover thickness**.

### 2.2.2 Failure Mechanisms
- **Spalling** (explosive loss of cover concrete due to pore-pressure build-up) — particularly in high-strength concrete (HSC > 60 MPa).
- **Loss of bond** between rebar and concrete when rebar temperature > 250 °C.
- **Re-bar softening** at > 550 °C.

### 2.2.3 Cover Requirements (IS 456 + IS 1642)

| Member Type | Min. Cover (mm) for 1 hr | Min. Cover (mm) for 2 hr | Min. Cover (mm) for 4 hr |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slabs (simply supported) | 20 | 30 | 50 |
| Beams | 25 | 45 | 65 |
| Columns (rectangular) | 40 | 50 | 65 |
| Ribbed slabs | 25 | 40 | 60 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 1642:1989** mandates minimum dimensions for RCC members based on fire rating. For example, an **RCC column** with 2-hour fire rating must have minimum **width = 250 mm** and **cover = 50 mm**.

---

## 2.3 Fire Protection of Timber (Wood) Members

### 2.3.1 Behaviour of Wood in Fire
Wood is combustible but exhibits **predictable charring behaviour** at rate of:
$$c = \beta_n = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8 \text{ mm/min (for softwood)}$$
$$c = \beta_n = 0.4 \text{ to } 0.5 \text{ mm/min (for hardwood)}$$

Charring rate for **glulam / heavy timber** per Eurocode 5:
$$\beta_0 = 0.65 \text{ mm/min (one-dimensional charring under standard fire)}$$

The **char layer** acts as an insulator, protecting inner wood which retains its strength up to 260 °C.

### 2.3.2 Effective Cross-Section Method

$$d_{\text{eff}} = d_{\text{actual}} - c \cdot t$$

$$d_{\text{char}} = \beta_0 \cdot t$$

Where:
- $d_{\text{char}}$ = charring depth (mm)
- $t$ = fire exposure time (min)
- $d_{\text{eff}}$ = effective residual cross-section dimension

### 2.3.3 Protection Methods
1. **Gypsum Plaster / Lath Encasing** — gives 1–2 hr rating.
2. **Intumescent Varnish / Fire-Retardant Coatings** — slows ignition, reduces flame spread.
3. **Impregnation with Fire Retardants** (boric acid, ammonium phosphate) — reduces heat release.
4. **Asbestos-Cement / Calcium Silicate Board Cladding**.

---

## 2.4 Fire Area — Calculation Framework

### 2.4.1 Basic Calculation

$$A_f = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot W_i$$

Where $A_f$ = total fire area (m²), $L_i$ and $W_i$ = length and width of each fire compartment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (NBC 2016 / IS 1641):**
> - All **floors, mezzanines, galleries, and lofts** are counted.
> - **Open parking floors** are counted at 50%.
> - **External balconies, verandas, and cantilevers** projecting > 1.2 m are NOT counted.
> - **Lift / stairwell shafts**, ducts > 1.5 m² are not counted unless they pierce multiple compartments.

### 2.4.2 Permissible Fire Area (as per IS 1641 / IS 875 / NBC Table)

| Occupancy Group | Fire Area (per floor, m²) | Max Height |
|---|---|---|
| Residential (Group A) | 250 | 15 m (no lift), 24 m (with lift) |
| Educational (Group B) | 1000 | 9 m |
| Institutional (Group C) | 1000 | 9 m |
| Assembly (Group D) | 400–2000 | 12 m |
| Mercantile (Group E) | 750–4000 | 15 m |
| Industrial (Group F/G/H) | 500–2000 | 18 m |
| Hazardous (Group J) | 250 | 9 m |
| Storage (Group I) | 1000 | 18 m |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Source / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yield strength of steel at temp T | $f_y(T) = f_{y,20} \cdot k_y(T)$ | MPa | EC3 / IS 1641 |
| 2 | Steel column rating (protected) | $R = 50 (H_p/(W/D))^{0.75}$ | min | IS 1641 |
| 3 | Charring rate of softwood | $c = 0.6$–$0.8$ | mm/min | EC5 |
| 4 | Effective char depth | $d_{\text{char}} = \beta_0 \cdot t$ | mm | EC5 |
| 5 | Fire area (composite) | $A_f = \sum L_i W_i$ | m² | NBC 2016 |
| 6 | Heat transfer through slab | $q = k A \Delta T / x$ | W | Fourier |
| 7 | Fire severity (t-eq) | $t_e = \sum q_i \cdot \Delta t_i$ / (Reference) | min | Eq. fire severity |
| 8 | Critical temperature of steel | $T_{\text{crit}} \approx 550$ | °C | EC3 |
| 9 | Concrete spalling (HSC threshold) | $f'_c > 60$ MPa triggers | MPa | Kodur |
| 10 | Heat of combustion (wood) | $H_c = 17.5$ | MJ/kg | EC5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Fire protection engineers use these formulas to (a) decide whether a column needs external insulation, (b) calculate the thickness of intumescent paint, and (c) size up fire compartments in shopping malls, IT parks, and high-rise apartments in Kerala (which fall under IS 1641 + Kerala Municipal Building Rules 2019).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fire Resistance Rating of a Protected Steel Column

**Problem Statement:** An ISMB 450 steel column (W/D = 110 kg/m²) is protected with 25 mm of vermiculite-cement spray. Find the fire resistance rating.

### Step 1: Identify the variables
- $W/D = 110 \text{ kg/m}^2$ (section factor)
- $H_p = 25 \text{ mm}$ (protection thickness)
- $R = ?$ (fire resistance in minutes)

### Step 2: Substitute in the rating equation
$$R = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{H_p}{W/D}\right)^{0.75}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the inside of the bracket
$$\frac{H_p}{W/D} = \frac{25}{110} = 0.2273 \text{ mm} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{kg}$$

### Step 4: Raise to power 0.75
$$(0.2273)^{0.75} = \exp(0.75 \cdot \ln 0.2273)$$
$$= \exp(0.75 \cdot (-1.4813))$$
$$= \exp(-1.1110)$$
$$= 0.3292$$

### Step 5: Multiply by 50
$$R = 50 \cdot 0.3292 = 16.46 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result: Fire rating ≈ 16 minutes.** This is far less than the 1-hour (60 min) required for typical buildings. Therefore, the protection thickness must be **increased to ~ 90 mm** to achieve a 1-hr rating.

### Reverse Calculation: Required Protection Thickness for 1-hour Rating

Setting $R = 60$ min:
$$60 = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{H_p}{110}\right)^{0.75}$$
$$\left(\frac{H_p}{110}\right)^{0.75} = 1.2$$
$$\frac{H_p}{110} = (1.2)^{1/0.75} = (1.2)^{1.333}$$
$$= 1.262$$
$$H_p = 110 \cdot 1.262 = 138.8 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implementation:** A 140 mm vermiculite spray is impractical — engineers prefer **intumescent paint + board encasement** (e.g., 25 mm Promatect board) which gives 2-hour rating at half the thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Char Depth in a Timber Beam

**Problem Statement:** A glulam beam 200 mm wide × 400 mm deep is exposed to standard fire for 60 minutes. Find the effective residual cross-section. (Use $\beta_0 = 0.65$ mm/min)

### Step 1: Compute charring depth
$$d_{\text{char}} = \beta_0 \cdot t = 0.65 \cdot 60 = 39 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Effective width and depth
$$b_{\text{eff}} = 200 - 2 \cdot 39 = 122 \text{ mm}$$
$$d_{\text{eff}} = 400 - 2 \cdot 39 = 322 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Effective cross-sectional area
$$A_{\text{eff}} = b_{\text{eff}} \cdot d_{\text{eff}} = 122 \cdot 322 = 39{,}284 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 4: Compare with original area
$$\text{Original area} = 200 \cdot 400 = 80{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$\text{Residual fraction} = \frac{39{,}284}{80{,}000} = 0.491 = 49.1\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** After 60 minutes, the timber beam retains ~49% of its cross-section, which is usually sufficient because the load is also reduced (no live load in fire case, dead load factor = 1.0). This is the **inherent fire advantage of heavy timber**.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Calculation of Fire Area of a Building

**Problem Statement:** A 3-storey residential building has the following plan on each floor:
- Living area: 8 m × 6 m = 48 m²
- Bedrooms (2): 4 m × 4 m each = 32 m²
- Kitchen: 3 m × 3 m = 9 m²
- Bath/Toilet (2): 2 m × 2 m each = 8 m²
- Staircase & common area: 4 m × 3 m = 12 m²
- Internal balcony (projecting 1.5 m, open on 2 sides): 4 m × 1.5 m = 6 m²

The building has **no fire wall subdivision**. Calculate the fire area per floor.

### Step 1: List all areas
- Living: 48 m²
- 2 Bedrooms: 32 m²
- Kitchen: 9 m²
- 2 Bathrooms: 8 m²
- Staircase/common: 12 m²

### Step 2: Exclude open balcony (per NBC rule)
- Balcony: 6 m² is **excluded** (open projection > 1.2 m).

### Step 3: Sum
$$A_f = 48 + 32 + 9 + 8 + 12 = 109 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4: Multiply by number of floors
$$A_{\text{total}} = 3 \cdot 109 = 327 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (per fire compartment)}$$

### Step 5: Check against permissible
From IS 1641 Table (Residential Group A): Permissible fire area per floor = **250 m²**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Result: 327 m² > 250 m² ⇒ Non-compliant!**
> The building **must** be subdivided by a **fire wall (2-hr rating)** into at least 2 fire compartments of ≤ 250 m² each. After subdivision:
> - Compartment 1: Living + Bedroom 1 + Kitchen = 75 m²
> - Compartment 2: Bedroom 2 + Bath + Staircase = 36 m²
> - Both comply (well under 250 m²).

---

## 3.4 Subdivision Rules — Worked Examples

### Example A: Industrial Building (Group G — Moderate Hazard)

A garment factory has a single hall of 60 m × 30 m with internal columns at 10 m spacing. Per IS 1641, fire area for Group G2 is **2000 m²** maximum. If hall is one compartment:
$$A_f = 60 \cdot 30 = 1800 \text{ m}^2 \rightarrow \text{Compliant (no subdivision)}$$

If hall is 80 m × 30 m:
$$A_f = 80 \cdot 30 = 2400 \text{ m}^2 \rightarrow \text{Non-compliant}$$

> **Solution:** Introduce a **2-hr fire wall** at 40 m mark → two compartments of 1200 m² each.

### Example B: Public Building (Group D — Assembly)

A community hall 40 m × 25 m has a stage and seating for 1000 persons. Group D2 (above 1000 persons) requires **subdivision every 2000 m²** with 1-hr fire separation.
$$A_f = 1000 \text{ m}^2 \rightarrow \text{One compartment, no subdivision}$$

### Example C: Residential (Group A)

A 12-storey apartment block, floor plate 30 m × 18 m = 540 m². Per IS 1641, residential buildings with **height > 15 m** must have:
- Each apartment as a separate fire compartment (1-hr walls + floors).
- Staircase/lift lobby separated by **fire-rated doors (2-hr)**.

> **Solution:** Each apartment (say 90 m²) becomes its own fire area, separated by 1-hr firewalls.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Behaviour Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Exposure Begins] --> B{Material Type}
    B -- Steel --> C1[Yield Strength Drops]
    B -- Concrete RCC --> C2[Spalling + Cover Failure]
    B -- Wood --> C3[Charring Layer Forms]
    C1 --> D1[Critical Temp ~550 C Reached]
    C2 --> D2[Rebar Temp >250 C Bond Loss]
    C3 --> D3[Char Insulation Protects Core]
    D1 --> E1[Apply: Spray / Intumescent Paint / Concrete Encasement / Mineral Wool / Water Filled]
    D2 --> E2[Apply: Increased Cover / Fire Plaster / FRP Wrapping / Polypropylene Fibres]
    D3 --> E3[Apply: Gypsum Lath / Fire Retardant Coat / Impregnation / Calcium Silicate Board]
    E1 --> F1[Fire Resistance Achieved R60 R120 R240]
    E2 --> F2[Fire Resistance Achieved R60 R120 R240]
    E3 --> F3[Fire Resistance Achieved R30 R60]
    F1 --> G[Compliance with IS 1641 / NBC 2016]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

## 4.2 Fire Area Subdivision Logic

```mermaid

    S -- Exceeds Limit --> T2[Insert Fire Wall 2 hr Rating]
    T2 --> U[Subdivide into Smaller Compartments]
    U --> V{Each New Compartment within Permissible Area}
    V -- Yes --> W1[Compliant Design]
    V -- No --> W2[Add More Fire Walls]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Fire Resistance and state the three criteria used to assess it. *(Module 2, KTU expected) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Model Answer:**

> **Fire Resistance** is the property of a structural member or assembly to withstand fire for a specified period without loss of its functional requirements, expressed in **hours (e.g., 1 hr, 2 hr, 4 hr)**.
>
> The three criteria (per **IS 1641 / EN 1363**) are:
> 1. **R — Resistance (Load-bearing capacity):** Member must support the design load without collapse.
> 2. **E — Integrity:** No through-cracks/openings that allow flames or hot gases to pass.
> 3. **I — Insulation:** Average temperature rise on the unexposed face ≤ **140 °C**, max single point ≤ **180 °C**.
>
> A member is rated as **REI-120** if it satisfies all three criteria for 120 minutes.

---

### Q2. List any four methods of protecting steel structural members against fire. *(Module 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Spray-applied cementitious (vermiculite/gypsum) coating** — 10–50 mm thickness.
> 2. **Intumescent paint** — thin film that swells 20–50× at 200–300 °C.
> 3. **Concrete encasement** — 50–100 mm cover around steel section.
> 4. **Gypsum board encasement** — 12–25 mm calcium-silicate/Promatect boards.
> 5. (Bonus) **Water-filled columns** or **mineral wool/ceramic fibre wraps**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a)** Compare the behaviour of steel, RCC, and timber structural members when exposed to fire. Discuss the protection strategy for each. *(7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

| Property | Steel | RCC | Timber (Wood) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Combustibility | Non-combustible | Non-combustible | Combustible |
| Critical temp | ~550 °C | Rebar > 250 °C, Cover loss | Char at 300 °C |
| Failure mode | Buckling, plastic collapse | Spalling, rebar softening | Progressive charring |
| Inherent rating | 10–15 min unprotected | 1–4 hr (with cover) | 30–60 min (heavy section) |
| Primary protection | Insulation encasement | Adequate cover | Char layer + impregnation |

**Steel Protection Strategy:**
- Apply vermiculite spray (10–50 mm) OR intumescent paint (0.5–3 mm film swells to 25–50 mm) OR concrete encasement (50–100 mm).
- Use $R = 50(H_p/(W/D))^{0.75}$ to size protection.
- Hollow columns can be **water-filled** for active cooling.

**RCC Protection Strategy:**
- Provide minimum cover as per IS 1642 (40–65 mm for columns).
- For HSC (> 60 MPa), add **polypropylene fibres (1–2 kg/m³)** to control spalling.
- For higher ratings, use **fire-rated plaster** or **FRP wrapping**.
- Add **silica fume** to improve thermal stability.

**Timber Protection Strategy:**
- Exploit **charring behaviour** (0.65 mm/min for glulam).
- Treat with **fire retardants** (boric acid, mono/di-ammonium phosphate).
- Encase with **gypsum lath** (12 mm → +60 min rating).
- Use **intumescent varnish** (slows ignition).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often forget to mention that **RCC SPALLING** is the biggest hidden risk in high-rise Kerala apartment buildings (commonly M60 grade). Always mention **polypropylene fibres** as a countermeasure. Marks are deducted for "vague" answers without quantification.

---

**Q3(b)** A steel column of section factor 90 kg/m² is protected with 35 mm of vermiculite-cement spray. Calculate the fire resistance rating. If the design requires a 2-hour rating, calculate the required protection thickness. *(7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Fire Rating Calculation:**
$$R = 50 \left(\frac{H_p}{W/D}\right)^{0.75} = 50 \left(\frac{35}{90}\right)^{0.75}$$

$$\frac{35}{90} = 0.3889$$

$$(0.3889)^{0.75} = \exp(0.75 \cdot \ln 0.3889) = \exp(0.75 \cdot -0.9445) = \exp(-0.7084) = 0.4924$$

$$R = 50 \cdot 0.4924 = 24.6 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Required thickness for 2 hr (120 min) rating:**
$$120 = 50 \left(\frac{H_p}{90}\right)^{0.75}$$
$$\left(\frac{H_p}{90}\right)^{0.75} = 2.4$$
$$\frac{H_p}{90} = (2.4)^{1/0.75} = (2.4)^{1.333}$$
$$\ln 2.4 = 0.8755, \quad 1.333 \cdot 0.8755 = 1.167$$
$$H_p/90 = e^{1.167} = 3.212$$
$$H_p = 90 \cdot 3.212 = 289 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** 290 mm vermiculite spray is **impractical** for a 120-min rating. Engineers should use **alternative method** (e.g., 25 mm intumescent paint + 25 mm Promatect board = 120 min). Always conclude with a practical design recommendation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q4(a)** Define Fire Area. Explain the factors that influence the permissible fire area for different occupancies as per IS 1641. *(7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Fire area is the aggregate floor area in square metres enclosed within the surrounding **fire walls** (or external walls) of a building (or fire compartment) at any horizontal plane.
>
> **Factors Influencing Permissible Fire Area:**
> 1. **Occupancy Type (Use Group):** Higher hazard occupancies (industrial, hazardous) have lower permissible fire areas.
> 2. **Type of Construction:** Fire-resisting construction allows larger areas.
> 3. **Height of Building:** Tall buildings need smaller compartments.
> 4. **Availability of Fire Fighting:** Sprinklered buildings may get 100% increase in fire area.
> 5. **Width of Streets / Open Spaces:** Buildings with 6 m+ access roads may be allowed larger areas.
> 6. **Presence of Fire Walls:** Each compartment must independently satisfy the limit.
>
> **Permissible Values (IS 1641 / NBC 2016):**
> - Residential (A): 250 m² per floor
> - Educational (B): 1000 m²
> - Assembly (D): 400–2000 m²
> - Industrial (F/G): 500–2000 m²
> - Hazardous (J): 250 m²
> - Storage (I): 1000 m²

---

**Q4(b)** A commercial complex (Mercantile, Group E) has a floor plate of 50 m × 30 m. The building is 4 storeys high, sprinklered throughout. Calculate the fire area per floor. As an engineer, propose a fire compartmentation strategy. *(7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Floor Fire Area:**
$$A_f = 50 \cdot 30 = 1500 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Permissible Area (Group E, sprinklered, 100% increase):**
- Base value: 1500 m² (Mercantile, 1 storey)
- With sprinklers + fire walls: 1500 × 1.5 = 2250 m² per floor

**Step 3 — Comparison:**
- 1500 m² < 2250 m² → **Compliant for sprinklered building** with 1-hr fire wall subdivision.

**Step 4 — Subdivision Strategy:**
1. **Each floor** must be a separate fire compartment by 1-hr fire-rated slab.
2. **Per floor**, insert a **2-hr fire wall** at 25 m mark to create two compartments of 750 m² each.
3. **Staircase and lift cores** enclosed in 2-hr fire-rated construction.
4. **Service shafts** (electrical, AC ducts) sealed with **fire dampers (1-hr)** at every floor penetration.
5. **All doors** to staircases/lobbies: 2-hr fire-rated self-closing.
6. **Travel distance** to nearest exit: ≤ **22.5 m** (per NBC 2016 for Mercantile).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Do NOT count **external cantilever projections > 1.2 m** in fire area.
> - Do NOT count **lift/staircase shaft area** unless it crosses multiple compartments without fire stopping.
> - Always mention **sprinkler concession** explicitly — 50% to 100% increase is granted per NBC 2016.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fire Resistance = R + E + I** criteria (Load-bearing, Integrity, Insulation).
- ✅ **Steel critical temperature = 550 °C** — protect with intumescent paint, spray, encasement.
- ✅ **Steel column rating formula:** $R = 50(H_p/(W/D))^{0.75}$ (IS 1641).
- ✅ **RCC cover:** 40 mm (1 hr), 50 mm (2 hr), 65 mm (4 hr) for columns.
- ✅ **HSC spalling** — counter with **polypropylene fibres (1–2 kg/m³)**.
- ✅ **Wood char rate** = 0.65 mm/min (softwood/glulam); char acts as insulator.
- ✅ **Effective char depth** $d_{\text{char}} = \beta_0 \cdot t$.
- ✅ **Fire Area** = aggregate enclosed floor area (m²); excludes open balconies, lift shafts.
- ✅ **Permissible Fire Area** depends on **occupancy group + sprinkler status + height**.
- ✅ **Residential** = 250 m²; **Mercantile** = 1500–2250 m² (sprinklered).
- ✅ **Subdivision** by **fire walls (2-hr)** + **fire floors (1-hr)**.
- ✅ **Staircase/lift lobby** in high-rise: separate 2-hr fire compartment.
- ✅ **Fire dampers** mandatory on duct penetrations in commercial buildings.
- ✅ **Sprinklers** can give up to **100% increase** in permissible fire area.
- ✅ **Travel distance** ≤ 22.5 m (Mercantile) and ≤ 30 m (Residential).
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** IS 1641 (fire resistance), IS 1642 (RCC), IS 875 (loading), NBC 2016 (India), EC3 (steel), EC5 (timber).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** This topic is a **favourite 14-marker**. Always:
> 1. State the **definition with standard reference**.
> 2. Tabulate **comparison** between materials.
> 3. Show **formula + step-by-step calculation** for fire rating.
> 4. End with a **practical engineering recommendation**.
> 5. Mention **Kerala-specific** code: KMBR 2019 + NBC 2016.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Separation between Building - Principle of Calculation of Safe Distance, Design Principles of Fire Resistant Walls and Ceilings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Separation between Building - Principle of Calculation of Safe Distance, Design Principles of Fire Resistant Walls and Ceilings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fire Separation Between Buildings — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Separation** is the structural and spatial provision made between adjacent buildings (or fire compartments) such that a fire occurring in one building is prevented from spreading to the neighboring building for a specified period of time, by means of adequate spatial distance (gap), fire-rated walls, fire-rated floors/ceilings, or a combination of these.

The governing philosophy of fire separation rests on the **triad of passive fire protection**:

1. **Spatial Separation (Safe Distance):** Creating a physical gap between buildings so that radiated heat at the receiver is below the threshold for ignition.
2. **Compartmentation:** Subdividing the building into discrete fire cells using fire-rated walls and floors.
3. **Protection of Openings:** Sealing doors, windows, ducts, and service penetrations with fire-rated assemblies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** As per the National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4) and IS 1641 through IS 1644, the minimum fire separation distance is derived from the **radiative heat flux criterion** — the heat flux received at the neighboring building façade must not exceed **12.5 kW/m²** for non-piloted ignition of cellulosic materials.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Campfire Intuition"

Imagine you are camping and have built a large bonfire. If you set up a second tent (your neighboring building) right next to the flames, the radiant heat will quickly scorch the canvas and ignite it. However, if you walk the tent some distance away, the heat you feel on your face becomes bearable. That "comfortable distance" is essentially what engineers calculate as the **safe distance** — it is the distance at which the radiant heat flux drops to a level that will **not** ignite a typical building façade (wood, paint, PVC window frames, etc.). Fire-resistant walls and ceilings act like a **fireproof blanket** — they slow the heat transfer even if the "tent" (building) is right next to the bonfire, so the fire can be contained and extinguished before it spreads.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** Distance = protection in *time* (delays ignition). Fire-rated walls = protection in *endurance* (resists ignition even when in contact with flames).

### 1.3 Why Fire Separation Matters — Engineering Rationale

- **Reduces external fire spread** between buildings on the same plot.
- **Prevents cascading conflagrations** in dense urban areas.
- **Provides time for evacuation** and fire service intervention.
- **Limits property loss** to a single fire compartment.
- **Mandatory under NBC 2016** and most municipal building bylaws in India.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Critical Heat Flux for Ignition (q″_crit)** | **12.5 kW/m²** | For piloted ignition of wood |
| **Threshold for Pain/Skin Damage** | **1–2 kW/m²** | Human exposure limit for short duration |
| **Minimum Fire Resistance Rating of Separation Wall** | **2 hours to 4 hours** | Depends on building height & occupancy |
| **Standard Fire Test Duration** | **120 minutes / 240 minutes** | IS 3809 / BS 476 Part 20–22 |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanisms of Fire Spread Between Buildings

A fire in one building can ignite an adjacent building through **four principal mechanisms**:

1. **Radiation (Dominant mechanism)** — Emitted electromagnetic energy from the flames/hot façade travels across the gap and is absorbed by the receiving surface.
2. **Convection** — Hot gases rise as a plume and can travel horizontally to the adjacent building, igniting upper floors.
3. **Conduction** — Heat travels through shared structural elements (party walls, common slabs).
4. **Flying Brands / Embers** — Burning debris carried by wind to ignite roofs or openings far from the original fire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For calculation of safe distance, **radiation** is the controlling mechanism and is the basis of all KTU-level numerical problems.

### 2.2 Principle of Calculation of Safe Distance

The safe distance $L$ between two buildings is determined by ensuring that the **radiative heat flux incident on the neighboring building** does not exceed the **critical heat flux for ignition** of the exposed façade.

#### Step-by-Step Logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the Heat Source**
The burning building's window (or fully developed fire in a compartment) acts as a radiating surface. The total radiative power $Q_{rad}$ depends on the **fire size (HRR)** and the **radiative fraction** $\chi_r$.

$$Q_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot Q_{total}$$

Typical value: $\chi_r \approx 0.3$ to $0.4$ for compartment fires.

**Step 2 — Geometric Relationship (View Factor)**
The view factor $F_{1 \to 2}$ describes the fraction of radiation leaving surface 1 (fire window) that strikes surface 2 (neighbor). For a point source model:

$$F \approx \frac{1}{4\pi r^{2}} \cdot A_{source} \cdot \cos\theta$$

For a vertical wall radiator to a parallel vertical wall (separated by distance $L$):

$$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\arctan\left(\frac{h}{L}\right) + \frac{h/L}{1+(h/L)^{2}}\right]$$

where $h$ = height of the radiating flame/window.

**Step 3 — Atmospheric Transmissivity $\tau$**
Smoke and hot gases in the path absorb some radiation. For a clear atmosphere:

$$\tau = 1.0$$

For smoky conditions, an empirical correction $\tau = 1 - \alpha L$ may be applied where $\alpha$ is the extinction coefficient (typically 0.005 to 0.01 per meter).

**Step 4 — Incident Heat Flux Equation**

$$q''_{inc} = \frac{Q_{rad} \cdot F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \tau}{4\pi L^{2}} \quad \text{(point source)}$$

Or, using the **planar source** approximation (commonly used in NBC):

$$q''_{inc} = \frac{\chi_r \cdot Q \cdot F_{1 \to 2}}{L^{2}} \cdot \tau$$

**Step 5 — Safety Criterion**

The safe distance is the value of $L$ for which:

$$q''_{inc} \leq q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

Rearranging for $L$:

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{\chi_r \cdot Q \cdot F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \tau}{q''_{crit}}}$$

**Step 6 — Practical Simplification (NBC 2016 Tables)**
For KTU problems, the NBC provides **direct look-up tables** that specify minimum separation in meters based on:
- Type of construction (Type 1, 2, 3, 4)
- Height of the building
- Occupancy classification

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot Q$ | Radiative power from total fire HRR $Q$ |
| 2 | $q''_{inc} = \dfrac{\chi_r \cdot Q \cdot F \cdot \tau}{4\pi L^{2}}$ | Incident heat flux at distance $L$ (point source) |
| 3 | $L_{safe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi_r \cdot Q \cdot F \cdot \tau}{q''_{crit}}}$ | Required safe distance |
| 4 | $F_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right) + \dfrac{h/L}{1+(h/L)^{2}}\right]$ | View factor, parallel vertical walls |
| 5 | $q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$ | Critical flux for cellulosic ignition |
| 6 | $\tau = 1 - \alpha \cdot L$ | Atmospheric transmissivity |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{L^{2} \cdot q''_{crit}}{\chi_r \cdot Q}$ | Inverse check: Heat flux at known distance |
| 8 | $\text{Fire Resistance} = 2\text{ h (Type 2)}, 4\text{ h (Type 1A)}$ | NBC 2016 minimum ratings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark problems, you are usually asked to verify or compute $L_{safe}$ using $q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$ and $\chi_r = 0.3$. Memorize these two values.

### 2.4 Design Principles of Fire-Resistant Walls

A fire-resistant wall is a **vertical compartmentation element** designed to resist the passage of fire and smoke for a specified duration. The design principles are:

1. **Material Selection:** Non-combustible materials only — reinforced concrete, brick masonry, autoclaved aerated concrete (AAC) blocks, gypsum boards (Type X/F), calcium silicate boards.
2. **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):** Must be **2 hours minimum** for party walls between buildings, **4 hours** for high-rise / Type 1A construction.
3. **Structural Independence:** The wall must remain stable even if the structure on one side collapses. Achieved by designing for fire-induced thermal bowing and eccentric loading.
4. **Continuity (Floor-to-Underside of Next Floor):** The wall must extend continuously from one floor slab to the underside of the next floor slab (or roof slab) without any gap.
5. **Penetration Sealing:** Every opening (pipe, duct, cable tray) must be sealed with **fire-rated sealants, collars, or fire dampers** matching the wall's FRR.
6. **No Combustible Cladding:** External façade claddings (e.g., ACP) must be of Class A1/A2 fire performance.
7. **Double-Door Fire Check:** Fire doors must be self-closing, with intumescent strips, and tested to IS 3614 Part 2.

### 2.5 Design Principles of Fire-Resistant Ceilings

A fire-resistant ceiling is a **horizontal compartmentation element** (also called a fire-rated floor-ceiling assembly). Its design principles include:

1. **Floor Slab Fire Rating:** Reinforced concrete slabs of minimum thickness (typically 100–150 mm) provide 2–4 hour ratings.
2. **Suspended Ceiling Assemblies:** Used to protect the structural slab from below or to create a service void. Must be tested as a **complete assembly** (slab + hangers + tiles + light fixtures).
3. **Integrity & Insulation Criteria:** Per IS 3809, the ceiling must satisfy both:
   - **Integrity (E):** No flames/hot gases pass through cracks/openings.
   - **Insulation (I):** Temperature on the unexposed face must not rise by more than **140 °C** above ambient (average) or 180 °C at any point.
4. **Concealed Space Protection:** Plenum spaces above ceilings can channel smoke and fire — must be subdivided with fire barriers at regular intervals.
5. **Protection of Structural Steel:** If structural steel is above the ceiling, it must be protected with **intumescent paint**, **sprayed vermiculite/gypsum**, or **encapsulation** to maintain load-bearing function.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise apartments:** 4-hour fire walls separate every floor in the vertical sense and every unit in the horizontal sense.
- **Industrial buildings:** Firewall separates high-hazard (paint shop, fuel storage) from low-hazard (office) areas.
- **Hospitals & Schools:** Compartmentation ensures safe horizontal evacuation within the same floor before vertical evacuation is required.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Safe Distance Using Point-Source Radiation Model

We derive the safe distance $L$ step by step, starting from fundamental energy conservation.

**Given:**
- Heat Release Rate (HRR) of fire, $Q$ (in kW)
- Radiative fraction, $\chi_r = 0.3$ (dimensionless)
- Critical ignition heat flux, $q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$
- Atmospheric transmissivity, $\tau = 1.0$ (clear air)
- View factor, $F_{1 \to 2}$ (dimensionless, $\leq 1$)

**Step 1 — Total Radiative Power**

The fraction of total fire energy that is radiated outward is the radiative fraction $\chi_r$. Therefore, the total radiated power from the fire is:

$$Q_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot Q$$

**Step 2 — Radiation Distributed Over a Sphere**

A point source radiates uniformly in all directions, so the power per unit area (heat flux) at distance $L$ from the source is:

$$q''_{spherical} = \frac{Q_{rad}}{4\pi L^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply View Factor (Geometric Correction)**

Not all of this spherical flux strikes the receiving building — only a fraction $F_{1 \to 2}$ based on geometry:

$$q''_{inc} = F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \frac{Q_{rad}}{4\pi L^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Atmospheric Transmissivity**

Smoke, dust, and water vapor absorb some of the radiation. Multiply by transmissivity $\tau$:

$$q''_{inc} = \frac{F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \tau \cdot Q_{rad}}{4\pi L^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $Q_{rad} = \chi_r Q$**

$$q''_{inc} = \frac{F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \tau \cdot \chi_r \cdot Q}{4\pi L^{2}}$$

This is the **fundamental incident heat flux equation** at the neighboring building.

**Step 6 — Apply Safety Criterion**

For the neighboring building *not* to ignite, we require:

$$q''_{inc} \leq q''_{crit}$$

$$\frac{F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \tau \cdot \chi_r \cdot Q}{4\pi L^{2}} \leq q''_{crit}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the Minimum Safe Distance $L_{safe}$**

Rearranging:

$$L^{2} \geq \frac{F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \tau \cdot \chi_r \cdot Q}{4\pi \cdot q''_{crit}}$$

$$\boxed{L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \tau \cdot \chi_r \cdot Q}{4\pi \cdot q''_{crit}}}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A multi-storey office building is on fire. The total HRR is estimated as $Q = 5000$ kW. The fire window is $3 \text{ m}$ wide and $2 \text{ m}$ high. Calculate the safe distance from an adjacent residential building.

**Given Data:**

$$Q = 5000 \text{ kW}, \quad \chi_r = 0.3, \quad q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}, \quad \tau = 1.0$$

**Step 1 — Calculate the View Factor**

For a vertical radiating rectangle (3 m wide, 2 m high) facing a parallel vertical receiver at distance $L$, the view factor $F$ depends on the ratio $h/L$ where $h$ is the effective flame height (assume $h = 4$ m extending above window).

For an initial guess, let $L = 6$ m, then $h/L = 4/6 = 0.667$.

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}(0.667) + \frac{0.667}{1+0.667^{2}}\right]$$

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[0.588 + \frac{0.667}{1.445}\right] = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[0.588 + 0.462\right] = \frac{1.050}{3.1416}$$

$$F \approx 0.334$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Safe Distance Formula**

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{F \cdot \tau \cdot \chi_r \cdot Q}{4\pi \cdot q''_{crit}}}$$

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{0.334 \times 1.0 \times 0.3 \times 5000}{4\pi \times 12.5}}$$

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{501.0}{157.08}}$$

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{3.190}$$

$$L_{safe} \approx 1.79 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Check Iterative Convergence**

Since the view factor depends on $L$, iterate with $L = 1.79$ m: $h/L = 4/1.79 = 2.235$.

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}(2.235) + \frac{2.235}{1+2.235^{2}}\right] = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[1.151 + \frac{2.235}{5.995}\right]$$

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[1.151 + 0.373\right] = \frac{1.524}{3.1416} \approx 0.485$$

Re-compute:

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{0.485 \times 0.3 \times 5000}{4\pi \times 12.5}} = \sqrt{\frac{727.5}{157.08}} = \sqrt{4.631} \approx 2.15 \text{ m}$$

Iterate again: $L \approx 2.15$ m → $h/L = 1.86$ → $F \approx 0.46$ → $L \approx 2.10$ m. Convergence is achieved.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{L_{safe} \approx 2.10 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, NBC 2016 mandates a **minimum 6 m separation** between non-residential and residential buildings regardless of calculation, due to additional factors like flying brands and fire service access. The calculation gives a *radiation-based* minimum, which is then compared with the regulatory minimum, and the **larger** value governs.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Wall vs. Ceiling

| Criterion | Fire-Resistant Wall | Fire-Resistant Ceiling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Function** | Vertical compartmentation | Horizontal compartmentation |
| **Orientation** | Vertical | Horizontal |
| **Fire Test Standard** | IS 3809 / BS 476 Part 22 | IS 3809 / BS 476 Part 21 |
| **Failure Mode** | Collapse, flame passage (integrity) | Collapse, excessive temp rise (insulation) |
| **Typical FRR** | 2–4 hours | 2–4 hours |
| **Critical Detail** | Continuity top/bottom, no gaps at slab | Penetration of services, recessed lights |
| **Key Material** | RCC, masonry, gypsum | RCC slab + (optional) suspended ceiling |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define fire separation. State the governing safety criterion used in NBC 2016 for the calculation of safe distance between buildings.*

**Model Answer:**
Fire separation is the spatial and/or structural provision made between two buildings (or fire compartments) to prevent the spread of fire from one to the other for a specified period. As per NBC 2016, the governing safety criterion is that the **radiative heat flux** incident on the neighboring building façade must not exceed **12.5 kW/m²** (the critical heat flux for piloted ignition of cellulosic materials). [3 marks]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*List any three design principles of fire-resistant walls as per IS 3809.*

**Model Answer:**
1. The wall must be constructed of **non-combustible materials** (RCC, brick, gypsum). [1 mark]
2. The wall must have a minimum **fire resistance rating of 2 hours** (4 hours for high-rise). [1 mark]
3. The wall must extend **continuously from floor to the underside of the next floor** without any gap, and all service penetrations must be **fire-stopped** with rated sealants. [1 mark]

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students use $Q$ instead of $\chi_r \cdot Q$ in the safe-distance formula — this **doubles the distance** and costs 3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **view factor** and assuming $F = 1$. The view factor is almost always less than 0.5 for realistic geometries.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not comparing the **calculated value with the NBC regulatory minimum** and adopting the larger.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In wall design questions, students describe *materials* but forget to address **continuity** and **penetration sealing** (2 marks lost).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **four mechanisms of fire spread between two adjacent buildings**. State which mechanism is dominant in KTU-level safe-distance calculations. [7 marks]

**Q.A (b)** A residential building has a fully developed fire with a Heat Release Rate of $Q = 8000$ kW. The fire window is $4 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m}$, with an effective flame height of $5 \text{ m}$. Taking $\chi_r = 0.3$ and $q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$, determine the **safe distance** from the adjacent building using the point-source radiation model. Assume $\tau = 1.0$. [7 marks]

**Model Solution — Q.A (a):**

The four mechanisms of fire spread between adjacent buildings are:

1. **Radiation:** Heat energy emitted as electromagnetic waves from the fire window/hot façade. The intensity at the receiver follows the inverse-square law. **This is the dominant mechanism** in safe-distance calculations. [2 marks]
2. **Convection:** Hot combustion gases rise as a buoyant plume and can travel horizontally under wind to ignite upper floors of the neighboring building. [1.5 marks]
3. **Conduction:** Heat transmitted through shared structural members (party walls, common slabs) — relevant for terraced/row housing. [1.5 marks]
4. **Flying Brands / Embers:** Burning debris transported by wind can land on roofs or in open windows, igniting the building at a distance. [1 mark]

A labelled sketch of two adjacent buildings with arrows showing the four mechanisms is expected. [1 mark]

**Model Solution — Q.A (b):**

**Given:** $Q = 8000$ kW, $\chi_r = 0.3$, $q''_{crit} = 12.5$ kW/m², $\tau = 1.0$, $h = 5$ m.

**Step 1:** Compute radiative power.

$$Q_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot Q = 0.3 \times 8000 = 2400 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Assume $L = 5$ m, compute view factor $F_{1 \to 2}$.

$$\frac{h}{L} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0$$

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}(1.0) + \frac{1.0}{1+1.0^{2}}\right] = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[0.7854 + 0.5\right] = \frac{1.2854}{3.1416} \approx 0.409$$

**Step 3:** Compute $L_{safe}$.

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{F \cdot \tau \cdot Q_{rad}}{4\pi \cdot q''_{crit}}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.409 \times 1.0 \times 2400}{4\pi \times 12.5}}$$

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{981.6}{157.08}} = \sqrt{6.249} \approx 2.50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Iterate with $L = 2.5$ m, $h/L = 5/2.5 = 2.0$.

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}(2.0) + \frac{2.0}{1+4}\right] = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[1.1071 + 0.4\right] = \frac{1.5071}{3.1416} \approx 0.480$$

Re-compute:

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{0.480 \times 2400}{4\pi \times 12.5}} = \sqrt{\frac{1152}{157.08}} = \sqrt{7.334} \approx 2.71 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Iterate again with $L = 2.71$ m, $h/L \approx 1.845$, $F \approx 0.474$.

$$L_{safe} \approx 2.70 \text{ m (converged)}$$

**Final Answer:** $L_{safe} \approx 2.70$ m. [7 marks: 1 for Q_rad, 2 for view factor calculation, 3 for L_safe calculation, 1 for iteration note]

> Compare with NBC minimum (6 m for residential). NBC value governs → **6 m**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**Q.B (a)** With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **design principles of a fire-resistant wall** as per IS 3809 and NBC 2016. Mention the minimum fire resistance rating for a party wall in a high-rise residential building. [7 marks]

**Q.B (b)** A $10 \text{ m}$ tall commercial building catches fire with HRR $Q = 12000$ kW. The effective flame height is $h = 8 \text{ m}$. Calculate the safe distance from the neighboring plot boundary. Use $\chi_r = 0.3$, $q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$, $\tau = 0.95$. [7 marks]

**Model Solution — Q.B (a):**

**Design Principles of Fire-Resistant Wall:**

1. **Material:** Non-combustible — RCC, brick masonry, AAC blocks, gypsum (Type X). [1 mark]
2. **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):** Minimum **4 hours** for party walls in high-rise residential (Type 1A) construction as per NBC 2016; 2 hours for low-rise (Type 2). [1 mark]
3. **Continuity:** Must extend from floor slab to the underside of the next floor slab (or roof) without gaps. [1 mark]
4. **Structural Independence:** Must remain stable even if the adjacent structure collapses — designed for thermal bowing and eccentric loads. [1 mark]
5. **Penetration Sealing:** All pipes, ducts, conduits must be sealed with fire-rated sealants/collars matching the wall's FRR. [1 mark]
6. **Opening Protection:** Fire doors must be self-closing with intumescent strips (tested to IS 3614 Part 2). [1 mark]

A neat labelled sketch showing wall layers, top/bottom connection to slab, and a fire door is expected. [1 mark]

**Model Solution — Q.B (b):**

**Given:** $Q = 12000$ kW, $\chi_r = 0.3$, $q''_{crit} = 12.5$ kW/m², $\tau = 0.95$, $h = 8$ m.

**Step 1:** $Q_{rad} = 0.3 \times 12000 = 3600$ kW.

**Step 2:** Assume $L = 5$ m, $h/L = 1.6$.

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}(1.6) + \frac{1.6}{1+2.56}\right] = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[1.0122 + 0.4494\right] = \frac{1.4616}{3.1416} \approx 0.465$$

**Step 3:** Compute $L_{safe}$.

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{F \cdot \tau \cdot Q_{rad}}{4\pi \cdot q''_{crit}}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.465 \times 0.95 \times 3600}{4\pi \times 12.5}}$$

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{1590.3}{157.08}} = \sqrt{10.124} \approx 3.18 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Iterate with $L = 3.18$ m, $h/L = 2.516$.

$$F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}(2.516) + \frac{2.516}{1+6.33}\right] = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[1.1944 + 0.343\right] = \frac{1.537}{3.1416} \approx 0.489$$

Re-compute:

$$L_{safe} = \sqrt{\frac{0.489 \times 0.95 \times 3600}{157.08}} = \sqrt{\frac{1672.4}{157.08}} = \sqrt{10.647} \approx 3.26 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Converged value: $L_{safe} \approx 3.27$ m.

**Final Answer:** $L_{safe} \approx 3.27$ m. [7 marks: 1 for Q_rad, 2 for F, 3 for L_safe, 1 for iteration]

> NBC minimum for commercial-to-boundary = **9 m**. **NBC value governs** → 9 m.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fire Separation & Fire-Resistant Walls/Ceilings**

- **Fire Separation** prevents fire spread by *spatial distance* (safe distance) and/or *structural compartmentation* (walls/ceilings).
- **Dominant heat transfer** between buildings is **radiation** — calculate safe distance using the point-source model.
- **Critical Heat Flux** $q''_{crit} = 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$ for cellulosic materials (wood, paint, PVC).
- **Radiative fraction** $\chi_r \approx 0.3$ for compartment fires — use $Q_{rad} = 0.3 \cdot Q$.
- **Safe distance formula:** $L_{safe} = \sqrt{(F \cdot \tau \cdot \chi_r \cdot Q) / (4\pi \cdot q''_{crit})}$.
- **View factor** for parallel vertical walls: $F = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\tan^{-1}(h/L) + \frac{h/L}{1+(h/L)^{2}}\right]$ — must iterate with $L$.
- **Always compare** calculated $L_{safe}$ with **NBC 2016 Table minimum** and adopt the **larger**.
- **Fire-resistant wall** must be: (i) non-combustible, (ii) FRR ≥ 2 h (4 h for high-rise), (iii) continuous floor-to-slab, (iv) all penetrations fire-stopped, (v) fire doors self-closing.
- **Fire-resistant ceiling** must satisfy **Integrity (E)** and **Insulation (I)** criteria per IS 3809 — unexposed face temperature rise ≤ 140 °C average / 180 °C single point.
- **Concealed spaces** (plenums, service voids) require **fire barriers** at intervals to prevent horizontal smoke/fire spread.
- **Common exam trap:** forgetting to use $Q_{rad}$ (not $Q$) in the formula → distance is $\sqrt{0.3} = 0.55$ times smaller than wrong answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Resistant Screens - Solid Screens and Water Curtains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Resistant Screens - Solid Screens and Water Curtains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Resistant Screens — Formal Definition

A **Fire Resistant Screen** is a passive (or active) fire separation element strategically placed between a potential fire source and an adjacent area, asset, or compartment, with the primary objective of **blocking, attenuating, or arresting the transmission of thermal radiation, hot gases, and flame impingement** during a fire event. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, fire resistant screens are classified into two principal families:

1. **Solid Screens** — Rigid, continuous physical barriers (masonry, concrete, gypsum, steel-clad, or intumescent).
2. **Water Curtains** — Active water-delivery systems producing a continuous water barrier (deluge, spray, cascade) that absorbs radiant heat via vaporization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> Fire resistant screens are evaluated against three classical fire-resistance criteria:
> **R (Load-bearing)**, **E (Integrity)**, and **I (Insulation)**. The combined rating **REI** governs the acceptance of any solid screen under IS 3614 (Part 2) and Eurocode EN 1992-1-2 / EN 1993-1-2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Umbrella and the Wet Towel"**
> - A **Solid Screen** behaves like a **thick concrete wall** that you stand behind on a hot summer day. The wall does not *absorb* the heat; it **refuses to let it pass**. It is a **passive mass barrier**.
> - A **Water Curtain** behaves like a **wet towel hung in a doorway** between a kitchen fire and a living room. The towel does not stop flames, but as water evaporates, it **drinks up the heat** (latent heat of vaporization, $h_{fg} \approx 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$). It is an **active energy-sink barrier**.

Together, they form the **"Two-Wall Defense"** of structural fire engineering — one by **mass resistance**, the other by **phase-change heat absorption**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Codes

| Parameter | Solid Screen | Water Curtain |
|---|---|---|
| Governing Standard | IS 3614 (Part 2), EN 1992-1-2 | NFPA 15, IS 15105, TAC Guidelines |
| Fire Resistance Rating | **30 / 60 / 120 / 180 / 240 min** | Equivalent Radiation Attenuation (ERA) |
| Critical Performance | Integrity + Insulation | Heat flux reduction $\geq 80\%$ |
| Typical Design W/D | kW/m² absorbed | kW/m² rejected |
| Acceptance Criterion | $\Delta T \le 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (cold face) | $q''_{\text{transmitted}} \le 12.5\ \text{kW/m}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorise for KTU Exam:**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water: $h_{fg} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$
> - Specific heat of water: $C_p = 4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Standard fire exposure curve: **ISO 834** ($T = 345\ \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$, with $T$ in $^\circ$C and $t$ in minutes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solid Screens — Operational Principles

A solid fire screen resists heat transfer by exploiting the **low thermal diffusivity** of dense, porous, or insulating materials. Three sequential heat-transfer phenomena govern its performance:

1. **Conduction** through the solid matrix — governed by Fourier's law.
2. **Heat storage** in the bulk material — governed by volumetric specific heat ($\rho C_p$).
3. **Radiation & convection** re-emission from the unexposed (cold) face.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Solid Screen Performance

- **Step 1 — Heat reaches the hot face:** The fire exposes one side to a time-varying temperature profile (ISO 834 standard fire).
- **Step 2 — Thermal wave penetrates:** Heat propagates inward following the **transient heat conduction equation**.

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

where $\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s).

- **Step 3 — Cold-face temperature rises:** After a lag (thermal inertia), the unexposed face temperature begins to climb. Failure of **Insulation (I)** occurs when $\Delta T_{\text{cold}} > 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (mean) or $180\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (max, single point) per IS 3614.
- **Step 4 — Integrity (E) check:** Cracks, fissures, or through-thickness penetration of flames/hot gases exceeding $10\ \text{s}$ duration leads to integrity failure.
- **Step 5 — Load-bearing (R) check:** For load-bearing screens, the structure must sustain the design load under fire exposure for the rated duration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a thick concrete wall outperform a thin steel plate?**
> Concrete has $\rho \approx 2400\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $C_p \approx 0.88\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, giving a high **thermal capacity** $\rho C_p \approx 2112\ \text{kJ/(m}^3\text{·K)}$. Combined with low thermal diffusivity, it absorbs and stores heat slowly — this is the **thermal flywheel effect**.

## 2.2 Water Curtains — Operational Principles

A water curtain protects by **extracting heat from the radiant flux** through three coupled physical processes:

- **Process A — Sensible Heating:** Water droplets are warmed from ambient to $100\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Process B — Latent Heat Absorption:** Water vaporizes, absorbing $h_{fg} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$.
- **Process C — Steam Plume Attenuation:** The resulting water vapor further scatters and absorbs infrared radiation.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Water Curtain Performance

- **Step 1 — Detection activation:** Heat/smoke detector or fusible link triggers the deluge valve.
- **Step 2 — Water discharge:** Open nozzles release water at **0.3 to 1.0 MPa** pressure.
- **Step 3 — Droplet formation:** Droplet size distribution (typically 0.3–1.5 mm) determines surface-to-volume ratio.
- **Step 4 — Radiant heat interception:** Droplets absorb radiation in transit; smaller droplets → higher absorption efficiency.
- **Step 5 — Cooling of hot gases & surfaces:** Evaporating water reduces gas temperature and cools adjacent structures.
- **Step 6 — Steam dilution:** Water vapor dilutes combustion gases, suppressing flame spread.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorise This for the KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ | ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve | $^\circ$C, $t$ in min |
| 2 | $q'' = \varepsilon \sigma (T_h^4 - T_c^4)$ | Radiative heat flux (Stefan-Boltzmann) | kW/m² |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{abs}} = \dot{m}(C_p \Delta T + h_{fg})$ | Heat absorbed by water curtain | kW |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho_w \cdot q \cdot A_{\text{screen}}$ | Water mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 5 | $\tau = \frac{x^2}{4\alpha}$ | Thermal penetration time (semi-infinite slab) | s |
| 6 | $\Delta T(x,t) = \Delta T_s \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$ | Temperature distribution (semi-infinite) | $^\circ$C |
| 7 | $q''_{\text{trans}} = q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot e^{-k \cdot w}$ | Water curtain transmittance (Beer-Lambert) | kW/m² |
| 8 | $R_{\text{insulation}} = \frac{x}{k}$ | Thermal resistance of solid screen | m²·K/W |
| 9 | $w = \frac{\dot{V}}{A}$ | Water density (L/min per m²) | L/(min·m²) |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{curtain}} = 1 - e^{-k \cdot w}$ | Curtain absorption efficiency | dimensionless |

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (≈ **0.8–0.95** for flames)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4)$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant
- $\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $k$ = extinction coefficient of water spray (≈ **0.5–1.5 m²/L**)
- $w$ = water density (L/m²)
- $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate (L/min)
- $A$ = screen area (m²)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Solid screens** are universally applied to **transformer enclosures, fire-rated walls in atriums, oil-storage battery rooms, and industrial process firewalls**.
> - **Water curtains** are deployed in **high-ceiling atria, aircraft hangars, LPG impounding areas, cable tunnels, and offshore platforms** where physical walls are impractical.

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix — Solid Screens vs. Water Curtains

| Feature | Solid Screen | Water Curtain |
|---|---|---|
| Type | **Passive** | **Active** |
| Activation | Immediate (always on) | Needs detection + valve action |
| Maintenance | Low | High (pumps, valves, water supply) |
| Spatial footprint | Permanent, large | Compact, can be demountable |
| Failure mode | Cracking, spalling, debonding | Pump failure, nozzle blockage, water supply loss |
| Reusability after fire | Often destroyed | Rechargeable, minimal damage |
| Best suited for | Permanent firewalls, transformer bays | Atria, process separators, temporary protection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fire Resistance Time of a Solid Screen (Semi-Infinite Slab Method)

We derive the **time required for the cold face of a solid screen to reach a critical temperature rise of $140\ ^\circ\text{C}$**, assuming one-dimensional transient heat conduction.

### 3.1.1 Governing Equation

For a semi-infinite solid exposed to a sudden surface temperature $T_s$ at $t=0$:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

with initial condition $T(x,0) = T_0$ (ambient, typically $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$) and boundary condition $T(0,t) = T_s$.

### 3.1.2 Solution — Error Function Form

The standard solution is:

$$
T(x,t) - T_0 = (T_s - T_0) \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)
$$

where $\text{erfc}$ is the complementary error function:

$$
\text{erfc}(u) = 1 - \text{erf}(u) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_u^{\infty} e^{-\xi^2}\, d\xi
$$

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Fire Resistance Time

**Step 1 — Apply the failure criterion.**
The screen fails when the cold face temperature at $x = L$ (screen thickness) rises by $\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$ above ambient.

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = (T_s - T_0) \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{L}{2\sqrt{\alpha \cdot t_{\text{FR}}}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the argument.**

$$
\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{L}{2\sqrt{\alpha \cdot t_{\text{FR}}}}\right) = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{crit}}}{T_s - T_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert the erfc function.**

$$
\frac{L}{2\sqrt{\alpha \cdot t_{\text{FR}}}} = \text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_{\text{crit}}}{T_s - T_0}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the fire-resistance time $t_{\text{FR}}$.**

$$
t_{\text{FR}} = \frac{L^2}{4\alpha \cdot \left[\text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{crit}}}{T_s - T_0}\right)\right]^2}
$$

### 3.1.4 Closed-Form Approximation

For typical fire temperatures $T_s \approx 1000\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_0 = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
\frac{\Delta T_{\text{crit}}}{T_s - T_0} = \frac{140}{980} \approx 0.143
$$

From standard tables: $\text{erfc}^{-1}(0.143) \approx 1.18$.

$$
t_{\text{FR}} \approx \frac{L^2}{4\alpha \cdot (1.18)^2} = \frac{L^2}{5.57\,\alpha}
$$

This is the **semi-infinite slab formula** commonly cited in KTU textbook problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Note:** This derivation assumes (a) no moisture migration, (b) constant material properties, (c) one-dimensional heat flow, and (d) a semi-infinite solid. For finite screens, a finite-difference or numerical (FEM) approach is required.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Water Curtain Heat Balance

We derive the **minimum water flow rate** required to fully absorb the incident radiant heat flux from a fire.

### 3.2.1 Incident Radiant Heat Flux

From a fire of emissive power $E$ and view factor $F$:

$$
q''_{\text{inc}} = E \cdot F \quad [\text{kW/m}^2]
$$

A typical large hydrocarbon pool fire may produce $E = 150\ \text{kW/m}^2$ at close range, with $F$ depending on geometry.

### 3.2.2 Heat to be Absorbed

The water curtain must absorb essentially all of $q''_{\text{inc}}$ through:
- Sensible heating: $q_{\text{sens}} = \dot{m} C_p (T_{\text{boil}} - T_{\text{amb}})$
- Latent heating: $q_{\text{lat}} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$

### 3.2.3 Energy Balance

$$
\dot{m} \left[ C_p (100 - T_{\text{amb}}) + h_{fg} \right] = q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot A_{\text{curtain}}
$$

**Step 1 — Insert numerical values.**
With $C_p = 4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $h_{fg} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $T_{\text{amb}} = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
C_p (100 - 20) = 4.186 \times 80 = 334.88\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Total energy per kg of water.**

$$
e_{\text{total}} = 334.88 + 2260 = 2594.88\ \text{kJ/kg} \approx 2595\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot A_{\text{curtain}}}{2595}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow.**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_w} = \frac{q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot A_{\text{curtain}}}{2595 \times 1000}\quad[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 5 — Express as water density (L/min per m²).**

$$
w = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_{\text{curtain}}} \times 60 \times 1000 = \frac{q''_{\text{inc}} \times 60}{2595}\ [\text{L/(min·m}^2\text{)}]
$$

### 3.2.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A transformer produces $q''_{\text{inc}} = 80\ \text{kW/m}^2$ at a proposed water-curtain location. Find the **required water density**.

**Solution:**

$$
w = \frac{80 \times 60}{2595} = \frac{4800}{2595} \approx 1.85\ \text{L/(min·m}^2\text{)}
$$

With a **safety factor of 2** (to account for non-uniform spray, droplet drift, wind effects):

$$
w_{\text{design}} = 2 \times 1.85 = 3.70\ \text{L/(min·m}^2\text{)}
$$

Rounded up to the nearest standard value per **NFPA 15** (typical design range **4–12 L/(min·m²)**), we select:

$$
\boxed{w_{\text{design}} = 4.0\ \text{L/(min·m}^2\text{)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This result confirms that the *theoretical minimum* is low (~1.85), but the **design water density is typically 4–12 L/(min·m²)** in codes to cover spray distribution losses, droplet scatter, and reliability margin.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmission Through a Water Curtain (Beer-Lambert Form)

### 3.3.1 Statement

The transmitted heat flux through a water curtain of water density $w$ is:

$$
q''_{\text{trans}} = q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot e^{-k_{\text{ext}} \cdot w}
$$

### 3.3.2 Derivation

Consider a thin layer of water spray of thickness $dy$. The radiative heat absorbed in $dy$ is proportional to the incident flux:

$$
dq'' = -k_{\text{ext}} \cdot q'' \cdot dw
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{dq''}{q''} = -k_{\text{ext}} \cdot dw
$$

Integrating across the full water density $0 \to w$:

$$
\int_{q''_{\text{inc}}}^{q''_{\text{trans}}} \frac{dq''}{q''} = -k_{\text{ext}} \int_0^w dw
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{q''_{\text{trans}}}{q''_{\text{inc}}}\right) = -k_{\text{ext}} \cdot w
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
q''_{\text{trans}} = q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot e^{-k_{\text{ext}} \cdot w}
$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example

Given $q''_{\text{inc}} = 100\ \text{kW/m}^2$, $k_{\text{ext}} = 0.5\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$, $w = 6\ \text{L/m}^2$:

$$
q''_{\text{trans}} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.5 \times 6} = 100 \cdot e^{-3} = 100 \times 0.0498 \approx 4.98\ \text{kW/m}^2
$$

The **attenuation efficiency** is:

$$
\eta = 1 - e^{-3} = 0.95 = 95\%
$$

This is well above the typical **80%** attenuation criterion required by NFPA 15.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to Choose Solid Screen vs. Water Curtain**

| Decision Criterion | Solid Screen | Water Curtain |
|---|---|---|
| Permanent protection required | ✅ Preferred | ✗ Possible but costly |
| Need for transparency / visibility | ✗ Solid | ✅ Possible (clear water) |
| Risk of explosion overpressure | ✅ Excellent | ✗ Vulnerable to blast |
| Limited floor space | ✗ Bulky | ✅ Compact |
| Aesthetics (e.g., atrium lobby) | ✗ Opaque | ✅ Visually unobtrusive |
| Outdoor / wind exposure | ✅ No effect | ⚠ Affected by drift |
| Continuous high-temperature exposure (e.g., transformer) | ✅ Reliable | ⚠ Needs redundancy |
| Rapid deployment | ✗ Slow construction | ✅ Fast installation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solid Fire Screen Heat Transfer Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[FIRE SOURCE] --> nodeB[HOT FACE: T_s approx 1000 C]
    nodeB --> nodeC[CONDUCTION THROUGH SOLID MATRIX k, rho, Cp]
    nodeC --> nodeD[COLD FACE: T_c monitored]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Failure Check}
    nodeE -- Insulation I OK --> nodeF[REI Rating Maintained]
    nodeE -- Insulation I Fails --> nodeG[T rises above 140 C, FIRE SPREAD]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Protected Compartment]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Water Curtain Operating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Fire Detected] --> nodeB[Deluge Valve Opens]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Pressurized Water Supply Activated]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Open Nozzles Discharge]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Droplet Cloud Forms]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Sensible Heating of Droplets]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Latent Heat Absorption: Evaporation]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Steam Plume Attenuates Radiation]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Radiation Attenuation > 80 percent}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ[Protected Area Maintained]
    nodeI -- No --> nodeK[Heat Flux Exceeds Limit]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Solid Screen with Temperature Gradient

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Fire Side T_s] -->|Exposure| nodeB[Hot Face T_1]
    nodeB -->|Conduction| nodeC[Mid-Plane T_mid]
    nodeC -->|Conduction| nodeD[Cold Face T_c]
    nodeD -->|Radiation plus Convection| nodeE[Adjacent Compartment]
    style nodeA fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C92A2A,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeB fill:#FFA94D,stroke:#E8590C,color:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#FFD43B,stroke:#FAB005,color:#000000
    style nodeD fill:#69DB7C,stroke:#2F9E44,color:#000000
    style nodeE fill:#4DABF7,stroke:#1971C2,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Design Sequence for a Water Curtain System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Identify Fire Source] --> nodeB[Step 2: Compute q_inc from View Factor]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Set Target Transmitted Flux q_trans]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Solve Beer Lambert for Water Density w]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Select Nozzle Type and Spacing]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: Compute Total Flow and Pump Sizing]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 7: Add Safety Factor 1.5 to 2.0]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 8: Final Design Submission]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above depict **functional heat-flow and process-control architectures** for fire screens. They are not physical drawings, but they clearly map cause-effect and energy-flow relationships as required by KTU module-level outcomes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a "Fire Resistant Screen" and list the three performance criteria used to classify its fire resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fire resistant screen** is a vertical or horizontal barrier — solid (e.g., concrete, masonry, gypsum, steel) or active (water curtain) — designed to **prevent or delay the spread of fire, smoke, and hot gases** from one fire compartment to another.

The three performance criteria (per IS 3614 Part 2 / EN 1364) are:

1. **R (Load-bearing capacity):** The ability of the screen to sustain its design load under fire exposure.
2. **E (Integrity):** The ability to prevent the passage of flames and hot gases through cracks or openings.
3. **I (Insulation):** The ability to limit the temperature rise on the unexposed face to $\le 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (mean) or $\le 180\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (maximum at any point).

A typical solid screen is designated as **REI 120**, meaning it satisfies all three criteria for **120 minutes** under standard fire exposure.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the principle of operation of a water curtain as a fire-resistant screen. What is the typical water density used in design?**

**Model Answer:**

A **water curtain** operates on the principle of **heat extraction through the latent heat of vaporization** of water. When the deluge system activates, water is discharged through open nozzles forming a uniform water sheet or droplet cloud. As radiant heat from the fire passes through this cloud, the droplets:

- Absorb sensible heat (raising water temperature from ambient to $100\ ^\circ\text{C}$),
- Undergo phase change (vaporization), absorbing $h_{fg} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$,
- Generate steam that further scatters and attenuates infrared radiation.

**Typical design water density:** $4$ to $12\ \text{L/(min·m}^2\text{)}$ of curtain area, per NFPA 15 and TAC guidelines. This range ensures an **attenuation efficiency of 80–95%** of the incident radiant heat flux.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Module Choice Instruction (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** of the following (either Question A or Question B). Each carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Design of a Solid Fire Screen
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

A **200 mm thick reinforced concrete solid screen** is proposed as a fire barrier between a transformer bay and an adjacent control room. The material properties are:

- Thermal conductivity: $k = 1.4\ \text{W/(m·K)}$
- Density: $\rho = 2400\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat: $C_p = 880\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$
- Ambient temperature: $T_0 = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Fire surface temperature: $T_s = 1000\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (assumed constant for simplicity)
- Insulation failure criterion: $\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compute the thermal diffusivity of the concrete and the fire resistance time of the solid screen using the semi-infinite slab approximation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute thermal diffusivity.**

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho C_p} = \frac{1.4}{2400 \times 880} = \frac{1.4}{2{,}112{,}000} \approx 6.63 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature ratio.**

$$
\frac{\Delta T_{\text{crit}}}{T_s - T_0} = \frac{140}{1000 - 20} = \frac{140}{980} \approx 0.1429
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse erfc value.**

From standard tables, $\text{erfc}^{-1}(0.1429) \approx 1.18$.

**Step 4 — Apply the semi-infinite formula.**

$$
t_{\text{FR}} = \frac{L^2}{4\alpha \cdot [\text{erfc}^{-1}(0.1429)]^2}
$$

With $L = 0.200\ \text{m}$:

$$
L^2 = 0.04\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
4\alpha = 4 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-7} = 2.652 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
[\text{erfc}^{-1}(0.1429)]^2 = (1.18)^2 = 1.3924
$$

$$
t_{\text{FR}} = \frac{0.04}{2.652 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.3924} = \frac{0.04}{3.692 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 10{,}834\ \text{s}
$$

Converting to minutes:

$$
t_{\text{FR}} = \frac{10{,}834}{60} \approx 180.6\ \text{minutes}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{FR}} \approx 180\ \text{minutes}\ (\text{i.e., REI 180 solid screen})}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
A **200 mm concrete wall** with $\alpha \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ provides approximately **3 hours of fire resistance** under the simplified semi-infinite assumption. This is consistent with IS 3614 tabulated values for normal-weight concrete.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the limitations of the semi-infinite slab assumption and list the factors that may reduce the actual fire resistance of the screen in practice.**

**Model Solution:**

The semi-infinite slab model **overestimates** the actual fire resistance because it neglects the following real-world effects:

| Factor | Effect on Fire Resistance |
|---|---|
