| **Moisture migration** | Water evaporation creates a heat sink initially but causes pore-pressure spalling at high temperatures. |
| **Thermal property variation with $T$** | Both $k$ and $C_p$ change with temperature; concrete $k$ may rise by 20–40% above $600\ ^\circ\text{C}$. |
| **Two-dimensional heat flow at edges** | Corners and joints experience bi-axial heat flux, lowering local resistance. |
| **Cracking and spalling** | Explosive spalling at $T > 300\ ^\circ\text{C}$ can reduce the effective thickness $L$ by 20–50%. |
| **Rebar temperature** | Steel reinforcement loses yield strength above $550\ ^\circ\text{C}$, reducing load-bearing capacity (R). |
| **Reinforcement cover loss** | Cover spalling exposes rebar directly to fire. |
| **Time-varying $T_s$** | ISO 834 fire is non-constant; the constant $T_s$ assumption under-predicts late-time penetration. |

**Conclusion:** While the semi-infinite model gives a useful **first estimate (≈ 180 min)**, the **actual rated fire resistance** is typically derived from **standard furnace tests** (IS 3614 Part 1) and is **lower by 15–30%** due to the above factors. Design should always reference tested REI values from accredited laboratories.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Design of a Water Curtain System
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

A **water curtain** is proposed to protect a high-value control room from an adjacent **diesel generator enclosure**. The proposed curtain is $6\ \text{m}$ wide and $4\ \text{m}$ tall. The incident radiant heat flux on the curtain from a postulated diesel fire is estimated at $q''_{\text{inc}} = 100\ \text{kW/m}^2$. The extinction coefficient of the spray is $k_{\text{ext}} = 0.6\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Determine the water density (L/min per m²) required to achieve a transmitted heat flux of $q''_{\text{trans}} \le 10\ \text{kW/m}^2$ (i.e., 90% attenuation).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Beer-Lambert equation.**

$$
q''_{\text{trans}} = q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot e^{-k_{\text{ext}} \cdot w}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
10 = 100 \cdot e^{-0.6 \cdot w}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the exponential.**

$$
e^{-0.6 w} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$
-0.6\, w = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $w$ (L/m²).**

$$
w = \frac{2.3026}{0.6} \approx 3.838\ \text{L/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Apply a safety factor of 1.5 to cover droplet drift, spray non-uniformity, and reliability.**

$$
w_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times 3.838 \approx 5.76\ \text{L/m}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to L/(min·m²). Note:** The Beer-Lambert $w$ is in **L/m² of curtain water mass**. To convert to **volumetric flow per minute**, we assume the curtain is "replenished" continuously; standard practice treats $w$ as the **applied flow density**:

$$
\boxed{w_{\text{design}} \approx 6\ \text{L/(min·m}^2\text{)}}
$$

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Calculate the total water flow rate required, the pump head, and recommend the nozzle type and spacing. Also state the design checks per NFPA 15.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Curtain area.**

$$
A_{\text{curtain}} = 6 \times 4 = 24\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Total design water flow rate.**

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{total}} = w_{\text{design}} \times A_{\text{curtain}} = 6 \times 24 = 144\ \text{L/min}
$$

Converting to litres per second:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{total}} = \frac{144}{60} = 2.4\ \text{L/s} = 0.0024\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Pump head calculation.**
A typical deluge nozzle requires $0.3$ to $0.5\ \text{MPa}$ at the nozzle. Adding pipe friction losses (assumed 10 m) and elevation head (5 m):

$$
H_{\text{pump}} = \frac{P_{\text{nozzle}}}{\rho_w g} + h_f + h_z
$$

$$
H_{\text{pump}} = \frac{0.4 \times 10^6}{1000 \times 9.81} + 10 + 5 \approx 40.8 + 10 + 5 \approx 55.8\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Nozzle type and spacing.**
- **Nozzle type:** Open deluge spray nozzle (e.g., **Type A — flat fan, 60° spray angle, K-factor 80**).
- **Spacing:** $1.5\ \text{m}$ horizontal × $1.5\ \text{m}$ vertical (per TAC and NFPA 15 guidelines for water curtains).

**Step 5 — Number of nozzles.**

$$
N = \frac{6}{1.5} \times \frac{4}{1.5} = 4 \times 2.67 \approx 8\ \text{nozzles (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 6 — Design checks per NFPA 15.**

| Check | Requirement |
|---|---|
| Water density | $\ge 6\ \text{L/(min·m}^2\text{)}$ ✅ |
| Nozzle pressure | $\ge 0.3\ \text{MPa}$ ✅ |
| Detection reliability | Flame/heat detector with redundancy ✅ |
| Power supply | Dual / battery backup ✅ |
| Water supply duration | $\ge 60\ \text{min}$ for hydrocarbon fires ✅ |
| Pump redundancy | Diesel-driven + electric ✅ |

**Conclusion:** The water curtain system, designed for **6 L/(min·m²)** with **8 spray nozzles** and a pump head of **≈ 56 m**, satisfies the **90% attenuation criterion** with an adequate safety margin per NFPA 15.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing up units:** Students often confuse **L/m²** (mass per unit area) with **L/(min·m²)** (volumetric flow per unit area per minute). Read the problem carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor:** A water curtain designed at the **theoretical minimum** (e.g., 1.85 L/(min·m²) in our example) will fail under real spray distribution losses. **Always apply SF = 1.5–2.0**.
> 3. **Wrong use of erfc vs erf:** For cold-face temperature, the equation uses $\text{erfc}$, not $\text{erf}$. A common error is $\text{erfc}(u) = 1 - \text{erf}(u)$; do not drop the complement.
> 4. **Neglecting ISO 834 fire variation:** The semi-infinite slab formula assumes constant $T_s$. The actual ISO 834 fire is **time-varying** ($T \propto \log t$), so the constant-$T_s$ result is conservative at early times and may be non-conservative at very long durations. Mention this in your answer.
> 5. **Missing REI notation:** KTU expects you to specify the screen as **REI 60, REI 120, etc.**, *not* just "fire resistant". A response stating only "R 120" loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Spalling and rebar not mentioned in solid-screen questions:** Failure to discuss **concrete spalling** and **rebar temperature limits (≈ 550 °C)** is a frequent cause of mark loss in concrete screen problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary for KTU ESE**

### Solid Screens
- ✅ Three performance criteria: **R (load), E (integrity), I (insulation)** → combined notation **REI**.
- ✅ Cold-face failure criterion: $\Delta T_{\text{mean}} \le 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta T_{\text{max}} \le 180\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ Fire resistance time (semi-infinite slab): $t_{\text{FR}} = L^2 / (4\alpha \cdot [\text{erfc}^{-1}(\Delta T_{\text{crit}}/(T_s - T_0))]^2)$.
- ✅ Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$ — lower $\alpha$ ⇒ better fire performance.
- ✅ Common materials: **concrete, masonry, gypsum board, steel-clad, intumescent-coated**.
- ✅ Limitations: cracking, spalling, rebar exposure, moisture migration, time-varying $T_s$.

### Water Curtains
- ✅ Principle: **latent heat of vaporization** absorbs radiant heat ($h_{fg} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$).
- ✅ Heat balance: $Q = \dot{m}(C_p \Delta T + h_{fg})$.
- ✅ Beer-Lambert transmission: $q''_{\text{trans}} = q''_{\text{inc}} \cdot e^{-k_{\text{ext}} w}$.
- ✅ Attenuation efficiency: $\eta = 1 - e^{-k_{\text{ext}} w}$.
- ✅ Design water density: **4–12 L/(min·m²)**, typical safety factor **1.5–2.0**.
- ✅ Codes: **NFPA 15, IS 15105, TAC**; minimum nozzle pressure **0.3 MPa**.
- ✅ Failure modes: pump failure, nozzle blockage, water supply loss, wind drift.

### Quick-Reference Comparison
- ✅ **Solid screen:** Passive, permanent, no moving parts, immune to water-supply failure.
- ✅ **Water curtain:** Active, requires detection + deluge valve, vulnerable to pump/water failure but **reusable** after a fire.
- ✅ Solid screens for **permanent firewalls**; water curtains for **atria, process separators, transformer enclosures, and offshore platforms**.

### Constants to Memorise
- ✅ $h_{fg} = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$
- ✅ $C_{p,\text{water}} = 4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- ✅ $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4)$
- ✅ $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- ✅ $\Delta T_{\text{crit,mean}} = 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- ✅ ISO 834: $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Barriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Local Barriers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Local Barrier** in the context of structural fire safety is a fire-resisting construction element (or assembly of elements) of limited extent, designed and installed at a specific location within a building to **subdivide, enclose, shield, or screen** a particular risk, asset, or zone from the effects of fire, smoke, heat radiation, and hot gases. Unlike full **fire compartments** (which enclose entire rooms or floors), local barriers are targeted, task-specific protective envelopes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Local barriers are non-load-bearing (or partially load-bearing) fire-resisting assemblies that interrupt flame spread, smoke migration, and thermal radiation at localized points. They are governed by **integrity (E)**, **insulation (I)**, and where applicable **load-bearing capacity (R)** criteria as per IS 3614 / IS 3809 / EN 1364 series.

### 1.2 Classification of Local Barriers (IS / NBC 2016)

| Category | Function | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire Screens** | Block flame & radiation passage | Around escalators, transformers, atriums |
| **Smoke Curtains** | Channelize / contain smoke | Large open-plan offices, malls, warehouses |
| **Draft / Curtain Boards** | Restrict lateral smoke spread | Industrial sheds, aircraft hangars |
| **Fire-rated Enclosures** | Protect vertical/shaft openings | Lift hoistways, staircases, MEP risers |
| **Radiation Shields** | Block radiant heat flux | Façades, adjacent property protection |
| **Fire-rated Glazing** | Maintain visibility + resist fire | Vision panels, atrium screens |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat Compartment" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a ship divided into many watertight compartments. If one section floods, the *whole ship* does not sink. **Local barriers** work exactly the same way for fire — instead of letting a single fire engulf an entire floor, the barrier **localizes** the damage to a small, manageable pocket. Think of them as the *bulkheads* inside a room rather than the *outer walls* of a building.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):** Typically **1 hr, 2 hr, 3 hr, or 4 hr** as per IS 3809.
- **Standard Fire Exposure Curve:** **IS 3809 / ISO 834** time-temperature relationship.
- **Critical Radiation Flux:** **12.5 kW/m²** at the barrier's unexposed face (failure threshold for spontaneous ignition of cellulosic materials).
- **Smoke Leakage Rate:** Typically limited to **≤ 25 m³/hr per m²** of barrier area under 25 Pa pressure differential.
- **Temperature Rise Limit (Insulation criterion):** **≤ 140 °C average / ≤ 180 °C maximum** above ambient on the unexposed face.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Local Barrier

A local barrier must simultaneously satisfy **three failure criteria** during a fire test:

1. **Stability / Load-bearing Capacity (R):** The barrier shall not collapse under its own load (if load-bearing) or under design lateral loads for the full fire duration.
2. **Integrity (E):** No through-cracks, gaps, or openings shall permit flames or hot gases to pass to the unexposed face.
3. **Insulation (I):** The average temperature rise on the unexposed face shall not exceed **140 °C** (with no single point exceeding **180 °C**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why all three?** A barrier with R & E but no I becomes a *radiation heater* — the unexposed face ignites adjacent combustibles even without direct flame contact. Conversely, I without E is meaningless because flames pass through cracks.

### 2.2 Design Philosophy — "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Compartmentation logic:** Buildings are divided into *macro* compartments (floors) and *micro* compartments (local barriers) to:
  - Limit fire to its point of origin
  - Provide safe egress time
  - Protect critical infrastructure (e.g., electrical rooms, data centres)
  - Reduce property damage
- **Heat transfer basis:** Heat reaches the unexposed face via **conduction** (through solid), **convection** (through gaps), and **radiation** (re-radiation from hot exposed face).
- **Material selection logic:** Gypsum board (dehydrates → forms water-vapor barrier), concrete (high thermal mass), intumescent coatings (expand 10–50× when heated), steel (high strength but low insulation → needs cladding).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T(t) = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | ISO 834 / IS 3809 standard fire curve | $T$ in °C, $t$ in minutes |
| 2 | $\dot{q}''_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Radiative heat flux from hot surface | W/m² |
| 3 | $q = \frac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ | Conduction heat transfer (Fourier's law) | W |
| 4 | $t_f = \frac{\rho \, c_p \, L \, \Delta T_{adm}}{\dot{q}''_{fire}}$ | Time to reach adiabatic surface temp limit | seconds |
| 5 | $F_{12} = \frac{1}{A_1} \int_{A_1}\int_{A_2} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2} \, dA_2 \, dA_1$ | View factor between surfaces (radiation geometry) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta p}$ | Smoke leakage through cracks (orifice eqn.) | kg/s |
| 7 | $R = \sum \frac{L_i}{k_i}$ | Total thermal resistance of composite barrier | m²·K/W |
| 8 | $FRR_{req} = \frac{W \cdot F \cdot L \cdot C}{K}$ | Required FRR (T.T. Lie method) | hours |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{mean} = \frac{1}{A}\int_A [T(t) - T_0]\,dA$ | Mean temperature rise (insulation check) | °C |
| 10 | $q_{cr} = 12.5$ kW/m² | Critical radiation flux for cellulosic ignition | kW/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions** for ISO 834: $T_0 = 20\,°C$ (initial furnace temp), $t$ starts at ignition, $T(t)$ is the average furnace gas temperature.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise buildings:** Local barriers around lift lobbies form **firefighting lobbies**.
- **Transformer protection:** Mineral oil-filled transformers are enclosed in **4-hr fire-rated barriers** to prevent pool fire propagation.
- **Atriums:** Smoke curtains deploy at ceiling level to create **dynamic smoke reservoirs**.
- **Industrial warehouses:** Draft curtains (typically 0.5 m deep) are installed at **rafters** to channelize smoke toward mechanical extract points.
- **Data centres / Server Rooms:** Local barriers achieve **1-hr FRR** to allow gas-suppression systems (FM-200 / Novec 1230) to discharge effectively.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve

The standard time-temperature relationship used in furnace testing of local barriers is defined empirically to mimic a fully developed compartment fire.

$$
T(t) = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8t + 1)
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the boundary condition.

At $t = 0$ (test ignition), the furnace gas temperature must equal ambient $T_0$.

$$
T(0) = T_0 + 345 \, \log_{10}(8 \cdot 0 + 1) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(1)
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $\log_{10}(1)$.

Since $\log_{10}(1) = 0$, we get:

$$
T(0) = T_0 + 345(0) = T_0 = 20\,°C
$$

This confirms the **initial condition** is satisfied.

**Step 3:** Evaluate at $t = 30$ min (a common ½-hr rating check).

$$
T(30) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 30 + 1) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(241)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\log_{10}(241)$.

$$
\log_{10}(241) = \log_{10}(2.41 \times 10^2) = 2 + \log_{10}(2.41) \approx 2 + 0.3820 = 2.3820
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and solve.

$$
T(30) = 20 + 345(2.3820) = 20 + 821.79 = 841.79\,°C \approx 842\,°C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's insight:** At $t = 30$ min, the furnace reaches ~842 °C; at $t = 60$ min, ~945 °C; at $t = 120$ min, ~1049 °C. These three checkpoints are the *most tested values* in KTU numericals.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Fire Resistance Rating (T.T. Lie Method)

For a steel column protected by a local barrier (e.g., concrete encasement or gypsum cladding), the required FRR is estimated by:

$$
FRR_{req} = R_f \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{H'}{0.04}\right)^{0.8}\right]
$$

where the column heat-up factor is:

$$
H' = \frac{F}{V} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{k \, \rho \, c_p}}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.

- $R_f$ = Fire resistance of bare steel (hr)  
- $F/V$ = Section factor (m⁻¹) = perimeter / cross-sectional area of the steel member
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of protection (W/m·K)
- $\rho$ = Density of protection (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat of protection (J/kg·K)

**Step 2:** Compute $H'$.

Let $F/V = 200$ m⁻¹, $k = 0.17$ W/m·K (gypsum), $\rho = 800$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1700$ J/kg·K.

$$
H' = \frac{200}{\sqrt{0.17 \cdot 800 \cdot 1700}}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate denominator.

$$
0.17 \times 800 = 136
$$

$$
136 \times 1700 = 231{,}200
$$

$$
\sqrt{231{,}200} \approx 480.83
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $H'$.

$$
H' = \frac{200}{480.83} \approx 0.4159
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into FRR formula (assume $R_f = 0.5$ hr).

$$
FRR_{req} = 0.5 \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{0.4159}{0.04}\right)^{0.8}\right]
$$

$$
\frac{0.4159}{0.04} = 10.3975
$$

$$
(10.3975)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \ln(10.3975)} = e^{0.8 \times 2.3417} = e^{1.8734} \approx 6.510
$$

**Step 6:** Final result.

$$
FRR_{req} = 0.5 \cdot [1 + 6.510] = 0.5 \cdot 7.510 = 3.755 \text{ hr} \approx 4 \text{ hr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The steel column requires a **4-hour** local fire-rated enclosure. This is the standard for transformer bays, generator rooms, and high-criticality structural members.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Temperature Rise on Unexposed Face (Insulation Check)

For a composite barrier (e.g., 12 mm gypsum + 100 mm concrete), the mean temperature rise after time $t$ is:

$$
\Delta T_{mean}(t) = \frac{\dot{q}''_{in} \cdot t}{\rho_{eff} \, c_{p,eff} \, L}
$$

where $\dot{q}''_{in}$ is the incident heat flux at the unexposed face, $L$ is total barrier thickness, and $\rho_{eff}, c_{p,eff}$ are effective (volume-weighted) properties.

**Step 1:** Compute effective density.

$$
\rho_{eff} = \frac{\rho_1 V_1 + \rho_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}
$$

For 12 mm gypsum ($\rho_1 = 800$ kg/m³) and 100 mm concrete ($\rho_2 = 2300$ kg/m³):

$$
\rho_{eff} = \frac{800(0.012) + 2300(0.100)}{0.012 + 0.100} = \frac{9.6 + 230}{0.112} = \frac{239.6}{0.112} = 2139.3 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute effective specific heat (assume $c_{p,1} = 1700$ J/kg·K, $c_{p,2} = 880$ J/kg·K).

$$
c_{p,eff} = \frac{\rho_1 V_1 c_{p,1} + \rho_2 V_2 c_{p,2}}{\rho_1 V_1 + \rho_2 V_2} = \frac{(800)(0.012)(1700) + (2300)(0.100)(880)}{239.6}
$$

$$
= \frac{16{,}320 + 202{,}400}{239.6} = \frac{218{,}720}{239.6} = 912.85 \text{ J/kg·K}
$$

**Step 3:** Assume $\dot{q}''_{in} = 25$ kW/m² (typical at 60-min exposure) and compute $\Delta T_{mean}$ for $t = 60$ min = 3600 s.

$$
\Delta T_{mean} = \frac{25{,}000 \times 3600}{2139.3 \times 912.85 \times 0.112}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate denominator.

$$
2139.3 \times 912.85 = 1{,}952{,}998
$$

$$
1{,}952{,}998 \times 0.112 = 218{,}735.8
$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$
\Delta T_{mean} = \frac{90{,}000{,}000}{218{,}735.8} = 411.5\,°C
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — 411 °C exceeds the 140 °C insulation limit!** This means the simple 12 mm gypsum + 100 mm concrete configuration **fails** the insulation criterion at 60 min. A thicker barrier or a low-conductivity insulation layer (e.g., mineral wool) must be added.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Local Barrier

| Risk Scenario | Recommended Local Barrier | FRR | Key Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Escalator opening between floors | Fire-rated curtains (auto-deploy) | 2 hr | Dynamic closure, atrium safety |
| Transformer yard (oil-filled) | Concrete masonry enclosure | 4 hr | Pool fire prevention |
| Lift hoistway | Reinforced concrete shaft | 2 hr | Vertical smoke migration |
| Data centre server racks | Intumescent-coated steel + gypsum | 1 hr | Compatibility with gas suppression |
| Warehouse roof | Draft curtain (non-rated) | N/A | Smoke management, not FRR |
| Glass atrium | Fire-rated glazing (EI 60) | 1 hr | Visibility + integrity |
| Generator room | Concrete + vermiculite plaster | 4 hr | Protects life-safety power |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Local Barrier System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fire Initiation Source] --> B{Heat Transfer Mechanism}
    B --> C1[Conduction Through Solid]
    B --> C2[Convection Through Gaps]
    B --> C3[Radiation Across Cavity]
    C1 --> D[Local Barrier Assembly]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E1[Stability Check R]
    D --> E2[Integrity Check E]
    D --> E3[Insulation Check I]
    E1 --> F{All Criteria Satisfied?}
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F -->|Yes| G[Protected Zone Maintained]
    F -->|No| H[Barrier Failure Mode]
    H --> H1[Collapse R]
    H --> H2[Through-cracks E]
    H --> H3[Unacceptable Temp Rise I]
    G --> I[Egress Time + Asset Protection Achieved]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Composite Local Barrier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Exposed Face] --> L2[Outer Cladding - 12mm Gypsum]
    L2 --> L3[Insulation Layer - 50mm Mineral Wool]
    L3 --> L4[Structural Core - 100mm Concrete]
    L4 --> L5[Unexposed Face - 12mm Gypsum]
    L5 --> L6[Protected Zone]

    subgraph fireSide[Fire Side]
        L1
        L2
    end
    subgraph barrierAssembly[Composite Barrier]
        L3
        L4
    end
    subgraph safeSide[Safe Side]
        L5
        L6
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing Barrier Material

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Start: Identify Local Risk] --> q1{Is transparency required?}
    q1 -->|Yes| m1[Fire-rated Glazing - EI 60 to 120]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is dynamic deployment needed?}
    q2 -->|Yes| m2[Auto-deploy Fire Curtain - 2 hr]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Is the asset critical infrastructure?}
    q3 -->|Yes| m3[Concrete / Masonry Enclosure - 4 hr]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Is space constraint a driver?}
    q4 -->|Yes| m4[Intumescent-coated Steel - 1 to 2 hr]
    q4 -->|No| m5[Gypsum + Mineral Wool Composite - 2 hr]
```

### 4.4 Failure Cascade Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1[Step 1: Fire Exposure] --> s2[Step 2: Thermal Stress on Material]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Micro-crack Initiation]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: Integrity Loss E]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: Flame + Hot Gas Passage]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6: Re-ignition of Adjacent Combustibles]
    s2 --> alt1[Alt Path: Excessive Temp Rise]
    alt1 --> alt2[Insulation Failure I]
    alt2 --> s6
    s2 --> alt3[Alt Path: Loss of Structural Capacity]
    alt3 --> alt4[Stability Failure R]
    alt4 --> s6
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a local barrier. List any three types of local barriers used in buildings.*

**Model Answer:**
A local barrier is a fire-resisting assembly of limited extent, installed at a specific location to subdivide, enclose, or shield a particular zone from fire, smoke, and heat. It is governed by stability (R), integrity (E), and insulation (I) criteria.

**Three types:**
1. Fire screens (around escalators / transformers)
2. Smoke curtains (in atriums)
3. Fire-rated enclosures (lifts, stairs, MEP shafts)

*(Expected answer length: 60–80 words for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the three failure criteria for a fire-rated local barrier and give the numerical limit for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Symbol | Numerical Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Stability / Load-bearing | R | No collapse for the rated duration |
| Integrity | E | No through-cracks, no flame/hot-gas passage |
| Insulation | I | Mean $\Delta T \leq 140\,°C$ AND max $\Delta T \leq 180\,°C$ on the unexposed face |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the design philosophy of local barriers in fire protection of buildings. Describe their role in compartmentation and protection of critical assets, with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composite local barrier consists of 12 mm gypsum board + 50 mm mineral wool + 100 mm concrete. Using the ISO 834 fire curve, determine the furnace gas temperature at $t = 60$ min and comment on whether the barrier is likely to satisfy the insulation criterion for a 2-hour rating. Use: $k_{gypsum} = 0.17$ W/m·K, $k_{wool} = 0.04$ W/m·K, $k_{concrete} = 0.93$ W/m·K, $\rho_{gypsum} = 800$ kg/m³, $\rho_{wool} = 30$ kg/m³, $\rho_{concrete} = 2300$ kg/m³.

**Model Solution (a):**

Local barriers implement **micro-compartmentation** within macro-compartments. Their design philosophy rests on four pillars:

1. **Containment at source** — fire is restricted to the point of origin (e.g., a transformer bay).
2. **Protection of life-safety assets** — lifts, stairs, electrical risers, escape corridors.
3. **Allowance of safe egress** — local barriers around lobbies buy time for evacuation.
4. **Protection of high-value assets** — server rooms, archives, control rooms.

> **Examples:** 2-hr fire-rated lobbies around lifts in high-rises; 4-hr concrete enclosures around oil-filled transformers; 1-hr fire-rated glazing in atrium screens.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute furnace temperature at $t = 60$ min.

$$
T(60) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 60 + 1) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(481)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(481) = \log_{10}(4.81 \times 10^2) = 2 + \log_{10}(4.81) = 2 + 0.6821 = 2.6821
$$

$$
T(60) = 20 + 345 \times 2.6821 = 20 + 925.32 = 945.32\,°C
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total thermal resistance $R$.

$$
R = \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3}
$$

$$
R = \frac{0.012}{0.17} + \frac{0.050}{0.04} + \frac{0.100}{0.93}
$$

$$
R = 0.0706 + 1.2500 + 0.1075 = 1.4281 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

**Step 3:** Estimate steady-state incident heat flux (assume exposed-face temperature ≈ furnace gas temp for conservative check, $T_h = 945.32\,°C = 1218.47$ K, $T_c = 293$ K):

$$
\dot{q}'' = \frac{T_h - T_c}{R} = \frac{1218.47 - 293}{1.4281} = \frac{925.47}{1.4281} = 648.04 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute unexposed-face temperature rise after 2 hours (assuming $c_{p,eff} \approx 900$ J/kg·K, $\rho_{eff}$ calculated similarly).

For a quick comparative check, the low flux (648 W/m²) combined with the thick insulation layer (50 mm mineral wool, $R_{wool} = 1.25$ m²·K/W alone) means the unexposed-face temperature rise will be **far below 140 °C**. 

**Conclusion:** The composite barrier **satisfies** the insulation criterion for a 2-hour rating. The 50 mm mineral wool layer provides the dominant thermal resistance, and the overall $R$ value is sufficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert $T(°C) \to T(K)$ when using radiation / Fourier equations. Always add 273.15. Also, the ISO 834 formula is log₁₀(8t+1), **not** logₑ.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled schematic, describe the construction details and performance criteria of a fire-rated smoke curtain used in large open spaces. Discuss its deployment mechanisms and integration with smoke management systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel column (H-section, $F/V = 220$ m⁻¹) is protected by 25 mm of vermiculite plaster ($k = 0.20$ W/m·K, $\rho = 700$ kg/m³, $c_p = 900$ J/kg·K). Using the T.T. Lie method (assume $R_f = 0.4$ hr), determine the fire resistance rating of the protected column.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Smoke Curtain — Construction & Performance:**

A smoke curtain is a **flexible, fire-resistant fabric assembly** (typically woven glass-fibre with intumescent / silicone coating) that is **stored in a recessed head-box** at ceiling level and **automatically deployed** on receipt of a fire alarm signal.

**Construction details:**

- **Fabric:** High-temperature fiberglass (operating up to 1000 °C) with PTFE or silicone coating
- **Head-box:** Steel housing containing the rolled curtain, typically 200 mm × 200 mm cross-section
- **Bottom bar:** Weighted steel bar (typically 3–5 kg/m) for vertical deployment stability
- **Side guides:** Channel-section steel tracks ensuring vertical alignment
- **Deployment mechanism:** Motorized spool with gravity-fail-safe (deploys on power loss)

**Performance criteria:**

| Criterion | Value |
|---|---|
| Integrity (E) | ≥ 60 min (typically) |
| Radiation | ≤ 15 kW/m² at 1 m distance |
| Deployment time | ≤ 60 seconds from alarm |
| Operating temperature | -20 °C to +70 °C ambient |
| Smoke leakage | ≤ 25 m³/hr·m² at 25 Pa |

**Integration with smoke management:**
- Linked to **detector signal** (smoke/heat) and **building management system (BMS)**
- Coordinated with **mechanical smoke extract fans** to form a *dynamic smoke reservoir*
- May interface with **sprinkler flow switches** for staged deployment

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute heat-up factor $H'$.

$$
H' = \frac{F/V}{\sqrt{k \, \rho \, c_p}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{0.20 \times 700 \times 900}}
$$

**Step 2:** Denominator.

$$
0.20 \times 700 \times 900 = 126{,}000
$$

$$
\sqrt{126{,}000} = 354.96
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $H'$.

$$
H' = \frac{220}{354.96} = 0.6198
$$

**Step 4:** Apply T.T. Lie formula.

$$
FRR = R_f \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{H'}{0.04}\right)^{0.8}\right] = 0.4 \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{0.6198}{0.04}\right)^{0.8}\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Ratio.

$$
\frac{0.6198}{0.04} = 15.495
$$

**Step 6:** Raise to 0.8 power.

$$
(15.495)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \times \ln(15.495)} = e^{0.8 \times 2.7395} = e^{2.1916} = 8.948
$$

**Step 7:** Final FRR.

$$
FRR = 0.4 \cdot [1 + 8.948] = 0.4 \cdot 9.948 = 3.979 \text{ hr} \approx 4 \text{ hr}
$$

**Conclusion:** The protected column achieves a **4-hour FRR** — suitable for critical structural members, transformer bays, and high-rise lift-shaft columns.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Lost:** (1) Forgetting to use $\log_{10}$ in ISO 834 (using ln instead). (2) Using °C directly in radiation equations without Kelvin conversion. (3) Mixing up "R" (resistance rating) and "R" (thermal resistance). Always define your symbols.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaways to memorize before the exam:**

- A **local barrier** is a *localized* fire-resisting assembly — it is **not** the same as a full compartment wall.
- Three test criteria: **R (stability), E (integrity), I (insulation)** — all three must pass.
- Insulation numerical limits: **140 °C average / 180 °C maximum** on the unexposed face.
- ISO 834 formula: $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ — remember the $\log_{10}$ and the **+1**.
- Critical radiation flux: **12.5 kW/m²** for cellulosic ignition.
- T.T. Lie FRR formula uses $H' = (F/V) / \sqrt{k \rho c_p}$.
- Mineral wool ($k \approx 0.04$ W/m·K) gives the **highest thermal resistance per unit thickness** — preferred in composite barriers.
- Smoke curtains use **gravity fail-safe deployment** — works even during power loss.
- Draft curtains in warehouses are **not** fire-rated — they manage smoke only.
- Fire-rated glazing is rated by **EI** (e.g., EI 60) — not "FR" alone.
- Material behavior under fire: gypsum (dehydrates), concrete (spalls at >300 °C), intumescent (expands 10–50×), steel (loses 50% strength at ~550 °C).
- Most-tested ISO 834 values: **842 °C at 30 min, 945 °C at 60 min, 1049 °C at 120 min**.
- Smoke leakage limit: **≤ 25 m³/hr per m²** at 25 Pa.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Stopped Areas - in Roof, in Partitioned Fire Areas, and in Connecting Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Stopped Areas - in Roof, in Partitioned Fire Areas, and in Connecting Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 MODULE 2: FIRE STOPPED AREAS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fire Stopped Area** is a specifically designed and constructed zone within a building — typically at junctions, penetrations, or concealed cavities — that is sealed, filled, or blocked using approved fire-resistive materials to **restrict the passage of flame, hot gases, and smoke** from one fire compartment to another, in compliance with **NBC 2016 (Part 4)**, **IS 1641:1988**, and **TAC (Technical Advisory Committee) guidelines** for a stipulated fire resistance rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fire stopping is a *passive fire protection* strategy and forms the third pillar (alongside structural fire resistance and active suppression) of compartmentation. Loss of fire stopping integrity is the **#1 reason** for uncontrolled fire spread in multi-storey buildings — a frequently tested KTU concept.

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaky Ship"

Imagine a ship divided into watertight compartments. Even if one section floods, the ship stays afloat **only if the bulkheads and seals between compartments are perfect**. A building is the same ship — and a fire is the "flood."

- The **compartments** = fire areas (walls, floors, slabs)
- The **bulkhead seals** = fire stops
- The **watertight doors** = fire-rated dampers, fire doors, sealed penetrations
- The **pipes & cables piercing bulkheads** = service penetrations that *must* be fire-stopped

If you have a perfect 2-hour fire-rated wall but a **single unsealed cable penetration** runs through it — the wall is **functionally compromised** within minutes. **Fire stopped areas are those critical sealing zones** that complete the compartmentation.

---

### 🎯 Three Contextual Domains (KTU Module 2 Focus)

| S.No | Domain | KTU High-Yield Aspect |
|------|--------|----------------------|
| 1 | **In Roof** | Concealed spaces, roof plenums, parapet junctions |
| 2 | **In Partitioned Fire Areas** | Inter-compartment walls, service shafts, movement joints |
| 3 | **In Connecting Structures** | Party walls, atria, service tunnels, building bridges |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Metric:** A fire stop must provide a **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** at least equal to the **lowest FRR** of the two building elements it joins. The standard test is **IS 3614 Part 2 / BS 476 Part 20 / ASTM E814 (UL 1479)**.

---

### 🧱 Standard Fire Stop Materials (IS 1641 / NBC Reference)

| Material Type | Typical Use | F-Rating |
|---------------|-------------|----------|
| **Intumescent Sealants** | Cable trays, small pipe penetrations | 1–4 hrs |
| **Mineral Wool (Rockwool)** | Large openings, cavity barriers | 1–4 hrs |
| **Fire-rated Mortar** | Cable/bus duct penetrations in floors | 2–4 hrs |
| **Fire-rated Boards (Promatect, Supalux)** | Large openings, ducts | 2–4 hrs |
| **Sprayed Fire-Resistive Material (SFRM)** | Complex geometry, structural penetrations | 1–4 hrs |
| **Fire Collars / Wraps** | Plastic pipe penetrations | 1–3 hrs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 2.1 Fire Stopping in Roof Assemblies

The **roof zone** is the most vulnerable concealed space in any building. Convective heat and flame travel **horizontally** through roof plenums at velocities of **1–3 m/s** once a fire breaches the ceiling. KTU expects students to understand:

#### A. Why Roof Fire Stops Are Critical

- Roof spaces often contain **combustible insulation** (PIR, PUF, woodwool)
- **Concealed attics** act as continuous chimneys
- **Solar panel** cable penetrations create ignition risks
- **HVAC ducts** pass through roofs — each is a fire pathway

#### B. Mandatory Fire Stop Locations in Roofs (NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.4)

1. **At every party wall continuation** — fire stop extending **300 mm minimum** on each side of the wall, full depth of the roof space.
2. **Around all roof penetrations** — ducts, pipes, conduits, exhaust vents.
3. **At junctions of roof and external walls** — cavity closers.
4. **In large uninterrupted roof voids** — cavity barriers at intervals not exceeding **20 m** in both directions.
5. **At edges of protected staircases** rising through the roof.

#### C. Construction Details

- Fire stops in roofs are typically **horizontal diaphragms** of non-combustible material (mineral wool batts, calcium silicate boards).
- They must be **wire-mesh reinforced** to prevent dislodgement under fire conditions.
- The **FRR** must match the supporting wall's FRR (typically **2 hours** for residential, **4 hours** for high-rise).

---

### 🔬 2.2 Fire Stopping in Partitioned Fire Areas

A **partitioned fire area** is a building sub-divided into smaller fire compartments per NBC Table 4 (Area Limitations based on Occupancy).

#### A. The Six Critical Fire Stopping Zones in Partitions

| Zone | Fire Stop Element | KTU-Mentioned Reference |
|------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Wall-to-Floor Junction** | Compression seal + intumescent strip | NBC 4.4.3.1 |
| **Wall-to-Wall (T-Junction)** | Vertical fire stop, full wall depth | NBC 4.4.3.2 |
| **Service Penetrations** | Fire-rated sealant/mortar/board | IS 1641 |
| **Movement Joints** | Fire-rated expansion joint cover | ASTM E1399 |
| **Curtain Wall Edge** | Perimeter fire stop (spandrel insulation) | NFPA 285 |
| **Slab Edge Spandrel** | Mineral wool + intumescent gasket | IS 1642 |

#### B. F-Rating vs. T-Rating — The KTU Favourite

> [!NOTE]
> **F-Rating (Flame Rating):** Time (in hours) the fire stop prevents passage of **flame and hot gases** through the assembly.
>
> **T-Rating (Temperature Rating):** Time (in hours) the fire stop limits **temperature rise** on the unexposed face to **163 °C (325 °F)** above ambient.

**KTU Exam Tip:** For **life safety egress paths**, T-rating is critical. For **property protection**, F-rating is critical.

#### C. Area & Spacing Calculations for Partitioned Fire Areas

The **maximum compartment area** $A_{max}$ is governed by:

$$A_{max} = \frac{q_{total}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot H_{eff}}$$

Where:
- $q_{total}$ = Total heat release rate of combustible contents (kW)
- $\rho$ = Density of air = **1.2 kg/m³**
- $c_p$ = Specific heat of air = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $\Delta T$ = Allowable temperature rise = **200 K** (for fire stops)
- $H_{eff}$ = Effective height of compartment (m)

**Practical Rule (NBC 2016):** For non-sprinklered buildings, maximum undivided floor area on any floor = **250 m² for Group A (Residential)** and **750 m² for Group B (Educational)**.

---

### 🔬 2.3 Fire Stopping in Connecting Structures

**Connecting structures** are architectural elements that physically link two or more fire compartments or buildings. These include:

- **Atria** (large open volumes)
- **Corridors & Passageways** between fire zones
- **Service Shafts** (electrical, plumbing, HVAC)
- **Building Bridges / Skywalks**
- **Party Walls** between adjacent buildings
- **Basement-to-Tower** transitions

#### A. KTU's "Three Rules of Connecting Structures"

1. **Rule of Continuity:** A fire stop at a connection must have an FRR ≥ the lower-rated of the two connected elements.
2. **Rule of Encapsulation:** All service penetrations through the connection must be **individually fire-stopped**.
3. **Rule of Symmetry:** Both sides of a connection must be fire-stopped identically (no "exposed side only" half-measures).

#### B. Atrium Fire Stopping (NBC Cl. 4.9)

For atria connecting multiple floors, KTU/NBC mandates:

- **Smoke barrier** at every alternate floor (or every floor for >4-storey atria)
- **FRR ≥ 2 hours** for atrium perimeter
- **Differential pressure** of **12.5 Pa minimum** between atrium and adjacent areas
- **Cavity barriers** within the atrium spandrel zone at **3 m vertical intervals**

#### C. Service Shaft Fire Stopping

Vertical service shafts are essentially **chimneys**. KTU's rule:

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Shaft Rule":** Every horizontal floor penetration by a service shaft **MUST** have a fire stop with the same FRR as the floor slab. Failure here accounts for **60% of high-rise fire spread incidents**.

---

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units | KTU Application |
|---|------------------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | $A_{max} = \dfrac{q_{total}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot H_{eff}}$ | $A_{max}$ max compartment area | m² | Fire stop sizing |
| 2 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{q \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Heat rise through assembly | °C | T-rating verification |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{smoke} = 0.071 \cdot A_{f}^{0.5} \cdot H^{1.5}$ | Smoke flow rate (NFPA 92) | m³/s | Roof smoke control |
| 4 | $F = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^{2} \cdot C_d \cdot A$ | Pressure differential force | N | Atrium damper sizing |
| 5 | $L_{cavity} \leq 20 \text{ m}$ | Max uninterrupted roof cavity length | m | Roof fire stop spacing |
| 6 | $FRR_{stop} \geq \min(FRR_{1}, FRR_{2})$ | Fire stop rating rule | hours | Connection rule |

> **Where:**
> - $A_f$ = Area of fire origin (m²)
> - $H$ = Height of smoke layer (m)
> - $C_d$ = Discharge coefficient ≈ **0.65**
> - $A$ = Cross-section area (m²)

---

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise residential towers (Kerala context):** Fire stops around AC refrigerant pipe penetrations through slab are mandatory per Kerala Municipality Building Rules.
- **Hospital OT suites:** T-rating ≥ 2 hours to prevent surgical fire spread.
- **Shopping mall atria:** Continuous fire stop rings at every floor for smoke management.
- **Data centers:** T-rating is paramount to protect servers from heat damage even without direct flame.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Uninterrupted Compartment Area for Fire Stops

We will derive the formula for $A_{max}$ — the maximum floor area that can be left without internal fire stops, given a target fire size.

**Starting Principle:** Under fire conditions, the heat must be absorbed by the air within the compartment until the temperature rise reaches the fire stop's **T-rating threshold** (163 °C above ambient).

**Step 1:** Total heat released by fire in time $t$:

$$Q_{total} = q_{HRR} \cdot t$$

Where $q_{HRR}$ is the steady-state Heat Release Rate (kW), and $t$ is the time to reach T-rating failure (s).

**Step 2:** This heat is absorbed by the air mass in the compartment:

$$Q_{total} = m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3:** Expressing air mass in terms of volume and density:

$$m_{air} = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A_{max} \cdot H_{eff}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 3 into Step 2:

$$q_{HRR} \cdot t = \rho \cdot A_{max} \cdot H_{eff} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $A_{max}$:

$$A_{max} = \frac{q_{HRR} \cdot t}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot H_{eff}}$$

Since $q_{HRR} \cdot t$ is the total energy $Q_{total}$, we can equivalently write:

$$A_{max} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot H_{eff}}$$

> **Note:** This is the simplified steady-state form. Real fires have a **t² growth** profile; the more rigorous form integrates over the HRR curve. For KTU exams, the simplified form is sufficient.

---

### 🧮 3.2 Worked Example: Roof Cavity Fire Stop Spacing

**Problem:** A warehouse has a flat concrete roof with a **150 mm** concealed roof space filled with PIR insulation. The roof void is divided into cavities by fire stops. Determine the maximum spacing between fire stops if the maximum fire size expected is **2 MW** and the roof is **3 m high** (effective cavity height).

**Given:**
- $q_{HRR}$ = **2,000,000 W** (2 MW)
- $t$ = **1,200 s** (assume 20-min fire stop rating)
- $\rho$ = **1.2 kg/m³**
- $c_p$ = **1,005 J/kg·K**
- $\Delta T$ = **200 K** (T-rating threshold)
- $H_{eff}$ = **0.15 m** (cavity height, not 3 m!)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute total heat:

$$Q_{total} = q_{HRR} \cdot t = 2{,}000{,}000 \times 1200 = 2.4 \times 10^{9} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into $A_{max}$ formula:

$$A_{max} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{9}}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 200 \times 0.15}$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$\text{Denom} = 1.2 \times 1005 \times 200 \times 0.15 = 36{,}180$$

**Step 4:** Final value:

$$A_{max} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{9}}{36{,}180} \approx 66{,}333 \text{ m}^{2}$$

This is unrealistically large — the **limiting factor is the code-prescribed spacing**, not the calculated area. Hence, NBC mandates:

$$L_{cavity} \leq 20 \text{ m (in both directions)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The **prescriptive code limit of 20 m** controls — not the calculated theoretical value. The derivation shows *why* such limits are conservative; they account for **flashover** and **uneven heat distribution**.

---

### 🧮 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Fire Stop Design

| Parameter | Roof Fire Stop | Partition Fire Stop | Connection Fire Stop |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Primary Hazard** | Convective horizontal spread through plenum | Vertical/lateral spread between compartments | Cross-compartment chimney effect |
| **Typical Material** | Mineral wool batt + wire mesh | Intumescent sealant + backer rod | Fire-rated board + mastic |
| **FRR Range** | 1–2 hrs | 2–4 hrs | 2–4 hrs |
| **Critical Spacing** | 20 m max | At every T-junction, every service | Every floor, every 3 m vertical |
| **NBC Reference** | Cl. 4.4.4 | Cl. 4.4.3 | Cl. 4.4.5, 4.9 |
| **Failing Test** | ASTM E814 (UL 1479) | IS 3614 Part 2 | ASTM E1966 / UL 2079 |
| **KTU 2024 Module Weight** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |

---

### 🧮 3.4 Derivation: Atrium Differential Pressure Requirement

The pressure difference across a fire stop in an atrium must overcome buoyancy-driven smoke flow.

**Step 1:** Buoyancy pressure of a smoke layer:

$$\Delta P = H \cdot (\rho_{air} - \rho_{smoke}) \cdot g$$

**Step 2:** Smoke density at elevated temperature:

$$\rho_{smoke} = \frac{\rho_{air} \cdot T_{air}}{T_{smoke}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $T_{smoke} = 200 °C = 473 \text{ K}$ and $T_{air} = 30 °C = 303 \text{ K}$:

$$\rho_{smoke} = \frac{1.2 \times 303}{473} = 0.769 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

**Step 4:** For a 3 m smoke layer height:

$$\Delta P = 3 \times (1.2 - 0.769) \times 9.81 = 12.69 \text{ Pa}$$

**Conclusion:** KTU/NBC mandates **12.5 Pa minimum** differential — which matches our derivation. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📐 4.1 Fire Stop Locations in a Roof Assembly

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Slab / Roof Deck] --> B[Roof Cavity Space]
    B --> C[Concealed Attic]
    C --> D[Mineral Wool Fire Stop at 20m intervals]
    C --> E[Party Wall Penetration Seal]
    C --> F[HVAC Duct Penetration with Fire Damper]
    C --> G[Pipe / Cable Fire Stop Sealant]
    C --> H[Staircase Roof Penetration FRR Match]
    D --> I[Spandrel Beam - Non-Combustible]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[External Parapet Wall]

    style A fill:#8B4513,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000000

    style F fill:#FF4500,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#FF4500,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#FF4500,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#696969,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#A9A9A9,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each red node is a **mandatory fire stop location** in the roof zone. The mineral wool batts (D) act as horizontal barriers; the wall/duct/cable seals (E, F, G, H) close penetrations.

---

### 📐 4.2 Fire Stop Architecture in Partitioned Fire Areas

### 📐 4.3 Fire Stop Strategy in Connecting Structures (Atrium + Service Shaft)

### 📐 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Roof Fire Stop System
