<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a fire stopped area. List FOUR locations in a roof assembly where fire stops are mandatory as per NBC 2016.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fire stopped area** is a constructed zone at junctions, penetrations, or concealed cavities sealed with fire-resistive materials to **prevent the spread of flame, hot gases, and smoke** between fire compartments, providing a fire resistance rating (FRR) equal to the adjoining elements.

**Four mandatory roof fire stop locations:**

1. At **every party wall continuation** — extending **300 mm minimum** on each side, full roof depth.
2. Around **all roof penetrations** — ducts, pipes, conduits, exhaust vents.
3. At **roof-to-external wall junctions** — cavity closers.
4. At **staircase penetrations** rising through the roof.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition — 1.5 marks; Four locations — 0.375 each = 1.5 marks.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between F-rating and T-rating of a fire stop. Why is T-rating more critical for escape routes?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | F-Rating | T-Rating |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Definition** | Time the stop resists **flame/hot gas passage** | Time the stop limits **temperature rise** to 163 °C on unexposed side |
| **Test Standard** | ASTM E814 / IS 3614 | ASTM E814 with thermocouples |
| **Focus** | Property protection | **Life safety** |
| **Typical Requirement** | 2 hrs | 2 hrs (for egress) |

**T-rating is critical for escape routes** because during evacuation, occupants may be exposed to **radiant heat** from the unexposed side of a fire stop. Even without direct flame, temperatures above **~80 °C** cause respiratory tract burns. Hence, T-rating directly governs **tenable conditions for human survival** in corridors and stairwells.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **six critical fire stopping zones** in a partitioned fire area. Mention the relevant code references. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital building has floor plates of **45 m × 25 m**. The building has a **concealed roof cavity of 200 mm** filled with mineral wool. The fire stop FRR required is **2 hours**. Determine whether the prescriptive 20 m spacing is sufficient, and justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

The **six critical fire stopping zones** in partitioned fire areas are:

1. **Wall-to-Floor Junction** — A horizontal fire stop at the wall-floorslab interface, typically using **intumescent sealant + mineral wool backer**. Reference: NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.3.1.

2. **Wall-to-Wall T-Junction** — A vertical fire stop inserted into the partition's hollow cavity at every T-intersection, ensuring **continuity of compartmentation**. Reference: NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.3.2.

3. **Service Penetrations** (cables, pipes, ducts) — Each penetration is individually sealed using **fire-rated sealant, mortar, or fire collars** matched to the wall's FRR. Reference: IS 1641:1988.

4. **Movement / Expansion Joints** — Sealed with **fire-rated expansion joint covers** that accommodate ±25% movement while maintaining FRR. Test standard: ASTM E1399 / UL 2079.

5. **Curtain Wall Edge (Spandrel Zone)** — A perimeter fire stop between the floor slab edge and the curtain wall, using **mineral wool + intumescent gasket** to prevent **flame spread up the external face**. Test standard: **NFPA 285**.

6. **Slab Edge Spandrel / Cavity Barrier** — A horizontal fire stop at the slab edge, especially in **rainscreen facades**, closing the cavity to prevent the **chimney effect** in the external wall. Reference: IS 1642:1989.

**Sketch:** (See Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the architectural layout.)

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark per zone × 6 = 6 marks; sketch/figure = 1 mark.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- Floor plate: **45 m × 25 m**
- Roof cavity height: **200 mm = 0.2 m**
- FRR required: **2 hours = 7200 s**
- Material: **Mineral wool** (non-combustible, $c_p \approx 840$ J/kg·K, $\rho \approx 40$ kg/m³)

**Step 1:** Apply the compartment area formula for the roof cavity:

$$A_{max} = \frac{q_{HRR} \cdot t}{\rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T \cdot H_{eff}}$$

**Step 2:** Assumed fire size for a hospital:

$$q_{HRR} = 1.5 \text{ MW} = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$A_{max} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{6} \times 7200}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 200 \times 0.2}$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:

$$\text{Denom} = 1.2 \times 1005 \times 200 \times 0.2 = 48{,}240$$

**Step 5:** Compute $A_{max}$:

$$A_{max} = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{10}}{48{,}240} \approx 2.24 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 6:** Compare to floor area:

$$A_{floor} = 45 \times 25 = 1125 \text{ m}^{2}$$

Since $A_{max} \gg A_{floor}$, the **compartmentation area** is not the limiting factor.

**Step 7:** Apply the prescriptive rule:

$$L_{max, code} = 20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8:** Compute the number of fire stops required:

$$n_{stops,length} = \lceil 45/20 \rceil = 3 \text{ strips}$$

$$n_{stops,width} = \lceil 25/20 \rceil = 2 \text{ strips}$$

$$\text{Total} = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ cavity barriers}$$

**Conclusion:** The **20 m prescriptive spacing is sufficient** and is, in fact, **more conservative** than the calculated $A_{max}$. The fire stops should be placed at **20 m centres**, dividing the 45 × 25 m roof into **6 compartments**, each not exceeding 20 × 12.5 m.

> **Mark Split:** Formula = 2 marks; Substitution & computation = 3 marks; Prescriptive rule application = 1 mark; Conclusion with sketch concept = 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Discuss the design philosophy of **fire stopping in connecting structures** like atria, service shafts, and party walls. Mention NBC 2016 clauses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An atrium connects 5 floors. The smoke layer is observed at **3 m height** and ambient temperature is **30 °C**. Calculate the **differential pressure required** to prevent smoke ingress into adjacent compartments. **(7 marks)**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

**Connecting structures** form the **highest-risk** fire spread pathways due to their inherently **continuous geometry**. KTU/NBC's design philosophy is based on **three rules**:

**Rule 1: Continuity Rule** — The fire stop FRR at the connection must be ≥ the lower FRR of the two connected elements (NBC 4.4.5).

**Rule 2: Encapsulation Rule** — All service penetrations (cables, pipes, ducts) crossing the connection must be **individually fire-stopped** with FRR equal to the connecting element (NBC 4.4.5.3).

**Rule 3: Symmetry Rule** — Both faces of the connection must be fire-stopped symmetrically; no single-sided treatment is permitted (NBC 4.4.5.5).

**Atria (NBC Cl. 4.9):**
- Smoke barriers at every floor for atria > 4 storeys
- Perimeter spandrel FRR ≥ 2 hours
- Differential pressurization ≥ 12.5 Pa
- Cavity barriers at 3 m vertical intervals in the spandrel zone

**Service Shafts (NBC Cl. 4.4.5.6):**
- Every floor penetration fire-stopped
- Shaft construction of non-combustible material
- FRR = floor slab FRR (typically 2 hrs)

**Party Walls (NBC Cl. 4.4.5.4):**
- Continuous from foundation to roof
- Cavity fully filled with non-combustible insulation
- FRR ≥ 4 hours for adjacent high-rise

> **Mark Split:** 3 rules = 3 marks; Atrium design = 2 marks; Service shaft = 1 mark; Party wall = 1 mark.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- Smoke layer height: $H = 3$ m
- Ambient temperature: $T_{air} = 30 °C = 303$ K
- Smoke temperature: $T_{smoke} = 200 °C = 473$ K (assumed)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1:** Compute smoke density using ideal gas relation:

$$\rho_{smoke} = \rho_{air} \cdot \frac{T_{air}}{T_{smoke}}$$

$$\rho_{smoke} = 1.2 \times \frac{303}{473} = 0.769 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the buoyancy pressure equation:

$$\Delta P = H \cdot (\rho_{air} - \rho_{smoke}) \cdot g$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$\Delta P = 3 \times (1.2 - 0.769) \times 9.81$$

**Step 4:** Compute:

$$\Delta P = 3 \times 0.431 \times 9.81 = 12.68 \text{ Pa}$$

**Conclusion:** The differential pressure required is **≈ 12.7 Pa**, which is just above the **NBC minimum of 12.5 Pa**. The system is **just adequate**; a safety margin of **15 Pa** is recommended for design.

> **Mark Split:** Formula statement = 2 marks; Density calculation = 2 marks; Pressure calculation = 2 marks; Conclusion = 1 mark.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing F-rating and T-rating** — These are **NOT** interchangeable. T-rating includes temperature limit; F-rating does not. Examiners deduct 2 marks per error.
>
> 2. **Using roof total height instead of cavity height** in the $A_{max}$ formula — Always use the **cavity thickness** (e.g., 0.2 m), not the building height. This is the **#1 calculation mistake** in KTU answer sheets.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the 20 m prescriptive rule** — Even if your calculation yields a higher permissible spacing, NBC's **20 m hard limit** is non-negotiable. Mentioning both is essential.
>
> 4. **Forgetting symmetry of fire stops** — A one-sided fire stop in a connecting structure is **NOT** a valid design. KTU examiners specifically test this.
>
> 5. **Wrong units** — $q_{HRR}$ must be in **W (watts)**, $t$ in **seconds**, and $\Delta T$ in **K or °C** (consistent). Mixed units = full mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Not citing NBC clauses** — A 14-mark answer without **at least 2–3 NBC references** is considered incomplete. Always cite: NBC 2016 Cl. 4.4.3, 4.4.4, 4.4.5, 4.9, IS 1641, IS 1642.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> **🎯 High-Yield KTU Bullet Points:**

- ✅ A **fire stop** is a passive fire protection element that seals openings/junctions in fire-rated assemblies.
- ✅ The governing rule: **$FRR_{stop} \geq \min(FRR_{1}, FRR_{2})$** — match the lower-rated element.
- ✅ **Roof fire stops** are mandatory at party walls, penetrations, and at every **20 m maximum** cavity length (NBC 4.4.4).
- ✅ **Partitioned area fire stops** include six critical zones: wall-floor, wall-wall, services, movement joints, curtain wall edges, slab edges.
- ✅ **F-rating** = flame resistance; **T-rating** = temperature resistance (163 °C threshold). T-rating governs life safety.
- ✅ **Connecting structures** (atria, service shafts, party walls) require **continuity, encapsulation, and symmetry** rules.
- ✅ Atrium design demands **smoke barriers, FRR ≥ 2 hr spandrels, and ≥ 12.5 Pa pressurization**.
- ✅ Service shafts are **chimneys** — fire stop at **every floor slab** crossing.
- ✅ Materials include intumescent sealants, mineral wool, fire mortars, SFRM, fire boards, fire collars.
- ✅ The compartment area formula: $A_{max} = \dfrac{q_{HRR} \cdot t}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot H_{eff}}$.
- ✅ Atrium differential pressure: $\Delta P = H(\rho_{air} - \rho_{smoke})g$; require **12.5 Pa minimum**.
- ✅ Codes: **NBC 2016 Part 4**, **IS 1641:1988**, **IS 1642:1989**, **IS 3614 Part 2**, **ASTM E814 (UL 1479)**, **NFPA 285**.
- ✅ Smoke flow rate: $\dot{Q}_{smoke} = 0.071 \cdot A_{f}^{0.5} \cdot H^{1.5}$ (NFPA 92).

> **🧠 Memory Mnemonic — "FIRE-SAFE":**
> **F** — F-rating first (flame)
> **I** — Intumescent materials swell to seal
> **R** — Roof, then partitions, then connections (sequence)
> **E** — Encapsulate every penetration
> **S** — Symmetry on both sides
> **A** — 20 m max cavity spacing
> **F** — Fire stop at every floor slab
> **E** — Establish 12.5 Pa pressurization for atria

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Doors - Low Combustible, Non-combustible and Spark-proof Doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fire Doors - Low Combustible, Non-combustible and Spark-proof Doors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fire Door A **fire door** is a specialized door assembly, including its frame, hardware, and closing mechanism, that is designed and tested to resist the spread of fire and smoke between compartments of a building for a specified duration (typically **30, 60, 90, 120, or 240 minutes** of fire resistance rating). As per the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4)** and **IS 3614 (Part 1 & 2)**, a fire door is a self-closing door that, when closed, restricts the passage of fire, heat, and smoke for a stipulated period under standard fire test conditions defined by **IS 3614 (Part 1):1966** or **BS 476 Part 20/22** or **IS 16205 (Part 24)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > A fire door is NOT just a "metal door." It is an engineered **door set** — comprising the **leaf (shutter), frame, intumescent seals, smoke seals, vision panels, louvers, hinges, and a self-closing device** — all tested together as a single unit. ### 1.2 Classification of Fire Doors by Material Composition For KTU 2024 Scheme examination purposes, fire doors are broadly classified into three categories based on the **combustibility of the door leaf material**: | S.No. | Type of Fire Door | Combustibility of Core Material | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Low Combustible Fire Door** | Core has limited combustible content (fire-retardant-treated wood, FR timber) | Residential, hotels, offices | | 2 | **Non-Combustible Fire Door** | Core is fully non-combustible (steel, GI sheet, mineral wool) | Industrial, commercial, stairwells | | 3 | **Spark-Proof Fire Door** | Non-combustible + spark-arresting construction | Petroleum refineries, chemical plants, gas stations | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Firewall Gatekeeper":** > Imagine a medieval castle's **iron-reinforced main gate**. When enemy forces (fire) attack, the gate must: > 1. **Resist flaming arrows** (radiant heat) — *Spark-proof capability*. > 2. **Not itself become fuel** — *Non-combustible or low combustible nature*. > 3. **Automatically close when the guard is absent** — *Self-closing mechanism*. > 4. **Seal the smoke out of the keep** — *Smoke seal & intumescent strip*. > > A modern fire door is exactly this — a pre-engineered, certified **gatekeeper** that compartmentalizes a building so that a fire in one room does not become a building-wide disaster. ### 1.4 Key Performance Parameters - **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):** Expressed in **minutes** (e.g., FRR = 120 min means 2 hours of integrity + insulation under standard fire exposure). - **Integrity (E):** Ability to prevent passage of flames and hot gases. - **Insulation (I):** Ability to restrict heat transfer to the non-fire side; the average temperature rise on the unexposed face must not exceed **140 °C** above ambient (per IS 3614). - **Stability:** The door must not collapse or develop through-openings during the rating period. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Low Combustible Fire Doors #### 2.1.1 Definition A **low combustible fire door** is a door whose leaf is constructed primarily from **fire-retardant-treated (FRT) timber or engineered wood products** (such as particle board, blockboard, or plywood) treated with **fire-retardant chemicals** that delay ignition, reduce flame spread, and lower the rate of heat release. > [!NOTE] > **Combustibility Criterion (BIS / NBC 2016):** > A material is classified as **"low combustible"** when its **Ignitability Index, Heat Release Rate, and Mass Loss Rate** are within the limits specified in **IS 3808** and the material has been treated with **fire-retardant solutions** (e.g., ammonium phosphate, borate, diammonium phosphate) under pressure impregnation. #### 2.1.2 Construction Details - **Core:** Solid timber (minimum density **640 kg/m³**) or particle board (minimum density **600 kg/m³**). - **Facing:** Veneer, laminate, or **0.5–0.8 mm thick galvanized steel sheet** cladded on both faces. - **Treatment:** Pressure-impregnated with fire-retardant chemicals at **0.5–1.0 MPa** for **4–8 hours**. - **Thickness:** Typically **45–50 mm** for a 2-hour rated door. - **Seals:** Intumescent strips (graphite-based) of **2 mm thickness** along edges. #### 2.1.3 Where Used Bedrooms of hotels, office cabins, residential apartments, IT offices — where aesthetics matter but a basic fire rating is mandatory. --- ### 2.2 Non-Combustible Fire Doors #### 2.2.1 Definition A **non-combustible fire door** is a door whose leaf is constructed entirely from **non-combustible materials** (per **IS 3808:1979** — material that does not ignite, burn, or release combustible vapors when subjected to fire). Typical materials are **cold-rolled steel, galvanized iron (GI), stainless steel, or mineral-core panels**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Non-combustible" per IS 3808:** > A material is deemed non-combustible if, when tested at **750 °C** in a furnace, it does not: > - Flame for more than **10 seconds**, > - Cause a temperature rise of more than **50 °C** above furnace temperature, AND > - Lose more than **50 % of its mass**. #### 2.2.2 Construction Details - **Skin:** **1.0–1.6 mm** thick cold-rolled steel / GI sheet. - **Core (Infill):** **Mineral rock wool** (density **45–80 kg/m³**) or **calcium silicate board** (density **200–240 kg/m³**). - **Stiffeners:** Internal vertical steel stiffeners at **150–200 mm c/c** to prevent buckling. - **Thickness:** **45–55 mm** for 2-hour rating. - **Hardware:** Stainless steel ball-bearing hinges (minimum **3 hinges per door** for height ≤ 2.1 m), fire-rated locks, panic bars. #### 2.2.3 Where Used Stairwell enclosures, lift lobbies, fire exits, transformer rooms, generator rooms, server rooms, and commercial high-rises. --- ### 2.3 Spark-Proof Fire Doors #### 2.3.1 Definition A **spark-proof fire door** is a non-combustible fire door with **additional design features** that prevent the **transmission of sparks, flames, or hot particles** from one side of the door to the other. These are mandatory in **hazardous (H) occupancies** handling flammable liquids, gases, or dust. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Examiner Note:** > A spark-proof door is NOT a separate certification category under IS 3614. It is a **functional requirement** fulfilled by combining non-combustible construction with **double-leaf design, spark-arrestor mesh, and self-closing gravity/spring mechanism** — typically conforming to **OISD-118 (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)** and **IS 2771** for hazardous storage. #### 2.3.2 Construction Details - **Double-leaf construction:** Two leaves of **1.6–2.0 mm GI sheet** that close in a sequence — **first the rear leaf, then the front leaf** (to prevent spark blow-back). - **Spark Arrester Mesh:** **Stainless steel / GI wire mesh** of **20–40 mesh size (0.4–0.8 mm aperture)** fixed in a **ventilation louver** at the top or bottom of the door. - **Self-Closing:** **Gravity-operated** (sliding on inclined rails) OR **spring-loaded swing-type** — closes automatically when released. - **Threshold:** **Bottom sill** raised **50–100 mm** to prevent liquid fuel seepage. #### 2.3.3 Where Used Petroleum refineries (IS 3614 Part 2 mentions), LPG/bulk fuel storage godowns, paint and varnish manufacturing units, ammunition storage, distilleries. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulae are central to KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions on fire door thermal performance. **Table 2.1 — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Fire Door Design** | # | Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Governing Standard | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Heat Transfer through door (Steady State — Fourier's Law)** | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{d}$ | W | Conduction physics | | 2 | **Time to reach insulation failure (lumped capacitance)** | $t_{ins} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{(T_{crit} - T_0)}{\dot{q}''}$ | s | IS 3614 Annex | | 3 | **Temperature rise on unexposed face (integrity criterion)** | $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140\,°C$ above ambient | °C | IS 3614 Part 1 | | 4 | **Point temperature rise (local criterion)** | $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180\,°C$ above ambient | °C | IS 3614 Part 1 | | 5 | **Standard fire curve (Cellulosic)** | $T_t = T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | °C, t in min | ISO 834 / IS 16205 | | 6 | **Standard fire curve (Hydrocarbon)** | $T_t = T_0 + 1080 \cdot (1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t})$ | °C, t in min | UL 1709 / OISD | | 7 | **Mineral wool thickness for FRR** | $d_{wool} = FRR \cdot k_{wool} \cdot A / \dot{Q}_{allow}$ | m | Thermal design | | 8 | **Door leaf minimum thickness (steel)** | $t_{steel} \geq \dfrac{0.6 \cdot FRR^{0.5}}{1.2}$ | mm | Empirical (NBC 2016) | | 9 | **Self-closing force (gravity door)** | $F = W \cdot \sin\theta$ | N | Mechanical design | | 10 | **Intumescent strip expansion ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{d_{final}}{d_{initial}} \geq 5$ to $10$ | ratio | Manufacturer data | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility in Real Projects:** > - **Eq. 1** lets designers compute heat flow through a steel door leaf and check whether the unexposed face temperature violates the **140 °C insulation limit** at the rated time. > - **Eq. 5 (ISO 834 curve)** is used in every fire-resistance certification test in India; the time-temperature exposure profile is identical for low-combustible, non-combustible, and spark-proof doors, but the **failure time differs** based on material class. --- ### 2.5 Operational Logic of a Fire Door in a Fire Scenario The **"Why" and "How"** behind every fire door step: 1. **Detection of fire** → occupant or heat detector triggers alarm. 2. **Door is held open with electromagnetic hold-open** (linked to fire alarm) → release command cuts power → door closes under gravity/spring. 3. **Intumescent strips** (around perimeter) heat up → expand **5×–10×** → seal the **gap between leaf and frame** (initial gap ≈ 3 mm, final seal ≈ 25–30 mm). 4. **Mineral wool / FRT core** acts as **thermal insulator** → slows the unexposed face temperature rise. 5. **Steel facings** maintain **structural integrity** (E criterion) — no through-cracks. 6. **Smoke seals** (EPDM rubber gaskets) prevent **cold smoke leakage** (often the bigger killer in real fires). 7. **After 60 / 120 / 240 min**, the door may lose insulation — but **integrity** is the primary KTU-tested criterion for life safety. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Time-Temperature Curve for Standard Cellulosic Fire (ISO 834 / IS 16205 Part 24) The standard fire exposure used in testing every fire door in India follows the **ISO 834 (t, in minutes) equation**: $$ T_t \;=\; T_0 \;+\; 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) $$ Where: - $T_t$ = furnace temperature at time $t$ (°C) - $T_0$ = initial ambient temperature (°C), typically **20 °C or 27 °C** - $t$ = time elapsed since start of fire test (minutes) **Derivation logic (qualitative, since this is an empirical standard):** The curve is designed to represent a **fully developed compartment fire** fueled by cellulosic materials (wood, paper, textiles). It is empirically fitted so that: - At $t = 0$ min: $T_t = T_0 + 0 = T_0$ ✓ - At $t = 30$ min: $T_t \approx T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(241) \approx T_0 + 842\,°C$ - At $t = 60$ min: $T_t \approx T_0 + 945\,°C$ - At $t = 120$ min: $T_t \approx T_0 + 1049\,°C$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Heat Transfer Through a Non-Combustible Steel Fire Door **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):** > A **non-combustible fire door** has the following construction: > - Two **GI steel facings**, each of thickness $d_s = 1.2$ mm. > - Core of **mineral rock wool**, thickness $d_c = 50$ mm. > - Door dimensions: **2100 mm (height) × 900 mm (width)**. > - Fire exposure on **one face** per ISO 834 standard fire curve. > - Initial temperature: $T_0 = 27$ °C. > - Thermal conductivity: $k_{steel} = 45$ W/m·K, $k_{wool} = 0.04$ W/m·K. > - **Assume steady-state 1-D conduction after 30 min of fire exposure.** > > **Calculate:** > 1. The **furnace temperature** at $t = 30$ min. > 2. The **heat flux** through the door. > 3. The **unexposed face temperature** at steady state. > 4. **Check whether insulation criterion is satisfied** (NBC limit: $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140\,°C$). #### Step 1 — Furnace Temperature at t = 30 min $$ \begin{aligned} T_{30} &= T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 30 + 1) \\ &= 27 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(241) \\ &= 27 + 345 \cdot (2.3820) \\ &= 27 + 821.78 \\ &= 848.78\,°C \end{aligned} $$ So the **fire-side face** of the door is exposed to **≈ 849 °C** at 30 minutes. #### Step 2 — Area of Door $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 2.1 \, \text{m} \times 0.9 \, \text{m} = 1.89 \, \text{m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3 — Thermal Resistance Network (3-Layer Composite Wall) For a **1-D steady-state conduction** through layers in series: $$ R_{th} \;=\; \frac{d_s}{k_{steel} \cdot A} \;+\; \frac{d_c}{k_{wool} \cdot A} \;+\; \frac{d_s}{k_{steel} \cdot A} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_{steel} &= \frac{0.0012}{45 \times 1.89} = \frac{0.0012}{85.05} = 1.411 \times 10^{-5}\,°C/W \\ R_{wool} &= \frac{0.050}{0.04 \times 1.89} = \frac{0.050}{0.0756} = 0.6614\,°C/W \\ R_{total} &= 2 \cdot (1.411 \times 10^{-5}) + 0.6614 \approx 0.6614\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ > **Key observation:** The **steel facings offer negligible resistance** — the **mineral wool core does the entire insulating job**. This is why **core material choice is critical** in non-combustible fire doors. #### Step 4 — Heat Flux Through the Door $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q} &= \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} = \frac{T_{fire} - T_{room}}{R_{total}} \end{aligned} $$ Assuming the **room-side** is at ambient $T_2 = 27\,°C$ (worst case for insulation check): $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q} &= \frac{848.78 - 27}{0.6614} = \frac{821.78}{0.6614} = 1242.5\,W \end{aligned} $$ Heat flux per unit area: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{q}'' &= \frac{\dot{Q}}{A} = \frac{1242.5}{1.89} = 657.4\,W/m^2 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 5 — Unexposed Face Temperature The **outer steel face** of the door (room side) is in excellent thermal contact with ambient air. Heat flux equals convective loss on the cold side: $$ \dot{q}'' = h_c \cdot (T_{unexp} - T_{room}) $$ Assuming $h_c = 5\,W/m^2K$ (natural convection on a vertical surface, conservative): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{unexp} &= T_{room} + \frac{\dot{q}''}{h_c} = 27 + \frac{657.4}{5} = 27 + 131.5 = 158.5\,°C \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 6 — Insulation Check $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T_{unexp} &= 158.5 - 27 = 131.5\,°C \end{aligned} $$ Since $131.5\,°C \leq 140\,°C$ (NBC limit) ✓ — **Insulation criterion is JUST satisfied** at 30 minutes. > [!NOTE] > If the door was constructed with **less than 50 mm of rock wool** (say 30 mm), the calculation would yield $\Delta T > 140\,°C$, and the door would FAIL the 30-min insulation test. This is the typical KTU Board question — **iterate on core thickness** to meet the rating. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Fire Door Types | Parameter | Low Combustible Door | Non-Combustible Door | Spark-Proof Door | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Material** | FRT wood / particle board | GI steel + mineral wool | Double GI steel + mesh | | **Density of core** | 600–750 kg/m³ | 45–80 kg/m³ (wool) | 45–80 kg/m³ (wool) | | **Combustibility class (IS 3808)** | Class 2 (combustible, treated) | Class 1 (non-combustible) | Class 1 (non-combustible) | | **Typical FRR** | 30 / 60 min | 60 / 90 / 120 / 240 min | 90 / 120 min | | **Standard tested to** | IS 3614 Pt 1, IS 16205 Pt 24 | IS 3614 Pt 1, BS 476 Pt 22 | IS 3614 Pt 2, OISD-118 | | **Self-closing** | Yes (overhead closer) | Yes (overhead closer) | Yes (gravity/swing) | | **Spark-arrestor mesh** | Not required | Not required | **Mandatory** | | **Hazardous area use** | No | Limited (no sparks) | **Yes** | | **Cost per m² (approx.)** | ₹ 2,500–4,500 | ₹ 4,500–8,000 | ₹ 8,000–15,000 | | **Limitation** | Limited FRR; smoke rating not inherent | Dents under impact; needs paint | Heavy; complex hardware | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fire Door Assembly & Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Fire Event Detected] --> B[FACP sends release signal] B --> C{Electromagnetic hold-open
de-energises} C --> D[Door leaf swings / slides shut
via overhead closer or gravity] D --> E[Intumescent strips heat up to ~200 C] E --> F[Strips expand 5x to 10x
sealing leaf-frame gap] D --> G[Mineral wool core resists
heat conduction] D --> H[GI steel facings maintain
structural integrity] D --> I[Smoke seals EPDM block
cold smoke leakage] F --> J[Compartment sealed
FRR up to 240 min] G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[Occupant safe egress
via protected route] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Fire Doors by Material ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fire Doors] --> B[By Material Combustibility] B --> C[Low Combustible Door] B --> D[Non-Combustible Door] B --> E[Spark-Proof Door] C --> C1[FRT Timber Core] C --> C2[FRT Particle Board Core] C --> C3[Typical FRR 30 to 60 min] D --> D1[GI Steel Skins] D --> D2[Mineral Wool Core] D --> D3[Typical FRR 60 to 240 min] E --> E1[Double Leaf GI Steel] E --> E2[Spark Arrester Mesh 20 to 40 mesh] E --> E3[Gravity or Spring Closing] E --> E4[For Petroleum and Chemical Plants] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Cross-Section Schematic — Non-Combustible Steel Fire Door ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cross_Section_View T1[Top Frame
GI 1.6 mm] --> T2[Intumescent Strip 2 mm] T2 --> T3[GI Steel Skin 1.2 mm] T3 --> T4[Mineral Rock Wool Core 50 mm] T4 --> T5[GI Steel Skin 1.2 mm] T5 --> T6[Bottom Sill 50 mm raised] T3 --> T7[Vertical Stiffener
at 150 mm c/c] end F[Fire Side
T fire 849 C] -->|ISO 834| T1 T6 --> R[Room Side
T unexp 158 C] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Design Selection Logic for KTU Students ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Identify building occupancy] --> S2{Hazardous area
with flammable liquids} S2 -- Yes --> S3[Select Spark-Proof Door
IS 3614 Part 2] S2 -- No --> S4{FRR requirement
over 60 min} S4 -- Yes --> S5[Select Non-Combustible Door
GI steel plus mineral wool] S4 -- No --> S6{Aesthetic priority
in residential or hotel} S6 -- Yes --> S7[Select Low Combustible Door
FRT wood or particle board] S6 -- No --> S5 S3 --> S9[Add spark arrester mesh
and double leaf closure] S5 --> S10[Verify insulation criterion
Delta T 140 C] S7 --> S10 S9 --> S10 S10 --> END[Door selected and certified] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]** *Distinguish between a "low combustible fire door" and a "non-combustible fire door". State one typical application of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Low Combustible Door | Non-Combustible Door | |---|---|---| | Core material | FRT wood / FRT particle board (combustible but treated) | Mineral rock wool / calcium silicate (non-combustible per IS 3808) | | Fire resistance | 30–60 min | 60–240 min | | Application | Bedroom doors in hotels, office cabins | Stairwell enclosures, lift lobbies, generator rooms | *(1 mark for material difference, 1 mark for FRR, 1 mark for application)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]** *What is a spark-proof fire door? Under which occupancy is it mandatory as per IS 3614 Part 2?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **spark-proof fire door** is a non-combustible fire door fitted with **spark-arrester mesh (20–40 mesh GI / SS)** and a **gravity / spring self-closing mechanism**, designed to prevent the transmission of sparks, flames, and hot particles between compartments. It is **mandatory in industrial occupancies (Group G)** handling **flammable liquids, gases, and explosive dusts** — e.g., petroleum refineries, LPG storage, paint factories, ammunition depots — as per **IS 3614 (Part 2):1972** and **OISD-118**. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mesh + self-closing feature, 1 mark for occupancy + standard)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2 Choice-1]** **(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the **constructional details of a non-combustible fire door**. List the materials used for the core, facings, and seals. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **non-combustible fire door** of size **2.1 m × 0.9 m** has two **1.2 mm GI facings** and a **40 mm mineral rock wool core** ($k_{wool} = 0.04$ W/m·K, $k_{steel} = 45$ W/m·K). The door is exposed to **ISO 834 standard fire** from one side. Determine: (i) Furnace temperature at **t = 60 min**, (ii) Total thermal resistance, (iii) Steady-state heat flux, and (iv) Verify whether the insulation criterion ($\Delta T \leq 140$ °C) is satisfied. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** A **non-combustible fire door** consists of the following components (sketch expected — refer Mermaid cross-section in Section 4.3): 1. **Door leaf (shutter):** - Two **galvanized iron (GI) steel facings** of thickness **1.0 to 1.6 mm**. - **Mineral rock wool** infill (density 45–80 kg/m³) OR **calcium silicate board**. - Internal **vertical steel stiffeners** at 150–200 mm c/c to prevent buckling under fire. 2. **Door frame:** **GI / steel channel frame** of 1.5–2.0 mm thickness, grouted into the wall opening with **cement mortar + anchor fasteners** (minimum 4 per jamb). 3. **Intumescent seals:** **Graphite-based strips** of 2 mm × 20 mm fixed in a groove around the **top and side edges** of the leaf. Expand at ~200 °C. 4. **Smoke seals:** **EPDM rubber gaskets** on the **meeting stile and bottom** to prevent cold smoke leakage. 5. **Hardware:** - **Stainless steel ball-bearing hinges** — minimum 3 per leaf for height ≤ 2.1 m. - **Fire-rated mortise lock** with stainless steel forend. - **Overhead hydraulic door closer** (self-closing device). - **Fire-rated vision panel** (6 mm clear ceramic glass with intumescent lining) if required. - **Kick plate** at bottom 300 mm height of 1.2 mm SS. 6. **Marking / Certification:** A **metal tag / engraved plate** indicating **FRR, manufacturer, IS standard, and test certificate number** is mandatory. *(1 mark for each major component category; sketch carries 2 marks)* --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Furnace temperature at t = 60 min:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{60} &= T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \cdot 60 + 1) \\ &= 27 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(481) \\ &= 27 + 345 \cdot (2.6821) \\ &= 27 + 925.32 \\ &= 952.32\,°C \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Total thermal resistance:** $$ A = 2.1 \times 0.9 = 1.89\,m^2 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_{steel} &= \frac{0.0012}{45 \times 1.89} = 1.411 \times 10^{-5}\,°C/W \\ R_{wool} &= \frac{0.040}{0.04 \times 1.89} = 0.5291\,°C/W \\ R_{total} &= 2 \cdot (1.411 \times 10^{-5}) + 0.5291 \approx 0.5291\,°C/W \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Steady-state heat flux:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{Q} &= \frac{952.32 - 27}{0.5291} = \frac{925.32}{0.5291} = 1748.8\,W \\ \dot{q}'' &= \frac{1748.8}{1.89} = 925.3\,W/m^2 \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Insulation check (unexposed face temperature):** Assuming natural convection $h_c = 5\,W/m^2K$ on room side: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{unexp} &= 27 + \frac{925.3}{5} = 27 + 185.06 = 212.06\,°C \\ \Delta T_{unexp} &= 212.06 - 27 = 185.06\,°C \end{aligned} $$ Since **185.06 °C > 140 °C** ✗ — **Insulation criterion FAILS at 60 min**. > **Engineering conclusion:** A 40 mm rock wool core is **insufficient for a 60-minute rated door**. The door will be classified as a **30-minute integrity-only door**. **To achieve 60 min insulation, increase rock wool thickness to ~70 mm** (or upgrade $k_{wool}$ to a lower conductivity product). *(2 marks for T₆₀, 2 marks for R, 2 marks for ΔT check, 1 mark for engineering conclusion)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2 Choice-2]** **(a)** What is a **spark-proof fire door**? List its **components** and explain its **operating principle** with reference to **IS 3614 (Part 2)** and **OISD-118**. Where is it used? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **low combustible, non-combustible, and spark-proof fire doors** in a **tabular matrix** with respect to: core material, FRR, applicable standard, hardware, typical application, and cost. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** A **spark-proof fire door** is a special-purpose fire door used in **hazardous industrial occupancies** where there is a risk of **sparks, flames, or hot particles** propagating between fire-rated compartments. **Operating principle:** - The door is built with **double-leaf GI steel construction** (each leaf of 1.6–2.0 mm thick). - When opened, both leaves are held against the wall by **magnetic hold-open devices** OR counterweights. - On fire alarm / loss of power, the leaves close **sequentially**: - **Rear leaf (fire-side) closes first** — prevents the flame front from advancing. - **Front leaf (room-side) closes 3–5 seconds later** — prevents the **back-draft** of hot gases. - A **spark-arrester mesh** (SS / GI wire mesh, 20–40 mesh) is fitted in a **top louver** that remains slightly open to permit **natural ventilation of flammable vapours** but traps **incandescent particles**. **Components (as per IS 3614 Part 2 / OISD-118):** 1. Double GI steel leaves (1.6–2.0 mm). 2. Spark-arrestor louver with SS mesh. 3. Gravity or counter-weight self-closing mechanism. 4. Fire-rated hinges, latches, and view window (with wire-reinforced glass). 5. Raised **threshold (50–100 mm)** to contain liquid fuel. 6. Bottom seal with intumescent strip. 7. Identification plate certifying **IS 3614 (Pt 2) compliance** and **OISD-118 hazard class**. **Used in:** Petroleum refineries, LPG storage godowns, bulk fuel stations, paint and varnish plants, ammunition magazines, distilleries, gas compressor rooms. *(2 marks for definition + operating principle, 3 marks for components, 1 mark for standards, 1 mark for application)* --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** | # | Parameter | Low Combustible | Non-Combustible | Spark-Proof | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Core material** | FRT wood / FRT particle board | Mineral rock wool / calcium silicate | Mineral wool + double GI skin | | 2 | **FRR (minutes)** | 30–60 | 60–240 | 90–120 | | 3 | **Governing standard** | IS 3614 (Pt 1), IS 16205 (Pt 24) | IS 3614 (Pt 1), BS 476 Pt 22 | IS 3614 (Pt 2), OISD-118 | | 4 | **Self-closing hardware** | Overhead hydraulic closer | Overhead hydraulic closer | Gravity / counterweight | | 5 | **Typical application** | Hotels, offices, residences | Stairwells, lobbies, server rooms | Refineries, LPG, chemical plants | | 6 | **Approx. cost (₹/m²)** | 2,500–4,500 | 4,500–8,000 | 8,000–15,000 | | 7 | **Hazardous area use** | No | Limited (no sparks) | **Yes — mandatory** | *(1 mark for each correctly filled row of the table)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing "non-combustible" with "incombustible"** — both terms are used, but as per **IS 3808**, only "non-combustible" is the legal classification. Avoid the colloquial term "incombustible". > 2. **Forgetting the role of intumescent strips** — students often write only "fire door has a steel core" but miss the **expansion seal mechanism**, which is the heart of compartmentation. > 3. **Incorrect ISO 834 evaluation** — at $t = 0$, students sometimes compute $T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(0)$ which is undefined. Always remember that at $t = 0$, $T_t = T_0$. > 4. **In heat-transfer numericals**, forgetting to convert **door area to m²** (students often leave it in mm²) leads to a 10⁶ error. > 5. **Stating "fire door is fireproof"** — it is NOT. It is **fire-resistive for a limited duration**. A "fireproof" door is a layman's term and will be marked wrong. > 6. **Conflating NBC 2016 Part 4 (Fire & Life Safety) with Part 3 (Development Rules)** — the fire door provisions are in **Part 4, Section 4.6**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam (Module 2 — Fire Doors):** > > ⬛ **Three categories:** Low Combustible (FRT wood) / Non-Combustible (GI + mineral wool) / Spark-Proof (double leaf + mesh). > > ⬛ **Governing standards:** IS 3614 (Part 1 & 2), IS 16205 (Part 24), BS 476 (Pt 20/22), NBC 2016 Part 4, OISD-118, IS 3808. > > ⬛ **Fire resistance criteria:** Integrity (E) + Insulation (I) + Stability. Limits: $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140$ °C, $\Delta T_{pt} \leq 180$ °C. > > ⬛ **ISO 834 equation:** $T_t = T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ — at $t = 30$ min, $T \approx 849$ °C; at $t = 60$ min, $T \approx 952$ °C; at $t = 120$ min, $T \approx 1049$ °C. > > ⬛ **Intumescent strips** expand 5×–10× at ~200 °C and seal leaf-frame gaps. > > ⬛ **Mineral wool** is the primary insulator; steel facings are for **integrity, not insulation** ($R_{steel} \approx 0$ vs $R_{wool} \approx 99.99\%$ of total). > > ⬛ **Self-closing is mandatory** for all three categories — never use a fire door without a closer. > > ⬛ **Spark-proof doors** are used only in **Group G / H industrial occupancies**; never required in residential or commercial. > > ⬛ **Smoke seals (EPDM)** are as critical as fire seals — **cold smoke kills more people than the fire itself**. > > ⬛ **Fire door is a "door SET"**, not just a shutter — leaf + frame + seals + hardware + closer must be **tested as a unit** to IS 3614. > > ⬛ **Maximum allowable gap** between leaf and frame is **3 mm** at sides/top and **5 mm** at bottom (per IS 3614). > > ⬛ **Certification tag** with FRR, IS standard, manufacturer, and test lab must be permanently fixed on the door.

topics.title: Method of Suspension of Fire Doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method of Suspension of Fire Doors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — METHOD OF SUSPENSION OF FIRE DOORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Method of Suspension of Fire Doors**
> The *method of suspension* of a fire door refers to the **engineered hardware assembly, support geometry, and kinematic arrangement** by which a fire-resistant door leaf is held, guided, and permitted to operate within its frame, while simultaneously ensuring that the door remains positively **self-closing, latched, and positively retained** under both normal service and fire-exposure conditions. As per **IS 3614 (Part 1 & Part 2)** and **NBC 2016 (Part 4)**, suspension methods include hinge sets, pivot sets, overhead tracks, sliding trolleys, and rolling-shutter barrel assemblies — each rated for a specified **fire-resistance rating (FRR)** of 30, 60, 90, or 120 minutes.

In the context of structural fire safety, the suspension is *not* merely a means of hanging a door — it is a **life-safety critical load path** that must transfer the self-weight of the door leaf to the structural frame, allow controlled release on fusible-link actuation, and maintain the integrity of the fire separation barrier.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Door is a Pendulum, the Frame is the Hinge" Analogy**
> Imagine you are holding a heavy wooden shield in front of a doorway. If you just prop it with a stick, the slightest push will topple it. But if you bolt it with a **piano hinge at the top edge**, attach a **hydraulic damper** (the same kind that stops elevator doors from slamming), and connect a **wax-filled link** that melts at 68 °C — then you have a *suspended fire door*. The damper guarantees the door always swings shut; the fusible link guarantees the door releases even if a panicked person wedges it open. The hinge/suspension is the **mechanical skeleton** that allows all this protective logic to function.

### 1.3 Classification of Fire Doors by Suspension Type

| Sl. No. | Suspension Type | Operating Action | Typical Application |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Single-Swing Hinged** | Hinges on one vertical edge; closes by gravity/closer | Stairwell doors, corridor doors |
| 2 | **Double-Swing Hinged** | Hinges on both vertical edges (double-acting) | Kitchen servery, hospital corridors |
| 3 | **Sliding (Top-Hung)** | Suspended from overhead track on trolleys | Industrial compartments, malls |
| 4 | **Sliding (Bottom-Rolling)** | Floor-mounted track, overhead guide only | Warehouses, aircraft hangars |
| 5 | **Rolling Shutter (Steel Slats)** | Coiled on a barrel/drum at head | Factories, car parks |
| 6 | **Vertical Guillotine** | Counterweighted lift | Industrial fire separation |
| 7 | **Folding / Accordion** | Multi-leaf concertina on overhead rail | Large openings needing egress |

### 1.4 Governing Code Metrics

- **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** — typically **30 / 60 / 90 / 120 minutes** as per **IS 3614 (Part 1):1966** and **IS 3614 (Part 2):1992**.
- **Integrity (E)** — ability to prevent passage of flames/hot gases.
- **Insulation (I)** — ability to limit temperature rise on unexposed face to **140 °C average / 180 °C at any point** (per **IS 3809:1979**).
- **Self-Closing Force** — per **NBC 2016 Part 4, Cl. 4.10**: a force of **≤ 25 N at the leading edge** shall be sufficient to close a latched fire door.
- **Standard test furnace temperature** — **834 °C at 30 min, 945 °C at 60 min** following **ISO 834** heating curve.

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** The word *suspension* in this topic is **not** limited to overhead-hung sliding doors. It is a **synonym for the door's hardware-and-bearing assembly** as a whole, including hinged supports. Always write this distinction clearly in your answers to gain a definitional edge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component Anatomy of a Suspended Fire Door

A fire door suspension system is a **6-component kinematic chain**. Every component must be rated for the full FRR of the assembly — failure of any single element (e.g., a melting hinge pin) voids the entire fire-resistance certification.

### The 6 Components

1. **Support Element** — Hinge / Pivot / Trolley / Barrel
2. **Closing Element** — Spring closer / Hydraulic damper / Counterweight
3. **Latching Element** — Latch bolt + Strike plate
4. **Release Element** — Fusible link / Electromagnetic hold-open
5. **Sealing Element** — Intumescent strip + Smoke seal
6. **Frame Element** — Hardwood / Steel pressed frame anchored to wall

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Chain of Integrity" Principle** (IS 3614 Cl. 5.4)
> Each link in the suspension chain must possess a fire-resistance rating **≥ the door leaf's rating**. The weakest link governs the assembly rating.

## 2.2 Method 1 — Hinged Single-Swing Suspension

### Working Logic (Step-by-Step)

- The door leaf is mounted on **two or three hinges** along the **hinge jamb** (vertical stile).
- Standard heights require:
  - **2 hinges** for doors ≤ 2100 mm height.
  - **3 hinges** for doors > 2100 mm height.
- The **top hinge** carries the **lateral load (wind/seismic)**; the **bottom hinge** carries the **vertical load (gravity)**; an **intermediate hinge** controls **deflection**.
- A **door closer** is mounted on the **pull side** of the door, with arm geometry sized to provide **5° to 7° of pre-load closing torque**.

### Hinge Material Specification

| Hinge Type | Material | FRR Capable | Cost Index |
|:--|:--|:-:|:-:|
| Mild Steel Ball-Bearing | EN 1.0503 / IS 2062 | 120 min | 1.0× |
| Stainless Steel 304 | AISI 304 | 120 min | 2.4× |
| Brass-Bodied with SS Pin | CuZn37 + SS 304 | 60 min | 1.8× |
| Continuous (Piano) Hinge | SS 304 / Aluminium | 90 min | 2.0× |

## 2.3 Method 2 — Pivot Suspension (Double-Acting)

- Pivots are mounted at the **top and bottom of the door**, set **~50 mm in from each edge**.
- The axis of rotation is **offset from the door edge**, allowing swing in **both directions** (double-acting).
- Floor spring or overhead spring closes the leaf.
- Common in **hospital OTs, kitchen serveries, and refuge-area doors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pivots vs. Hinges?**
> Hinges transfer load eccentrically; pivots transfer load **axially** — pivots can carry **~30% heavier leaves** for the same FRR because there is no bending moment on the pin.

## 2.4 Method 3 — Top-Hung Sliding Door Suspension

### Kinematic Layout

- An **overhead track** (typically **MS hot-rolled, IS 2062 grade**) is bolted to the structural lintel.
- The door leaf is suspended via **two/three hanger trolleys** running on sealed ball-bearing rollers.
- A **bottom guide channel** prevents lateral sway but does **not carry vertical load**.
- A **counterweight cable-and-pulley** OR a **fusible-link-released spring** provides self-closing action.

### Track Sag and Deflection Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The track shall not deflect more than **1/360 of the span** under the full dead load of the door leaf plus a **safety factor of 1.5** (per IS 800:2007 deflection limits for secondary members).

## 2.5 Method 4 — Rolling Shutter (Steel Slat) Suspension

- The shutter curtain consists of **interlocked galvanised steel / stainless steel slats** (typical profile: 75 mm pitch, 1.0–1.2 mm thick).
- The slats wrap around a **barrel (octagonal or circular) mounted on bearing housings** at the lintel.
- Vertical guides on either side house **slat-end lugs**.
- Fire-rated variants use a **gravity-drop governor** (fuse melts → governor releases → shutter descends by gravity at controlled rate).

### Self-Closing Velocity (Fire-Rated Rolling Shutters)

> **Closing Speed** = **0.1 to 0.3 m/s** (per IS 3614 Part 2 Annex C)
> Slower than typical non-fire rolling shutters (0.5 m/s) to allow safe egress.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Source / Application |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Hinge Bending Moment | $M_h = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{n}$ | N·mm | W = leaf weight (N), L = leaf width (mm), n = number of hinges |
| 2 | Hinge Shear Force | $V_h = \dfrac{W}{n}$ | N | Per-hinge vertical load |
| 3 | Max Track Sag | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot q \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | mm | Uniform load on simply-supported track |
| 4 | Track Moment of Inertia (Reqd.) | $I_{reqd} = \dfrac{5 \cdot q \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot \delta_{allow}}$ | mm⁴ | For deflection-limited design |
| 5 | Slat Curvature Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{6 \cdot M}{b \cdot t^2}$ | N/mm² | b = slat width, t = slat thickness |
| 6 | Closing Force at Latch | $F_{close} = \dfrac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{d_{latch}}$ | N | d_cg = distance to CoG, d_latch = distance to latch |
| 7 | Fusible Link Melting Temp. | $T_f = 68\,°C$ (std) or $93\,°C$ (high-temp) | °C | IS 3614 Part 1 |
| 8 | Hinge Spacing (Max) | $s_{max} = \dfrac{L_{door}}{3}$ (for 3-hinge) | mm | Equal spacing rule |
| 9 | Frame Anchorage Pull-Out | $P_{anchor} = \dfrac{M_{frame}}{d_{anchor}} \leq 0.25 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_{anchor}$ | N | Concrete-frame interaction |
| 10 | Roller Reaction (Top-Hung) | $R = \dfrac{W}{2}$ per trolley pair | N | Two-trolley suspension |

### 🧭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospitals & Airports** — sliding fire doors on trolleys enable **>3 m wide clear openings** for stretcher/cart movement while preserving FRR-120.
- **High-Rise Stairwells** — single-swing hinged fire doors with **electromagnetic hold-open + fusible link** form the universal corridor-to-stairwell transition.
- **Industrial Warehouses** — rolling steel shutters provide **low-cost FRR-120** over **large spans (up to 12 m)**.
- **IT Data Centres** — pivot-suspended double-acting fire doors allow **bi-directional egress** under fire alarm without manual unlocking.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Hinge Bending Moment for a Single-Swing Fire Door

### Given Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Setup)

> A single-swing fire door measures **900 mm × 2100 mm** (width × height), with a leaf mass of **45 kg**. The door is hung on **3 hinges** equally spaced along the height. Determine:
> 1. Per-hinge shear force.
> 2. Per-hinge bending moment.
> 3. Required hinge pin diameter assuming allowable shear stress **60 N/mm²**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Total Weight of the Door Leaf

$$W = m \cdot g = 45 \times 9.81 = 441.45 \;\text{N}$$

**Logic:** The leaf's dead weight is the primary vertical action transferred to each hinge. We use $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$ (not 10) for KTU exam precision.

### Step 2 — Per-Hinge Vertical Shear Force

For **3 hinges** equally sharing the vertical load:

$$V_h = \frac{W}{n} = \frac{441.45}{3} = 147.15 \;\text{N}$$

**Logic:** A statically determinate simply-supported model is used — IS 3614 assumes equal load sharing when hinges are of identical make and equally spaced.

### Step 3 — Per-Hinge Bending Moment

The bending moment on each hinge arises because the door's centre of gravity lies at **width/2 = 450 mm** from the hinge jamb, while the **latch edge** is at **900 mm**.

For an off-axis gravity load, each hinge experiences a moment proportional to its distance from the door's CoG. Conservatively, the **critical hinge moment** is taken at the **top hinge** (carries lateral wind/seismic component too):

$$M_h = \frac{W \cdot L_{width}}{n} = \frac{441.45 \times 900}{3}$$

$$M_h = 132{,}435 \;\text{N·mm} = 132.435 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Logic:** This is a worst-case distribution; the top hinge also resists wind load, so it gets the *full* width moment divided by 3.

### Step 4 — Required Hinge Pin Diameter

For a pin in **double shear** (most fire-door hinges):

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{V_h}{2 \cdot A_{pin}} = \frac{V_h}{2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2\right)}$$

Rearranging for diameter $d$:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot V_h}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 147.15}{\pi \times 60}} = \sqrt{\frac{294.30}{188.50}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{1.5613} = 1.2495 \;\text{mm}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Standard Pin Size (Practical Selection)

Per **IS 2062 / Standard Hardware Schedule**, fire-door hinge pins come in **6, 8, 10, 12, 16 mm** standard sizes. We select the **next higher standard size**:

$$\boxed{d_{provided} = 8 \;\text{mm} \quad \text{(IS 1341 standard hinge pin)}}$$

### Step 6 — Verification of Bending Stress in the Pin

The pin acts as a simply-supported beam between the two leaves. Maximum bending moment on pin:

$$M_{pin} = \frac{V_h}{2} \times \frac{t_{leaf}}{2} = \frac{147.15}{2} \times \frac{45}{2}$$

$$M_{pin} = 36.79 \times 22.5 = 827.7 \;\text{N·mm}$$

Section modulus of an 8 mm pin:

$$Z_{pin} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 8^3}{32} = 50.27 \;\text{mm}^3$$

Bending stress:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{pin}}{Z_{pin}} = \frac{827.7}{50.27} = 16.46 \;\text{N/mm}^2$$

This is well below the allowable **165 N/mm²** for SS 304 — the 8 mm pin is **safe**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Track Deflection for a Top-Hung Sliding Fire Door

### Given

- Sliding door leaf: **3000 mm wide × 2400 mm tall**, mass = **120 kg**.
- Track: simply supported at both ends, span **L = 3.2 m**.
- Track material: **MS IS 2062**, E = **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²**.
- Allowable deflection: **L/360**.

### Step 1 — Total Load on Track

$$W = 120 \times 9.81 = 1177.2 \;\text{N}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Uniformly Distributed Load

Assume the load is shared equally between two trolleys, each trolley reaction = W/2 = 588.6 N. For approximate analysis, treat as a UDL:

$$q = \frac{W}{L} = \frac{1177.2}{3200} = 0.3679 \;\text{N/mm}$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Deflection Formula (Simply Supported Beam, UDL)

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \cdot q \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

### Step 4 — Required Moment of Inertia

$$\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{360} = \frac{3200}{360} = 8.889 \;\text{mm}$$

Rearrange for $I_{reqd}$:

$$I_{reqd} = \frac{5 \cdot q \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot \delta_{allow}}$$

$$I_{reqd} = \frac{5 \times 0.3679 \times (3200)^4}{384 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 8.889}$$

Numerator:

$$5 \times 0.3679 = 1.8395$$

$$(3200)^4 = 1.0486 \times 10^{14}$$

$$1.8395 \times 1.0486 \times 10^{14} = 1.929 \times 10^{14}$$

Denominator:

$$384 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 8.889 = 6.827 \times 10^{8}$$

$$I_{reqd} = \frac{1.929 \times 10^{14}}{6.827 \times 10^{8}} = 2.826 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^4$$

### Step 5 — Select Standard Channel Section

A **ISMC 75 (Indian Standard Medium Channel, 75 mm)** has $I_{xx} = 1.12 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^4$ — **insufficient**.

A **ISMC 100** has $I_{xx} = 2.86 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^4$ — **just sufficient**.

**Select ISMC 100 channel** as the track section.

### Step 6 — Verify Track Bending Stress

Maximum bending moment:

$$M_{max} = \frac{q \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{0.3679 \times (3200)^2}{8} = 471{,}040 \;\text{N·mm}$$

For ISMC 100, $Z_{xx} = 5.72 \times 10^4 \;\text{mm}^3$:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{471{,}040}{57{,}200} = 8.23 \;\text{N/mm}^2$$

This is far below the allowable **165 N/mm²** — track is **safe and serviceable**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Suspension Methods

| Parameter | Hinged | Pivot | Top-Hung Sliding | Rolling Shutter |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Max Leaf Mass (kg) | 80 | 120 | 250 | 600+ |
| Max Clear Opening (m) | 1.2 | 1.5 | 6.0 | 12.0 |
| FRR Achievable (min) | 30–120 | 30–90 | 60–120 | 60–240 |
| Closing Mechanism | Spring/Hydraulic closer | Floor spring | Counterweight/Spring | Gravity-drop governor |
| Egress Clearance Loss | None | None | 100 mm at jamb | Track housing 250–400 mm |
| Maintenance Frequency | Annual | Quarterly | Bi-annual | Annual |
| Cost per m² (₹, indicative) | 4500 | 6200 | 7800 | 5200 |
| Typical Failure Mode in Fire | Hinge pin shear | Pivot seizure | Trolley derailment | Barrel bearing jam |

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Fire Door Operation on Fusible-Link Trigger

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Normal as NORMAL STATE
    participant Alarm as FIRE ALARM
    participant Fuse as FUSIBLE LINK 68C
    participant EM as EM HOLD OPEN
    participant Door as FIRE DOOR LEAF
    participant Closer as CLOSER MECHANISM
    participant Latch as LATCH and STRIKE

    Normal->>EM: Energised, door held open
    Alarm->>EM: Signal sent to de-energise
    EM-->>Door: Releases hold
    Normal->>Fuse: Heat exceeds 68C
    Fuse-->>Door: Link melts, mechanical release
    Door->>Closer: Closer actuates
    Closer->>Door: Swings to closed position
    Door->>Latch: Latch engages strike plate
    Latch-->>Normal: Door positively latched, FRR active
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Define the term "method of suspension" as applied to fire doors. List any four types of suspension methods.**

**Model Answer:**

The *method of suspension* of a fire door refers to the engineered hardware-and-bearing arrangement by which a fire-resistant door leaf is supported, guided, and permitted to close within its frame, while maintaining the door's fire-resistance rating (FRR) under fire exposure.

The four principal types of suspension methods are:
1. **Hinged single-swing suspension** — two/three hinges along the hinge jamb.
2. **Pivot (double-acting) suspension** — top and bottom pivots allowing bidirectional swing.
3. **Top-hung sliding suspension** — overhead track with trolley hangers.
4. **Rolling-shutter suspension** — barrel and slat assembly with gravity-drop governor.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for four types with brief description)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the function of a fusible link in the suspension system of a fire door. Specify the standard fusible-link temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fusible link** is a heat-sensitive release element in the suspension/hold-open system. Its function is to **automatically release the door from its held-open position** when ambient temperature at the ceiling exceeds a pre-set value, allowing the closer mechanism to shut the door. This ensures the fire door is **positively closed and latched** even if building occupants fail to close it manually during evacuation.

The standard fusible-link operating temperature is **68 °C** (low-temperature grade) or **93 °C** (high-temperature grade) as per **IS 3614 (Part 1):1966, Cl. 5.3.4**.

*(3 marks: 1 for function + 1 for temperature + 1 for code reference)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**

**A 1200 mm × 2400 mm single-swing fire door is suspended on three equally-spaced hinges. The door leaf has a self-weight of 60 kg.**
**(a)** Determine the shear force and bending moment on the top hinge. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Design the hinge pin diameter assuming allowable shear stress of 55 N/mm², and state the standard pin size to be adopted. Verify bending stress in the pin. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Compute total leaf weight:**

$$W = m \cdot g = 60 \times 9.81 = 588.6 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Per-hinge vertical shear (3-hinge equal share):**

$$V_h = \frac{W}{3} = \frac{588.6}{3} = 196.2 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Bending moment on the top hinge (carries lateral + gravity-moment):**

$$M_{top} = \frac{W \cdot L_{width}}{3} = \frac{588.6 \times 1200}{3} = 235{,}440 \;\text{N·mm} \approx 235.4 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Result (a):** $V_h = 196.2 \;\text{N}$ and $M_{top} = 235{,}440 \;\text{N·mm}$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Hinge pin diameter (double-shear design):**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot V_h}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 196.2}{\pi \times 55}} = \sqrt{\frac{392.4}{172.79}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{2.271} = 1.507 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Adopt next standard size** (IS 1341 standard hinge pins: 6, 8, 10, 12, 16 mm):

$$\boxed{d_{provided} = 8 \;\text{mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Bending stress verification (pin as simply-supported beam between leaves):**

For a 50 mm leaf thickness:

$$M_{pin} = \frac{V_h}{2} \times \frac{t_{leaf}}{2} = \frac{196.2}{2} \times \frac{50}{2} = 2452.5 \;\text{N·mm}$$

Section modulus of 8 mm pin:

$$Z_{pin} = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 512}{32} = 50.27 \;\text{mm}^3$$

Bending stress:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{pin}}{Z_{pin}} = \frac{2452.5}{50.27} = 48.79 \;\text{N/mm}^2$$

**Result (b):** $\sigma_b = 48.79 \;\text{N/mm}^2 \ll 165 \;\text{N/mm}^2$ (allowable for SS 304). **Pin is safe.** Provide 8 mm IS-standard pin.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice, Module 2]**

**A top-hung sliding fire door leaf measures 3.0 m wide × 2.4 m tall, weighs 90 kg, and is suspended from a simply-supported MS track spanning 3.5 m. Allowable deflection is L/360 and E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm².**
**(a)** Calculate the required moment of inertia of the track section. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Select a standard ISMC channel section and verify its bending stress. Take the uniform load assumption. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Total weight:**

$$W = 90 \times 9.81 = 882.9 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Uniformly distributed load:**

$$q = \frac{W}{L} = \frac{882.9}{3500} = 0.2523 \;\text{N/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Allowable deflection:**

$$\delta_{allow} = \frac{3500}{360} = 9.722 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Required moment of inertia:**

$$I_{reqd} = \frac{5 \cdot q \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot \delta_{allow}}$$

$$I_{reqd} = \frac{5 \times 0.2523 \times (3500)^4}{384 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 9.722}$$

Numerator: $5 \times 0.2523 = 1.2614$; $(3500)^4 = 1.5006 \times 10^{14}$; Product $= 1.8926 \times 10^{14}$

Denominator: $384 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 9.722 = 7.466 \times 10^8$

$$I_{reqd} = \frac{1.8926 \times 10^{14}}{7.466 \times 10^8} = 2.535 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^4$$

**Result (a):** $I_{reqd} = 2.535 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^4$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Standard ISMC selection:**

| Section | $I_{xx}$ (mm⁴) | $Z_{xx}$ (mm³) |
|:--|:-:|:-:|
| ISMC 75 | 1.12 × 10⁵ | 2.98 × 10⁴ |
| **ISMC 100** | **2.86 × 10⁵** | **5.72 × 10⁴** |
| ISMC 125 | 5.84 × 10⁵ | 9.34 × 10⁴ |

Select **ISMC 100** — $I_{xx} = 2.86 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^4 > I_{reqd} = 2.535 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^4$. ✔

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{q \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{0.2523 \times (3500)^2}{8} = 386{,}375 \;\text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Bending stress:**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{386{,}375}{57{,}200} = 6.76 \;\text{N/mm}^2$$

**Result (b):** $\sigma_b = 6.76 \;\text{N/mm}^2 \ll 165 \;\text{N/mm}^2$ ✔ — **ISMC 100 is safe and adequate.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "suspension" with "overhead hanging only"** — *Method of suspension* is the **entire hardware-and-bearing assembly**, including hinged supports. Marks lost: ~3.
> 2. **Using g = 10 instead of 9.81** — In KTU ESE, use **9.81 m/s²** unless problem explicitly says "take g = 10 m/s²".
> 3. **Forgetting to divide by n hinges** — Many students compute total moment as $W \cdot L$ without dividing by the number of hinges. **3 marks lost** in Part (a).
> 4. **Not selecting the next standard pin size** — A computed 1.5 mm diameter is not a *standard* size. You **must** state that the next standard size (6 or 8 mm) is adopted.
> 5. **Ignoring the double-shear condition** — Hinge pins are in **double shear** (two leaves). Using single-shear formula gives a **larger** required diameter — wrong side of safety.
> 6. **Skipping the verification step** — Always verify bending stress in the pin and track. It is a **"must-show" step** for full marks.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Suspension ≠ overhead hanging only.** It includes *all* hardware that supports, guides, and releases the fire door.
- ✅ The **fire door's FRR** is governed by the **weakest component** in the suspension chain (chain-of-integrity principle).
- ✅ **Three hinge types:** mild steel ball-bearing, stainless steel 304, brass-bodied with SS pin.
- ✅ **Pivot** is used for **double-acting doors** and carries **30% more load** than hinges of equal FRR.
- ✅ **Top-hung sliding** doors are ideal for **wide openings (3–6 m)** with **counterweight or spring** closing.
- ✅ **Rolling shutters** provide the **largest clear opening (up to 12 m)** at the **lowest per-m² cost**.
- ✅ **Track deflection** must not exceed **L/360** under full dead load × safety factor 1.5.
- ✅ **Fusible link** operates at **68 °C** (low-temp) or **93 °C** (high-temp) per **IS 3614 Part 1**.
- ✅ **Door closer force** at the leading edge must not exceed **25 N** (NBC 2016 Part 4 Cl. 4.10).
- ✅ **Hinge pin** is in **double shear**; always use $\frac{2V}{\pi d^2}$ form, not single-shear.
- ✅ **Self-closing velocity** of fire-rated rolling shutters = **0.1 to 0.3 m/s** (slower than non-fire shutters).
- ✅ **Code references** to quote: **IS 3614 (Parts 1 & 2), IS 3809, IS 14609, NBC 2016 Part 4, ISO 834**.
- ✅ **Standard pin sizes:** 6, 8, 10, 12, 16 mm (per IS 1341). Always adopt the **next-higher** standard size.
- ✅ **ISMC sections** commonly used for tracks: **ISMC 75, 100, 125** — select based on $I_{reqd}$.
- ✅ **Intumescent seals** expand at **~200 °C** to seal gaps; **smoke seals** prevent cold-smoke leakage.
- ✅ **EM hold-open devices** must de-energise on **fire alarm signal** or **power failure** — fail-safe design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air-tight Sealing of Doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air-tight Sealing of Doors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air-tight Sealing of Doors** in the context of structural fire safety refers to the engineered provision of continuous, gap-free, pressure-resistant barriers at the perimeter, threshold, and meeting stiles of fire-rated door assemblies, designed to prevent the uncontrolled passage of **flames, hot combustion gases, smoke, and toxic products** between fire compartments during a fire incident.

According to **IS 3614 (Part 2): 1992** and **IS 3614 (Part 1): 2021**, fire doors are defined as *"a door, provided for the passage of persons, goods, etc., and which, together with its frame and furniture, is designed to resist the passage of fire, smoke, and hot gases for a specified period under specified test conditions."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCSFT501 - Module 2):**
> Air-tight sealing is a sub-component of **Passive Fire Protection (PFP)**. It is essential to maintain the **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** and the **Smoke Leakage Rating** of a door assembly. A door, however fire-rated, is only as effective as its sealing system.

The three primary sealing zones on a fire door assembly are:

1. **Perimeter Seals (Head, Jambs, and Meeting Stiles):** Located around the door edges to seal gaps between the leaf and frame.
2. **Threshold Seals / Bottom Seals:** Located at the base of the door leaf to seal the gap to the floor.
3. **Glazing Seals:** Sealing the interface between fire-resistant glass and the door leaf.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pressure Cooker Gasket**
> Think of an air-tight fire door seal like the **rubber gasket on a pressure cooker**. When the cooker is cold, the gasket sits loosely. But once heat and pressure build up inside, the gasket expands and forms a perfect, impenetrable barrier. Similarly, an **intumescent fire seal** is dormant at room temperature but **expands dramatically (often 5x to 10x its original volume)** when exposed to fire heat (typically **> 200°C**), closing off all gaps through which smoke and flames could travel. The door, the frame, and the seals must work *as a system* — just like the cooker body, lid, and gasket.

### 1.3 Why Air-Tight Sealing Matters — The Fire Triangle of Compartmentation

In a compartmented building, the **three pillars of fire containment** are:

| Pillar | Function | Failure Consequence |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| **Fire Walls / Floors** | Resist structural collapse | Spread to upper floors |
| **Fire-rated Doors** | Permit access while blocking fire | Allow authorized evacuation only |
| **Air-tight Seals** | Block smoke and gas leakage | Smoke kills 60–70% of fire fatalities |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Statistic (NFPA / NBC Data):** Over **60% of fire deaths** are caused by **smoke inhalation**, not direct flame contact. Even a **6 mm gap** around a fire door can allow massive smoke spread. This is precisely why air-tight sealing is non-negotiable.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized in fire door testing:

- **Test Pressure Differential:** Typically **8.5 Pa to 20 Pa** across the door (as per **EN 1634-3 / IS 3614 Part 2** for smoke control doors — denoted **Sa** or **S200**).
- **Intumescent Activation Temperature:** Typically **180°C to 220°C** for graphite-based seals.
- **Maximum Permissible Gap at Installation:** **3 mm (head and jambs)**, **10 mm (threshold)** as per **IS 3614**; the international **NFPA 80** allows up to **3.175 mm (1/8 inch)** at head/jambs.
- **Leakage Rate at 25 Pa:** Smoke control doors must limit leakage to **≤ 3 m³/hr per linear meter** (per **UL 1784 / EN 1634-3**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Requirements of an Air-Tight Fire Door Seal

A properly engineered air-tight seal must satisfy **four simultaneous performance criteria**, often abbreviated as the **"3 T's + 1 I"** framework in fire engineering literature:

1. **Temperature Resistance (T₁):** Survive elevated temperatures (**> 870°C** for 2-hour FRR doors) without losing integrity.
2. **Thermal Insulation (T₂):** Limit heat transfer to the unexposed face to below **140°C average / 180°C peak** (per **IS 3614 / BS 476 Part 22**).
3. **Toxicity / Smoke Restriction (T₃):** Prevent passage of CO, HCN, and particulates.
4. **Integrity (I):** No through-cracks, openings, or sustained flaming on the unexposed face.

## 2.2 Classification of Sealing Systems

### 2.2.1 Intumescent Seals
- **Material:** Graphite-based, sodium silicate-based, or expandable graphite compounds.
- **Activation:** Begins expanding at **~180°C**; full expansion by **300°C**.
- **Expansion Ratio:** Typically **5:1 to 10:1** (volume).
- **Location:** Mounted in grooves machined into the door leaf edge OR in the frame reveal.
- **Function:** Closes the gap as the door deforms in fire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Intumescent seals are *passive* devices — they do nothing at ambient temperature. They only activate when fire occurs. This is why they are often paired with **cold smoke seals** for life-safety applications.

### 2.2.2 Cold Smoke Seals (Acrylic / Silicone-based)
- **Material:** Extruded silicone, EPDM rubber, or brush-type nylon pile.
- **Activation:** Functional from **ambient temperature**.
- **Function:** Prevent **cold smoke leakage** (i.e., smoke at < 50°C) during the early stages of a fire — the most lethal phase.
- **Standard:** Compliant with **EN 1634-3** for smoke leakage at **25 Pa, 50 Pa** differential pressure.

### 2.2.3 Combined Seals (Intumescent + Smoke)
- Modern assemblies often integrate both into a single **"combined seal strip"** mounted in a common channel.
- These are the **most common** in KTU-referenced designs.

### 2.2.4 Mechanical / Compression Seals
- **Type:** Self-adhesive rubber compression gaskets, drop-down seals (automatic thresholds).
- **Use:** Primarily for **smoke control doors (Sa/S200)** rather than integrity-only doors.

## 2.3 Critical Gap Tolerances — The Geometry of Sealing

The **permitted gap dimensions** are not arbitrary — they are derived from the **expansion capacity** of the intumescent material under fire conditions.

| Location | Maximum Permitted Gap (IS 3614) | Maximum Permitted Gap (NFPA 80) | Function of Seal |
|----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------|
| **Head (Top)** | 3 mm | 3.175 mm (1/8") | Intumescent + smoke |
| **Jambs (Sides)** | 3 mm | 3.175 mm (1/8") | Intumescent + smoke |
| **Meeting Stiles** | 3 mm | 3.175 mm (1/8") | Intumescent + smoke |
| **Threshold (Bottom)** | 10 mm | 6.35 mm (1/4") | Drop-down / automatic seal |
| **Under Door** | ≤ 6 mm (with seal) | ≤ 3.175 mm | Smoke control |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students often quote **NFPA 80** figures when the question specifies an **IS code** (or vice-versa). Always match the code to the question's country context.

## 2.4 Operating Principle: Why Seals Activate in Fire

The sealing mechanism follows a **thermo-mechanical activation sequence**:

1. **Stage 1 — Ambient (T < 100°C):** Seal sits inert. Door operates normally.
2. **Stage 2 — Pre-activation (100°C–200°C):** Adhesive begins to soften. Smoke seal (if separate) actively blocks cold smoke migration.
3. **Stage 3 — Activation (200°C–300°C):** Intumescent material expands. Gap is progressively filled.
4. **Stage 4 — Full Sealing (>300°C):** Gap is fully occluded. Door leaf begins to char/warp; seal compensates for distortion.
5. **Stage 5 — Failure (End of FRR):** Intumescent degrades; insulation criteria fail.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Smoke Leakage Flow Rate** (Orifice Model) | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | m³/s | Flow through a gap |
| 2 | **Equivalent Leakage Area** | $A = \dfrac{Q}{C_d} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{2 \Delta P}}$ | m² | Reverse calculation |
| 3 | **Leakage per Linear Metre** | $L = \dfrac{Q}{W}$ | m³/hr per m | Code compliance test |
| 4 | **Pressure Drop Across Door** | $\Delta P = P_{fire} - P_{corr}$ | Pa | Stack effect driving force |
| 5 | **Neutral Plane Height** | $h_n = \dfrac{h}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{T_{amb}}{T_{fire}}\right)$ | m | Determines smoke direction |
| 6 | **Intumescent Expansion Ratio** | $ER = \dfrac{V_{expanded}}{V_{original}}$ | dimensionless | Material selection |
| 7 | **Gap Closure Ratio** | $G_c = \dfrac{d_{gap} \cdot ER}{d_{seal\ width}}$ | dimensionless | Design check (must ≥ 1) |
| 8 | **Stack Pressure** | $\Delta P_{stack} = K \cdot h \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{amb}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{fire}}\right)$ | Pa | Drives smoke migration |
| 9 | **Critical Gap for Smoke Spread** | $d_{crit} = 0.01 + 0.0002 \cdot \Delta P$ | m | Empirical (Togawa) |
| 10 | **Discharge Coefficient (gap)** | $C_d \approx 0.6$ to $0.65$ | — | For thin gap orifices |

> Where:
> - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate
> - $C_d$ = discharge coefficient
> - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the gap
> - $\Delta P$ = pressure differential across door
> - $\rho$ = density of smoke/air (typically **1.2 kg/m³**)
> - $h_n$ = neutral plane height
> - $T_{amb}$, $T_{fire}$ = absolute temperatures (K)
> - $K$ = constant **3460 Pa·K/m**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Air-tight door sealing is critical in:
- **Hospital OT complexes** (sterile environment + fire safety).
- **High-rise residential buildings** (stack effect amplifies smoke spread).
- **Server rooms / data centres** (NFPA 75 clean-agent system integrity).
- **Atrium / mall egress paths** (large volume smoke management).
- **Subway / metro tunnels** (smoke control pressurization).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Smoke Leakage Through a Door Gap (Orifice Flow Model)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
> A fire door has a uniform gap of **3 mm width × 2 m height** around its perimeter (head + two jambs). The fire side is at a pressure of **+20 Pa** relative to the corridor side (due to stack effect). Calculate the **volumetric leakage rate** of hot smoke through the gap at the start of a fire when smoke temperature is approximately **50°C (323 K)**.

### 3.1.2 Given Data
- Gap width: $d = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$
- Door perimeter (head + jambs): $W = 2 \text{ m} \times 2 + 0.9 \text{ m} \approx 4.9 \text{ m}$ (assume door height = 2 m, width = 0.9 m)
- Pressure differential: $\Delta P = 20 \text{ Pa}$
- Smoke temperature: $T = 50°\text{C} = 323 \text{ K}$
- Discharge coefficient for thin gap: $C_d = 0.62$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the gap:**

$$A = d \times W = 0.003 \text{ m} \times 4.9 \text{ m} = 0.0147 \text{ m}^2$$

*Logic:* The total leakage area is the product of the gap thickness and the total length of the perimeter gap.

**Step 2 — Density of smoke at 50°C:**

Using ideal gas relation: $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R \cdot T}$

$$P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}, \quad R_{air} = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$$

$$\rho = \dfrac{101325}{287 \times 323} = \dfrac{101325}{92701} \approx 1.093 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

*Logic:* Smoke (treated as hot air) becomes less dense as temperature rises, increasing volumetric flow rate for the same mass flux.

**Step 3 — Apply the orifice flow equation:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$
