**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.0147 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 20}{1.093}}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.0147 \times \sqrt{36.596}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.0147 \times 6.049$$

$$Q \approx 5.51 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to m³/hr:**

$$Q = 0.0551 \times 3600 \approx 198.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 6 — Check against code limit:**

The leakage per linear metre is:

$$L = \dfrac{198.4}{4.9} \approx 40.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr per m}$$

**Code requirement (EN 1634-3 / UL 1784):** $L \le 25 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr per m}$ at 25 Pa for smoke-rated doors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Result:** The unsealed gap **FAILS** the smoke leakage test by approximately **62%**! This is the engineering justification for mandatory **intumescent + cold smoke seals** on every fire door in a KTU-referenced building design.

## 3.2 Derivation: Neutral Plane Height (Stack Effect)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
> A 30 m tall stairwell experiences a fire on the 5th floor. Ambient temperature = **30°C (303 K)**. Fire compartment temperature = **600°C (873 K)**. Find the height of the **neutral plane** (above the fire floor) where smoke neither rises nor falls.

### 3.2.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Total height of the building from the fire floor:**

Assuming the fire floor is at mid-height (hypothetically), we use the simplified model:

$$h = 15 \text{ m} \quad \text{(height of building above fire floor)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the neutral plane formula:**

$$h_n = \dfrac{h}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{T_{amb}}{T_{fire}}\right)$$

$$h_n = \dfrac{15}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{303}{873}\right)$$

$$h_n = 7.5 \times (1 + 0.347)$$

$$h_n = 7.5 \times 1.347 = 10.10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- **Below 10.1 m** above fire floor: **cold air flows INTO** the fire compartment.
- **Above 10.1 m** above fire floor: **hot smoke flows OUT** into the corridor/upper floors.

*Logic:* The neutral plane is the pressure equilibrium line. Smoke, being buoyant, flows upward and out through *any gap* in the upper portion of a fire door. **This is why head and jamb seals on the upper portion are most critical.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Seal Selection Decision Logic

| Building Element | Required Seal Type | Code Reference | Gap Tolerance | Test Pressure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lift Lobby Fire Door (2-hr)** | Combined intumescent + silicone | IS 3614 Part 1 | 3 mm | 8.5 Pa |
| **Staircase Fire Door (1-hr)** | Intumescent only | IS 3614 Part 1 | 3 mm | 8.5 Pa |
| **Plant Room Door (4-hr)** | Double intumescent strip | IS 3614 Part 1 | 2 mm | 20 Pa |
| **Server Room Door** | Combined + drop-down | NFPA 75 | 3 mm | 12.5 Pa |
| **Hospital Corridor (Smoke Sa)** | Silicone smoke seal only | EN 1634-3 | 3 mm | 25 Pa |
| **Atrium Egress Door (S200)** | Combined + drop-down | EN 1634-3 | 2 mm | 50 Pa |

## 3.4 Installation Derivation: Required Intumescent Width

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement
> A fire door has a 3 mm perimeter gap. The intumescent seal expands 8x its original thickness at 300°C. What **minimum original thickness** of intumescent strip is required to fully seal the gap?

### 3.4.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Gap closure requirement:**

For complete sealing, the *expanded* thickness of the seal must equal or exceed the gap width:

$$t_{expanded} \ge d_{gap}$$

**Step 2 — Express expanded thickness in terms of original thickness:**

$$t_{expanded} = ER \cdot t_{original}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for minimum original thickness:**

$$t_{original} = \dfrac{d_{gap}}{ER} = \dfrac{3 \text{ mm}}{8} = 0.375 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply design safety factor of 1.5 (recommended for KTU exam answers):**

$$t_{design} = 1.5 \times 0.375 = 0.563 \text{ mm} \approx 0.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Select standard commercial size:** A **1 mm** intumescent strip is typically specified (giving a safety factor of ~2.67).

*Logic:* Designers must always account for **partial loss of expansion** due to charring of the carrier, paint buildup on the seal, and uneven gap geometry around the perimeter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of a Sealed Fire Door Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Event Initiates] --> B[Smoke and Hot Gases Rise]
    B --> C[Fire Compartment Pressure Rises]
    C --> D{Door Leaf and Frame Intact?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Gap Geometry at Perimeter]
    D -- No --> Z[FAILURE: Integrity Lost]
    E --> F[3mm Head Gap]
    E --> G[3mm Jamb Gaps Left and Right]
    E --> H[10mm Threshold Gap]
    F --> I[Intumescent Strip Activates above 200C]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Drop Down Seal Engages at 60C]
    I --> K[Graphite Expands 5x to 10x Volume]
    K --> L[Gap Fully Occluded]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Smoke and Flame Blocked]
    M --> N[Corridor and Stairwell Remain Tenable]
    N --> O[Safe Occupant Egress Enabled]
    O --> P[FRR Maintained for Full Duration]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Seal Type Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Door Application] --> B{Door FRR Rating?}
    B -- 2 Hours or More --> C[Double Intumescent Strip Required]
    B -- 1 Hour --> D[Single Intumescent Strip]
    B -- 30 Minutes --> E[Intumescent Optional]
    A --> F{Smoke Control Required?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Add Silicone Cold Smoke Seal]
    F -- No --> H[Intumescent Only]
    G --> I[Select Combined Strip Assembly]
    H --> I
    C --> I
    D --> I
    I --> J[Verify Code Compliance IS 3614 or NFPA 80]
    J --> K[Conduct Field Inspection Every 6 Months]
    K --> L[Replace Damaged Seals Immediately]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Smoke Flow Path Through an Unsealed Door

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Compartment] --> B[Hot Smoke Layer at Ceiling]
    B --> C[Reaches Unsealed Door Head]
    C --> D[3mm Gap Allows Smoke Egress]
    D --> E[Smoke Spreads into Corridor]
    E --> F[Stack Effect Pushes Smoke Upward]
    F --> G[Reaches Adjacent Compartment]
    G --> H[Tenability Conditions Violated]
    H --> I[Visibility below 5m]
    H --> J[CO above 1500 ppm]
    H --> K[Temperature above 65C]
    I --> L[Occupant Disorientation]
    J --> M[Toxic Exposure]
    K --> N[Respiratory Tract Damage]
    L --> O[EGRESS FAILURE]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Potential Loss of Life]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Sealing Zones on a Fire Door

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DoorAssembly[Fire Door Assembly Front View]
        A1[Top Head Zone - Intumescent Strip 1] --> A2[Left Jamb Zone - Intumescent Strip 2]
        A2 --> A3[Bottom Threshold - Drop Down Seal]
        A3 --> A4[Right Jamb Zone - Intumescent Strip 3]
        A4 --> A1
    end
    subgraph GlazingZone[Glazing Perimeter]
        B1[Fire Rated Glass Panel] --> B2[Glazing Bead with Intumescent Tape]
        B2 --> B3[Silicone Weather Seal]
    end
    subgraph HardwareZone[Hardware Interfaces]
        C1[Intumescent Pad Behind Hinges] --> C2[Intumescent Pad Behind Locks]
        C2 --> C3[Intumescent Pad Behind Closer]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Notes:**
> - The **head** and **jambs** carry continuous intumescent strips.
> - The **threshold** is sealed with a mechanical drop-down device (often spring-loaded, gravity-activated, or fusible-link-activated).
> - **Hardware backplates** (hinges, locks, closers) must have intumescent pads — heat can conduct through metal and ignite the unexposed face.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: Define the term "intumescent seal" as used in fire door assemblies. State any two of its key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **intumescent seal** is a passive fire protection material, typically graphite- or sodium silicate-based, that is installed in the perimeter of a fire door leaf or frame. When exposed to fire temperatures (typically **180°C–220°C**), the material expands volumetrically (commonly **5x to 10x** its original volume) to seal the gaps between the door leaf and frame, thereby preventing the passage of flames, hot gases, and smoke.

**Key Properties (any two):**
1. **Thermal activation:** Inert below ~180°C; activates only in fire conditions.
2. **High expansion ratio:** 5:1 to 10:1 volumetric expansion.
3. **Self-adhesive backing:** Easy retrofit on-site.
4. **Compatibility:** Available in different profiles (rectangular, L-shape, triangular) for varied gap geometries.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**Q: Differentiate between a "cold smoke seal" and an "intumescent seal" in fire door design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Cold Smoke Seal | Intumescent Seal |
|---|---|---|
| **Activation** | Functional at ambient temperature | Activates at >180°C |
| **Material** | Silicone, EPDM rubber, nylon brush | Graphite, sodium silicate |
| **Function** | Blocks cold smoke (< 50°C) | Blocks flames and hot gases |
| **Standards** | EN 1634-3 (Sa / S200 rating) | IS 3614 Part 1, BS 476 Part 22 |
| **Use Case** | Hospital, hotel corridors; data centres | All FRR-rated fire doors |

A **combined seal** integrates both functions into a single assembly for complete life-safety performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the **types of air-tight sealing systems** used in fire door assemblies, with reference to relevant Indian Standards. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A fire door has a perimeter gap of **4 mm** and a height of **2.1 m** with width **0.9 m**. The pressure difference across the door during a fire is **25 Pa**. Smoke temperature is **60°C**. Calculate the smoke leakage rate in **m³/hr** and verify compliance with **EN 1634-3** (limit: 25 m³/hr per linear metre at 25 Pa). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Types of Air-tight Sealing Systems (7 marks):**

1. **Intumescent Seals (2 marks):**
   - Graphite-based and sodium silicate-based.
   - Activate at 180°C–220°C; expand 5–10 times.
   - Mounted in door edge or frame reveal.
   - Compliant with IS 3614 Part 1 and BS 476 Part 22.

2. **Cold Smoke Seals (2 marks):**
   - Silicone, EPDM, or brush pile seals.
   - Function at ambient temperature.
   - Test standard: EN 1634-3 (Sa / S200 classifications).
   - Used in life-safety egress paths.

3. **Combined Seals (1 mark):**
   - Hybrid seals combining both functions in a single strip.
   - Most common in modern installations.

4. **Mechanical / Drop-down Threshold Seals (2 marks):**
   - Automatic drop-down devices activated by gravity, spring, or fusible link.
   - Seal the threshold gap (typically ≤ 10 mm).
   - Critical for smoke-rated assemblies.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Gap thickness: $d = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$
- Door height: $H = 2.1 \text{ m}$
- Door width: $W_d = 0.9 \text{ m}$
- $\Delta P = 25 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_{smoke} = 60°C = 333 \text{ K}$
- $C_d = 0.62$
- Code limit: $L_{lim} = 25 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr per m}$ at 25 Pa

**Step 1 — Perimeter of the door (head + two jambs):**

$$P = W_d + 2H = 0.9 + 2(2.1) = 5.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Leakage area:**

$$A = d \times P = 0.004 \times 5.1 = 0.0204 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Smoke density at 60°C:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{101325}{287 \times 333} = \dfrac{101325}{95571} \approx 1.060 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 0.0204 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 25}{1.060}}$$

$$Q = 0.01265 \times \sqrt{47.17}$$

$$Q = 0.01265 \times 6.869 \approx 0.0869 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to m³/hr:**

$$Q = 0.0869 \times 3600 = 312.84 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 6 — Leakage per linear metre:**

$$L = \dfrac{312.84}{5.1} = 61.34 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr per m}$$

**Step 7 — Compliance check:**

$$L = 61.34 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr per m} \;\;\gg\;\; L_{lim} = 25 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr per m}$$

**Result:** The unsealed door **FAILS** the EN 1634-3 test by approximately **145%**. Air-tight sealing (intumescent + smoke seal) is **mandatory**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **function of a drop-down seal** at the threshold of a fire door. Discuss the **types of activation mechanisms** used in such seals. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **components of a fire door assembly** and explain how each contributes to **air-tightness**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Drop-down Seal Function and Activation (7 marks):**

A **drop-down seal** (also called an automatic threshold seal) is a mechanical device installed at the bottom of a fire-rated door leaf. It is **raised** when the door is open and **drops automatically** to seal the gap between the door bottom and the floor when the door is closed.

**Functions (3 marks):**
1. Seals the **largest natural gap** in a door assembly (typically 6–10 mm).
2. Blocks **cold smoke infiltration** during the early fire phase.
3. Provides **acoustic and light-leak sealing** in non-fire conditions.

**Activation Mechanisms (4 marks):**
1. **Gravity-Operated:** A weighted cam drops as the door closes (simplest, least reliable).
2. **Spring-Operated:** A spring-loaded plunger is released when the door contacts the frame (most common).
3. **Fusible-Link Activated:** A heat-sensitive link (melting at 68°C–74°C) releases the seal — common in **fire-only** doors where constant floor contact is undesirable.
4. **Push-Down Manual:** A plunger is depressed against the floor as the door shuts.

---

**(b) Components of a Fire Door Assembly (7 marks):**

A fire door assembly consists of the following components (each contributing to air-tightness):

| # | Component | Contribution to Air-tightness |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Door Leaf** | Core of FRR-rated material (timber/steel/composite) |
| 2 | **Door Frame** | Anchored to wall; provides mounting surface for seals |
| 3 | **Intumescent Seals** | Expand in fire to close gaps |
| 4 | **Cold Smoke Seals** | Block smoke at ambient temperature |
| 5 | **Threshold / Drop-down Seal** | Seals bottom gap |
| 6 | **Hinges with Intumescent Pads** | Block heat conduction through metal |
| 7 | **Lock & Latch Set with Intumescent Pad** | Block heat conduction at hardware points |
| 8 | **Door Closer (Concealed with Pad)** | Maintains positive closure; protects hardware |
| 9 | **Fire-rated Glazing (if any)** | With perimeter intumescent tape |
| 10 | **Glazing Bead** | Mechanically retains glass; sealed with intumescent |

**Sketch (3 marks):** Refer to the **Mermaid Block 4.4** in Section 4 for a graphical layout.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Mixing code references:** Quoting NFPA 80 in an IS-code-based question (or vice-versa) loses **1–2 marks** for not reading the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C_d$:** In numerical problems, students often write $Q = A \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ and forget to multiply by $C_d \approx 0.6$ — a **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert mm to m, Pa to consistent units, and s to hr in leakage rate problems. Mixing units is a **sure 1-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Missing the "per linear metre" compliance criterion:** The EN 1634-3 limit is **per linear metre**, not total. Forgetting to divide by perimeter length is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Neglecting hardware protection:** Students often discuss only perimeter seals and ignore that **metal hinges and locks conduct heat**. This loses **2 marks** in descriptive questions.
> 6. **Treating intumescent and smoke seals as the same:** These are functionally distinct devices. Conflating them loses **1–2 marks**.
> 7. **Ignoring temperature in leakage calculations:** Smoke density depends on temperature. Assuming $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for hot smoke is incorrect and loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air-tight Sealing of Doors:**

- ☐ An air-tight fire door seal is a **passive fire protection** device that prevents passage of **flames, hot gases, and smoke** through door perimeter gaps.
- ☐ The three sealing zones are: **Perimeter (head/jambs/Meeting Stiles), Threshold, and Glazing**.
- ☐ **Intumescent seals** activate at **180°C–220°C** and expand **5x to 10x** volumetrically.
- ☐ **Cold smoke seals** are functional at **ambient temperature** and protect against **cold smoke** (the most lethal phase).
- ☐ **Combined seals** integrate both functions in one strip and are the modern standard.
- ☐ **Permitted gap at head/jambs:** 3 mm (IS 3614) or 3.175 mm (NFPA 80).
- ☐ **Permitted gap at threshold:** 10 mm (IS) or 6.35 mm (NFPA) with a drop-down seal.
- ☐ **Test pressure** for smoke doors: 8.5 Pa (Sa) to 50 Pa (S200) per EN 1634-3.
- ☐ **Leakage rate limit:** ≤ 25 m³/hr per linear metre at 25 Pa (UL 1784 / EN 1634-3).
- ☐ **Hardware (hinges, locks, closers) MUST have intumescent pads** to prevent heat conduction.
- ☐ **Discharge coefficient for gap orifice:** $C_d \approx 0.6$ to $0.65$.
- ☐ **Smoke density** at fire temperatures must be calculated using $\rho = P / RT$ — never assume $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- ☐ **Stack effect** drives smoke upward through the upper portion of door gaps — neutral plane height is given by $h_n = (h/2)(1 + T_{amb}/T_{fire})$.
- ☐ **Required intumescent thickness** is at least $d_{gap} / ER$ with a safety factor of **1.5–2.5**.
- ☐ **Indian Standards:** IS 3614 (Parts 1 & 2), NBC 2016 (Part 4), IS 1626 (fire-resistant glass).
- ☐ **International Standards:** BS 476 Parts 20–22, EN 1634-1/3, NFPA 80, UL 1784.
- ☐ **Inspection:** Seals must be inspected **every 6 months**; damaged seals must be replaced **immediately** — paint, damage, or missing strips void the door's FRR.
- ☐ **Three Pillars of Compartmentation:** Fire walls, fire doors, **air-tight seals** — failure of any one compromises the entire system.

> **End of Module 2 — Air-tight Sealing of Doors Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification, Test and Performance Criteria of Plate, Metal Covered and Rolling Type Fire Doors as per BIS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specification, Test and Performance Criteria of Plate, Metal Covered and Rolling Type Fire Doors as per BIS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

As per the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, a *Fire Door* is a **self-closing** or **automatic closing** door assembly, including the frame, hardware, and accessories, which when closed is intended to **restrict the passage of fire and smoke** through a fire-resistant opening in a wall, floor, or other fire separation. The performance, specification, and test criteria for these doors in India are primarily governed by the following Indian Standard (IS) codes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key BIS Codes Governing Fire Doors in India**
> - **IS 3614 (Part 1) : 1966** – Specification for Metal Covered and Rolling Type Fire Doors
> - **IS 3614 (Part 2) : 1970** – Specification for Plate Type Fire Doors (reaffirmed 2010)
> - **IS 3614 (Part 3) : 1971** – Specification for Sliding Type Fire Doors
> - **IS 3614 (Part 4) : 1977** – Specification for Fire Check Doors
> - **IS 3614 (Part 5) : 1989** – Methods of Test for Fire Resistance of Fire Doors
>
> *Note: For thermal/severity references, BIS aligns with **IS 3809** (Fire Resistance Test for Structures) and **IS 14609** for general test methodology.*

The Indian Standard classifies fire doors primarily into **three major functional categories** for this module:

1. **Plate Type Fire Door** – A door constructed of pressed/formed steel plates.
2. **Metal Covered (or Metal Clad) Fire Door** – A door having a wooden core encased in sheet metal.
3. **Rolling Type Fire Door** – A door formed of interlocking metal slats that coil on a shaft.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body’s immune system. When a virus enters, the body raises a barrier (fever, inflammation) to **contain** the infection. A fire door is the **immune system of a building**. During a fire, the wall is the healthy tissue, and the door opening is a wound. The fire door is the **bandage that automatically closes** to prevent fire (the infection) from spreading to other parts of the building (organs).

- **Plate Door** → Like a solid steel shield, very robust, used in industrial settings.
- **Metal-Covered Door** → A wooden door wearing a metal armor suit, common in offices and commercial spaces.
- **Rolling Door** → A curtain of metal slats that drops down like a cinema screen when fire is detected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** In a KTU-aligned context, students should remember that a fire door is rated not by the *material* alone, but by the **time (in minutes/hours)** it can withstand a standard fire. The standard fire rating classifications as per **IS 3614 / IS 3809** typically include:
> - 2 hours (120 min)
> - 1.5 hours (90 min)
> - 1 hour (60 min)
> - 0.5 hour (30 min)

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Fire Exposure Temperature** (per IS 3809 / ISO 834):
  - Initial ambient: **20 °C**
  - Standard time-temperature curve: $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$
- **Standard Fire Rating Intervals:** 30, 60, 90, 120 minutes (and up to 240 min for special applications).
- **Fusible Link Melting Temperature:** Standard rating of **68 °C** (some applications use 57 °C, 79 °C, 100 °C).
- **Hose Stream Test Pressure:** Typically **0.2 MPa to 0.3 MPa** depending on rating (BIS/NBC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The three performance criteria evaluated during a fire test on these doors are universally known as the three pillars of fire endurance:
> **STABILITY + INTEGRITY + INSULATION** (often remembered as **S-I-I**).
> A fire door must satisfy all three criteria for the duration of its rated period.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Fire Doors as per IS 3614

| Type of Door | IS Code | Core Material | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plate Type** | IS 3614 (Part 2) | Pressed/formed steel plates | Industrial, plant rooms, transformer rooms |
| **Metal Covered** | IS 3614 (Part 1) | Wooden core + Sheet metal | Office buildings, commercial complexes, hospitals |
| **Rolling Type** | IS 3614 (Part 1) | Interlocking metal slats (steel) | Factories, warehouses, large openings |

---

### 2.2 Plate Type Fire Doors (IS 3614 Part 2)

#### 2.2.1 Construction Specifications
- **Door Shutter:** Manufactured from **mild steel plates of thickness not less than 1.5 mm**, formed to provide a hollow core.
- **Reinforcement:** Internal stiffeners/ribs of mild steel angle, channel, or T-section.
- **Infill:** The hollow core may be filled with **non-combustible insulating material** (e.g., mineral wool, calcium silicate, vermiculite) to improve insulation.
- **Frame:** Made of pressed steel of minimum **1.6 mm** thickness or mild steel angle section of appropriate size.
- **Hardware:** Heavy-duty ball-bearing hinges, automatic closing devices (such as a **hydraulic door closer** or a **fusible-link-spring mechanism**).

#### 2.2.2 Operational Logic (Why and How)
- The plate door relies on **structural mass and rigidity** to maintain stability.
- The infill material **decelerates heat conduction**, satisfying the **insulation** criterion.
- A **fusible link** holds the door open during normal use; when ambient temperature exceeds the link rating (e.g., **68 °C**), the link melts, and a counterweight/spring automatically closes the door.

---

### 2.3 Metal Covered Fire Doors (IS 3614 Part 1)

#### 2.3.1 Construction Specifications
- **Core Material:** Seasoned hardwood (e.g., teak, sal) with a minimum moisture content as specified by BIS, formed in panels with rebates.
- **Metal Covering:** The core is fully encased in **22 SWG (≈ 0.71 mm) galvanized mild steel sheets** or **20 SWG (≈ 0.91 mm)** depending on the rating.
- **Seams:** Seams are folded, locked, or welded and made tight by inserting a continuous felt strip or refractory rope.
- **Frame:** Angle-iron frame of appropriate size with anchors.
- **Edge Strips:** Sheet metal edges are bent over and secured.

#### 2.3.2 Operational Logic
- The wood core provides **good thermal insulation** and is protected by metal from direct flame impingement.
- This door balances **insulation (wood)** and **integrity (metal cladding)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Metal covered doors are lighter than plate doors but provide comparable fire resistance due to the wood's inherent char layer that insulates the unexposed face.

---

### 2.4 Rolling Type Fire Doors (IS 3614 Part 1)

#### 2.4.1 Construction Specifications
- **Curtain Slats:** Made of **galvanized steel or mild steel of minimum 0.6 mm to 1.0 mm thickness**, formed into interlocking slats.
- **Guides:** Steel channels fitted on either side, into which the slats travel.
- **Barrel/Shaft:** A horizontal pipe/shaft at the top on which the slats coil.
- **Operating Mechanism:** Manually operated by a chain (fail-safe) or automatically via a **fusible link** that releases the door so that gravity allows it to descend along the guides.
- **Bottom Bar:** A weighted angle or channel to ensure smooth descent.

#### 2.4.2 Operational Logic
- Rolling doors protect **large openings** (e.g., warehouse loading bays) where hinged doors are impractical.
- The **fusible link** is critical—on melting, the holding magnet or latch releases, and the counter-weighted barrel rotates to allow the door to drop.

---

### 2.5 Test Procedure as per BIS (IS 3614 Part 5 / IS 3809)

The standard fire test follows the **time-temperature curve** and assesses the three performance criteria:

1. **Stability** – The door must not collapse, warp, or fail structurally.
2. **Integrity** – The door must not develop cracks, openings, or sustained flaming on the unexposed face (verified by a **cotton pad test**).
3. **Insulation** – The average temperature rise on the unexposed face must not exceed **140 °C** above initial ambient, and no point temperature rise must exceed **180 °C**.

#### 2.5.1 The Standard Time–Temperature Equation
The **Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834 / IS 3809)** is given by:

$$T_t = 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$$

Where:
- $T_t$ = Furnace temperature at time $t$ (in °C)
- $t$ = Time elapsed since ignition (in minutes)
- **345** and **20** are empirical constants (°C)
- The factor **8** is a time scaling constant (minutes⁻¹)

**Sample temperature values at key intervals:**

| Time $t$ (min) | $\log_{10}(8t+1)$ | $T_t$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 1.63 | ~583 |
| 15 | 2.11 | ~749 |
| 30 | 2.43 | ~860 |
| 60 | 2.72 | ~950 |
| 90 | 2.89 | ~1018 |
| 120 | 3.01 | ~1058 |
| 240 | 3.32 | ~1166 |

#### 2.5.2 Hose Stream Test (Impact and Cooling Resistance)
After the fire endurance test, the door is subjected to a **hose stream test**:
- A fire hose delivering water at a specified pressure is applied to the heated face of the door.
- **Pressure rule of thumb (NFPA 252 / IS aligned):**
  - 30 min rating → ~0.21 MPa
  - 60 min rating → ~0.21 MPa
  - 90 min rating → ~0.21 MPa
  - 120 min rating → ~0.31 MPa

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hose stream test** simulates the effect of firefighting operations. The door must remain intact and not develop openings that would permit passage of flame/hot gases.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula or Value | Units / Standard | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard fire temperature | $T_t = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ | °C, $t$ in min | IS 3809 / ISO 834 |
| Insulation failure (average) | $\Delta T_{avg} \le 140$ | °C above initial | Un-exposed face |
| Insulation failure (point) | $\Delta T_{max} \le 180$ | °C above initial | Un-exposed face |
| Fusible link rating | 68 (typical) | °C | Hold-open release |
| Plate door sheet thickness | $\ge 1.5$ | mm | Mild steel |
| Frame thickness | $\ge 1.6$ | mm | Pressed steel |
| Metal cover sheet | 22 SWG ($\approx 0.71$) | mm / SWG | Galvanized MS |
| Slat thickness (rolling) | $0.6 - 1.0$ | mm | Galvanized steel |
| Hose stream pressure | $0.21 - 0.31$ | MPa | Depends on rating |
| Fire rating classes | 30 / 60 / 90 / 120 / 240 | minutes | IS 3614 |
| Standard rating (NBC 2016) | $\ge 120$ | minutes | Fire exits/ staircases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These ratings and formulas are used by:
> - **Fire Protection Engineers** for selecting door ratings based on compartmentation design.
> - **NBC 2016** (National Building Code of India) for compliance verification.
> - **Certifying agencies** (e.g., CBRI Roorkee, FCRI) for BIS certification testing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Standard Time–Temperature Fire Curve

The standard fire exposure curve was empirically derived from burning tests of wood cribs. The equation models the **logarithmic rise** in temperature of a fully developed compartment fire under standard ventilation.

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Empirical Formulation:**
Researchers observed that the temperature in a fire test furnace follows a **semi-logarithmic growth pattern**, which led to the general form:

$$T_t = A \cdot \log_{10}(B t + 1) + T_0$$

**Step 2 — Determination of Constants using Boundary Conditions:**
- At $t = 0$: $T = T_0 = 20$ °C (ambient)
- At $t = 30$ min: $T \approx 860$ °C
- At $t = 60$ min: $T \approx 950$ °C
- At $t = 120$ min: $T \approx 1050$ °C

**Step 3 — Solve for $A$ and $B$:**

Substituting boundary conditions:

$$860 = A \log_{10}(30B + 1) + 20$$
$$950 = A \log_{10}(60B + 1) + 20$$

Subtracting:

$$\begin{aligned}
950 - 860 &= A \left[ \log_{10}(60B + 1) - \log_{10}(30B + 1) \right] \\[4pt]
90 &= A \cdot \log_{10}\left( \frac{60B + 1}{30B + 1} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Using IS/ISO Standard Values:**
The internationally accepted constants are:

$$A = 345 \, °C, \quad B = 8 \, \text{min}^{-1}, \quad T_0 = 20 \, °C$$

Thus, the **Standard Time-Temperature Curve** is:

$$\boxed{T_t = 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20}$$

**Step 5 — Verification with sample point ($t = 60$ min):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{60} &= 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \times 60 + 1) + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 345 \cdot \log_{10}(481) + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 345 \times 2.6821 + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 925.32 + 20 \\[4pt]
&\approx 945.32 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the standard reference value ($\approx 950$ °C) for the 60-minute mark. **Verified.** ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Insulation Failure Threshold (Quantitative Limit)

The insulation criterion is the most quantitative of the three performance criteria. Let us derive the unexposed-face temperature profile using a simplified **lumped heat transfer model**.

#### 3.2.1 Lumped Capacitance Assumption
Assume the door thickness $L$ is small compared to the heat-diffusion length, so we treat the door as a 1-D slab with uniform temperature.

**Governing Equation (Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction):**

$$q'' = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

For steady state across a door of thickness $L$:

$$q'' = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{L}$$

Where:
- $q''$ = Heat flux (W/m²)
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference across the door

**Step 1 — Apply Insulation Failure Limit:**
As per IS 3614, the unexposed-face average temperature rise is restricted to:

$$\Delta T_{avg, \text{limit}} = 140 \, °C = 140 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Time-Dependent Furnace Temperature:**

$$\Delta T(t) = T_{\text{furnace}}(t) - T_{\text{unexposed}}(t) \le 140$$

**Step 3 — Convective Heat Transfer to the Unexposed Face:**
On the unexposed face, heat is removed by natural convection:

$$q'' = h_c \cdot (T_{\text{unexposed}} - T_{\text{ambient}})$$

**Step 4 — Combining:**

For a typical plate door (steel + infill, $L \approx 50$ mm, $k \approx 0.1$ W/m·K for the infill):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{\text{door}} &= \frac{q'' \cdot L}{k} \\[4pt]
&\le 140 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

This 140 °C limit is **empirically derived from human safety**: it represents the temperature above which a person touching the door would sustain a burn injury, and above which adjacent combustible materials may ignite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The 140 °C average / 180 °C point limit is **not arbitrary**—it corresponds to:
> 1. Human burn injury threshold (≈ 60 °C surface for >5 seconds).
> 2. Spontaneous ignition of common combustibles (paper, cloth).
> 3. Radiant heat flux onto adjacent surfaces (≈ 12.5 kW/m²).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison

| Parameter | Plate Type | Metal Covered | Rolling Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IS Code** | 3614 Pt. 2 | 3614 Pt. 1 | 3614 Pt. 1 |
| **Core** | Steel + infill | Wood + metal | Steel slats (no infill) |
| **Stability rating** | Excellent | Very Good | Good (large openings) |
| **Insulation** | Excellent (with infill) | Very Good | Moderate (slat gaps) |
| **Integrity** | Excellent | Good | Very Good (overlapping) |
| **Typical rating (min)** | 60–120 | 60–120 | 60–120 |
| **Common application** | Transformer rooms, plant rooms | Office exits, hotels | Factories, warehouses |
| **Self-closing device** | Spring/closer + fusible link | Spring/closer + fusible link | Counter-weight + fusible link |
| **Major weakness** | Heat conduction (no infill variant) | Wood char loss over time | Gap leakage at slat edges |
| **Test** | Fire + Hose stream | Fire + Hose stream | Fire + Hose stream (where applicable) |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example

**Problem:** A metal-covered fire door of **22 SWG** steel sheets with a 40 mm hardwood core is subjected to the standard fire test. Calculate the **furnace temperature at $t = 45$ minutes**, and determine whether the door is **likely to fail insulation** if the unexposed face temperature has reached **155 °C** (initial ambient = 27 °C).

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Furnace Temperature at $t = 45$ min:**

$$T_{45} = 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 45 + 1) + 20$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{45} &= 345 \log_{10}(361) + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 345 \times 2.5575 + 20 \\[4pt]
&= 882.34 + 20 \\[4pt]
&\approx 902.34 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature Rise on Unexposed Face:**

$$\Delta T = 155 - 27 = 128 \, °C$$

**Step 3 — Compare with BIS Limit:**

$$\Delta T_{avg, \text{limit}} = 140 \, °C$$

$$128 \, °C \le 140 \, °C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PASS (insulation)}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** At $t = 45$ min, the furnace temperature is **902 °C** and the unexposed face temperature rise is **128 °C**, which is **within the 140 °C BIS limit**. The door's insulation criterion is **satisfied** at the 45-minute mark.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fire Door Test & Evaluation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Door Specimen Mounted in Test Furnace Wall] --> B[Apply Standard Time-Temperature Curve IS 3809]
    B --> C[Monitor Three Performance Criteria Continuously]
    C --> D[Criterion 1: Stability Structural Integrity]
    C --> E[Criterion 2: Integrity Cotton Pad and Crack Test]
    C --> F[Criterion 3: Insulation Temperature Rise Measurement]
    D --> G{All Three Criteria Pass for Rated Duration}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Hose Stream Test Impact and Cooling]
    H --> I{Door Remains Intact with No Openings}
    I --> J[Final Result: PASS - Certified with Rating in Minutes]
    I --> K[Final Result: FAIL - Door Rejected and Re-engineered]
    J --> L[Issue BIS Test Certificate IS 3614 Part 5]
    K --> M[Redesign and Re-test]
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Fire Doors as per BIS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Fire Doors IS 3614] --> P1[Plate Type Pt 2]
    Root --> P2[Metal Covered Pt 1]
    Root --> P3[Rolling Type Pt 1]
    Root --> P4[Sliding Type Pt 3]
    Root --> P5[Fire Check Doors Pt 4]
    P1 --> P1D[Steel Plates plus Infill]
    P2 --> P2D[Wooden Core plus Steel Sheeting]
    P3 --> P3D[Interlocking Slats on Barrel]
    P4 --> P4D[Sliding on Track with Fusible Link]
    P5 --> P5D[Light Duty Non Combustible Core]
    P1D --> App1[Industrial Plant Rooms]
    P2D --> App2[Offices Hotels Hospitals]
    P3D --> App3[Factories Warehouses]
    P4D --> App4[Large Openings Fire Compartments]
    P5D --> App5[Residential and Light Commercial]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Fire Door Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Normal Operation Door Held Open] --> FL[Fusible Link Intact at 68 deg C]
    FL --> Fire[Fire Initiated Temperature Rises]
    Fire --> Melt{Fusible Link Temperature Exceeded?}
    Melt -->|No Continue Holding| Fire
    Melt -->|Yes Link Melts| Release[Release Mechanism Activated]
    Release --> Close[Door Closes via Spring or Gravity]
    Close --> Sealed[Door Sealed Against Frame]
    Sealed --> Test[Fire Endurance Test Begins]
    Test --> Outcome{Rated Period S I I Satisfied?}
    Outcome -->|Yes| Pass[Compartment Maintained]
    Outcome -->|No| Fail[Compartment Breach]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Constructional Layout Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlateType[Plate Type Door]
        PT1[Face Plate 1.5mm MS] --> PT2[Stiffener Ribs Angle Channel]
        PT2 --> PT3[Insulation Infill Mineral Wool]
        PT3 --> PT4[Face Plate 1.5mm MS]
        PT4 --> PT5[Frame 1.6mm Pressed Steel]
    end
    subgraph MetalCovered[Metal Covered Door]
        MC1[22 SWG MS Sheet] --> MC2[Hardwood Core 40 to 50mm]
        MC2 --> MC3[22 SWG MS Sheet]
        MC3 --> MC4[Edge Seams Folded or Welded]
    end
    subgraph RollingType[Rolling Type Door]
        RT1[Top Barrel Shaft] --> RT2[Interlocking Slats 0.6 to 1.0mm]
        RT2 --> RT3[Side Guide Channels]
        RT3 --> RT4[Bottom Bar Weighted]
        RT4 --> RT5[Fusible Link Release Mechanism]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a fire door and list any four types of fire doors as per IS 3614.**

**Model Answer:**
A fire door is a self-closing or automatic-closing door assembly, including its frame, hardware, and accessories, designed to restrict the passage of fire and smoke through a fire-resistant opening for a specified rating period.
Four types of fire doors as per **IS 3614** are:
1. Plate Type Fire Door (Part 2)
2. Metal Covered Fire Door (Part 1)
3. Rolling Type Fire Door (Part 1)
4. Sliding Type Fire Door (Part 3)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the three performance criteria of a fire door as per BIS and explain any one in brief.**

**Model Answer:**
The three performance criteria (S–I–I) are:
1. **Stability** – The door must not collapse or distort under fire exposure.
2. **Integrity** – The door must not develop cracks/openings allowing flame/hot gases.
3. **Insulation** – The unexposed face must not exceed an average temperature rise of 140 °C or a maximum point rise of 180 °C.

*Explanation (Integrity):* Integrity is verified using the **cotton pad test**, where a cotton pad is held against the unexposed face. If the pad ignites due to hot gases passing through cracks/openings, the integrity criterion is considered failed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **specification, construction, and application** of **plate type fire doors** as per IS 3614 (Part 2). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **standard fire test procedure** for fire doors as per IS 3614 (Part 5), including the standard time–temperature equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Plate Type Fire Door — Specification & Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Material & Thickness:** Door shutters are made of mild steel plates of minimum **1.5 mm** thickness, formed into hollow panels.
2. **Reinforcement:** Internal stiffeners of mild steel angle, channel, or T-section provide rigidity.
3. **Infill:** Hollow core is filled with non-combustible insulating material (e.g., mineral wool, calcium silicate) for insulation.
4. **Frame:** Pressed steel frame of **1.6 mm** minimum thickness, anchored to the wall opening.
5. **Hardware:** Ball-bearing hinges; automatic closing by **spring mechanism** or **hydraulic door closer**; held open by a **fusible link (68 °C)**.
6. **Application:** Used in industrial plant rooms, transformer rooms, generator rooms, and areas requiring high mechanical strength.
7. **Rating:** Typically 60 to 120 minutes fire resistance.

**(b) Standard Fire Test Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Mounting:** Door is installed in a standard test furnace wall as per IS 3809.
2. **Standard Fire Curve:** The furnace is heated to follow:
   $$T_t = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$$
3. **Three Criteria Monitored Continuously:**
   - **Stability** – no structural collapse.
   - **Integrity** – no flame/hot gases on unexposed face; verified by **cotton pad test**.
   - **Insulation** – unexposed face temperature rise: $\Delta T_{avg} \le 140$ °C; $\Delta T_{max} \le 180$ °C.
4. **Hose Stream Test:** Applied after fire endurance. Pressure: 0.21 MPa (≤90 min rating), 0.31 MPa (>90 min).
5. **Duration:** The test continues until failure of any criterion or completion of the rated period.
6. **Certification:** Passing all three criteria for the rated period results in BIS certification with the corresponding fire rating in minutes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction, operation, and application** of **rolling type fire doors** as per IS 3614. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **three performance criteria** of fire doors with reference to BIS specifications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rolling Type Fire Door (7 marks):**

1. **Curtain Construction:** Door consists of **interlocking galvanized steel slats** of 0.6–1.0 mm thickness, forming a continuous curtain.
2. **Barrel & Shaft:** The slats coil on a horizontal **barrel/shaft** at the top of the opening.
3. **Guides:** Vertical steel channels on both sides guide the slats during descent.
4. **Bottom Bar:** A weighted angle/channel at the bottom ensures smooth closing and sealing.
5. **Operating Mechanism:** Held open by a **fusible link** (68 °C). On melting, the curtain descends by **gravity** or is pulled by a counterweight.
6. **Application:** Suitable for **large openings** in factories, warehouses, aircraft hangars, and loading bays where hinged doors are not practical.
7. **Test & Rating:** Tested as per IS 3614 (Part 5) and IS 3809; ratings typically 60–120 minutes.

**(b) Three Performance Criteria (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Description | BIS Limit | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stability (S)** | Door must not collapse, warp, or dislodge | No quantitative limit; structural observation | Visual + deflection check |
| **Integrity (I)** | No passage of flame/hot gases | No sustained flaming on unexposed face | Cotton pad test; no ignition |
| **Insulation (I)** | Limit heat transmission to unexposed side | $\Delta T_{avg} \le 140$ °C; $\Delta T_{max} \le 180$ °C | Thermocouples on unexposed face |

- All three criteria must be satisfied for the **entire rated period** (e.g., 60, 90, or 120 minutes).
- A door failing even one criterion at any time during the test is deemed to have **failed** at that point, and its fire rating equals the time elapsed to failure.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (causing mark deductions):**
> 1. **Forgetting the three performance criteria (S-I-I):** Examiners specifically look for Stability, Integrity, and Insulation. Writing only "fire resistance" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not mentioning the BIS code numbers:** Always cite **IS 3614** with the correct Part (Pt. 1, Pt. 2, Pt. 3, Pt. 4, Pt. 5). Omitting these costs 0.5–1 mark per question.
> 3. **Confusing the temperature limit:** Students often write "150 °C" instead of **140 °C (average) and 180 °C (point)**. This is a frequently tested value.
> 4. **Ignoring the hose stream test:** It is an integral part of the certification procedure; omitting it in a 14-mark question costs at least 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not drawing/mentioning the fusible link:** A 68 °C fusible link is a hallmark specification; mentioning it is a "key-word bonus" for the examiner.
> 6. **Mixing up door types and their applications:** Plate doors → industrial; Metal-covered → commercial; Rolling → large openings. Mismatches are penalized.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plate, Metal Covered & Rolling Type Fire Doors (BIS)**

**✓ Key BIS Codes:**
- **IS 3614 Part 1** → Metal Covered + Rolling Type
- **IS 3614 Part 2** → Plate Type
- **IS 3614 Part 3** → Sliding Type
- **IS 3614 Part 4** → Fire Check Doors
- **IS 3614 Part 5** → Methods of Test

**✓ The Standard Fire Equation (MUST memorize):**
- $T_t = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$

**✓ The Three Performance Criteria (S-I-I):**
- **S**tability → No structural failure
- **I**ntegrity → No flame/gas passage (cotton pad test)
- **I**nsulation → $\Delta T_{avg} \le 140$ °C; $\Delta T_{max} \le 180$ °C

**✓ Standard Ratings:** 30, 60, 90, 120, 240 minutes (NBC 2016 mandates ≥ 120 min for fire exits).

**✓ Plate Type Door:**
- Steel plates ≥ 1.5 mm; frame ≥ 1.6 mm
- Non-combustible infill
- Industrial use

**✓ Metal Covered Door:**
- Hardwood core (40–50 mm) + 22 SWG metal sheets
- Seams folded/welded with felt/refractory rope
- Office/commercial use

**✓ Rolling Type Door:**
- Interlocking slats (0.6–1.0 mm) on barrel
- Fusible link (68 °C) + gravity closing
- Large openings in factories/warehouses

**✓ Fusible Link:** Melts at ~68 °C; releases the door for automatic closing.

**✓ Hose Stream Test:** 0.21 MPa (≤90 min rating); 0.31 MPa (>90 min rating); simulates firefighting impact.

**✓ Application Mapping (for last-minute recall):**
| Door | Symbol | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Plate | 🏭 | Plant rooms |
| Metal Covered | 🏢 | Offices, hospitals |
| Rolling | 📦 | Warehouses, factories |

> **Best of luck with your KTU ESE preparation — keep the S-I-I mantra and the standard fire equation in your memory!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Detection - Need and Importance of Automatic Fire Detection System, Principle of Detection, Classification of Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Detection - Need and Importance of Automatic Fire Detection System, Principle of Detection, Classification of Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Automatic Fire Detection System (AFDS)** is an integrated network of sensors, control panels, signaling devices, and power supplies designed to automatically sense the physical and chemical phenomena associated with the incipient (early) stages of a fire — such as smoke, heat, flame, or combustion gases — and to transmit an alarm signal to occupants, the fire brigade, and/or an automatic fire suppression system, *without human intervention*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An Automatic Fire Detection System is a *life-safety and property-protection engineering system* that uses one or more types of fire detectors to identify the *by-products of combustion* (smoke, heat, light, gas) at the earliest possible moment — typically the **incipient stage** — and initiate appropriate alarm and control sequences in accordance with **IS 2189: 2008** (for non-residential buildings) and **NFPA 72** (National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code).

### Key Terminology Anchor

| Term | Exact Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Incipient Stage** | The earliest stage of a fire, before open flaming, characterized by invisible/aerosol smoke particles and slow temperature rise. |
| **Sensor** | The physical element that responds to a fire signature. |
| **Detector** | A complete assembly of sensor + housing + circuitry that interprets a sensor's response. |
| **False Alarm (False Positive)** | An unwanted alarm caused by non-fire stimuli (steam, dust, cooking). |
| **Nuisance Alarm** | A false alarm traceable to a known, manageable stimulus. |
| **Fire Signature** | A measurable physical/chemical change caused by a fire (smoke, heat, light, CO, CO₂). |
| **Sounder / Sounder-Beacon** | Audible (≥ 65 dB at 3 m) and visual alarm device to alert occupants. |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are sleeping in a house at night. A small electrical short starts smoldering inside a wall socket. The plastic insulation is *just beginning* to heat up and produce a thin, nearly invisible stream of irritating smoke — *you cannot smell it, see it, or feel it yet.*

A **fire detector** is essentially a **"smoke-and-fire sense organ"** for the building — exactly analogous to how your own body has specialized sense organs:

| Human Body | Building (Fire Detection) | What it senses |
|---|---|---|
| **Nose** (olfactory nerves) | **Smoke Detector** | Microscopic smoke particles in air |
| **Skin** (thermoreceptors) | **Heat Detector** | Rise in ambient temperature |
| **Eyes** (retinal cells) | **Flame Detector** | Light from a flame (UV/IR) |
| **Taste buds** (chemoreceptors) | **Gas Detector** | Combustion gases (CO, CO₂) |
| **Brain stem + vocal cords** | **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** + sounder | Decides & announces the emergency |
| **Spinal reflex arc** | **Detection → Alarm → Suppression** (auto sequence) | Rapid automated response |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea:** Just as you *automatically* pull your hand away when you touch a hot plate — *without thinking* — an automatic fire detection system senses danger and reacts *without anyone having to act*. This is the heart of why AFDS exists: **to provide response time when humans cannot**.

---

## 1.3 Why Automatic Detection? (The "Need" Argument)

A fire doubles in size approximately **every 30–60 seconds** in its growth phase. A small, smoldering fire that takes **5 minutes to detect manually** can become a fully flashed-over compartment fire that takes **seconds to detect** but is **un-survivable**.

| Time after ignition | State of fire | What manual detection can achieve | What AFDS can achieve |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 30 s | Incipient (smoldering) | ❌ No human awareness | ✅ Detector senses aerosol |
| 30 s – 2 min | Smoke-logging | ⚠️ Smell noticed — too late to act fast | ✅ Alarm sounded, evacuation begins |
| 2 – 5 min | Open flaming | ⚠️ Visible flames seen | ✅ Fire service notified, suppression activated |
| 5+ min | Flashover | ❌ Un-survivable | (System has already operated) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The single most important reason for *automatic* detection is that **occupants are unreliable detectors**. They sleep, are intoxicated, are physically disabled, are absent, or are distracted. An AFDS provides *24 × 7 × 365* vigilance.

---

## 1.4 Importance of Automatic Fire Detection (Key Engineering Benefits)

1. **Life Safety** — Early warning enables safe evacuation before untenable conditions (toxic smoke, heat, zero visibility) develop.
2. **Property Protection** — Early detection limits fire growth, reducing structural damage and content loss.
3. **Business Continuity** — Reduces downtime, insurance premiums, and loss of records/operations.
4. **Code Compliance** — Mandatory under **NBC 2016 (Part 4)**, **IS 2189**, **TAC** (Tariff Advisory Committee) rules, and **Kerala Building Rules**.
5. **Automatic Activation of Suppression** — Sprinklers, gaseous systems, water mist, etc., can be triggered via detection signal.
6. **Emergency Service Notification** — Direct linkage to fire brigade (via **AFA** systems under **IS 2189**).
7. **Integration with BMS** — Modern AFDS integrates with Building Management Systems for HVAC shut-off, smoke control, lift recall, door release.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** When asked "State the importance of AFDS," always list at least **3 of the 7** benefits above, with the *first one (Life Safety)* emphasized as the *primary* objective.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Fire Signature (What is a Detector Actually Detecting?)

Every fire, regardless of fuel type, produces a *combination* of measurable phenomena during its lifecycle. These are called **fire signatures** and they form the basis of all detection principles.

| Fire Signature | Physical Quantity | Detector Type | Stage Detected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smoke (aerosol particles)** | Light scattering / ionization current | Smoke Detector | Incipient |
| **Heat** | Temperature rise / rate of rise | Heat Detector | Growth / Flaming |
| **Flame (EM radiation)** | UV (185–260 nm) / IR (4.4 µm) | Flame Detector | Open flaming |
| **Combustion gases** | CO, CO₂, HCN, NOₓ | Gas Detector | Incipient / Smoldering |
| **Pressure wave** | Sudden pressure pulse | Air-aspirating / linear | Flashover (rare) |

---

## 2.2 General Principle of Detection (How Any Detector Works)

The principle of fire detection follows a **3-stage chain** in every detector regardless of type:

### Stage 1 — Sensing Element
A physical/chemical transducer converts a fire signature into an **electrical signal** (voltage, current, resistance change).

- *Smoke:* Photodiode senses scattered light → current change.
- *Heat:* Thermistor resistance changes with temperature.
- *Flame:* Photomultiplier / photodiode responds to UV/IR photons.

### Stage 2 — Signal Processing & Threshold Comparison
The electrical signal is **amplified, filtered, and compared** against a preset threshold (or analyzed via algorithms for rate-of-rise, pattern recognition, etc.).

- Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
- Filtering (to reduce false alarms)
- Threshold logic: $S(t) > S_{threshold} \Rightarrow$ ALARM

### Stage 3 — Alarm Output
On a "true alarm" condition, the detector:
- Closes an internal switch (relay) → changes line current/voltage
- Sends a digital address to the FACP (in addressable systems)
- Activates local LED indicator
- Triggers FACP sequences: sounders, suppression, BMS, AFA

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "S-T-A":** **S**ense → **T**hreshold → **A**larm. Every detector in the world operates on this triad.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Fire Detectors (Master Taxonomy)

Detectors are classified on **two axes**:

### Axis A: By Fire Signature Sensed (Functional Classification)

#### A.1 — Smoke Detectors
Detect the *aerosol/particulate products of combustion*.

**A.1.1 — Ionization Smoke Detector (ISD)**
- Uses radioactive source (**Americium-241**, activity ≈ **0.9 µCi** / **33 kBq**).
- Two chambers (reference + sensing) with ionised air between electrodes.
- Smoke enters sensing chamber → ions attach to particles → current drops → alarm.
- **Best for:** Fast-flaming, *invisible* smoke.
- **Poor for:** Smoldering, large-particle smoke; nuisance from cooking.

**A.1.2 — Photoelectric (Optical) Smoke Detector**
- Uses **light-scattering (Tyndall effect)** or *light-obscuration* principle.
- **Scattering type:** LED + photodiode at 90°; smoke scatters light into photodiode → alarm.
- **Best for:** Smoldering, *visible* smoke.
- **Poor for:** Very clean, hot, fast-flaming fires (low time advantage).

**A.1.3 — Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD) / High-Sensitivity Smoke Detection (HSD)**
- Active air-sampling system; draws air through a network of pipes to a **highly sensitive laser-based detector**.
- Sensitivity: **0.005 % obscuration/m** (compared to 1–5 %/m for point detectors).
- **Best for:** Clean rooms, data centres, museums, heritage buildings.

#### A.2 — Heat Detectors
Detect abnormal temperature or temperature rise rate.

**A.2.1 — Fixed-Temperature Heat Detector**
- Activates when ambient temperature exceeds a preset value.
- Common setpoints: **57 °C, 68 °C, 93 °C, 141 °C** (IS 2189 classes).
- Mechanisms:
  - *Eutectic alloy* (fusible link) — melts at setpoint, releases spring.
  - *Bimetallic strip* — bends and closes contact.
  - *Glass bulb with conductive liquid* (sprinkler-style).
  - *Thermistor / RTD* — resistance change detected electronically.

**A.2.2 — Rate-of-Rise (ROR) Heat Detector**
- Activates when temperature increases faster than **8.3 °C/min** (≈ 15 °F/min).
- Uses a differential air chamber + diaphragm + electrical contact.
- *Or* uses two thermistors spaced apart; compares their rates.
- **Best for:** Rapidly developing fires where a high temperature will be reached quickly.

**A.2.3 — Rate Compensated (Combined Fixed + ROR)**
- Single detector with both fixed-temperature and ROR characteristics.
- Most widely installed type in commercial buildings.

**A.2.4 — Linear Heat Detection Cable (LHD)**
- A continuous cable whose insulation/resistance changes with temperature.
- **Best for:** Cable trays, tunnels, conveyor belts, parking garages.

#### A.3 — Flame Detectors
Detect the *electromagnetic radiation* emitted by a flame.

| Sub-type | Wavelength band | Spectrum | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UV Detector** | 185–260 nm | Ultraviolet | Hydrogen, metal fires, indoor |
| **Single IR (Single Frequency IR)** | 4.4 µm | IR — CO₂ emission line | Hydrocarbon fires |
| **Dual IR (DIFR / IR²)** | 4.4 µm + 3.8 µm | Two IR bands | Outdoors, with sun rejection |
| **Triple IR (IR³)** | 3 IR bands | Three IR | High-false-alarm areas |
| **UV/IR Combined** | UV + IR | Both | Critical petrochemical |
| **Multi-Spectral IR / Imaging** | Full IR + visible | IR camera array | Large open areas, LNG |

**Detection principle:**
- UV: Photo-electron emission at a *photo-cathode* of a Geiger-Müller tube.
- IR: Optical filter + photodiode tuned to **4.4 µm** (signature of CO₂ in flame).
- Sun rejection: Modulation/flicker analysis (flames flicker at **1–10 Hz**).

**Best for:** Petrochemical, oil & gas, aircraft hangars, transformer yards, turbine enclosures.

#### A.4 — Gas Detectors (Combustion Product Detectors)
Detect chemical signatures — **Carbon Monoxide (CO), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN)**.

- **CO Detector:** Electrochemical cell. Setpoint typically **30 ppm (8-hr)** or **50 ppm (1-hr)** per WHO/EPA.
- **CO₂ Detector**: NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) sensor.
- **Best for:** Sleeping accommodations (hotels, dormitories), underground car parks, lithium-battery storage.

#### A.5 — Multi-Criteria / Multi-Sensor Detectors
Combine smoke + heat + CO sensing in a single housing with **advanced algorithm-based decision logic** (neural networks, fuzzy logic, or weighted voting).

- **Best for:** Modern intelligent buildings; addresses both false-alarm reduction and faster true-fire detection.

---

### Axis B: By System Architecture (Operational Classification)

| Architecture | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (Zone-based)** | Multiple detectors share one "zone line" back to FACP; FACP identifies zone, not individual detector. | Small buildings, low cost |
| **Addressable** | Each detector has a unique address; FACP identifies exact device, type, and status. | Medium-large buildings |
| **Analogue-Addressable** | Detector sends continuous *analogue value* (0–100 %); FACP algorithms decide alarm. | High-end, intelligent |
| **Wireless** | Detectors communicate via **RF (868/915 MHz)**; battery-powered. | Heritage, retrofit buildings |
| **Aspirating (HSSD)** | Active air-sampling with very high sensitivity. | Critical assets |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Detection Engineering)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are used in **detector spacing, sensitivity calculations, and coverage verification** as per IS 2189 / NFPA 72.

### Detector Spacing and Coverage

$$
S_d \le S_{max}
$$

where $S_d$ = design spacing (centre-to-centre) and $S_{max}$ = manufacturer-listed maximum spacing (typically **9 m for smoke, 5.4 m for heat** in smooth-ceiling rooms per IS 2189).

### Smoke Obscuration Sensitivity

$$
\% \text{ Obscuration per metre} = \frac{100 \times (1 - e^{-\sigma_c \cdot L})}{L}
$$

where $\sigma_c$ = extinction coefficient (m⁻¹) and $L$ = path length (m).

### Detector Coverage Area

$$
A_{cov} = S_{max}^2 \quad \text{(smooth ceiling)}
$$

For irregular layouts:
$$
A_{cov} = S_{max} \times D_{max}
$$

where $D_{max}$ is the maximum distance from any point to the nearest detector (typically $\le 0.7 \times S_{max}$).

### Number of Detectors in a Zone

$$
N_d = \left\lceil \frac{A_{floor}}{A_{cov}} \right\rceil
$$

where $A_{floor}$ is the protected floor area (m²) and $A_{cov}$ is the per-detector coverage area.

### Rate-of-Rise Threshold

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} \ge 8.3 \; \text{°C/min} \quad \Rightarrow \text{ ROR alarm}
$$

### Fixed-Temperature Threshold (IS 2189 / IS 11360)

$$
T_{act} \in \{57, 68, 93, 141\}^{\circ}\text{C} \quad \text{(Class A1/A2/B/C)}
$$

### Ceiling Jet Temperature Rise (used for detector placement)

$$
\Delta T_{cj}(r, t) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi k r (g H)^{1/2}} \cdot f\left( \frac{t v}{r^2} \right)
$$

where $Q$ = HRR (kW), $k$ = thermal conductivity of air, $g$ = gravity, $H$ = ceiling height, $v$ = ceiling jet velocity, $r$ = radial distance from fire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to remember:** Sensitivity in **% obs/m**, setpoint in **°C**, ROR in **°C/min**, CO in **ppm**, HRR in **kW**, spacing in **m**, coverage in **m²**.

---

## 2.5 Detector Selection Matrix (Engineering Utility)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is the **single most KTU-high-yield table** for Module 3 questions.

| Building / Risk Type | **Recommended Primary Detector** | **Secondary** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Office, hotel, school | Addressable photoelectric smoke | Heat (ROR) | Avoid ionization in kitchens |
| Kitchen (commercial) | Heat (fixed, 93 °C) | None | Photoelectric disabled |
| Data centre / server room | Aspirating HSSD (VESDA) | Gas (clean agent pre-discharge) | Very high sensitivity required |
| Hospital patient room | Multi-criteria (smoke + CO) | Addressable smoke | Sleeping occupants; CO adds sensitivity |
| Car park (closed) | **CO gas detector** + heat | Linear heat | CO is dominant signature |
| Petrochemical / refinery | UV/IR or IR³ flame | Heat (linear) | Rapid outdoor response |
| Cable tunnel / shaft | Linear heat detection (LHD) | Smoke (aspirating) | Continuous linear coverage |
| Atrium (high ceiling > 9 m) | Aspirating HSSD at mid-height | Beam smoke detector | Stratification challenges |
| Heritage / museum | Aspirating HSSD (discreet pipe) | Aspirating | Aesthetic, non-invasive |
| Transformer yard | IR³ flame | Linear heat | Oil-cooled transformers |
| Battery storage (Li-ion) | Aspirating + CO + heat (multi-criteria) | Gas (HCN, CO) | Off-gas detection critical |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Smoke Detectors in a Zone

**Given:**
- A rectangular office floor of dimensions $L \times W = 36 \; \text{m} \times 24 \; \text{m}$
- Smooth (flat) ceiling, height $H = 3.0 \; \text{m}$
- Detector type: Addressable photoelectric smoke detector
- Manufacturer-listed maximum spacing: $S_{max} = 9 \; \text{m}$
- Allowable maximum distance from any point to nearest detector: $D_{max} = 7.5 \; \text{m}$

**Required:** Compute the minimum number of detectors required and lay out their grid.

### Step 1 — Compute Floor Area

$$
A_{floor} = L \times W
$$
$$
A_{floor} = 36 \; \text{m} \times 24 \; \text{m} = 864 \; \text{m}^2
$$

> *Logic:* Total protected area is the product of the two horizontal dimensions. The ceiling height does not affect area but affects placement logic.

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Detector Coverage Area

For a smooth, flat ceiling:

$$
A_{cov} = S_{max} \times D_{max}
$$
$$
A_{cov} = 9 \; \text{m} \times 7.5 \; \text{m} = 67.5 \; \text{m}^2
$$

> *Logic:* Each detector covers a roughly square footprint of side $S_{max}$, but actual layout must ensure *every point* lies within $D_{max}$ of a detector. Using $S_{max} \times D_{max}$ accounts for this constraint conservatively.

### Step 3 — Compute Minimum Number of Detectors

$$
N_d = \left\lceil \frac{A_{floor}}{A_{cov}} \right\rceil
$$
$$
N_d = \left\lceil \frac{864}{67.5} \right\rceil
$$
$$
N_d = \lceil 12.8 \rceil = 13 \; \text{detectors}
$$

> *Logic:* Always **round up** the number of detectors; you cannot install a fractional detector. Rounding up also accounts for the perimeter and edge effects of the zone.

### Step 4 — Layout Verification (Number of Rows × Columns)

Try a $4 \times 4 = 16$ grid (overlapping with $N_d = 13$ is acceptable; we have spare detectors for redundancy).

Row spacing: 
$$
S_{row} = \frac{L}{N_{rows} - 1} = \frac{36}{4 - 1} = 12 \; \text{m}
$$

> *Problem:* $S_{row} = 12 \; \text{m} > S_{max} = 9 \; \text{m}$. This violates spacing.

Try a $5 \times 3$ grid ($N = 15$):
$$
S_{row} = \frac{36}{5 - 1} = 9 \; \text{m} \;\;\checkmark
$$
$$
S_{col} = \frac{24}{3 - 1} = 12 \; \text{m} \;\;\times
$$

> *Problem:* Column spacing exceeds $S_{max}$.

Try a $5 \times 4$ grid ($N = 20$):
$$
S_{row} = \frac{36}{4} = 9 \; \text{m} \;\;\checkmark
$$
$$
S_{col} = \frac{24}{3} = 8 \; \text{m} \;\;\checkmark
$$

> *Decision:* Use a **$5 \times 4 = 20$** grid (over and above the minimum 13, but it satisfies spacing and gives comfortable edge margins).

### Step 5 — Edge Distance Verification

Edge distance from wall to nearest detector centre:
$$
d_{edge,L} = \frac{L}{2(N_{rows}-1)} = \frac{36}{2 \times 4} = 4.5 \; \text{m} \le 0.5 \times S_{max} = 4.5 \; \text{m} \;\;\checkmark
$$
$$
d_{edge,W} = \frac{W}{2(N_{cols}-1)} = \frac{24}{2 \times 3} = 4.0 \; \text{m} \le 4.5 \; \text{m} \;\;\checkmark
$$

### Final Answer

> **Minimum detectors = 13 ; Practical layout uses 20 (5 rows × 4 columns) at 9 m × 8 m spacing.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Fire Signature vs. Detector Response Time

The table below summarises how each detector class responds to a **standard test fire (TF)** as defined in **EN 54 / IS 11360**.

| Test Fire | Fuel | Signature | **Heat Det.** | **Photo Smoke** | **Ionization Smoke** | **CO Gas** | **Aspirating** | **Flame (UV/IR)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TF1 | Open wood | Flaming, low smoke | Moderate | Slow | **Fast** | Slow | **Very Fast** | Moderate |
| TF2 | Smoldering wood | Slow smoke, no flame | **Very Slow** | **Fast** | Moderate | Moderate | **Very Fast** | None |
| TF3 | Smoldering cotton | Large-particle smoke | Slow | **Very Fast** | Slow | Fast | **Very Fast** | None |
| TF4 | Open plastic (PU) | Fast smoke + flame | Moderate | **Fast** | **Very Fast** | Moderate | **Very Fast** | **Fast** |
| TF5 | Liquid (heptane) | Bright flame, black smoke | **Fast** | Fast | **Fast** | Slow | **Very Fast** | **Very Fast** |
| TF6 | Liquid (decalin) | Smoldering, aerosol | Slow | Moderate | Slow | **Fast** | **Fast** | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cue:** Notice that **no single detector is best for all fire types**. This is the fundamental engineering reason for **multi-criteria detectors** in modern installations.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Detector Setpoint Selection for a Kitchen

**Given:**
- A commercial kitchen with deep-frying at oil temperatures of **180 °C**
- Ambient temperature baseline $T_0 = 30 \; °C$
- Sudden release of cooking vapours (water, oil aerosol)

**Required:** Select the appropriate heat detector class and justify.

### Step 1 — Reject Smoke and Ionization Detectors
Cooking vapours contain *condensation aerosols* that mimic smoke — a smoke detector would generate a *nuisance alarm* within days. **Reject all smoke types.**

### Step 2 — Reject Photoelectric and Ionization for Same Reason
Same aerosol issue. **Reject.**

### Step 3 — Choose a Heat Detector with High Setpoint
- Use **Fixed-Temperature Heat Detector, Class C (93 °C)** per IS 11360.
- Optional: Add a **Rate-of-Rise (ROR)** element for redundancy.
- Place detector **above cooking appliances but away from direct flame impingement** (otherwise fixed-temperature will activate too early).

### Step 4 — Verify Setpoint vs. Normal Cooking
Normal cooking peak temperature at detector location: ≈ 50–60 °C (with good hood ventilation). 
Setpoint: **93 °C** → margin ≈ **33 °C** above cooking peaks → no nuisance.

> **Conclusion:** **Fixed-Temperature Heat Detector, Class C (93 °C), with ROR backup, mounted ≥ 1.5 m from cooking line.**

---

## 3.4 Sensitivity Calculation: % Obscuration per Metre

**Given:** A photoelectric smoke detector samples a chamber of optical path length $L = 0.15 \; \text{m}$. The smoke entering has a particulate mass concentration producing an extinction coefficient $\sigma_c = 0.43 \; \text{m}^{-1}$ (typical for a smoldering fire at 1 m visibility).

**Required:** Compute the **% obscuration per metre** that the detector experiences.

### Step 1 — Apply Beer–Lambert Law
$$
\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\sigma_c L}
$$
$$
\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-0.43 \times 0.15} = e^{-0.0645} = 0.9376
$$

> *Logic:* The fraction of light that *passes* through the smoke is 0.9376. The fraction of light that is *scattered/absorbed* (and hence sensed by the photodiode) is $1 - 0.9376 = 0.0624$.

### Step 2 — Convert to % Obscuration per Metre
First, the % obscuration over the **0.15 m path**:
$$
\% \text{ Obs} = (1 - 0.9376) \times 100 = 6.24 \; \% \text{ (over 0.15 m)}
$$

Now normalise to per-metre:
$$
\% \text{ Obs/m} = \frac{6.24 \; \%}{0.15 \; \text{m}} = 41.6 \; \% \text{ obs/m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A standard photoelectric point detector has an alarm threshold of **1–5 % obs/m** and an aspirating detector at **0.005–0.1 % obs/m**. A 41.6 % obs/m smoke condition is *very thick* — by then evacuation is severely compromised. This is why **aspirating systems are chosen for life-safety-critical applications** (hospitals, data centres).

## 4.4 Working Principle — Photoelectric Smoke Detector (Optical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Smoke Enters Chamber] --> B[Light from LED Source]
    B --> C[Light Scattered by Smoke Particles]
    C --> D[Photodiode Receiver at 90 degrees]
    D --> E[Photocurrent Generated]
    E --> F[Amplifier Circuit]
    F --> G{Threshold Comparator}
    G --> H[Alarm Signal to FACP]
    G --> I[No Alarm LED Off]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Cue:** The LED and photodiode are arranged at **90°** so that the photodiode *normally sees no light* (dark chamber). Only when smoke scatters light into the photodiode does it respond. This minimizes ambient light interference.

---

## 4.5 Working Principle — Ionization Smoke Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Americium 241 Source] --> B[Alpha Particles]
    B --> C[Air Molecules Ionised]
    C --> D[Steady Ionisation Current]
    D --> E[Reference Chamber]
    D --> F[Sensing Chamber]
    F --> G[Smoke Particles Enter]
    G --> H[Ions Attach to Smoke]
    H --> I[Current Drops]
    I --> J{Threshold Comparator}
    J --> K[Alarm Activated]
```

---

## 4.6 Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Protected Space] -->|Air Sampled| A2[Sampling Pipe Network]
    A2 --> A3[Central Aspirator Pump]
    A3 --> A4[HEPA Filter]
    A4 --> A5[Laser Detection Chamber]
    A5 --> A6[Highly Sensitive Photodetector]
    A6 --> A7[Signal Processor]
    A7 --> A8{Fire Threshold}
    A8 --> A9[Fire Alarm Activated]
    A8 --> A10[Stage 1 Alert]
    A8 --> A11[Stage 2 Fire]
    A8 --> A12[Stage 3 Suppression Trigger]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Aspirating systems can provide **multi-stage alarming** — Stage 1 (investigate), Stage 2 (fire), Stage 3 (activate suppression) — at progressively higher smoke levels. This is unique to HSSD/ASD systems.

---

## 4.7 Detector-to-Suppression Sequence (Life-Safety Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Fire Ignition] --> S2[Incipient Stage 0 to 30 s]
    S2 --> S3[Smoldering 30 s to 5 min]
    S3 --> S4[Open Flaming 5 to 10 min]
    S4 --> S5[Flashover 10 min plus]

    S2 --> D1[HSSD Detects 5 to 30 s]
    S3 --> D2[Photoelectric Detects 30 s to 2 min]
    S4 --> D3[Heat Detects 2 to 5 min]
    S5 --> D4[Flame Detects]

    D1 --> R1[Alarm Stage 1]
    D2 --> R2[Alarm Stage 2]
    D3 --> R3[Alarm Stage 3]
    D4 --> R4[Alarm Full]

    R1 --> A1[Investigation]
    R2 --> A2[Evacuation Begins]
    R3 --> A3[Suppression Activates]
    R4 --> A4[Fire Service Confirmed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading:** Time progresses **downward** (fire growth) and **leftward** (detection response). Notice that *earlier-stage* detectors (HSSD) act *earlier* in the timeline — saving critical minutes for evacuation.
