# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define an *Automatic Fire Detection System (AFDS)* and list any **two** benefits it provides over manual detection.

**Model Answer:**

> An **Automatic Fire Detection System (AFDS)** is a coordinated assembly of fire detectors (sensors), a Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP), alarm-signalling devices, and power supplies, designed to sense the *incipient by-products of combustion* — smoke, heat, flame, or combustion gases — and to automatically initiate an alarm and downstream control actions *without human intervention*, in conformance with **IS 2189: 2008** and **NFPA 72**.
>
> **Two benefits over manual detection:**
> 1. **24 × 7 vigilance** — Operates continuously regardless of occupant presence, sleep state, intoxication, or physical disability.
> 2. **Incipient-stage response** — Detects fires during the *smoldering* phase (before open flaming), allowing evacuation and suppression while the fire is small and easily controllable.

*(Any two of the seven benefits listed in Section 1.4 are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between an **ionization smoke detector** and a **photoelectric smoke detector** based on their operating principle and best application.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Ionization Smoke Detector (ISD) | Photoelectric Smoke Detector (PSD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Uses a radioactive source (**Am-241**, 0.9 µCi) to ionize air in two chambers. Smoke particles attach to ions, reducing the ionization current → alarm. | Uses a **light-scattering (Tyndall effect)** principle. An LED emits light into a dark chamber; a photodiode at 90° receives light *only when smoke particles scatter it* → alarm. |
| **Smoke Particle Size** | Sensitive to **small (< 1 µm)** combustion particles. | Sensitive to **larger (0.3–10 µm)** particles typical of smoldering fires. |
| **Best Application** | **Fast-flaming fires** with invisible sub-micron aerosols. | **Smoldering fires** producing visible smoke (e.g., overheated electrical insulation). |
| **Nuisance Trigger** | Cooking vapours. | Steam, dust, large aerosols. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q:** Describe in detail the **principle of operation and classification of fire detectors** with neat diagrams. Also explain the **need and importance of automatic fire detection systems** in modern buildings.

#### Part (a) — Principle of Operation and Classification (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. General Principle of Detection**

Every fire detector, regardless of type, operates on a **3-stage chain**:
- **Stage 1 — Sensing:** A transducer converts a *fire signature* (smoke, heat, flame, gas) into an electrical signal.
- **Stage 2 — Processing:** The signal is amplified, filtered, and compared against a threshold (or pattern).
- **Stage 3 — Alarm Output:** When the threshold is exceeded, an alarm output is sent to the FACP.

**2. Classification (by fire signature sensed):**

**(i) Smoke Detectors**
- *Ionization Chamber Detector:* Uses Am-241 radioactive source; ionization current drops when smoke enters. (1 mark)
- *Photoelectric (Scattering) Detector:* LED + photodiode at 90°; smoke scatters light. (1 mark)
- *Photoelectric (Obscuration) Detector:* Light beam across a chamber; smoke blocks the beam. (0.5 mark)
- *Aspirating HSSD (VESDA-type):* Active air-sampling with laser detection. (0.5 mark)

**(ii) Heat Detectors**
- *Fixed-Temperature:* Activates at preset temperature (57 / 68 / 93 / 141 °C) via eutectic alloy, bimetallic strip, or thermistor. (0.5 mark)
- *Rate-of-Rise (ROR):* Activates when temperature rises faster than 8.3 °C/min. (0.5 mark)
- *Rate Compensated:* Combines both characteristics. (0.5 mark)
- *Linear Heat Detection (LHD) Cable:* Continuous cable sensing temperature along its length. (0.5 mark)

**(iii) Flame Detectors**
- *UV:* Senses 185–260 nm radiation via Geiger-Müller tube.
- *IR:* Senses 4.4 µm emission from CO₂ in flame.
- *Dual IR / Triple IR / UV-IR:* Improved false-alarm rejection.

**(iv) Gas Detectors**
- *CO (Electrochemical),* *CO₂ (NDIR),* *HCN* — sense chemical combustion products.

**(v) Multi-Criteria Detectors**
- Combine smoke + heat + CO with intelligent algorithm-based decision.

*(Diagrams: Draw the photoelectric and ionization chamber schematics as in Section 4.5 and 4.6.)*

#### Part (b) — Need and Importance (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Need — Why Automatic Detection?**

- **Speed:** A fire doubles in size every 30–60 s; manual detection is far too slow.
- **Reliability:** Occupants are unreliable detectors — they sleep, are absent, intoxicated, or disabled. An AFDS provides **continuous, unattended vigilance**.
- **Incipient-stage detection:** Manual detection typically catches a fire only at *open flaming*; AFDS can detect at the *smoldering* stage, providing critical minutes for evacuation.
- **Code mandate:** Required by NBC 2016, IS 2189, Kerala Building Rules, TAC, and insurance tariff rules.

**2. The Importance — Engineering Benefits**

- **Life safety** (primary objective): early warning → safe evacuation.
- **Property protection**: limits fire growth → reduced structural and content loss.
- **Automatic suppression activation**: AFDS triggers sprinklers, gaseous systems, water mist.
- **Fire service notification**: direct AFA communication to fire brigade.
- **Integration with BMS**: HVAC shut-off, smoke control, lift recall, door release.
- **Business continuity & insurance compliance**: reduced downtime and premiums.
- **Remote monitoring** of unoccupied facilities.

> **Conclusion:** The AFDS is the *first line of defence* in the fire-safety chain; without it, every downstream protection measure is delayed and compromised.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q:** With the help of a **detector selection matrix**, explain how you would select appropriate fire detectors for:
**(a)** A hospital patient ward (sleeping occupants), and
**(b)** A petroleum storage tank farm.
**Justify your selection with reference to the dominant fire signature in each case.**

#### Part (a) — Hospital Patient Ward (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Dominant Fire Signature:** Slow, smoldering combustion (e.g., overheated bed-linen electrical equipment, medical-device malfunction). For sleeping occupants, *toxic gases* (especially **CO**) reach lethal levels *before* smoke or heat is detected.

**Selected Detector:** **Multi-Criteria Addressable Detector (Photoelectric Smoke + Heat + CO)** in a **fully analogue-addressable system**.

**Justification:**
- **Photoelectric smoke element:** Detects smoldering, large-particle smoke from bedding or electrical insulation.
- **Heat element (ROR):** Adds robustness against false alarms from steam (bathrooms) and provides backup.
- **CO sensor:** Critically important because **CO is the dominant life-safety threat during sleep** (occupants are incapacitated by CO before they wake). WHO threshold: **30 ppm (8-hr), 50 ppm (1-hr)**.
- **Multi-criteria algorithm:** Voting logic reduces nuisance alarms from steam, aerosol medications, etc.
- **Addressable architecture:** Each room has individually identified devices — essential for hospital evacuation phasing.
- **Aspirating HSSD as supplementary detection** in ICU/operation theatres where very early warning is required.

*(Detector selection matrix row referenced: "Hospital patient room → Multi-criteria (smoke + CO) → Addressable smoke".)*

#### Part (b) — Petroleum Tank Farm (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Dominant Fire Signature:** *Open flame with intense electromagnetic radiation* (UV + IR) — pool fires, jet fires, vapour cloud fires. Outdoor, large open area, fast fire growth.

**Selected Detector:** **Triple-IR (IR³) or UV/IR Combined Flame Detectors** mounted on perimeter poles, with **Linear Heat Detection (LHD) cable** on tank shells as a secondary layer.

**Justification:**
- **Flame detectors** respond in **seconds** to open flame — appropriate for fast-developing hydrocarbon fires.
- **IR³ (Triple-IR) chosen over single IR or UV** because:
  - **Sun rejection** — outdoor environment with strong solar IR background.
  - **False alarm rejection** from arc-welding, lightning, hot exhausts.
  - **Flicker analysis** at 1–10 Hz distinguishes flames from steady IR sources (sun, hot equipment).
- **UV component** in UV/IR adds hydrogen-fire and metal-fire sensitivity.
- **LHD cable on tank shells** detects fire impingement on the tank wall (especially rim-seal fires on floating-roof tanks).
- **Coverage pattern:** Flame detectors arranged in overlapping cones of detection; spacing per manufacturer (typically 30–40 m for IR³, 15–20 m for UV/IR).
- **Integration with foam pourer system** — flame detector trip → automatic foam activation.
- **AFA communication** mandatory per Petroleum Rules.

*(Detector selection matrix row referenced: "Petrochemical/refinery → UV/IR or IR³ flame → Heat (linear)".)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the *system architecture* aspect** — students describe only the sensor, not the FACP, sounder, addressable loop, and output actions. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing the *incipient* and *flaming* stages** — heat detectors are NOT for incipient-stage detection. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Not justifying detector selection with the *dominant fire signature*** — KTU 2024 examiners expect the answer to explicitly link signature → detector → application. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 4. **Mixing up ionization vs. photoelectric sensitivity** — ionization is best for *fast-flaming*, photoelectric for *smoldering*. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the *code reference* (IS 2189, NBC 2016, NFPA 72)** — always cite the relevant standard. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Spelling errors on standard names** (e.g., "IS 2189" as "IS 21.89" or "NBC 2016" as "NBC 2015") — penalized.
> 7. **In numerical problems, forgetting to round up the detector count** and not verifying edge distance. (–1 to –2 marks)

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Fire Detection: Things You MUST Remember**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- An **AFDS** is automatic, incipient-stage sensing of *smoke, heat, flame, or combustion gases* using detectors, FACP, and alarm devices.
- The detection principle follows the **S–T–A triad: Sense → Threshold → Alarm.**
- AFDS exists because **occupants are unreliable detectors**; speed and vigilance are paramount.

### ✅ Detector Classification (Two Axes)
- **By Signature:** Smoke (ionization, photoelectric, obscuration, aspirating) | Heat (fixed, ROR, rate-compensated, LHD) | Flame (UV, IR, dual/triple IR, UV/IR, imaging) | Gas (CO, CO₂, HCN) | Multi-criteria.
- **By Architecture:** Conventional | Addressable | Analogue-Addressable | Wireless | Aspirating.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- **Am-241 activity in ionization detector:** **0.9 µCi (33 kBq)**.
- **Rate-of-Rise threshold:** **8.3 °C/min**.
- **Fixed-temperature classes (IS 11360):** **57 / 68 / 93 / 141 °C** (A1 / A2 / B / C).
- **IR flame detection wavelength:** **4.4 µm** (CO₂ emission).
- **UV flame detection wavelength:** **185–260 nm**.
- **CO alarm thresholds (WHO):** **30 ppm (8-hr), 50 ppm (1-hr)**.
- **Aspirating detector sensitivity:** **0.005–0.1 % obs/m**.
- **Standard point smoke detector sensitivity:** **1–5 % obs/m**.
- **Sounder output:** **≥ 65 dB at 3 m**.
- **Smoke detector max spacing (smooth ceiling):** **9 m**.
- **Heat detector max spacing (smooth ceiling):** **5.4 m**.
- **Edge distance:** **≤ 0.5 × S_max** (typically ≤ 4.5 m from wall).

### ✅ Selection Logic
- **Smoldering fire** → Photoelectric smoke.
- **Fast-flaming fire** → Ionization smoke (or photoelectric + heat).
- **Kitchen / dusty / hot** → Heat only.
- **Sleeping risk** → Multi-criteria with **CO**.
- **Outdoor petrochemical** → **IR³ or UV/IR flame**.
- **Clean room / data centre** → **Aspirating HSSD**.
- **Cable tunnel** → **Linear heat detection cable**.

### ✅ Standards to Cite
- **IS 2189: 2008** — Selection, installation, and maintenance of automatic fire detection systems.
- **IS 11360** — Heat detectors (point-type).
- **NBC 2016 (Part 4)** — Fire and Life Safety.
- **NFPA 72** — National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code.
- **EN 54** — European standard for fire detection components.

### ✅ Examination Strategy (KTU 2024 Pattern)
- **Part A (3 marks):** Define + list 2–3 features. Be concise.
- **Part B (14 marks):** Internal choice — practice both selections. Use sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Always include: (i) principle, (ii) classification, (iii) selection rationale, (iv) standard reference, (v) a labelled diagram.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Fire Detection**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Detectors - Fixed Temperature, Rate of Rise, Thermistor Rate of Rise and Rate Compensated Type Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Detectors - Fixed Temperature, Rate of Rise, Thermistor Rate of Rise and Rate Compensated Type Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heat Detectors — Formal Academic Definition

A **Heat Detector** is a fire sensing device that responds to the thermal energy (convective and conductive heat) released by a combustion event. Unlike smoke detectors that respond to particulate matter, heat detectors respond to either an **absolute temperature threshold**, the **rate of change of temperature**, or a **combination of both phenomena**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCSFT501 — Module 3):**
> *"Heat detectors are fire detection devices that operate on the principle of thermal energy sensing. They are classified into four functional categories: Fixed Temperature, Rate of Rise (ROR), Thermistor-based Rate of Rise, and Rate Compensated type detectors, each exploiting a different thermodynamic principle for actuation."*

The four canonical types studied in KTU 2024 Scheme are:

| # | Detector Type | Actuation Principle |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fixed Temperature** | Triggers when ambient air temperature reaches a pre-set threshold (e.g., 57°C, 68°C, 93°C) |
| 2 | **Rate of Rise (ROR)** | Triggers when rate of temperature change exceeds a threshold (e.g., 8.3°C/min) |
| 3 | **Thermistor ROR** | Electronic version of ROR using NTC/PTC thermistor sensing |
| 4 | **Rate Compensated** | Combination detector that responds to absolute temperature AND rate of rise |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Plain English Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Fixed Temperature (The Thermometer):**
> Imagine a mercury thermometer in your kitchen. The moment the mercury column crosses the red line marked "Fire," it triggers the alarm. It does NOT care how fast the temperature rose — only that it *arrived* at a dangerous level. **It is patient, but slow.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Rate of Rise (The Speed Camera):**
> Think of a speed camera on a highway. It doesn't care that you were driving 60 km/h — it cares that you *suddenly accelerated* from 40 to 120 km/h in 10 seconds. The rate of *change* is what triggers the alarm. **It is fast, but can be fooled by rapid but harmless temperature swings (e.g., a bakery oven starting up).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Rate Compensated (The Smart Hybrid):**
> Imagine a smart fire alarm that combines BOTH — it watches both your speed AND the speed limit. If you are speeding OR if the temperature is dangerously high (irrespective of rate), it triggers. **It is the most reliable, but also the most expensive.**

## 1.3 Governing Standard & Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Standard:** **IS 2175 : 1988 (Reaffirmed 2017)** and **EN 54-5 : 2017** govern the classification and testing of heat detectors in India and Europe respectively.
>
> **Key Performance Metrics (per IS 2175):**
> - **Fixed temperature rating:** typically **57°C, 68°C, 93°C** (with Class A1, A2, B, C designations)
> - **Rate of rise threshold:** typically **8.3 °C/min** (Class A1R)
> - **Operating voltage:** **12V DC / 24V DC** (conventional) or **loop-powered (addressable)**
> - **Response Time Index (RTI):** measures detector sensitivity, units are **(m·s)^½**

The **Response Time Index (RTI)** is a critical performance metric that quantifies the speed of detector response. Lower RTI = faster detector.

$$\text{RTI} = \tau \cdot \sqrt{u}$$

Where $\tau$ is the time constant (s) and $u$ is the air velocity (m/s) at the detector location.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Fixed Temperature Heat Detectors

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle

A fixed temperature heat detector contains a sensing element that is **calibrated to trigger at a specific absolute temperature**. The element undergoes an irreversible (or in some designs, resettable) physical/electrical change at the threshold temperature.

### 2.1.2 Constructional Variants

**(a) Bimetallic Strip Type:**
- Two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion are bonded together
- When heated, differential expansion causes the strip to bend
- At threshold, the bent strip closes an electrical contact → alarm

**(b) Fusible Alloy Type (Eutectic):**
- A low-melting-point alloy (typically Wood's metal, melting point ~70°C) holds a spring-loaded contact
- When the alloy melts, the spring releases and closes the contact → alarm
- **Non-resettable** — must be replaced after activation

**(c) Quartzoid Bulb (Sprinkler-style):**
- A glass bulb containing a heat-sensitive liquid (e.g., glycerine) with an air bubble
- On heating, the liquid expands, the bubble shrinks, and finally the bulb shatters
- Releases a sprinkler valve OR closes an alarm contact
- Used in **wet sprinkler systems** as a heat-actuated trigger

**(d) Continuous Line (Heat-Sensing Cable):**
- A coaxial cable with two conductors separated by a heat-sensitive insulator
- At threshold, the insulator softens → the conductors short-circuit → alarm
- Used in **tunnels, cable trays, conveyor belts**

### 2.1.3 Governing Equation — Heat Transfer to the Sensing Element

The detector element absorbs heat from the surrounding hot gas layer. The energy balance per unit mass of the element is:

$$m \cdot c_p \cdot \frac{dT_e}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_g - T_e)$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of sensing element (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity of element (J/kg·K)
- $T_e$ = temperature of element (°C)
- $T_g$ = temperature of surrounding gas (°C)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area of element (m²)
- $t$ = time (s)

The detector triggers when $T_e \geq T_{threshold}$ (typically 57°C, 68°C, or 93°C).

## 2.2 Type 2 — Rate of Rise (ROR) Heat Detectors

### 2.2.1 Operational Principle

A rate of rise detector triggers when the **rate of change of temperature (dT/dt)** exceeds a pre-set threshold, regardless of the *absolute* temperature. It is designed to detect the rapid temperature growth characteristic of flaming fires.

### 2.2.2 Pneumatic Diaphragm Construction (Classical Design)

- A sealed chamber is vented to ambient air through a small calibrated **leak orifice**
- A flexible **diaphragm** separates the chamber from a contact mechanism
- **Slow heating:** air pressure equalizes through the leak → diaphragm does NOT move → no alarm
- **Rapid heating:** air in the chamber expands faster than it can leak out → diaphragm deflects → contact closes → alarm
- The leak orifice is sized such that the threshold rate is **8.3 °C/min** (per IS 2175 Class A1R)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 8.3 °C/min?** This value is empirically derived to:
> 1. Exceed normal ambient fluctuations (which are typically < 2°C/min)
> 2. Be slow enough to avoid false alarms from HVAC cycles
> 3. Be fast enough to detect a developing fire in its early minutes

### 2.2.3 Mathematical Description

The pressure rise inside the chamber follows the ideal gas law. For a small leak orifice, the chamber pressure dynamics are:

$$\frac{dP_c}{dt} = \frac{P_a}{V_c} \cdot \frac{dV_{eff}}{dt} - \frac{\dot{m}_{leak} \cdot R \cdot T_c}{V_c}$$

Where:
- $P_c$ = chamber pressure (Pa)
- $P_a$ = ambient pressure (Pa)
- $V_c$ = chamber volume (m³)
- $\dot{m}_{leak}$ = mass flow rate through leak orifice (kg/s)
- $R$ = specific gas constant (J/kg·K)
- $T_c$ = chamber temperature (K)

For slow heating, $\dot{m}_{leak}$ matches the volumetric expansion → $P_c \approx P_a$ → no alarm.
For rapid heating, $\dot{m}_{leak}$ is insufficient → $P_c > P_a$ → diaphragm deflects → alarm.

## 2.3 Type 3 — Thermistor-Based Rate of Rise Detectors

### 2.3.1 Operational Principle

This is the **modern electronic replacement** for the pneumatic ROR detector. It uses a **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** as the sensing element and a microcontroller/analog circuit to compute $dT/dt$ electronically.

### 2.3.2 The NTC Thermistor

The resistance of an NTC thermistor decreases non-linearly with temperature, following the **Steinhart-Hart equation** or the simplified **B-parameter equation**:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (Ω)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 25°C = 298.15 K) (Ω)
- $B$ = beta constant of the thermistor (typically 3000–5000 K)
- $T, T_0$ = absolute temperatures (K)

### 2.3.3 Signal Conditioning

A voltage divider converts the resistance to a voltage, and a differentiator computes the rate:

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}}$$

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = f(V_{out}) \text{ (via analog differentiator or digital sampling)}$$

The detector triggers when $\frac{dT}{dt} \geq 8.3 \, °C/min$ (programmable threshold in addressable systems).

### 2.3.4 Advantages over Pneumatic ROR

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **No moving parts** → higher reliability, longer MTBF
> 2. **Programmable threshold** → field-adjustable
> 3. **Self-diagnostic capability** → drift detection, contamination compensation
> 4. **Addressable** → individual identification on the loop
> 5. **Lower air dependence** → works in low-ventilation environments

## 2.4 Type 4 — Rate Compensated Heat Detectors

### 2.4.1 Operational Principle

A rate compensated detector is a **hybrid** that combines BOTH the fixed temperature and rate of rise principles in a single device. It uses a **bimetallic element with a specific thermal mass and geometry** such that:

- At **slow temperature rises** (low $dT/dt$): the detector acts like a fixed temperature detector
- At **fast temperature rises** (high $dT/dt$): the detector triggers EARLIER than the fixed temperature threshold, providing a "thermal pre-alarm"

### 2.4.2 Construction

- A **bimetallic coil** is wound around a central post
- The coil's free end holds an electrical contact
- As temperature rises, the coil unwinds (or winds tighter)
- The contact closes at a position that depends on BOTH absolute temperature AND rate of rise
- An **external heat-conducting fin** is sized to provide the desired thermal lag

### 2.4.3 Triggering Logic (KTU High-Yield)

The alarm condition is:

$$\text{ALARM} = \left[ T_{ambient} \geq T_{threshold} \right] \text{ OR } \left[ \frac{dT}{dt} \geq \left(\frac{T_{threshold} - T_{ambient}}{\Delta t_{max}}\right) \right]$$

Where $\Delta t_{max}$ is the maximum allowable response time (typically 30 s per IS 2175).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m \cdot c_p \cdot \frac{dT_e}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_g - T_e)$ | $m$ (kg), $c_p$ (J/kg·K), $h$ (W/m²·K) | Energy balance on any heat sensing element |
| 2 | $\text{RTI} = \tau \cdot \sqrt{u}$ | $\tau$ (s), $u$ (m/s) | Detector speed-of-response metric |
| 3 | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | $R$ (Ω), $B$ (K), $T$ (K) | NTC thermistor resistance vs. temperature |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}}$ | $V$ (V), $R$ (Ω) | Thermistor voltage divider output |
| 5 | Trigger condition: $\frac{dT}{dt} \geq 8.3 \, °C/min$ | $T$ (°C), $t$ (min) | ROR and thermistor ROR detection threshold |
| 6 | Fixed temperature thresholds: **57°C, 68°C, 93°C** | $T$ (°C) | IS 2175 / EN 54-5 class ratings |
| 7 | Sprinkler link rating: $T_{link} = T_{amb,max} + 30°C$ | $T$ (°C) | NFPA 13 / IS 15105 design rule |
| 8 | Alarm logic: $\text{ALARM} = [T \geq T_{th}] \text{ OR} [\frac{dT}{dt} \geq K]$ | Boolean | Rate Compensated detection logic |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where each type is preferred (real applications):**
> - **Fixed Temperature (57°C):** Kitchens, laundries, garages — environments with normal heat but no fire
> - **Fixed Temperature (68°C):** Boiler rooms, mechanical spaces
> - **Fixed Temperature (93°C):** Industrial furnaces, kilns, foundries
> - **ROR / Thermistor ROR:** Offices, classrooms, hospitals — clean environments with rapid fire growth
> - **Rate Compensated:** Atria, warehouses, plant rooms — environments with wide temperature swings
> - **Line-type (cable):** Tunnels, cable trays, conveyor belts, aircraft hangars

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Heat detectors are the **slowest** class of fire detectors. They are used in environments where **smoke detectors give false alarms** (steam, dust, cooking vapors) and where **rapid flaming fires** produce intense heat but minimal smoke (e.g., flammable liquid fires).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time Response of a Fixed Temperature Detector (Lumped Capacitance Model)

**Problem Statement:** A fixed temperature heat detector has a sensing mass $m = 0.002$ kg, specific heat $c_p = 500$ J/kg·K, surface area $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², and convective heat transfer coefficient $h = 50$ W/m²·K. The detector is initially at $T_e(0) = 30°C$ and the surrounding gas temperature is suddenly raised to $T_g = 200°C$. Determine the time required for the detector to reach its 68°C threshold.

**Step 1 — Write the energy balance equation.**

The energy balance on the sensing element (lumped capacitance, since Biot number is small) is:

$$m \cdot c_p \cdot \frac{dT_e}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_g - T_e)$$

**Step 2 — Define the time constant.**

The thermal time constant $\tau$ of the sensing element is:

$$\tau = \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\tau = \frac{0.002 \times 500}{50 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.0}{0.025} = 40 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the ODE in standard first-order form.**

$$\frac{dT_e}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau} \cdot (T_g - T_e)$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\frac{dT_e}{T_g - T_e} = \frac{dt}{\tau}$$

Integrating from $T_e(0) = T_i$ to $T_e(t)$:

$$-\ln(T_g - T_e) \Big|_{T_i}^{T_e(t)} = \frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_e(t)$.**

$$\ln\left( \frac{T_g - T_i}{T_g - T_e(t)} \right) = \frac{t}{\tau}$$

$$T_g - T_e(t) = (T_g - T_i) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$\boxed{T_e(t) = T_g - (T_g - T_i) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values.**

With $T_g = 200°C$, $T_i = 30°C$, and $\tau = 40$ s:

$$T_e(t) = 200 - (200 - 30) \cdot e^{-t/40} = 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-t/40}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the time when $T_e = 68°C$.**

$$68 = 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-t/40}$$

$$170 \cdot e^{-t/40} = 200 - 68 = 132$$

$$e^{-t/40} = \frac{132}{170} = 0.7765$$

$$-t/40 = \ln(0.7765) = -0.2529$$

$$t = 40 \times 0.2529 = 10.12 \text{ seconds}$$

**Result:** The detector reaches its 68°C threshold in approximately **10.1 seconds** under these conditions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Trigger Time of a Rate of Rise Detector

**Problem Statement:** A thermistor-based ROR detector has a programmed trigger threshold of $K = 8.3°C/min$. If the ambient temperature rises linearly from 25°C at a rate of 20°C/min, determine:
1. Whether the detector triggers
2. The time at which it triggers

**Step 1 — Express the temperature profile.**

Linear temperature rise starting at $T_0 = 25°C$ with rate $r = 20°C/min$:

$$T(t) = T_0 + r \cdot t = 25 + 20t$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rate of rise.**

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = r = 20 \, °C/min$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the detector threshold.**

$$r = 20 \, °C/min \quad \text{vs.} \quad K = 8.3 \, °C/min$$

Since $r > K$, the detector **WILL trigger**.

**Step 4 — Find the trigger time.**

The detector triggers as soon as the rate is established. With a real detector, there is a small lag due to thermal inertia. Assuming a sensor time constant $\tau_s = 5$ s = 0.0833 min, the detector triggers when:

$$t_{trigger} \approx 3\tau_s = 15 \text{ seconds} = 0.25 \text{ min}$$

(Thermal sensors typically need ~3 time constants to register a step change in rate.)

**Result:** The detector triggers after approximately **15 seconds** (or 0.25 min) from the start of the rapid temperature rise.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NTC Thermistor Resistance at 68°C Threshold

**Problem Statement:** An NTC thermistor used in a heat detector has $R_0 = 10 \, k\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25°C$ (298.15 K) and a beta value $B = 3950$ K. Calculate the thermistor resistance at 68°C (341.15 K) — the typical alarm threshold.

**Step 1 — Write the thermistor equation.**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$R_{68} = 10000 \cdot e^{3950 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{341.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature reciprocal difference.**

$$\frac{1}{341.15} = 0.002931 \, K^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{298.15} = 0.003354 \, K^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = 0.002931 - 0.003354 = -0.000423 \, K^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent.**

$$3950 \times (-0.000423) = -1.6709$$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponential.**

$$e^{-1.6709} = 0.1880$$

**Step 6 — Compute the resistance.**

$$R_{68} = 10000 \times 0.1880 = 1880 \, \Omega \approx 1.88 \, k\Omega$$

**Result:** The thermistor resistance drops from **10 kΩ at 25°C to 1.88 kΩ at 68°C** — an 81% reduction. The voltage divider output therefore drops by approximately the same ratio.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is **HIGH-YIELD for KTU exams** and should be memorized.

| Parameter | Fixed Temperature | Rate of Rise | Thermistor ROR | Rate Compensated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Triggering variable** | Absolute $T$ | $dT/dt$ | $dT/dt$ (electronic) | $T$ AND $dT/dt$ |
| **Standard threshold** | 57, 68, 93°C | 8.3°C/min | 8.3°C/min (programmable) | 57°C OR 8.3°C/min |
| **Sensing element** | Bimetallic / fusible alloy / quartzoid | Pneumatic diaphragm + leak | NTC thermistor + μC | Bimetallic coil with thermal mass |
| **Response to slow fires** | Slow (waits for threshold) | Excellent (low $dT/dt$) | Excellent | Good |
| **Response to fast fires** | Slow (thermal lag) | Fast (within 30 s) | Fast (within 30 s) | Very fast |
| **False alarm immunity** | Excellent (immune to rate) | Poor (HVAC cycles) | Moderate (filtered digitally) | Very good |
| **Resettability** | Fusible: No; Bimetallic: Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate-High | High |
| **Suitable locations** | Kitchens, plant rooms | Clean offices, classrooms | Modern intelligent systems | Atria, warehouses |
| **IS 2175 class** | A1S, A2S, BS, CS | A1R (combination in 1 unit) | A1R (electronic) | A1R (mechanical hybrid) |
| **Failure mode** | Element melts / strips permanently bent | Diaphragm stuck (mechanical) | Sensor drift, μC failure | Coil fatigue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU exams, students often confuse the *class A1R* designation. Note that:
> - **A1S** = A1 + **S**prinkler-style (fixed only) OR **S**low response
> - **A1R** = A1 + **R**ate of rise (combination)
> - **A2** = higher fixed threshold (typically 70°C)
> - **B, C** = industrial-grade higher thresholds
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Heat Detector Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HEAT DETECTORS"]:::root
    B["FIXED TEMPERATURE TYPE"]:::cat1
    C["RATE OF RISE TYPE"]:::cat2
    D["RATE COMPENSATED TYPE"]:::cat3

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D

    B --> B1["Bimetallic Strip"]
    B --> B2["Fusible Alloy Link"]
    B --> B3["Quartzoid Bulb"]
    B --> B4["Heat Sensing Cable"]

    C --> C1["Pneumatic Diaphragm"]
    C --> C2["Thermistor Based Electronic"]
    C2 --> C2a["NTC Thermistor Sensor"]
    C2 --> C2b["Signal Conditioner Amplifier"]
    C2 --> C2c["Differentiator Circuit"]
    C2 --> C2d["Threshold Comparator 8.3 C per min"]

    D --> D1["Bimetallic Coil with Thermal Mass"]
    D --> D2["Hybrid Logic OR Gate"]

    B1:::subnode
    B2:::subnode
    B3:::subnode
    B4:::subnode
    C1:::subnode
    C2a:::subnode
    C2b:::subnode
    C2c:::subnode
    C2d:::subnode
    D1:::subnode
    D2:::subnode

    classDef root fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffd700,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef cat1 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cat2 fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cat3 fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef subnode fill:#ecf0f1,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Thermistor ROR Detector (Signal Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HOT GAS LAYER FROM FIRE"]:::input
    B["NTC THERMISTOR SENSOR R0 10kohm B 3950K"]:::sensor
    C["VOLTAGE DIVIDER VCC R REF R T"]:::cond
    D["BUFFER AMPLIFIER OPAMP"]:::amp
    E["ANALOG DIFFERENTIATOR dV dt"]:::diff
    F["THRESHOLD COMPARATOR 8.3 C per min"]:::comp
    G["ALARM RELAY OR ADDRESSABLE LOOP"]:::output
    H["MICROCONTROLLER ATmega328 in modern units"]:::mcu

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    H -.monitors.-> F

    classDef input fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef sensor fill:#f39c12,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef cond fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef amp fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef diff fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef comp fill:#3498db,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mcu fill:#34495e,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Pneumatic Rate of Rise Detector (Mechanical Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["AMBIENT AIR AT TEMPERATURE T"]:::input
    B["CALIBRATED LEAK ORIFICE DIAMETER D"]:::orifice
    C["SEALED AIR CHAMBER VOLUME Vc"]:::chamber
    D["FLEXIBLE DIAPHRAGM SPRING LOADED"]:::diaphragm
    E["ELECTRICAL CONTACT PAIR NORMALLY OPEN"]:::contact
    F["ALARM CIRCUIT CLOSES WHEN DIAPHRAGM DEFLECTS"]:::alarm

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F

    G["SLOW HEATING: AIR LEAKS OUT FASTER THAN EXPANSION DIAPHRAGM STAYS FLAT NO ALARM"]:::slow
    H["RAPID HEATING: AIR EXPANDS FASTER THAN LEAK DIAPHRAGM DEFLECTS CONTACT CLOSES ALARM"]:::fast

    G -.-> D
    H -.-> D

    classDef input fill:#3498db,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef orifice fill:#e67e22,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef chamber fill:#16a085,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef diaphragm fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef contact fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef alarm fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef slow fill:#7f8c8d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef fast fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.4 Logic Flow — Rate Compensated Detector Triggering Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: SAMPLE TEMPERATURE EVERY 5 SECONDS"]:::start
    B["READ TEMPERATURE T FROM BIMETALLIC COIL"]:::read
    C["COMPUTE RATE OF RISE dT dt"]:::compute
    D{"T GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 57 C"}:::cond1
    E{"dT dt GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 8.3 C per min"}:::cond2
    F["TRIGGER ALARM FIXED TEMPERATURE PATH"]:::alarm1
    G["TRIGGER ALARM RATE OF RISE PATH"]:::alarm2
    H["NO ALARM CONTINUE MONITORING"]:::monitor
    I["END: ALARM LATCHED UNTIL MANUAL RESET"]:::end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -->|YES| F
    D -->|NO| E
    E -->|YES| G
    E -->|NO| H
    H --> A
    F --> I
    G --> I

    classDef start fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef read fill:#3498db,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef compute fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef cond1 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef cond2 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef alarm1 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef alarm2 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef monitor fill:#7f8c8d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef end fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.5 Comparative Selection Matrix — Detector Type vs. Fire Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FIRE SCENARIO"]:::input
    A --> B["SMOLDERING FIRE SLOW TEMP RISE"]
    A --> C["FLAMING FIRE RAPID TEMP RISE"]
    A --> D["FLAMMABLE LIQUID POOL FIRE VERY FAST"]
    A --> E["ELECTRICAL FIRE ARCING MODERATE"]

    B --> B1["PREFER FIXED TEMP 57C OR SMOKE DETECTOR"]:::rec
    C --> C1["PREFER ROR OR THERMISTOR ROR"]:::rec
    D --> D1["PREFER ROR OR RATE COMPENSATED"]:::rec
    E --> E1["PREFER FIXED TEMP 68C OR ROR"]:::rec

    classDef input fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef rec fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]**
**"Differentiate between Fixed Temperature and Rate of Rise heat detectors with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Temperature | Rate of Rise |
|---|---|---|
| Actuation parameter | Absolute temperature threshold (e.g., 57°C) | Rate of temperature change (≥ 8.3°C/min) |
| Sensing principle | Bimetallic strip / fusible alloy / quartzoid bulb | Pneumatic diaphragm with leak orifice / thermistor + differentiator |
| Response to slow fire | Slow (waits for threshold) | Fast (responds to low dT/dt) |
| False alarm immunity | High (immune to rapid non-fire transients) | Lower (HVAC cycles can trigger) |
| Example application | Kitchen (57°C) | Office room (ROR) |

*(Allocation: 1 mark for each clear difference × 3 differences; or 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for application)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]**
**"Explain the working principle of a thermistor-based rate of rise heat detector."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A thermistor-based ROR detector uses an **NTC thermistor** whose resistance decreases as temperature increases. The thermistor forms a **voltage divider** with a reference resistor, and the output voltage is fed to:
1. A **signal conditioner** (op-amp buffer)
2. An **analog differentiator** that computes dV/dt ∝ dT/dt
3. A **threshold comparator** set to the equivalent of 8.3°C/min

When the computed rate exceeds the threshold, the comparator output goes HIGH, triggering the **alarm relay** or transmitting an addressable signal to the FACP. Unlike pneumatic detectors, the thermistor version has **no moving parts** and offers **field-programmable thresholds** and **self-diagnostics**.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for NTC principle, 1 mark for signal conditioning chain, 1 mark for advantages)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Format: Each Module question is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, and a **Module Internal Choice** is provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Q3(a)+3(b) modified]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *"With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a pneumatic rate of rise heat detector. Derive the relationship between chamber pressure and the rate of temperature rise."*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A pneumatic ROR detector consists of:
1. A **sealed air chamber** (volume $V_c$) with a **calibrated leak orifice** (diameter $d$)
2. A **flexible metallic diaphragm** forming one wall of the chamber
3. An **electrical contact pair** behind the diaphragm (normally open)
4. A **mounting base** with terminals for the fire alarm loop

**Working (2 marks):**

- **Slow heating** (dT/dt < 8.3°C/min): The air inside the chamber expands slowly. The expansion air escapes through the leak orifice faster than it accumulates, so chamber pressure remains ≈ ambient. Diaphragm does not deflect. **No alarm.**
- **Rapid heating** (dT/dt > 8.3°C/min): The air tries to expand faster than it can leak. Pressure builds up, deflects the diaphragm against its spring, and closes the contact. **Alarm triggered.**

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Applying ideal gas law to chamber air: $P_c V_c = m R T_c$.

Differentiating: $V_c \frac{dP_c}{dt} = R \frac{d(m T_c)}{dt}$

For an adiabatic rapid process with leak flow $\dot{m}_{leak}$:

$$V_c \frac{dP_c}{dt} = R \left( T_c \frac{dm}{dt} + m \frac{dT_c}{dt} \right)$$

Where $\frac{dm}{dt} = -\dot{m}_{leak}$ (mass loss through leak) and $\frac{dT_c}{dt} \approx \frac{dT_g}{dt}$ (gas temperature rate):

$$V_c \frac{dP_c}{dt} = m R \frac{dT_g}{dt} - R T_c \dot{m}_{leak}$$

The diaphragm deflects when $\Delta P = P_c - P_a \geq \Delta P_{threshold}$, corresponding to a specific $dT_g/dt$ threshold. Calibrating $d$ sets this threshold at **8.3°C/min**.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *"An NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10 \, k\Omega$ at 25°C and B = 3950 K. Calculate its resistance at 70°C. If this thermistor is used in a voltage divider with $R_{ref} = 10 \, k\Omega$ and $V_{cc} = 5V$, find the output voltage at 70°C."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin (1 mark):**
$$T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \, K$$
$$T = 70 + 273.15 = 343.15 \, K$$

**Step 2 — Apply the B-parameter equation (2 marks):**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{343.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$= 0.002914 - 0.003354 = -4.40 \times 10^{-4} \, K^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent and resistance (1 mark):**

$$B \cdot \Delta(1/T) = 3950 \times (-4.40 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.738$$

$$e^{-1.738} = 0.1759$$

$$R_{70} = 10000 \times 0.1759 = 1759 \, \Omega \approx 1.76 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Apply the voltage divider formula (2 marks):**

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}} = 5 \times \frac{1759}{1759 + 10000}$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \times \frac{1759}{11759} = 5 \times 0.1496 = 0.748 \, V$$

**Step 5 — Comment on detector application (1 mark):**

The output voltage drops from 2.5V (at 25°C) to 0.748V (at 70°C), a clear and easily detectable change that the comparator can use to trigger the alarm at the 68°C threshold.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2 (ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q3 alt]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *"Explain the construction and working of a rate compensated heat detector. How is it different from a combination fixed + ROR detector?"*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A rate compensated detector uses a **bimetallic coil** wound around a central post. The coil's free end holds an electrical contact. An **external heat-conducting fin** is attached to provide a calibrated thermal mass and thermal lag. The whole assembly is hermetically sealed in a metal housing.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

The bimetallic coil responds to the **net heat absorbed**, which depends on BOTH:
- The **absolute temperature** of the surrounding air
- The **rate of heat transfer** (i.e., the rate of temperature rise)

The coil's deflection at any time $t$ is given by:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_{eq}(T) \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_c} \right)$$

Where $\theta_{eq}(T)$ is the steady-state deflection at temperature $T$ and $\tau_c$ is the coil's thermal time constant (typically 30–60 s).

**Key difference from a combination detector (1 mark):**

A rate compensated detector is a **single integrated bimetallic element** that produces a *graded* response based on rate + magnitude. A combination detector is **two separate elements** (one fixed temperature + one ROR) wired in an OR configuration, each operating independently. The rate compensated design is more **compact, more reliable, and less prone to false alarms from transient spikes** that affect only one element of a combination unit.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *"A fixed temperature heat detector has the following specifications: $m = 0.005$ kg, $c_p = 450$ J/kg·K, $A = 10^{-3}$ m², $h = 75$ W/m²·K. The detector is rated at 68°C and starts at 25°C. If the surrounding gas temperature suddenly rises to 250°C, calculate: (i) the time constant, (ii) the time to reach the threshold, and (iii) the element temperature after 30 seconds."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the time constant (2 marks):**

$$\tau = \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A} = \frac{0.005 \times 450}{75 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.25}{0.075} = 30 \, s$$

**Step 2 — Write the temperature response equation (1 mark):**

$$T_e(t) = T_g - (T_g - T_i) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

With $T_g = 250°C$, $T_i = 25°C$, $\tau = 30$ s:

$$T_e(t) = 250 - 225 \cdot e^{-t/30}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate time to reach 68°C threshold (2 marks):**

$$68 = 250 - 225 \cdot e^{-t/30}$$

$$225 \cdot e^{-t/30} = 250 - 68 = 182$$

$$e^{-t/30} = \frac{182}{225} = 0.8089$$

$$-t/30 = \ln(0.8089) = -0.2122$$

$$t = 30 \times 0.2122 = 6.37 \, s$$

**Step 4 — Calculate element temperature at $t = 30$ s (2 marks):**

$$T_e(30) = 250 - 225 \cdot e^{-30/30} = 250 - 225 \cdot e^{-1}$$

$$T_e(30) = 250 - 225 \times 0.3679 = 250 - 82.78 = 167.2 \, C$$

**Results Summary:**
- Time constant: **τ = 30 s**
- Time to reach 68°C threshold: **t ≈ 6.37 s**
- Element temperature at 30 s: **T_e = 167.2°C**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES (verified from KTU valuation reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Rate of Rise" with "Rate of Change"** — Both are the same, but students often describe ROR as a *percentage* of temperature. The KTU standard threshold is **8.3°C/min** (a rate), not a percentage.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin in the thermistor equation** — The B-parameter equation uses **absolute temperature (K)**. Using °C directly produces a completely wrong answer.
>
> 3. **Stating "the rate compensated detector is a combination of fixed + ROR"** — This is **inaccurate**. A rate compensated detector is a *single hybrid element*; a combination detector uses **two separate elements**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Always specify °C, °C/min, kΩ, V, s.
>
> 5. **Not stating IS standard number** — IS 2175 is the governing Indian standard. Examiners expect this citation.
>
> 6. **Writing the wrong threshold for the rate of rise** — The correct KTU/IS 2175 value is **8.3°C/min** (or in some references, 9°C/min for Class A1R). Do not confuse with smoke detector sensitivity values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — memorize before the exam:**

- ☐ Heat detectors respond to **thermal energy**, not smoke — slower than smoke detectors but more reliable in harsh environments
- ☐ Four types: **Fixed Temperature, Rate of Rise, Thermistor ROR, Rate Compensated**
- ☐ **Fixed Temperature thresholds (IS 2175):** **57°C, 68°C, 93°C** for ordinary, intermediate, and high-temperature applications
- ☐ **Rate of Rise threshold (IS 2175):** **8.3 °C/min**
- ☐ **Fixed temperature variants:** Bimetallic strip, Fusible alloy, Quartzoid bulb, Heat-sensing cable
- ☐ **Pneumatic ROR** uses a **diaphragm + calibrated leak orifice**; threshold set by orifice diameter
- ☐ **Thermistor ROR** uses an **NTC thermistor** + voltage divider + differentiator + comparator
- ☐ NTC equation: $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ — **T in Kelvin always**
- ☐ **Rate Compensated** = single bimetallic element that responds to both **T** and **dT/dt**
- ☐ **Combination detector** = two separate elements (fixed + ROR) in OR logic
- ☐ **RTI** = detector response speed metric; lower is better
- ☐ **Governing standards:** **IS 2175:1988 (India)**, **EN 54-5:2017 (Europe)**, **NFPA 72 (USA)**
- ☐ **Lumped capacitance equation:** $m c_p dT_e/dt = h A (T_g - T_e)$ → $T_e(t) = T_g - (T_g - T_i) e^{-t/\tau}$
- ☐ **Time constant:** $\tau = m c_p / (h A)$
- ☐ **Best for kitchens:** Fixed 57°C | **Best for offices:** ROR/Thermistor ROR | **Best for atria:** Rate Compensated
- ☐ Heat detectors are **NOT suitable** for smoldering fires — use smoke detectors instead
- ☐ Heat detectors are **mandatory** in: server rooms with HVAC, kitchens, garages, plant rooms per **NBC 2016 (India)**
- ☐ **Class designations (EN 54-5):** A1 (54–65°C), A2 (54–70°C), B (69–85°C), C (84–100°C); suffix **S** = fixed only, **R** = rate-of-rise
- ☐ The **3τ rule**: a thermal sensor typically needs **3 time constants** to fully respond to a step change
- ☐ For sprinkler fusible links: $T_{link} = T_{ceiling,max} + 30°C$ (NFPA 13 design rule)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoke Detectors - Optical and Ionization Type, Photo Electric Light Scattering and Light Obstruction Type Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smoke Detectors - Optical and Ionization Type, Photo Electric Light Scattering and Light Obstruction Type Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Smoke Detector — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **smoke detector** is an automatic fire-sensing device that detects the presence of combustion products (aerosolised smoke particulates, ionized gases, and invisible pyrolysis by-products) in the atmosphere and generates an alarm signal to initiate fire-protective action. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies smoke detectors into two primary families:

1. **Ionization-type smoke detectors (ISDs)** — based on ionisation current reduction caused by smoke particles entering a radioactive chamber.
2. **Optical / Photoelectric-type smoke detectors (PSDs)** — subdivided into:
   - **Photoelectric Light Scattering Type** (a.k.a. *Tyndall-effect detector*).
   - **Photoelectric Light Obstruction / Obscuration Type** (a.k.a. *Beam-type smoke detector*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** Under KTU PCSFT501 Module 3, students are expected to *distinguish the operational physics* of ionization vs. optical detectors, and *qualify the suitability* of each detector for different fire classes (smouldering vs. flaming).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Club" Intuition

Imagine a nightclub with two bouncers working parallel doors:

- **Ionization chamber = the "electric-eye" bouncer** who only allows tiny invisible particles (charged ions) to pass through a current. When smoke (larger, neutral particles) shows up, it *bumps into* the ions and reduces the current → bouncer pulls the alarm cord.
- **Light-scattering chamber = the "spotlight in fog" bouncer** who shines a beam into empty air. In clean air, no light reaches the photodiode. When smoke enters, particles *scatter* light into the photodiode → bouncer rings the bell.
- **Light-obstruction chamber = the "laser tripwire" bouncer** who keeps a beam pointing at a receiver. In clean air, the beam arrives normally. When smoke drifts through, it *blocks* part of the beam → intensity drops → bouncer triggers.

This single mental picture separates the **three working principles** instantly.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (per IS / NFPA / EN 54)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Radioactive source (Ionization) | **Americium-241 (²⁴¹Am), ~33 kBq (≈ 0.9 µCi)** | IEC 62321 / IS 11360 |
| Response threshold (Light Scattering) | **0.05 – 0.2 obs/m (obscuration per metre)** | EN 54-7 |
| Response threshold (Beam-type) | **25 %, 35 %, 50 % obscuration (user-selectable)** | EN 54-12 |
| Alarm sound level | **≥ 85 dB(A) at 3 m** | IS 2189 |
| Operating voltage | **24 V DC (typical) / 9 V battery (residential)** | NFPA 72 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to memorise:** The activity of ²⁴¹Am in a domestic ISD is **~33 kBq** — this is *below* the regulatory exemption limit and is considered safe when the device is intact.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ionization-Type Smoke Detector (ISD) — Working Principle

The ionization chamber consists of two electrically-charged electrodes and a trace amount of radioactive **Americium-241** (²⁴¹Am).

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

1. ²⁴¹Am emits **α-particles (helium nuclei, energy ≈ 5.5 MeV)** continuously.
2. The α-particles **ionize** the air molecules inside the chamber, creating positive ions (O₂⁺, N₂⁺) and free electrons.
3. A small, steady **ionization current** (typically 1–10 pA) flows between the electrodes under an applied DC voltage of about **9 V**.
4. In **clean air**, this current is constant and acts as the calibration baseline.
5. When **smoke particles** (size 0.01 – 1 µm) enter the chamber, they *attach* to the ions (ion–smoke attachment).
6. The heavy, slow-moving ion-smoke agglomerates **reduce the mobility** of the charge carriers.
7. The **ionization current drops** (typically by 25–50 %).
8. A comparator circuit detects this drop and triggers the alarm.

### Why it works better for **flaming fires**
Flaming fires (e.g., burning paper, wood, flammable liquids) produce many **small, sub-micron particulates** that easily attach to ions — *high sensitivity*.
Smouldering fires produce **large, agglomerated particles** that are not as effective at ion-attachment — *low sensitivity*.

## 2.2 Photoelectric Light-Scattering Detector — Working Principle

This is the dominant **residential** smoke detector in modern installations.

1. An **LED (typically infrared, 880 nm)** emits a pulsed light beam *inside* a dark sensing chamber.
2. A **photodiode** is mounted at an angle (typically 90° or 120°) so that in clean air **no light reaches it** — the geometry ensures a "blind" receiver.
3. When smoke enters, individual particles **scatter** the light (Mie scattering regime, since particle size ≈ wavelength).
4. A fraction of this scattered light strikes the photodiode.
5. The photodiode produces a small photocurrent → amplified → compared with a threshold → alarm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is excellent for smouldering fires:** Smouldering fires (e.g., overheated PVC, smouldering upholstery) produce **large, white, reflective particulates** that scatter infrared light very efficiently — *high sensitivity*.
> Flaming fires produce **soot (black, absorbing) particles** that absorb light rather than scatter it — *lower sensitivity*.

## 2.3 Photoelectric Light-Obstruction (Beam) Detector — Working Principle

Used in **large open spaces** (atriums, warehouses, churches, aircraft hangars) where point detectors are impractical.

1. A **transmitter unit** projects a focused IR (or visible red) beam across the protected space to a **separate receiver unit** or a reflector.
2. In clean air, the receiver measures the full reference intensity $I_0$.
3. When smoke crosses the beam path, particulates **absorb + scatter** the light out of the direct path.
4. The received intensity $I$ drops below a preset threshold (e.g., 25 %, 35 %, 50 % obscuration).
5. The comparator fires the alarm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold vocabulary:** A "**25 % obscuration**" beam detector means the alarm triggers when light intensity has dropped by 25 % of the clean-air value. Lower % threshold ⇒ more sensitive.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha\,x}$ | $\alpha$ = extinction coeff.; $x$ = path length | m⁻¹, m | Beer-Lambert law (obstruction-type) |
| 2 | $OD = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $OD$ = Optical Density | dimensionless | Smoke obscuration |
| 3 | $\%\,OBS/m = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times \dfrac{100}{L}$ | $L$ = beam length | % per metre | EN 54 sensitivity rating |
| 4 | $I_{scat} = I_0 \cdot N \cdot \sigma_{sca} \cdot V$ | $N$ = particle number density; $\sigma_{sca}$ = scattering cross-section; $V$ = scattering volume | A (photocurrent) | Light-scattering output |
| 5 | $\Delta I \;=\; I_0 - I$ | Threshold drop before alarm | mA or V | Beam detector trigger |
| 6 | $\sigma_{sca} \propto r^6$ (Rayleigh) for $r \ll \lambda$ | $r$ = particle radius; $\lambda$ = wavelength | m² | Smouldering smoke response |
| 7 | $V_{alarm} = V_{ref} - k \cdot \Delta I$ | Comparator output; $k$ = amplifier gain | V | Detector electronics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical table hygiene:** All absolute-value expressions (such as $\vert x \vert$) are written using `\vert` so the markdown table pipes are not corrupted during rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISDs** are still mandated in many countries (including parts of the EU) for **fast-flaming fire** detection (e.g., kitchens, electrical rooms).
- **Light-scattering PSDs** are now the **default residential choice** because most home fires start as **smouldering** events (e.g., cigarette on sofa, faulty wiring overheating).
- **Beam-type detectors** are the *only economically viable* option for spaces **> 12 m ceiling height** (NFPA 72 guidance) — point detectors fail because smoke stratifies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Beer–Lambert Smoke Obscuration Law

The Beer–Lambert law is the fundamental governing equation for the **photoelectric light-obstruction detector**. We derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Setting up the differential equation

Consider a thin slice of smoke-laden air of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the transmitter. Let $I(x)$ be the light intensity reaching that slice.

The fractional loss of intensity across $dx$ is proportional to:
- the intensity $I(x)$ present, and
- the slice thickness $dx$.

Therefore:

$$dI \;=\; -\,\alpha\,I(x)\,dx$$

where $\alpha$ is the **extinction (attenuation) coefficient** of the smoke aerosol (units: m⁻¹).

### 3.1.2 Separating variables

$$\frac{dI}{I(x)} \;=\; -\,\alpha\,dx$$

### 3.1.3 Integrating from $0$ to $L$ (the beam path)

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} \;=\; -\alpha \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\Big[\ln I\Big]_{I_0}^{I} \;=\; -\,\alpha\,L$$

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 \;=\; -\,\alpha\,L$$

### 3.1.4 Exponentiating both sides

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \;=\; -\,\alpha\,L$$

$$\frac{I}{I_0} \;=\; e^{-\alpha L}$$

$$\boxed{\;I \;=\; I_0 \, e^{-\alpha L}\;}$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** of the light-obstruction smoke detector.

### 3.1.5 Deriving the percent obscuration per metre

The percent obscuration of the beam over the full path length $L$ is:

$$\%\,OBS_{total} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$$

Per metre of beam length:

$$\%\,OBS/m \;=\; \frac{\%\,OBS_{total}}{L} \;=\; \frac{100}{L}\!\left(1 - e^{-\alpha L}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical insight:** EN 54-7 sensitivity classifications for light-scattering point detectors are quoted in **%OBS/m**. A typical "normal sensitivity" detector is rated at **0.05 – 0.2 obs/m**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Beam Detector Threshold

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark sub-part):**
A light-obstruction beam smoke detector spans $L = 30 \text{ m}$ across an atrium. The clean-air intensity is $I_0 = 4.80 \text{ V}$ at the photodiode. The detector is set to alarm at **35 % obscuration**. Find (a) the alarm-trigger intensity $I_{alarm}$ and (b) the corresponding extinction coefficient $\alpha$ for the smoke.

### 3.2.1 Part (a) — Alarm intensity

At 35 % obscuration, the transmitted fraction is:

$$\frac{I_{alarm}}{I_0} \;=\; 1 - 0.35 \;=\; 0.65$$

$$I_{alarm} \;=\; 0.65 \times 4.80 \text{ V} \;=\; 3.12 \text{ V}$$

### 3.2.2 Part (b) — Extinction coefficient

Using the Beer–Lambert equation:

$$\frac{I_{alarm}}{I_0} \;=\; e^{-\alpha L} \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha \;=\; -\frac{1}{L}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{alarm}}{I_0}\right)$$

$$\alpha \;=\; -\frac{1}{30}\ln(0.65) \;=\; -\frac{1}{30}\times(-0.4308)$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha \;\approx\; 0.01436 \text{ m}^{-1}\;}$$

**Sensitivity in %OBS/m:**

$$\%\,OBS/m \;=\; \frac{100}{30}\times 0.35 \;\approx\; 1.167\,\%\,\text{per m}$$

## 3.3 Light-Scattering Photocurrent Derivation (Outline)

For a small scattering volume $V$ containing $N$ particles per unit volume, each with scattering cross-section $\sigma_{sca}$, the scattered power is:

$$P_{scat} \;\approx\; P_0 \, N \, \sigma_{sca} \, V$$

The corresponding photodiode current (for quantum efficiency $\eta$ and photon energy $h\nu$):

$$I_{scat} \;=\; \frac{\eta \, e \, P_{scat}}{h\nu}$$

$$\boxed{\;I_{scat} \;=\; \frac{\eta \, e \, P_0 \, N \, \sigma_{sca} \, V}{h\nu}\;}$$

For Rayleigh-sized smoke particles ($r \ll \lambda$):

$$\sigma_{sca} \;\propto\; \frac{r^6}{\lambda^4}$$

This is why **smaller wavelength LEDs (blue, ~470 nm)** give marginally higher sensitivity than infrared (880 nm) for the same smoke — a frequent **14-mark conceptual question** in KTU exams.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Ionization (ISD) | Light Scattering (PSD-LS) | Light Obstruction (PSD-LO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensing element | ²⁴¹Am + ionization chamber | LED + photodiode (90° geometry) | Tx + Rx (or Tx + reflector) |
| Fire-type best for | Flaming | Smouldering | Smouldering + Flaming (large spaces) |
| Particle size sensitivity | 0.01 – 0.3 µm | 0.3 – 10 µm | All sizes (integrated effect) |
| Governing equation | $\Delta I_{ion} \propto n_{smoke}$ | $I_{scat} \propto N\sigma_{sca} V$ | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha L}$ |
| Cost (per detector) | Low (₹) | Low–Medium (₹₹) | High (₹₹₹₹) |
| Typical application | Kitchens, electrical rooms | Residences, offices, hotels | Atriums, warehouses, malls |
| False-alarm triggers | Cooking aerosols, steam | Steam, dust (must use optical filter) | Building sway, partial beam blockage |
| Maintenance | Source half-life (²⁴¹Am t½ = 432.2 yr) | Lens cleaning every 1–2 yr | Lens cleaning + alignment check |
| Standards | IS 11360, EN 14604 | IS 11360, EN 54-7 | EN 54-12, NFPA 72 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory anchor:** Ionization = *sub-micron + flaming*; Light scattering = *micron + smouldering*; Light obstruction = *large spaces + any smoke type*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ionization Smoke Detector — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Am241 Source] --> A2[Alpha Particles]
    A2 --> A3[Ionization Chamber]
    A3 --> A4[Steady Ionization Current]
    S1[Smoke Inlet] --> A3
    A3 --> A5[Current Reduction]
    A4 --> A6[Comparator Circuit]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Threshold Check]
    A7 --> A8[Alarm Relay]
    A8 --> A9[Sounder and Notification]
```

## 4.2 Photoelectric Light-Scattering Detector — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Pulsed IR LED 880nm] --> B2[Light Beam Inside Chamber]
    S2[Smoke Entry] --> B2
    B2 --> B3[Mie Scattering by Smoke Particles]
    B3 --> B4[Photodiode at 90 Degree]
    B4 --> B5[Photocurrent I scat]
    B5 --> B6[Amplifier]
    B6 --> B7[Comparator vs Reference]
    B7 --> B8[Alarm Output]
```

## 4.3 Photoelectric Light-Obstruction (Beam) Detector — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Transmitter IR LED] --> C2[Collimated Beam Across Space]
    C2 --> C3[Receiver Photodiode OR Reflector]
    S3[Smoke Plume Crossing Beam] --> C2
    C2 --> C4[Intensity Drop I less than I0]
    C3 --> C5[Signal Conditioning]
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6[Obscuration Percentage Calculator]
    C6 --> C7[Threshold Comparator 25 35 50 percent]
    C7 --> C8[Fire Alarm Panel]
```

## 4.4 Master Decision Flow — Which Detector Where?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Identify Protected Space] --> D2{Ceiling Height}
    D2 -->|Less than 12 m| D3{Dominant Fire Risk}
    D2 -->|Greater than 12 m| D4[Use Light Obstruction Beam Detector]
    D3 -->|Flaming Fast| D5[Use Ionization Detector]
    D3 -->|Smouldering Slow| D6[Use Light Scattering Detector]
    D3 -->|Mixed Residential| D7[Use Dual Sensor Ion plus Photo]
    D5 --> D8[Reference IS 11360]
    D6 --> D8
    D7 --> D8
    D4 --> D9[Reference EN 54-12 and NFPA 72]
```

## 4.5 Operational Timeline — Detector Trigger Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as Fire Source
    participant S as Smoke Plume
    participant D as Detector Chamber
    participant C as Comparator Circuit
    participant P as Fire Alarm Panel
    participant N as Occupant Notification
    F->>S: Combustion produces particulates
    S->>D: Smoke enters sensing chamber
    D->>D: Physical interaction (ion attach or light scatter or obstruction)
    D->>C: Electrical signal change (current drop OR photocurrent rise OR intensity loss)
    C->>C: Compare with preset threshold
    C->>P: Trigger signal sent
    P->>N: Activate sounder, strobe, BMS link
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between an ionization-type smoke detector and a photoelectric light-scattering smoke detector with respect to: (i) working principle, (ii) best-suited fire type.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

**(i) Working Principle:**
- **Ionization detector:** Uses a radioactive source (²⁴¹Am) to ionize air inside a chamber. A steady ionization current flows. When smoke particles enter, they attach to the ions, reducing the current. The drop in current triggers the alarm.
- **Photoelectric light-scattering detector:** Uses a pulsed LED and a photodiode mounted orthogonally inside a light-trap chamber. In clean air no light reaches the photodiode. Smoke particles scatter the LED light into the photodiode; the resulting photocurrent triggers the alarm.

**(ii) Best-Suited Fire Type:**
- Ionization → **fast, flaming fires** (sub-micron particulates).
- Photoelectric light scattering → **slow, smouldering fires** (larger, scattering particulates). *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**State the governing equation of a light-obstruction smoke detector and explain the term "obscuration per metre."**

**Model Answer (5–6 lines):**

Governing equation (Beer–Lambert law):

$$I \;=\; I_0\, e^{-\alpha L}$$

where $I$ is the received intensity, $I_0$ the clean-air intensity, $\alpha$ the extinction coefficient (m⁻¹), and $L$ the beam path (m).

**Obscuration per metre (%OBS/m)** is the percentage of light blocked per unit beam length, defined as:

$$\%\,OBS/m \;=\; \frac{100}{L}\!\left(1 - e^{-\alpha L}\right)$$

It is the standard EN 54-7 sensitivity metric for light-scattering and beam detectors. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the construction, working principle, and advantages/disadvantages of an **ionization-type smoke detector** used in fire-protection systems.

**(b) [7 marks]** A photoelectric light-obstruction beam detector spans **40 m** across a warehouse. The clean-air reference voltage at the photodiode is **$I_0 = 5.0 \text{ V}$**. The detector is set to alarm at **30 % obscuration**. Calculate (i) the alarm-trigger voltage, and (ii) the corresponding extinction coefficient of the smoke.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Two electrodes (anode + cathode) separated by a few millimetres.
- A small disc of **Americium-241 (²⁴¹Am)** sealed inside the chamber as the α-source.
- Reference chamber (sealed, smoke-free) is connected in a Wheatstone-bridge arrangement with the sensing chamber to compensate for temperature, humidity, and source-decay effects.
- Connected to a comparator circuit, sounder, and battery (typically 9 V).

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. α-particles from ²⁴¹Am ionize air → steady ionization current $I_0$.
2. Smoke enters the sensing chamber through insect screens / baffles.
3. Smoke particles *attach* to ions → mobility drops → current falls by 25–50 %.
4. Comparator detects the imbalance with the reference chamber and triggers the alarm.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Excellent sensitivity to **fast, flaming, sub-micron particulates**.
- Low cost; compact; long life (²⁴¹Am half-life = 432.2 yr).

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Contains radioactive material — disposal and regulatory issues.
- High false-alarm rate from cooking aerosols, steam, and cigarette smoke.
- Poor sensitivity to smouldering (large-particle) fires.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $L = 40 \text{ m}$, $I_0 = 5.0 \text{ V}$, obscuration = 30 % = 0.30.

**(i) Alarm-trigger voltage (3 marks):**

$$\frac{I_{alarm}}{I_0} \;=\; 1 - 0.30 \;=\; 0.70$$

$$I_{alarm} \;=\; 0.70 \times 5.0 \;=\; 3.50 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Extinction coefficient (4 marks):**

$$\alpha \;=\; -\frac{1}{L}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{alarm}}{I_0}\right) \;=\; -\frac{1}{40}\ln(0.70)$$

$$\ln(0.70) \;=\; -0.3567$$

$$\alpha \;=\; -\frac{1}{40}\times(-0.3567) \;\approx\; 0.00892 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Sensitivity in %OBS/m:**

$$\%\,OBS/m \;=\; \frac{100}{40}\times 0.30 \;=\; 0.75\,\%\,\text{per m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $I_{alarm} = I_0 \times 0.30$ instead of $I_0 \times (1 - 0.30)$. **30 % obscuration means 30 % is blocked, so 70 % is transmitted.** This is the single most common 2-mark loss in beam-detector problems.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **photoelectric light-scattering smoke detector**. Why is it preferred over an ionization detector in residential buildings?

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **ionization, photoelectric light-scattering, and photoelectric light-obstruction** smoke detectors under the following heads: (i) sensing principle, (ii) fire-type suitability, (iii) typical application, (iv) cost, and (v) governing equation.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)

**Sketch description (2 marks):**
- A dark chamber with a **pulsed IR LED** (880 nm) on one wall and a **photodiode** mounted at ~90° on the opposite wall.
- Light baffles ensure the photodiode is in the LED's "geometric shadow" in clean air.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. LED emits a pulsed IR beam inside the dark chamber.
2. In clean air, no light reaches the photodiode (orthogonal geometry).
3. When smoke enters, individual particles **scatter** the light (Mie scattering).
4. A fraction of scattered light strikes the photodiode → photocurrent $I_{scat}$.
5. $I_{scat}$ is amplified and compared with a threshold. If exceeded → alarm.

**Why preferred in residences (2 marks):**
- Most residential fires start as **smouldering** (overheated wiring, cigarette ember, smouldering mattress). Smouldering smoke consists of large, light-coloured particles that scatter IR efficiently — high sensitivity.
- Ionization detectors are prone to **nuisance alarms** from cooking steam and bathroom aerosols, leading to occupant disabling of the device.
- No radioactive source — easier to dispose of; preferred under green-building codes (e.g., GRIHA, LEED).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)

| Head | Ionization (ISD) | Light Scattering (PSD-LS) | Light Obstruction (PSD-LO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Sensing principle | Current reduction in ionised air (²⁴¹Am) | Light scatter onto photodiode | Light beam intensity drop at receiver |
| (ii) Fire-type suitability | Flaming (sub-micron soot) | Smouldering (micron-size white smoke) | Both, in large spaces |
| (iii) Typical application | Kitchens, electrical rooms, plant rooms | Residences, hotels, offices, hospitals | Atriums, warehouses, aircraft hangars |
| (iv) Cost | Lowest | Low | Highest (Tx/Rx + cabling) |
| (v) Governing equation | $\Delta I_{ion} \propto N_{smoke}$ | $I_{scat} \propto N \sigma_{sca} V$ | $I = I_0\, e^{-\alpha L}$ |

*(1.4 marks per row, distributed appropriately.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *omit the governing equation column* or replace it with a vague phrase like "uses light." KTU 2024 scheme requires **quantitative treatment** in 14-mark answers — always state the **formula**, not just the principle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Smoke detector** = automatic device that senses combustion aerosols and raises an alarm; two families: **ionization** and **optical/photoelectric**.
- **Ionization detector** uses **²⁴¹Am (Americium-241)** α-source (~33 kBq) to ionize air; smoke particles **attach to ions** and **reduce ionization current**.
- **Photoelectric light-scattering detector** uses **LED + photodiode at ~90°**; **Mie scattering** by smoke particles directs light onto the photodiode.
- **Photoelectric light-obstruction (beam) detector** uses a **transmitter + receiver (or reflector)** across a long path; smoke **attenuates** the beam.
- **Beer–Lambert governing equation:** $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha L}$ — must be memorised verbatim.
- **% Obscuration per metre** formula: $\%\,OBS/m = (100/L)(1 - I/I_0)$.
- **30 % obscuration ⇒ 70 % transmission** (most common error in numericals).
- **²⁴¹Am half-life = 432.2 yr** — practically permanent for design life.
- **Smouldering fires → photoelectric (light-scattering)** preferred.
- **Flaming fires → ionization** preferred.
- **High ceilings (> 12 m) → beam (light-obstruction)** mandatory.
- **Threshold levels for beam detectors:** 25 % (high sensitivity), 35 % (normal), 50 % (low sensitivity, for dirty environments).
- **Standards to cite:** IS 11360, IS 2189, EN 54-7, EN 54-12, NFPA 72.
- **Dual-sensor detectors** (ionization + photoelectric) are now the residential industry default.
- **False-alarm prevention:** insect screens, drift-compensation algorithms, regular cleaning.
- **Disposal of ionization detectors** is regulated (radioactive waste) — do not incinerate or landfill casually.
- **Beam detector** mounting rule: Tx/Rx must be on a **rigid structure** — building sway triggers false alarms.
- **KEEP** in the answer: at least one numerical demonstration using Beer–Lambert for full marks in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Detectors - Infra Red and Ultra Violet Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flame Detectors - Infra Red and Ultra Violet Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Flame Detector?

A **Flame Detector** is a specialized optical fire sensor designed to identify the presence of a flame by recognizing the specific electromagnetic radiation (EMR) signatures emitted during the combustion process. Unlike smoke or heat detectors, flame detectors respond to the radiant energy released by flames across specific bands of the electromagnetic spectrum — primarily in the **Ultra-Violet (UV)** and **Infra-Red (IR)** regions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCSFT501, Module 3), flame detectors are classified as **line-of-sight, optical fire detection devices** primarily deployed in high-value, high-hazard environments such as:
- Aircraft hangars
- Petrochemical refineries and offshore platforms
- Power generation turbine halls
- Paint spray booths
- Munitions storage magazines
- Hydrogen fuel stations and battery energy storage systems (BESS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Terminology):** A flame detector is an opto-electronic transducer that senses the UV and/or IR emissions characteristic of a flaming fire and converts this radiant flux into an electrical signal that triggers a fire alarm or suppression release, in accordance with **IS 2189, NFPA 72, and EN 54-10** standards.

---

### 1.2 The Physics of Flame Radiation — Why UV and IR?

When a fuel combusts, the chemical reaction releases energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation distributed across a broad spectrum. Two narrow windows in this spectrum are particularly significant for fire detection:

| Spectral Band | Wavelength | Origin of Emission |
|---|---|---|
| **Ultra-Violet (UV)** | **180 – 260 nm** | Excited free radicals (OH*, CH*, C₂*) in flame front |
| **Near Infra-Red (NIR)** | **0.7 – 1.1 µm** | Hot CO₂ vibrational-rotational transitions |
| **Mid Infra-Red (MIR)** | **1.3, 2.7, 4.4 µm** | CO₂ and H₂O hot gas emissions |
| Visible | 400 – 700 nm | Soot particle incandescence |

> [!NOTE]
> The sun emits strong UV-C radiation, but Earth's **ozone layer (O₃)** absorbs wavelengths shorter than **290 nm**. Therefore, UV flame detectors are typically designed to detect radiation below **285 nm** — making the sun effectively invisible to them. This is the fundamental principle that gives UV detectors excellent **solar false-alarm rejection**.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Senses of a Flame Watchman"

Imagine a watchman stationed on a tall tower in a chemical yard. He has two special super-senses:

- **Sense 1 — The "Ultra-Violet Eye" (UV):** Detects the invisible "chemical scream" of excited molecules in the flame front, which lasts for mere microseconds. He ignores ordinary sunlight because the ozone "curtain" already filtered out those rays.
- **Sense 2 — The "Heat-Eye" (IR):** Detects the rhythmic "flicker pulse" of hot gases, similar to how you can feel a candle's heat from across a room. Modern IR detectors are smart enough to ignore steady IR sources (sun, heaters) by checking the **flicker frequency (1–10 Hz)** unique to flames.

Together, these two senses give the watchman an almost supernatural ability to **spot a fire within seconds**, even from a distance of **30 meters** or more, while ignoring every other bright or hot object in the plant.

---

### 1.4 Classification Overview

```
Flame Detectors
├── 1. UV (Ultra-Violet) Detectors
│   ├── UV-only (single band)
│   └── UV/IR combined
├── 2. IR (Infra-Red) Detectors
│   ├── Single-frequency IR (4.4 µm)
│   ├── Dual-frequency IR (IR/IR — 4.4/4.8 µm ratio)
│   └── Triple-IR (3, 4.4, 4.8 µm multi-spectral)
├── 3. Visual Flame Detectors (CCTV-based — covered separately)
└── 4. Multi-criteria (combination with smoke/heat)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Focus:** This topic covers **(1) UV Detectors** and **(2) IR Detectors** in detail, including their operating principles, spectral bands, sensor elements, signal processing, and field-of-view geometry.

---
