### 1.5 Why Flame Detectors are Essential — The Detection Speed Advantage

The KTU syllabus emphasizes that flame detectors are the **fastest optical fire detectors** in the fire-protection hierarchy:

| Detector Type | Response Time | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| Smoke (aspirating) | 30 – 120 s | Clean rooms, data centres |
| Heat (linear) | 30 – 300 s | Cable tunnels, warehouses |
| **Flame (UV/IR)** | **1 – 10 s** | **High-hazard hydrocarbon fires** |

For a gasoline pool fire, a UV detector typically triggers in **less than 1 second** at a distance of **10 m**, whereas a smoke detector may require **30+ seconds** — a critical difference in explosion-risk environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ultra-Violet (UV) Flame Detectors — Operating Principle

#### 2.1.1 The Photophysical Process

A UV flame detector uses a **photo-emissive tube** (commonly called a Geiger–Müller "gas discharge tube" or a solid-state silicon carbide photodiode) that is sensitive only to radiation in the **180 – 260 nm** band.

The operational sequence is:

1. **Photon Incidence:** UV photons emitted by excited OH* and CH* radicals in the flame front strike the cathode of the sensor tube.
2. **Photoelectric Emission:** Each absorbed photon with energy $E \ge \phi_{\text{work}}$ releases one photo-electron from the cathode surface.
3. **Gas Multiplication:** The electron is accelerated by a high-voltage field (~350 V DC) inside a gas-filled tube, ionizing the fill gas (typically a halogen-quenched noble gas) and producing a Townsend avalanche.
4. **Pulse Generation:** A discrete current pulse of amplitude **~10⁻¹¹ A** and duration **~1 µs** appears at the anode.
5. **Signal Counting:** Pulses above a preset threshold are counted over a sliding time window (typically **3 – 10 seconds**).

> [!NOTE]
> The photoelectric effect is governed by **Einstein's photoelectric equation**, which is foundational to the entire UV detector operation:
>
> $$E_{\text{photon}} = h \nu = \frac{h c}{\lambda} \ge \phi_{\text{work}}$$
>
> where $h = \mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\, J \cdot s}$ (Planck's constant), $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^8\, m/s}$ (speed of light), and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

#### 2.1.2 Why 185 nm Cut-Off?

The cathode work function of typical UV cathodes (e.g., tungsten or molybdenum) and the transmission window of the quartz/fused-silica envelope restricts sensitivity to:
- **Below 290 nm** → Rejects terrestrial sunlight (ozone absorbs below 290 nm).
- **Above 180 nm** → Avoids vacuum-UV region, which is absorbed by air over short distances.

#### 2.1.3 UV Sensor Technologies

| Technology | Spectral Peak | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geiger–Müller (gas-filled tube)** | 185 – 260 nm | High gain (10⁶ – 10⁸) | Limited life (~10⁴ hrs), high voltage |
| **Silicon Carbide (SiC) photodiode** | 200 – 360 nm | Solid-state, robust | Lower sensitivity, needs amplification |
| **Aluminum Gallium Nitride (AlGaN)** | 200 – 365 nm | Tunable, no HV needed | Costly, newer technology |

---

### 2.2 Infra-Red (IR) Flame Detectors — Operating Principle

#### 2.2.1 Spectral Signatures of Flames in the IR

Flame radiation in the IR region originates from **vibrational-rotational transitions of hot gases**, primarily:
- **CO₂ emission at 4.4 µm** (the strongest and most diagnostic IR flame signature).
- **H₂O emission at 2.7 µm and 6.3 µm**.
- **Hot soot particle emission** in the 1 – 3 µm region (continuum).

IR detectors use one of three strategies:

**(a) Single-Frequency IR Detector**
- Sensor: Thermopile or pyro-electric detector.
- Center wavelength: **4.4 µm** (CO₂ emission).
- Logic: Triggers when 4.4 µm radiation exceeds a threshold.
- Susceptible to false alarms from black-body radiators (sun, heaters, lamps).

**(b) Dual-Frequency IR (IR/IR) Detector — Industry Standard**
- Two sensors: one at **4.4 µm** (flame signal), one at **4.8 µm** (background reference, where flames are weak but solar/thermal radiation is strong).
- The ratio $R = \frac{S_{4.4}}{S_{4.8}}$ is computed.
- If $R$ exceeds a calibrated threshold AND a **flicker** of 1–10 Hz is present → fire confirmed.
- Excellent solar false-alarm rejection.

**(c) Triple-IR (Multi-Spectral) Detector**
- Three or more sensors at 3.0, 4.4, and 4.8 µm (sometimes 5.0 µm).
- Uses neural-network or fuzzy-logic algorithms to discriminate flame spectra from spurious sources.

#### 2.2.2 The Flicker Principle

Real flames flicker at **1 – 10 Hz** due to turbulent eddies in the buoyant fire plume. A steady IR source (sun, tungsten lamp, engine exhaust) does **not** flicker in this band. Therefore, IR detectors band-pass filter the sensor signal to **1 – 10 Hz** and require **multiple flicker pulses** within a sliding window (typically **2 – 8 seconds**) before alarming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The flicker frequency band **1 – 10 Hz** is the cornerstone of IR detector false-alarm rejection and is directly examinable.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{photon}} = h c / \lambda$ | Photon energy (Einstein) | J or eV |
| 2 | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b$ | Wien's displacement law (black-body peak) | m·K, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K |
| 3 | $E_{\text{radiated}} = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann total radiated power | W/m² |
| 4 | $I_{\text{reaching detector}} = \dfrac{I_0}{d^2}$ | Inverse-square law for radiant intensity | W/m² |
| 5 | $S/N = \dfrac{S_{\text{flame}}}{N_{\text{background}}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio (detection criterion: $S/N \ge 3$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{S_{4.4\,\mu m}}{S_{4.8\,\mu m}}$ | IR/IR dual-band ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_{\text{flicker}} \in [1, 10]$ Hz | Flame flicker band | Hz |
| 8 | $\theta_{\text{FOV}} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d_{\text{fire}}}{2 L_{\text{det}}}\right)$ | Detector cone of vision (FOV) | degrees |
| 9 | $t_{\text{alarm}} = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{d_{\text{ref}}}\right)^2$ | Response time vs distance scaling | s |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{sensor}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{photoelectrons}}}{N_{\text{incident photons}}}$ | Quantum efficiency of UV cathode | dimensionless |
| 11 | $E_{\text{field}} = \dfrac{I_0 \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Irradiance from a small source at distance $d$ | W/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants (memorize for KTU):**
> - Planck's constant: $h = \mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\, J \cdot s}$
> - Speed of light: $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^{8}\, m/s}$
> - Stefan–Boltzmann: $\sigma = \mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}\, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$
> - Wien's displacement: $b = \mathbf{2.898 \times 10^{-3}\, m \cdot K}$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flame detectors are mandatory under:
- **NFPA 72** (National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code) — Table 5.7.5.5.2 for flame detector spacing rules.
- **EN 54-10** — European standard classifying flame detectors into Classes 1, 2, and 3 based on sensitivity.
- **IS 2189** — Indian Standard for fire detection systems.
- **API RP 2001** — Fire protection in refineries (mandates flame detection in hydrocarbon handling areas).
- **OISD-STD-144** — Indian Oil Industry Safety Directorate standard for petroleum refineries.

**Field-of-View (FOV) and Coverage Geometry:**
A typical flame detector has a horizontal FOV of **90°** and a vertical FOV of **90°** (total cone 90° ± 5°). Coverage radius $R$ at mounting height $H$ (for floor-mounted detectors):
$$R = H \cdot \tan(\theta_{\text{FOV}}/2)$$

For a detector mounted at $H = 8\,\text{m}$ with $\theta = 90°$:
$$R = 8 \cdot \tan(45°) = 8\,\text{m radius}$$

For EN 54-10 Class 1 detectors, the **maximum detection distance** is **25 m** for a 0.1 m² heptane reference fire.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photon Energy of a UV Flame Emission at 200 nm

We will derive the energy carried by a single UV photon emitted by a flame radical, to confirm that it exceeds the work function of a typical UV cathode.

**Given:**
- Wavelength of UV flame emission: $\lambda = 200\,\text{nm} = 200 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J \cdot s}$
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$

**Step 1 — Write Einstein's photoelectric equation:**
$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (numerator):**
$$h c = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^{8})$$
$$h c = 19.878 \times 10^{-26}$$
$$h c = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}\,\text{J \cdot m}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by wavelength $\lambda$:**
$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{200 \times 10^{-9}}$$
$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{2.00 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$E_{\text{photon}} = 0.9939 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):**
$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{9.939 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$
$$E_{\text{photon}} \approx 6.20\,\text{eV}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with typical UV cathode work function:**
For a tungsten cathode, $\phi_{\text{work}} \approx 4.5\,\text{eV}$. Since
$$E_{\text{photon}} = 6.20\,\text{eV} > \phi_{\text{work}} = 4.5\,\text{eV}$$
photoelectron emission **is possible**, confirming the detector can respond to 200 nm flame radiation.

**Step 6 — Maximum wavelength for photoemission (cut-off wavelength):**
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{h c}{\phi_{\text{work}}} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{4.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{7.209 \times 10^{-19}}$$
$$\lambda_{\max} = 2.758 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 275.8\,\text{nm}$$

This matches the design target of UV flame detectors (< 285 nm).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stefan–Boltzmann Total Radiant Power of a Flame

To size the IR detector's optical collection budget, we need to compute the radiated power per unit area of a flame at temperature $T = 1200\,\text{K}$ (typical hydrocarbon flame).

**Step 1 — Stefan–Boltzmann law:**
$$E_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the emissivity (assume $\varepsilon = 0.9$ for a sooty flame).

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 0.9 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1200)^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T^4$:**
$$T^{4} = (1200)^{4} = (1.2 \times 10^{3})^{4} = 1.2^{4} \times 10^{12}$$
$$1.2^{4} = 2.0736$$
$$T^{4} = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}\,\text{K}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12}$$
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 0.9 \times 11.757 \times 10^{4}$$
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 10.581 \times 10^{4}\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$
$$E_{\text{rad}} \approx 1.058 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

This is the **bolometric** radiant exitance. Only a fraction lies in the 4.4 µm band (Planck distribution gives ~ 5 % of total power), so the usable IR signal is roughly:
$$E_{4.4} \approx 0.05 \times 1.058 \times 10^{5} \approx 5.3 \times 10^{3}\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Detection Range via Inverse-Square Law

A 0.1 m² gasoline pool fire at $T = 1200\,\text{K}$ is observed by a UV detector with a minimum detectable irradiance of $E_{\min} = 10\,\text{W/m}^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Radiant intensity of the fire (assuming a small source):**
$$I_{0} = E_{\text{rad}} \times A_{\text{fire}}$$
$$I_{0} = (1.058 \times 10^{5}) \times 0.1 = 1.058 \times 10^{4}\,\text{W/sr}$$

**Step 2 — Inverse-square irradiance at distance $d$:**
$$E(d) = \frac{I_{0}}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Set equal to $E_{\min}$ and solve for $d$:**
$$\frac{1.058 \times 10^{4}}{d^{2}} = 10$$
$$d^{2} = \frac{1.058 \times 10^{4}}{10} = 1058$$
$$d = \sqrt{1058} = 32.53\,\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor of 0.7 (per NFPA 72 spacing rules):**
$$d_{\text{design}} = 0.7 \times 32.53 = 22.77\,\text{m} \approx 22\,\text{m}$$

This matches typical manufacturer ratings of **25 m for EN 54-10 Class 1 detectors**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — UV vs IR Detector Comparison

| Parameter | UV Detector | IR Detector |
|---|---|---|
| Spectral band | 180 – 260 nm | 0.7 – 5.0 µm (typically 4.4 µm) |
| Sensor element | Gas discharge tube / SiC photodiode | Pyroelectric / thermopile / PbSe |
| Response time | < 1 s | 1 – 10 s |
| Solar immunity | Excellent (O₃ cut-off) | Moderate (requires 4.4/4.8 ratio) |
| False alarm sources | Arc welding, X-rays, lightning, halogen lamps | Sun, heaters, lamps, hot machinery |
| Smoke penetration | Poor (smoke absorbs UV) | Good (IR penetrates smoke) |
| Hydrocarbon fires | Excellent | Excellent |
| Hydrogen / metal fires | Limited (H₂ flame weak in UV) | Good with triple-IR |
| Outdoor use | Limited (rain, fog attenuate UV) | Suitable |
| Detection range | Up to 25 m | Up to 50 m |
| Typical FOV | 90° cone | 90° cone |
| Lifespan | 10,000 – 30,000 hrs | > 50,000 hrs |
| Standards | EN 54-10, NFPA 72 | EN 54-10, NFPA 72 |

---

### 3.5 Verification of Detection Band Using Wien's Law

For a flame at $T = 1200\,\text{K}$, the wavelength of maximum emission is:

**Step 1 — Wien's displacement law:**
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1200}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**
$$\lambda_{\max} = 2.415 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 2.415\,\mu\text{m}$$

This lies in the **mid-IR region**, confirming that an IR detector operating at 2.7 µm (H₂O band) or 4.4 µm (CO₂ band, falling tail of the Planck curve) captures a meaningful fraction of the flame's radiant power. The peak IR emission from a flame is roughly 30 % of the total black-body intensity, justifying the use of **bandpass-filtered IR sensors**.

---

### 3.6 Numerical Example — Signal-to-Noise Ratio at the Detector

A 4.4 µm IR detector has a Noise Equivalent Irradiance (NEI) of $0.5\,\text{W/m}^{2}$. At 15 m from a 0.1 m² heptane fire:

**Step 1 — Irradiance at 15 m:**
$$E = \frac{I_0}{d^{2}} = \frac{1.058 \times 10^{4}}{(15)^{2}} = \frac{1.058 \times 10^{4}}{225} = 47.0\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Fraction in 4.4 µm band (~ 5 %):**
$$E_{4.4} = 0.05 \times 47.0 = 2.35\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — SNR:**
$$S/N = \frac{E_{4.4}}{NEI} = \frac{2.35}{0.5} = 4.7$$

Since $S/N > 3$, the detection is reliable (the standard detection criterion is $S/N \ge 3$, i.e., the signal must be at least 3× the noise floor for a reliable alarm).

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic UV Flame Detector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flame Source] -->|UV photons 180-260 nm| B[Quartz Window Optics]
    B --> C[UV Bandpass Filter]
    C --> D[UV Photosensor Tube Geiger Muller]
    D --> E[High Voltage Bias Supply 350 V DC]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Pulse Amplifier and Discriminator]
    F --> G[Microcontroller Pulse Counter]
    G --> H{Threshold Exceeded}
    H -->|Yes| I[Fire Alarm Relay Output]
    H -->|No| J[Reset and Continue Monitoring]
    I --> K[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]
    K --> L[Suppression System Activation]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Triple-IR Flame Detector (Multi-Spectral)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flame Source] --> B[Sapphire IR Window]
    B --> C1[Sensor Channel 1 - 4.4 micrometer CO2]
    B --> C2[Sensor Channel 2 - 4.8 micrometer Reference]
    B --> C3[Sensor Channel 3 - 5.0 micrometer Auxiliary]
    C1 --> D1[Preamplifier A]
    C2 --> D2[Preamplifier B]
    C3 --> D3[Preamplifier C]
    D1 --> E[Analog Multiplexer]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[ADC Conversion]
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    G --> H[Flicker Analysis 1 to 10 Hz Bandpass]
    H --> I[Spectral Ratio Computation]
    I --> J{Fire Algorithm Match}
    J -->|Yes| K[Alarm Output Relay]
    J -->|No| L[Background Monitor]
    K --> M[FACP and Suppression]
```

### 4.3 Detection Process Flow — From Photon to Alarm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Photon Emission by Flame] --> P2[Step 2 Optical Collection Lens or Mirror]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Spectral Filtering UV or IR Band]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Photon to Electron Conversion in Sensor]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Amplification and Pulse Shaping]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Signal Processing Threshold and Flicker]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Decision Logic Alarm or Hold]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Output to FACP]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Suppression Activation if Wired]
```

### 4.4 Field of View and Coverage Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Site [Hazardous Plant Area]
        D[Flame Detector Mounted at Height H]
        D -->|Beam Axis| F1[Fire Location at Distance L]
        D -->|Cone FOV Theta Degrees| F2[Fire at Edge of Coverage]
        D -.->||R1[Ground Coverage Radius R = H tan Theta/2]
    end
```

**Geometric Relationship (derived):**

$$L_{\text{max}} = \frac{H}{\cos(\theta_{\text{FOV}}/2)}$$

For $H = 8\,\text{m}$ and $\theta_{\text{FOV}} = 90°$:
$$L_{\text{max}} = \frac{8}{\cos(45°)} = \frac{8}{0.7071} = 11.31\,\text{m along the axis}$$

The **horizontal coverage radius** on the ground is:
$$R = H \cdot \tan(45°) = 8 \cdot 1.0 = 8\,\text{m}$$

Therefore, **four such detectors** mounted at the corners of a **16 m × 16 m** square provide overlapping coverage — a standard NFPA 72 layout pattern.

### 4.5 Functional Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UV [UV Detector Subsystem]
        UV1[Window] --> UV2[Filter 185 to 260 nm] --> UV3[Geiger Tube] --> UV4[Pulse Counter]
    end
    subgraph IR [IR Detector Subsystem]
        IR1[IR Window] --> IR2[Sensor 4.4 micrometer] --> IR3[Sensor 4.8 micrometer] --> IR4[DSP and Ratio Logic]
    end
    subgraph COMBINED [UV/IR Dual Detector]
        C1[Optical Bench] --> C2[UV Channel] --> C5[AND Logic]
        C1 --> C3[IR Channel 4.4] --> C4[Flicker Check] --> C5
        C5 --> C6[Fire Confirmation Output]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **UV/IR combination detector** is now the **industry standard** for offshore platforms and ammunition depots. It uses AND-logic: alarm is declared only when **both** the UV pulse count threshold **and** the IR 1–10 Hz flicker signature are simultaneously present — virtually eliminating false alarms from welding, lightning, or sunlight.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define a flame detector. State any two applications where flame detectors are preferred over smoke detectors.**

**Model Answer:**
A flame detector is an opto-electronic fire sensor that detects the UV and/or IR radiant emissions characteristic of a flaming combustion reaction and converts them into an electrical alarm signal.

**Two applications where flame detectors are preferred:**
1. **Petrochemical refineries and offshore oil platforms:** where hydrocarbon fires develop within seconds and smoke detectors respond too slowly.
2. **Aircraft hangars and paint spray booths:** where high air movement can dilute smoke, but the radiant flame signature remains strong and is detectable from long range.

*(Any two valid applications with one-line justification = full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Why are UV flame detectors designed to operate below 290 nm wavelength?**

**Model Answer:**
UV flame detectors operate below **290 nm** (typically 180 – 260 nm) because Earth's **stratospheric ozone layer absorbs all UV radiation shorter than 290 nm** from the sun. Therefore, terrestrial sunlight contains essentially zero radiation below 290 nm, making this spectral window a "dark background" where the UV signature of a flame is unmistakable. This gives UV detectors **excellent immunity to solar false alarms**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-3 Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the operating principle of a **UV flame detector**. Discuss the role of the Geiger–Müller tube and the high-voltage bias supply. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 0.2 m² gasoline pool fire at 1200 K is monitored by a UV detector with minimum detectable irradiance of 8 W/m². The detector is mounted with a 90° cone of vision. Taking the flame emissivity as 0.9, determine:
   - (i) the maximum detection range, and
   - (ii) the mounting height required to cover a circular floor area of radius 10 m. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) UV Flame Detector — Operating Principle (7 marks)**

A UV flame detector identifies the presence of a flame by sensing ultraviolet photons emitted by excited free radicals (mainly OH* and CH*) in the flame front, in the spectral band **180 – 260 nm**.

**Block diagram and explanation:**

1. **Optical Window (quartz/fused silica):** transmits UV photons in the 180 – 260 nm band but blocks longer wavelengths. Quartz is selected because ordinary glass absorbs UV below 320 nm.

2. **UV Bandpass Filter:** further restricts the incoming radiation to the desired narrow band (~ 200 – 260 nm), eliminating UV-A and visible light interference.

3. **Geiger–Müller (GM) tube sensor:** filled with a halogen-quenched noble gas (e.g., argon + bromine), with a tungsten cathode and central anode wire.

4. **High-Voltage Bias Supply (~ 350 – 500 V DC):** applies a stable potential between anode and cathode. This creates the strong electric field needed for **Townsend avalanche multiplication**.

5. **Photon-to-Pulse conversion:** each UV photon striking the cathode releases one photoelectron. The high-voltage field accelerates this electron, which ionizes the fill gas, producing a cascade of secondary electrons. The result is a discrete current pulse of amplitude **~ 10⁻¹¹ A** and duration **~ 1 µs**.

6. **Pulse Amplifier and Discriminator:** amplifies the small anode pulses and rejects noise pulses below a set threshold.

7. **Microcontroller Pulse Counter:** counts the validated pulses over a sliding time window (typically **3 – 10 seconds**). If the count exceeds a preset threshold (e.g., 8 – 15 pulses in 5 seconds), the controller declares a fire.

8. **Alarm Relay Output:** a dry-contact or solid-state relay is energized, signaling the FACP.

**Role of the GM tube:** It acts as a single-photon detector with internal gain of **10⁶ – 10⁸**, converting individual UV photons into easily countable electrical pulses — eliminating the need for ultra-low-noise external amplifiers.

**Role of the HV bias:** It creates the electric field required for gas multiplication. Without sufficient bias, photoelectrons would simply be collected without amplification and the signal would be lost in electronic noise.

**(b) Detection Range and Mounting Height Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Fire area $A_f = 0.2\,\text{m}^{2}$
- Flame temperature $T = 1200\,\text{K}$
- Emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.9$
- Minimum detectable irradiance $E_{\min} = 8\,\text{W/m}^{2}$
- Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$
- FOV $\theta = 90°$

**Step (i) — Maximum detection range:**

Radiant exitance of the flame (Stefan–Boltzmann):
$$E_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$$
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 0.9 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1200)^{4}$$
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 0.9 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (2.0736 \times 10^{12})$$
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 0.9 \times 1.176 \times 10^{5} = 1.058 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

Radiant intensity of the fire (point-source approximation):
$$I_{0} = E_{\text{rad}} \times A_f = 1.058 \times 10^{5} \times 0.2 = 2.116 \times 10^{4}\,\text{W/sr}$$

Using the inverse-square law:
$$E(d) = \frac{I_{0}}{d^{2}} = E_{\min}$$
$$d^{2} = \frac{I_{0}}{E_{\min}} = \frac{2.116 \times 10^{4}}{8} = 2645$$
$$d_{\max} = \sqrt{2645} = 51.43\,\text{m}$$

Applying the **NFPA 72 safety factor of 0.7**:
$$d_{\text{design}} = 0.7 \times 51.43 = 36.0\,\text{m}$$

**Maximum design detection range ≈ 36 m.**

**Step (ii) — Mounting height for 10 m radius coverage:**

For a detector with FOV $= 90°$, the ground coverage radius is:
$$R = H \cdot \tan(\theta/2) = H \cdot \tan(45°) = H$$

Given $R = 10\,\text{m}$:
$$H = 10\,\text{m}$$

**Required mounting height = 10 m.**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe with a block diagram the working of a **dual-frequency IR flame detector**. Explain how the 4.4 µm / 4.8 µm ratio method and the **flicker analysis** help in rejecting false alarms from sunlight and other IR sources. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 0.1 m² heptane fire at 1100 K is observed by an IR detector with two sensors: one at 4.4 µm and another at 4.8 µm. The radiant exitance in the 4.4 µm band is 8 % of the total black-body emission, and in the 4.8 µm band it is 2 %. The detector's Noise Equivalent Irradiance (NEI) is 1.0 W/m² in each band.
   - (i) Calculate the **signal-to-noise ratio** at 20 m for the 4.4 µm channel.
   - (ii) Calculate the **IR/IR ratio $R = S_{4.4}/S_{4.8}$** at the same distance, and comment on whether the ratio confirms a flame. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Dual-Frequency IR Flame Detector — Working (7 marks)**

A **dual-frequency IR (IR/IR) flame detector** uses two independent IR sensors tuned to different wavelengths to discriminate flames from background thermal sources.

**Block diagram components:**

1. **IR-transparent Sapphire Window:** allows wavelengths > 1 µm to pass; blocks visible light.

2. **Sensor 1 — 4.4 µm (Flame Channel):** a narrow-bandpass thermopile or pyro-electric sensor centered on the **CO₂ emission peak** at 4.4 µm, which is the strongest IR signature of hydrocarbon flames.

3. **Sensor 2 — 4.8 µm (Reference Channel):** a matching narrow-bandpass sensor at 4.8 µm, where flame emission is weak but **solar, lamp, and thermal background** radiation is significant.

4. **Preamplifiers:** low-noise amplifiers for both channels.

5. **ADC and Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** digitizes both signals and computes:
   - The **ratio** $R = S_{4.4}/S_{4.8}$
   - The **flicker signature** by bandpass filtering to 1 – 10 Hz

6. **Decision logic:** an alarm is declared only when **both** conditions are satisfied:
   - $R > R_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically 1.5 – 3.0)
   - Flicker pulses detected in the 1 – 10 Hz band for at least 2 – 8 seconds.

**How the 4.4/4.8 µm ratio rejects false alarms:**

- A **flame** emits strongly at 4.4 µm (CO₂) but weakly at 4.8 µm, giving a **high ratio** ($R \approx 4$ for heptane).
- **Sunlight** and **incandescent lamps** emit a smooth Planck black-body spectrum across 4 – 5 µm, giving a **ratio close to 1**.
- **Heaters and engines** have surface temperatures around 400 – 600 K, peaking at 5 – 7 µm — again giving a low ratio at 4.4 µm.

Thus, the ratio method alone discriminates **flame vs background**.

**How flicker analysis further refines detection:**

- A real flame exhibits turbulent **flickering at 1 – 10 Hz** because of buoyant eddies in the fire plume.
- Sun, heaters, and lamps are essentially **steady (DC) sources** in this band.
- By AC-coupling the IR signal and bandpass filtering to 1 – 10 Hz, the detector becomes virtually blind to steady sources while remaining highly sensitive to flames.
- This **double criterion (ratio + flicker)** makes modern IR/IR detectors one of the most reliable fire detection technologies in use today.

**(b) SNR and Ratio Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $A_f = 0.1\,\text{m}^{2}$, $T = 1100\,\text{K}$, $\varepsilon \approx 1$ (assume), $d = 20\,\text{m}$
- NEI $= 1.0\,\text{W/m}^{2}$ for each channel
- 4.4 µm band fraction = 8 % = 0.08
- 4.8 µm band fraction = 2 % = 0.02

**Step (i) — SNR at the 4.4 µm channel:**

Total radiant exitance (Stefan–Boltzmann):
$$E_{\text{rad}} = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1100)^{4}$$
$$T^{4} = (1.1 \times 10^{3})^{4} = 1.4641 \times 10^{12}$$
$$E_{\text{rad}} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.4641 \times 10^{12} = 8.302 \times 10^{4}\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

Radiant intensity of fire:
$$I_{0} = E_{\text{rad}} \times A_f = 8.302 \times 10^{4} \times 0.1 = 8.302 \times 10^{3}\,\text{W/sr}$$

Irradiance at 20 m:
$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{I_{0}}{d^{2}} = \frac{8.302 \times 10^{3}}{(20)^{2}} = \frac{8302}{400} = 20.76\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

Irradiance in the 4.4 µm band:
$$E_{4.4} = 0.08 \times 20.76 = 1.661\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

SNR in the 4.4 µm channel:
$$\text{SNR}_{4.4} = \frac{E_{4.4}}{NEI} = \frac{1.661}{1.0} = 1.66$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake students make is computing SNR using the **total broadband irradiance** instead of the **band-limited** irradiance (0.08 × total). Always filter to the detector's spectral passband before computing the SNR. Also, do not confuse **NEI (Noise Equivalent Irradiance)** with **NEP (Noise Equivalent Power)** — the former is in W/m², the latter in W.

Since SNR = 1.66 < 3, the 4.4 µm channel alone would not confirm a flame at 20 m — illustrating the importance of **long integration times or closer mounting**.

**Step (ii) — IR/IR Ratio $R = S_{4.4}/S_{4.8}$:**

Irradiance in 4.8 µm band:
$$E_{4.8} = 0.02 \times 20.76 = 0.415\,\text{W/m}^{2}$$

Ratio:
$$R = \frac{E_{4.4}}{E_{4.8}} = \frac{1.661}{0.415} = 4.00$$

**Interpretation:**
For a hydrocarbon (heptane) flame, the expected ratio is **R ≈ 3 – 5**, dominated by the strong 4.4 µm CO₂ emission. Since $R = 4.0$ lies within this flame-confirming range and exceeds the typical alarm threshold of $R_{\text{threshold}} = 1.5$ – 3.0, the ratio **strongly confirms a flame**, even though the absolute SNR is modest. This demonstrates the power of the dual-IR ratio method.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing UV with IR bands:** UV = 180 – 260 nm; IR = 0.7 – 5 µm (especially 4.4 µm). Mixing these up costs easy marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor:** Always multiply the calculated detection range by **0.7** for NFPA-compliant design.
> 3. **Using the wrong Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ (not $10^{-9}$ or $10^{-7}$).
> 4. **Ignoring emissivity:** Real flames have $\varepsilon < 1$; assume $\varepsilon = 0.9$ unless told otherwise.
> 5. **Forgetting flicker band:** IR flame detectors REJECT 50/60 Hz line interference; they look at 1 – 10 Hz. State this explicitly in answers.
> 6. **Omitting the AND-logic in UV/IR dual detectors:** Marks are awarded for explaining that BOTH UV and IR conditions must be satisfied simultaneously.
> 7. **Sketching the block diagram:** Always include a labelled block diagram — KTU awards **2–3 marks** purely for a correct, neat diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flame Detectors (UV & IR)**

- ✅ **Flame detectors sense radiant EM energy** (UV: 180 – 260 nm; IR: 1 – 5 µm) emitted by flames.
- ✅ **UV detectors** use the photoelectric effect in a Geiger–Müller tube or SiC photodiode; **solar-blind** because ozone absorbs < 290 nm.
- ✅ **IR detectors** sense the **4.4 µm CO₂ emission band**; use **dual-frequency (4.4 / 4.8 µm) ratio** to reject background.
- ✅ **Flicker band: 1 – 10 Hz** is the diagnostic signature of real flames (rejecting steady IR sources).
- ✅ **UV/IR combined detectors** use **AND-logic** for maximum false-alarm rejection (used in offshore and ammunition storage).
- ✅ **Photon energy equation:** $E = h c / \lambda$; memorize $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J \cdot s}$ and $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$.
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $E = \sigma T^{4}$; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$.
- ✅ **Inverse-square law:** $E = I_0 / d^{2}$; remember to apply the **0.7 safety factor** for design range.
- ✅ **Standards:** NFPA 72, EN 54-10, IS 2189, API RP 2001, OISD-STD-144.
- ✅ **FOV:** typical 90° cone; coverage radius $R = H \tan(\theta/2)$.
- ✅ **EN 54-10 Classes:** Class 1 = most sensitive (~ 25 m range); Class 3 = least sensitive.
- ✅ **Response time:** UV < 1 s; IR 1 – 10 s; UV/IR combined ~ 1 – 5 s.
- ✅ **False alarm sources to MEMORIZE:** UV → arc welding, lightning, X-rays, halogen lamps; IR → sun, heaters, tungsten lamps, hot machinery.
- ✅ **Hydrogen and metal fires** are weak UV emitters → use **triple-IR** detectors instead.
- ✅ **Always sketch a labelled block diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — it is a guaranteed mark-earner.
