topics.title: Flammable Gas Detection - Pellistor and Laser Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flammable Gas Detection - Pellistor and Laser Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flammable Gas Detection — The Big Picture

**Flammable Gas Detection** is the systematic identification, quantification, and continuous monitoring of combustible gases (hydrocarbons, hydrogen, methane, propane, etc.) within an enclosed or open environment, typically to prevent fire/explosion incidents before the gas concentration reaches its **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)**. The detection systems translate the physical or chemical interaction of gas molecules with a sensing element into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or frequency change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically expects students to differentiate between **contact-type catalytic sensors (Pellistors)** and **non-contact optical sensors (Laser/IR Detectors)**, including their operating principles, response characteristics, and suitability for structural fire safety.

## 1.2 Pellistor (Catalytic Bead) Detector — Formal Definition

A **Pellistor** (also called a **Catalytic Bead Sensor** or **Catalytic Combustion Sensor**) is a point-type flammable gas sensor that operates on the principle of **catalytic oxidation (combustion)** of the target gas on the surface of a heated catalyst bead. The heat released from this micro-combustion raises the temperature of the bead, which in turn changes its electrical resistance, producing a measurable Wheatstone bridge imbalance proportional to the gas concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Note:** The word "Pellistor" is a portmanteau of *Pellet* + *Resistor*, coined by British researchers in the 1960s. The active element is literally a small ceramic pellet coated with a noble-metal catalyst (typically **palladium (Pd)** or **platinum (Pt)**).

## 1.3 Laser Detector (Laser-Based Flammable Gas Detector) — Formal Definition

A **Laser Flammable Gas Detector**, often implemented as an **Open-Path Tunable Diode Laser Absorption Spectroscopy (TDLAS)** or **Laser Methane Detection** system, uses a narrow-beam coherent light source (typically a near-infrared laser diode) to detect gas molecules based on their **selective absorption of photons** at characteristic wavelengths (e.g., methane strongly absorbs at **1653.7 nm**). The reduction in received light intensity follows the **Beer–Lambert Law**, allowing the path-integrated gas concentration to be calculated.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy for the Pellistor (Catalytic Bead) Sensor
Imagine a tiny **electrically-heated metal ring on a gas stove**. When flammable gas (like LPG) leaks near it, the gas *ignites on its own* on the hot surface (no flame visible to the eye — it's catalytic combustion). This small, invisible "burn" makes the ring **warmer**. Because the ring's electrical resistance depends on temperature, the warmer it gets, the more its resistance changes. A precision circuit (Wheatstone bridge) measures this tiny resistance change and shouts: *"Gas detected!"* The "ring" is the **pellistor bead**, the "electric stove" is the **heater coil**, and the "shouting circuit" is the **Wheatstone bridge**.

### Analogy for the Laser Detector
Picture a **laser pointer beam crossing a smoky room** (a "light curtain"). If you place a **specific colored filter** (a gas that absorbs one precise wavelength) in the path, the beam that reaches the far wall becomes **dimmer** — and the amount of dimming tells you exactly **how much filter-gas** is in the room. The laser detector is essentially that laser pointer, sending a beam of *just the right color* across a plant or corridor, and the receiver measures how much the gas "ate" the light.

> [!TIP]
> **At a Glance — Pellistor vs. Laser:**
> - **Pellistor →** *Touches & burns* the gas (catalytic combustion).
> - **Laser →** *Shines light through* the gas (optical absorption). No contact, no combustion.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Definitions)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Standard Remark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Explosive Limit | **LEL** | **% vol** | The minimum concentration in air that can ignite. |
| Upper Explosive Limit | **UEL** | **% vol** | Maximum ignitable concentration. |
| LEL Fraction (commonly used) | **% LEL** | dimensionless | Detector typically alarms at **10–25% LEL** (warning) and **>60% LEL** (danger). |
| Response Time (T90) | $t_{90}$ | seconds | Time to reach 90% of final reading. |
| Measuring Range | — | **0–100% LEL** | Pellistor standard range. |
| Beam Path Length (Laser) | $L$ | **meters** | Open-path lasers span **5 m to 100+ m**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **% LEL** with **% by volume**. A 100% LEL reading for methane means **5% vol CH₄ in air**, not 100% methane. Always quote both values for a full-marks answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pellistor Sensor — Operating Principle (Structured Logic)

A pellistor sensor consists of **two matched platinum coils** embedded in two porous ceramic beads:

1. **Active Bead (Detector Element):** Coated with a high-surface-area catalyst such as **palladium (Pd)** or **platinum (Pt)** supported on **alumina (Al₂O₃)**. This bead is heated to approximately **500 °C** by passing current through the embedded platinum coil.
2. **Reference Bead (Compensator Element):** Structurally identical but its catalyst is **chemically poisoned** (e.g., using lead or rhodium treatment) so it cannot promote combustion. It serves purely as a temperature/thermal reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two beads are connected as adjacent arms of a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit. This differential configuration is the heart of pellistor operation.

### Step-by-Step Sensing Logic

- **Step 1 — Baseline Heating:** A constant voltage (typically **2.0 V to 3.5 V DC**) is applied across the bridge, heating both beads to a stable operating temperature of about **500 °C** in clean air. The bridge is balanced — output voltage is **0 V**.
- **Step 2 — Gas Adsorption:** Flammable gas molecules diffuse through the porous ceramic matrix of the active bead and contact the hot catalyst surface.
- **Step 3 — Catalytic Oxidation:** The catalyst lowers the activation energy, and the gas undergoes **heterogeneous catalytic combustion** (flameless oxidation), e.g. for methane:
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pd/Pt, 500°C}} \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$$
- **Step 4 — Temperature Rise:** The exothermic reaction releases heat (typically **ΔT ≈ 10–80 °C** depending on gas concentration). The active bead's temperature rises.
- **Step 5 — Resistance Change:** Platinum's resistance increases with temperature according to the **Callendar–Van Dusen relation** (simplified as linear):
$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$$
where $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{°C}^{-1}$ for platinum.
- **Step 6 — Bridge Unbalance:** The active bead's $R$ increases while the poisoned reference bead's $R$ remains constant. The Wheatstone bridge produces a differential output voltage:
$$V_\text{out} = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{R_\text{active} - R_\text{reference}}{R_\text{active} + R_\text{reference}}$$
- **Step 7 — Signal Conditioning:** $V_\text{out}$ is amplified, linearized, and displayed as **% LEL**.

## 2.2 Pellistor — Why and How (Engineering Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two beads?** A single heated bead would also respond to **ambient temperature changes, humidity, and air flow** — producing false alarms. The matched reference bead cancels out all *non-gas* thermal effects, giving a clean differential signal.

**Advantages of Pellistors:**
- **Linear output** over a wide range (0–100% LEL).
- **Low cost** (~$50–200 per element).
- Works for **almost all combustible gases** (broad-spectrum response).
- Proven, mature technology with decades of field data.

**Limitations / Failure Modes:**
- **Catalyst poisoning** by silicon vapors (silicones), lead, sulfur compounds, phosphates — irreversible.
- **Oxygen dependency:** Requires **O₂ ≥ 10% vol** to burn the gas; fails in inerted environments.
- Cannot detect **non-combustible** gases (CO₂, N₂).
- **Drift** with age; requires periodic calibration (typically every 90 days).
- **Combustible mixture risk:** The very flame on the bead is a *potential ignition source* in explosive atmospheres (mitigated by intrinsic safety barriers).

## 2.3 Laser Detector — Operating Principle (Structured Logic)

A laser-based flammable gas detector typically uses **Tunable Diode Laser Absorption Spectroscopy (TDLAS)**. The configuration is **open-path** (a transmitter and receiver separated by the area to be monitored) or **point-type** (a small gas cell).

1. **Laser Source:** A **distributed feedback (DFB) laser diode** emits a narrow linewidth (< 0.001 nm) beam tuned to a gas-specific absorption line in the near-infrared (NIR) region.
2. **Wavelength Selection:** For methane, the line at **λ = 1653.7 nm** (R(3) rotational-vibrational line of the 2ν₃ band) is chosen because it lies in a *transparent window* of the atmosphere (minimal H₂O/CO₂ interference).
3. **Beam Propagation:** The beam traverses the monitored path length $L$ (typically **5–100 m**).
4. **Gas Absorption:** Methane (or other target gas) molecules absorb photons at exactly this wavelength, transitioning to a higher vibrational state.
5. **Received Intensity Drop:** The receiver photodiode measures a reduced intensity.
6. **Concentration Calculation:** Applying the **Beer–Lambert Law**:
$$I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-\sigma(\lambda) \cdot N \cdot L}$$
where $N$ is the molecular number density and $\sigma(\lambda)$ is the absorption cross-section.

## 2.4 Why and How (Engineering Insight for Laser)

> [!TIP]
> **Why a laser instead of a simple LED?** An ordinary LED emits a broad spectrum — gas absorption is "diluted" by all the other wavelengths. A laser's ultra-narrow linewidth means *almost every photon* can be absorbed by the target gas, giving **1000× to 10,000× higher sensitivity** (down to **ppm·m** levels).

**Key Engineering Advantages of Laser Detectors:**
- **No false alarms from poisoning** — the laser cannot be "poisoned" because there is no catalyst surface.
- **Works in oxygen-deficient / inerted environments** (e.g., inside LNG tanks, nitrogen-purged zones).
- **Long path coverage** — one beam replaces dozens of point detectors.
- **Fast response** — typical $t_{90} < 1\ \text{s}$ (vs. 10–30 s for pellistors).
- **No catalytic combustion** — *intrinsically safe* in flammable atmospheres.
- **Selective** — detects only the target gas (no cross-sensitivity to other hydrocarbons).

**Limitations:**
- **High cost** (laser optics + DFB diode — typically **$3,000–$20,000+** per system).
- **Line-of-sight requirement** — beam obstruction (dust, fog, birds, snow) can attenuate the signal.
- **Single-gas** per laser (multi-gas systems use multiple DFB diodes at higher cost).
- **Vibration-sensitive** mounting (alignment drifts).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ | Platinum resistance vs. temperature (Pellistor) | $R$ in Ω, $T$ in °C |
| 2 | $V_\text{out} = V_\text{cc} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$ | Wheatstone bridge output (Pellistor) | $V$ in volts |
| 3 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha(\lambda) C L}$ | Beer–Lambert Law (Laser) | $I$ in W/m² |
| 4 | $\tau(\lambda) = e^{-\sigma(\lambda) N L}$ | Spectral transmittance (Laser) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{1}{\sigma(\lambda)\,L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Concentration from absorbance | mol/m³ or ppm·m |
| 6 | $A(\lambda) = \sigma(\lambda) \, N \, L = \ln(I_0/I)$ | Absorbance definition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\%LEL = \dfrac{C_\text{measured}}{C_\text{LEL}} \times 100$ | LEL fraction | % |
| 8 | $P_\text{bridge} = \dfrac{V_\text{cc}^2}{4R}$ | Bridge dissipated power | watts |
| 9 | $N = \dfrac{P}{k_B T}$ | Number density (ideal gas) | molecules/m³ |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m c_p}$ | Temperature rise from combustion heat | K or °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on formula presentation:** In the table, vertical bars inside math expressions have been replaced with parentheses to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. For example, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ would be written as $(\,x\,)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Output for Pellistor

### Statement
Derive the bridge output voltage $V_\text{out}$ of a pellistor in terms of the resistance change $\Delta R$ of the active bead, given that the bridge has two fixed resistors $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and the two pellistor beads initially have resistance $R_\text{active} = R_\text{ref} = R$.

### Setup
The classic pellistor bridge has:
- Arm AB: Active pellistor bead, $R_\text{active} = R + \Delta R$
- Arm BC: Reference poisoned bead, $R_\text{ref} = R$
- Arm AD: Fixed resistor, $R_1 = R$
- Arm DC: Fixed resistor, $R_2 = R$
- Excitation voltage $V_\text{cc}$ applied across A–C; output $V_\text{out}$ measured across B–D.

### Derivation

The voltage at node B (from divider A→B→C):
$$V_B = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{R_\text{active}}{R_\text{active} + R_2} = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{R + \Delta R + R}$$

The voltage at node D (from divider A→D→C):
$$V_D = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_\text{ref}} = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = \frac{V_\text{cc}}{2}$$

Therefore, the differential output is:
$$V_\text{out} = V_B - V_D = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{V_\text{cc}}{2}$$

Take common denominator $(2R + \Delta R)$:
$$V_\text{out} = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

Simplify the numerator:
$$2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R) = 2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R = \Delta R$$

Hence:
$$V_\text{out} = V_\text{cc} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = \frac{V_\text{cc} \, \Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R}$$

For small $\Delta R$ (linear regime, $\Delta R \ll R$):
$$V_\text{out} \approx \frac{V_\text{cc} \, \Delta R}{4R}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{\,V_\text{out} \;\approx\; \frac{V_\text{cc}}{4R}\,\Delta R\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The bridge output is **linearly proportional** to the resistance change of the active bead. This is why the pellistor gives a clean, near-linear gas concentration reading over its working range.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Pellistor

**Given:** $V_\text{cc} = 3.0\ \text{V}$, $R = 10\ \Omega$ (each arm at 500 °C), and gas exposure causes $\Delta R = 0.20\ \Omega$.

**Find:** $V_\text{out}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the linearized formula:**
$$V_\text{out} = \frac{V_\text{cc}\,\Delta R}{4R} = \frac{3.0 \times 0.20}{4 \times 10} = \frac{0.60}{40} = 0.015\ \text{V} = 15\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the exact formula (for accuracy check):**
$$V_\text{out} = \frac{V_\text{cc}\,\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R} = \frac{3.0 \times 0.20}{40 + 0.40} = \frac{0.60}{40.40} \approx 0.01485\ \text{V} \approx 14.85\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Error of linear approximation:**
$$\text{Error} = \frac{15.00 - 14.85}{14.85} \times 100\% \approx 1.0\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The linear approximation is excellent for $\Delta R \ll R$. Engineers use the linear formula for calibration, while the exact formula is used for high-concentration analysis (above 80% LEL).

## 3.3 Derivation: Beer–Lambert Concentration for Laser Detector

### Statement
For a laser gas detector, derive an expression for the path-integrated gas concentration $C \cdot L$ (in ppm·m) from measured transmitted intensity $I$ and reference intensity $I_0$ at a known absorption line.

### Derivation

Starting from the **Beer–Lambert Law** in differential form:
$$\frac{dI}{dx} = -\sigma(\lambda)\, n\, I(x)$$

where $n$ is the number density of absorbing molecules (molecules/m³) and $\sigma(\lambda)$ is the absorption cross-section (m²/molecule).

Integrate over the path length $L$ with $I(0) = I_0$ and $I(L) = I$:
$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI'}{I'} = -\sigma(\lambda)\, n \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\sigma(\lambda)\, n\, L$$

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\sigma(\lambda)\, n\, L}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:
$$\sigma(\lambda)\, n\, L = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

Convert number density $n$ to volumetric concentration $C$ (in ppm) using the ideal gas relation at standard conditions ($T = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 101{,}325\ \text{Pa}$):
$$n = \frac{P}{k_B T} = \frac{101{,}325}{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298.15} \approx 2.46 \times 10^{25}\ \text{molecules/m}^3$$

So 1 ppm of gas corresponds to $n_\text{ppm} = 2.46 \times 10^{19}\ \text{molecules/m}^3$.

Hence the concentration in ppm is:
$$C = \frac{1}{\sigma(\lambda)\,L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

And the path-integrated concentration:
$$\boxed{\,C \cdot L = \frac{1}{\sigma(\lambda)} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)\,}$$

### Final Expression in Practical Engineering Units
Engineers often work with **ppm·m**, the **absorption coefficient** $\alpha$ in $\text{ppm}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$, and the **absorbance** $A = \ln(I_0/I)$:
$$C \cdot L = \frac{A}{\alpha(\lambda)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common derivation mistake is mixing $n$ (number density) with $C$ (concentration in ppm). Always state the conversion: $1\ \text{ppm} = 10^{-6}$ mole fraction, and use $n = n_\text{air} \times 10^{-6} \times C$ for $C$ in ppm.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Laser Detector

**Given:** A methane (CH₄) laser detector operating at $\lambda = 1653.7\ \text{nm}$ with absorption coefficient $\alpha = 0.16\ \text{ppm}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$. The reference intensity is $I_0 = 1.000\ \text{mW}$, and the received intensity through a **20 m** path is $I = 0.900\ \text{mW}$.

**Find:** The path-integrated methane concentration $C \cdot L$ and the volumetric concentration $C$.

**Step 1 — Compute the absorbance:**
$$A = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1.000}{0.900}\right) = \ln(1.1111) \approx 0.1054$$

**Step 2 — Compute the path-integrated concentration:**
$$C \cdot L = \frac{A}{\alpha} = \frac{0.1054}{0.16} \approx 0.6588\ \text{ppm·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volumetric concentration:**
$$C = \frac{C \cdot L}{L} = \frac{0.6588}{20} \approx 0.0329\ \text{ppm} \approx 32.9\ \text{ppb}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to % LEL** (LEL of methane = 5% vol = 50,000 ppm):
$$\%LEL = \frac{32.9 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{ppm}}{50{,}000\ \text{ppm}} \times 100 \approx 6.58 \times 10^{-5}\ \%LEL$$

> [!NOTE]
> This example illustrates the **exceptional sensitivity** of laser detectors — they can detect methane at sub-ppm levels across tens of meters, far below the LEL alarm threshold.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pellistor vs. Laser

| Parameter | Pellistor (Catalytic Bead) | Laser (TDLAS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Principle** | Catalytic combustion (heat) | Optical absorption (light) |
| **Output Quantity** | Resistance change | Intensity ratio |
| **Governing Law** | Wheatstone bridge / Callendar–Van Dusen | Beer–Lambert Law |
| **Oxygen Required?** | Yes (≥ 10% vol) | No |
| **Response Time $t_{90}$** | 10–30 s | < 1 s |
| **Poisoning Risk** | Yes (Si, Pb, S, P) | None |
| **Selectivity** | Broad (all combustibles) | Narrow (one gas per laser) |
| **Range** | 0–100% LEL | ppm to % vol |
| **Cost (per point/meter)** | Low (~$100) | High (~$1,000/m of path) |
| **Coverage per unit** | Point (few cm) | Open-path (5–100+ m) |
| **Maintenance** | Calibrate every 90 days | Annual window cleaning |
| **Intrinsic Safety** | Needs barriers (hot surface) | Inherently safe (no heat) |
| **Best Use Case** | Point leak detection in air-rich zones | Perimeter monitoring, inerted tanks, LNG terminals |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pellistor — Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a **Pellistor-based flammable gas detector**, from the sensing element through signal conditioning to the alarm output. This is the *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* that maps subsystem interactions as required by the protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR["DC Power Supply 2V to 3.5V"] --> HTR["Heater Coil Embedded in Bead"]
    HTR --> ABEAD["Active Bead Palladium Catalyst 500 deg C"]
    HTR --> RBEAD["Reference Bead Poisoned Catalyst 500 deg C"]
    GAS["Flammable Gas Molecules CH4 H2 LPG"] --> ADSORB["Diffuse into Porous Ceramic"]
    ADSORB --> ABEAD
    ABEAD --> COMBUST["Catalytic Oxidation Exothermic Reaction"]
    COMBUST --> HEAT["Temperature Rise Delta T 10 to 80 deg C"]
    HEAT --> DRES["Resistance Change Delta R in Active Bead"]
    RBEAD --> CONST["Reference Resistance Constant"]
    DRES --> BRIDGE["Wheatstone Bridge Four Arms"]
    CONST --> BRIDGE
    BRIDGE --> VOUT["Differential Output Voltage mV Signal"]
    VOUT --> AMP["Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 1000"]
    AMP --> LIN["Linearization Circuit Log to Linear Conversion"]
    LIN --> DISP["Display as Percent LEL"]
    DISP --> ALARM["Alarm Logic Warning at 10 percent LEL Danger at 60 percent LEL"]
    ALARM --> RELAY["Relay Output Ventilation Shutdown"]
```

### Diagram Walk-Through (Engineering Reading Order)

- The **DC Power Supply** simultaneously energizes the heater coil and excites the bridge.
- The **Active Bead** and **Reference Bead** are co-located in the same gas stream for matched thermal exposure.
- **Catalytic Oxidation** occurs *only* on the active bead, generating **exothermic heat**.
- The **Wheatstone Bridge** converts the differential temperature into a millivolt signal.
- The **Amplifier** and **Linearizer** condition the signal for display and alarm logic.
- The **Relay** can shut down HVAC, close valves, or activate deluge systems.

## 4.2 Laser Detector — Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **Open-Path TDLAS Laser Flammable Gas Detector** architecture, showing how the optical, electronic, and safety subsystems interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL["Microcontroller and DSP"] --> DFB["DFB Laser Diode Driver"]
    DFB --> LASER["NIR Laser Beam 1653.7 nm for CH4"]
    LASER --> BEAM1["Collimating Optics Beam Expander"]
    BEAM1 --> PATH["Open Path 5 m to 100 m Across Monitored Zone"]
    GAS["Methane Plume in Path"] --> ABSORB["Selective Photon Absorption by CH4 Molecules"]
    ABSORB --> PATH
    PATH --> BEAM2["Receiver Optics Focusing Lens"]
    BEAM2 --> PD["InGaAs Photodiode Detector"]
    PD --> TIA["Transimpedance Amplifier Converts uA to mV"]
    TIA --> ADC["Analog to Digital Converter 16 bit or 24 bit"]
    ADC --> DSP["Digital Signal Processing Lock in Detection Harmonic Detection"]
    DSP --> BEER["Beer Lambert Inversion Computes C times L"]
    BEER --> DISPL["Display Concentration in ppm or percent LEL"]
    DISPL --> ALARM2["Alarm Logic Threshold Check"]
    ALARM2 --> OUTPUT["Modbus or 4-20 mA Output to Fire Alarm Panel"]
    ALARM2 --> STATUS["Self Test Window Contamination Laser Health"]
```

### Diagram Walk-Through

- The **DFB Laser Driver** modulates the laser current to wavelength-tune across the absorption line (a technique called *wavelength modulation spectroscopy*).
- The **Open Path** is the heart of the system — one beam replaces dozens of point sensors.
- **Methane molecules** absorb photons only at the precise 1653.7 nm line.
- The **Photodiode** + **Transimpedance Amplifier** converts the small photocurrent to a usable voltage.
- The **DSP** uses **harmonic detection** (1f, 2f components) to suppress baseline drift and 1/f noise.
- The **Beer–Lambert Inversion** step is where the absorbance $A = \ln(I_0/I)$ is converted to concentration.
- **Self-Test** continuously monitors window cleanliness and laser output for fail-safe operation.

## 4.3 Combined Decision Logic — Which Detector to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start Selection of Flammable Gas Detector"] --> Q1{"Is oxygen greater than 10 percent in monitored zone?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Is poisoning risk high (silicones, lead, sulfides)?"}
    Q1 -->|"No INERTED ZONE"| LASER["Choose Laser TDLAS Detector"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| LASER
    Q2 -->|"No"| Q3{"Is fast response less than 1 s required?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| LASER
    Q3 -->|"No"| Q4{"Is long open path coverage 5 m to 100 m needed?"}
    Q4 -->|"Yes"| LASER
    Q4 -->|"No point detection is sufficient"| PELL["Choose Pellistor Catalytic Bead Detector"]
    PELL --> NOTE1["Cost effective mature technology calibrate every 90 days"]
    LASER --> NOTE2["Intrinsically safe selective ppm level sensitivity"]
    NOTE1 --> END["End"]
    NOTE2 --> END
```

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Rule of Thumb (Industry Standard):**
> - **Pellistor** → *Inside* the plant, point detection, normal air atmosphere, low cost.
> - **Laser** → *Perimeter / fenceline* monitoring, LNG storage, inerted enclosures, high-reliability zones.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between a pellistor and a laser-based flammable gas detector. List any two advantages of laser detectors over pellistors.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pellistor (Catalytic Bead)** sensor detects flammable gas by burning it catalytically on a heated bead and measuring the resulting resistance change in a Wheatstone bridge. A **Laser Detector (TDLAS)** detects gas by shining a narrow-linewidth laser beam across the monitored zone and measuring the absorption of light at a gas-specific wavelength using the Beer–Lambert Law.

**Two advantages of Laser over Pellistor:**

1. **No catalyst poisoning** — the laser has no catalytic surface that can be poisoned by silicones, lead, or sulfur compounds. Hence, it has a much longer operational life and lower maintenance in harsh chemical environments.
2. **Operates in oxygen-deficient / inerted atmospheres** — since the laser does not require combustion, it can detect gas even when O₂ < 10% vol, making it suitable for nitrogen-purged tanks and LNG enclosures.

*(Other valid points: faster response < 1 s, intrinsic safety, longer path coverage, higher selectivity — any two accepted.)*

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Q: What is the LEL of methane? Why are flammable gas detectors usually calibrated to alarm at 10% LEL and 60% LEL?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of methane (CH₄) is 5% by volume in air.** Below this concentration, the mixture is too lean to ignite; above the Upper Explosive Limit (UEL = 15% vol), it is too rich.

**Alarm Calibration Rationale:**

- **10% LEL — Pre-alarm / Warning:** Provides an *early* indication of gas accumulation, allowing operators to investigate, ventilate, or shut down processes *well before* the mixture becomes explosive. The 10% threshold is below the 25% LEL safety margin recommended by **NFPA 72** and **EN 60079-29-2**.
- **60% LEL — Main / Danger Alarm:** Triggers **automatic emergency actions** — shutting off gas supply, activating deluge/sprinkler systems, isolating electrical equipment, and initiating evacuation. 60% LEL is a *practical upper limit* below the 100% LEL ignition threshold, providing a safety margin of 40% LEL to account for sensor drift and response delays.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Pellistor-type flammable gas detector. Derive the expression for the Wheatstone bridge output voltage in terms of the resistance change of the active bead.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (Block Diagram Reference: Section 4.1):**

A pellistor consists of two matched platinum coils embedded in porous ceramic beads:
- **Active Bead:** Coated with **palladium or platinum catalyst** on an alumina support. Heated to ~500 °C.
- **Reference Bead:** Structurally identical but its catalyst is **poisoned** (with lead or rhodium) to prevent combustion. Heated to the same temperature for thermal matching.
- **Wheatstone Bridge:** The two beads form two arms of a four-arm bridge, with two fixed resistors completing the circuit.
- **Housing:** A sintered-metal (flame arrestor) enclosure allows gas diffusion but prevents flame propagation — required for **Ex d** or **Ex i** certification.

**Working Principle:**

1. A constant DC voltage $V_\text{cc}$ (2–3.5 V) is applied, heating both beads to ~500 °C. The bridge is balanced, output = 0 V.
2. Flammable gas diffuses through the flame arrestor and ceramic matrix of the active bead.
3. The catalyst promotes **catalytic oxidation** of the gas, releasing heat. For methane: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat}$.
4. The active bead's temperature rises by $\Delta T$, increasing its resistance by $\Delta R$ (platinum PTC effect).
5. The reference bead's resistance stays constant (no combustion).
6. The Wheatstone bridge produces a differential output voltage $V_\text{out}$ proportional to $\Delta R$ and hence to gas concentration.

**Derivation of Bridge Output:**

Let $R$ = nominal resistance of each arm, $\Delta R$ = change in active bead resistance.

- Voltage at node B (active bead midpoint): $V_B = V_\text{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$
- Voltage at node D (reference midpoint): $V_D = \dfrac{V_\text{cc}}{2}$
- Output: $V_\text{out} = V_B - V_D = V_\text{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{V_\text{cc}}{2}$

Simplifying (as derived in Section 3.1):
$$V_\text{out} = \frac{V_\text{cc}\,\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R}$$

For $\Delta R \ll R$ (linear regime):
$$\boxed{V_\text{out} \approx \frac{V_\text{cc}\,\Delta R}{4R}}$$

This voltage is amplified, linearized, and displayed as **% LEL**. *(Full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: A pellistor sensor is excited by 3.0 V. Each bridge arm has a nominal resistance of 12 Ω at 500 °C. During exposure to 25% LEL methane, the active bead resistance increases by 0.18 Ω. Calculate (i) the bridge output voltage, (ii) the sensitivity in mV per % LEL, and (iii) the bridge dissipation power.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $V_\text{cc} = 3.0\ \text{V}$, $R = 12\ \Omega$, $\Delta R = 0.18\ \Omega$ at 25% LEL.

**(i) Bridge Output Voltage:**

Using the exact formula:
$$V_\text{out} = \frac{V_\text{cc}\,\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R} = \frac{3.0 \times 0.18}{4 \times 12 + 2 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.54}{48.36}$$

$$V_\text{out} \approx 0.01117\ \text{V} = 11.17\ \text{mV}$$

(Linear approximation: $V_\text{out} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 0.18}{4 \times 12} = \dfrac{0.54}{48} = 11.25\ \text{mV}$ — error < 0.7%)

**(ii) Sensitivity:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{V_\text{out}}{\%LEL} = \frac{11.17\ \text{mV}}{25} = 0.4468\ \text{mV/\%LEL} \approx 0.447\ \text{mV/\%LEL}$$

**(iii) Bridge Dissipation Power:**

Each arm has resistance $R = 12\ \Omega$, so total bridge resistance (two parallel pairs) = $R$. Bridge current $I = V_\text{cc}/(2R) = 3.0/24 = 0.125\ \text{A}$.
$$P_\text{bridge} = V_\text{cc} \cdot I = 3.0 \times 0.125 = 0.375\ \text{W}$$

Or directly: $P_\text{bridge} = \dfrac{V_\text{cc}^2}{4R} = \dfrac{9.0}{48} = 0.1875\ \text{W}$ per pair — verify via full analysis: $P_\text{total} = I^2 \cdot R_\text{eq} = (0.125)^2 \cdot 24 = 0.375\ \text{W}$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Marks lost if:** students use the *exact* formula with the wrong sign convention for $\Delta R$ (it is a *positive* increase, not a decrease). Pellistor beads have a **positive temperature coefficient (PTC)**, so gas exposure → temperature rise → resistance rise.
> - **Common unit error:** Writing sensitivity as 0.447 V/%LEL instead of 0.447 mV/%LEL. Always check units.
> - **Power calculation pitfall:** Some students compute per-arm power. The question asks for *bridge dissipation* — sum across all four arms or use $V_\text{cc} \cdot I_\text{total}$.

---

### Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the working principle of a Tunable Diode Laser Absorption Spectroscopy (TDLAS) flammable gas detector. State why 1653.7 nm is chosen for methane detection. Derive the Beer–Lambert expression for concentration.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of TDLAS:**

1. A **Distributed Feedback (DFB) laser diode** emits a narrow-linewidth beam (< 0.001 nm) tuned to a specific absorption line of the target gas.
2. The laser driver **modulates** the diode current to sweep the wavelength across the absorption line (typically ±0.1 nm around line center).
3. The beam traverses the monitored open path of length $L$ (5–100 m) and is received by an **InGaAs photodiode**.
4. Methane (or target gas) molecules absorb photons at the line center, producing a dip in the received intensity.
5. The **second-harmonic (2f) detection** technique isolates the absorption signal from background and 1/f noise.
6. The absorbance $A = \ln(I_0/I)$ is computed, and the **Beer–Lambert inversion** yields concentration.

**Why 1653.7 nm for Methane?**

The 1653.7 nm wavelength corresponds to the **R(3) rotational-vibrational line of the 2ν₃ overtone band of CH₄**. It is selected because:
- It lies within an **atmospheric transparency window** (between H₂O absorption bands), minimizing interference.
- It is a **strong isolated line** for methane with minimal overlap from other hydrocarbons.
- The wavelength is achievable with **commercial InGaAs DFB laser diodes** at room temperature — no cryogenic cooling needed.
- It is **eye-safe** (Class 1 laser) at typical output powers (< 10 mW).

**Derivation of Beer–Lambert Expression:**

The intensity at depth $x$ in the gas follows:
$$\frac{dI(x)}{dx} = -\sigma(\lambda)\, n\, I(x)$$

Integrating from $0$ to $L$:
$$I(L) = I_0 \, e^{-\sigma(\lambda)\, n\, L}$$

Taking natural log:
$$\sigma(\lambda)\, n\, L = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = A(\lambda)$$

Converting number density $n$ to volumetric concentration $C$ (ppm) using $n = n_\text{air} \times 10^{-6} \times C$:
$$C = \frac{1}{\sigma(\lambda)\, L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{A(\lambda)}{\alpha(\lambda)\, L}$$

where $\alpha(\lambda) = \sigma(\lambda) \cdot n_\text{air} \times 10^{-6}$ is the absorption coefficient in $\text{ppm}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$.

$$\boxed{C \cdot L = \frac{\ln(I_0/I)}{\alpha(\lambda)} \quad \text{(path-integrated concentration in ppm·m)}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: An open-path methane laser detector operates at 1653.7 nm with absorption coefficient 0.16 ppm⁻¹m⁻¹. Across a 25 m path, the reference intensity is 1.000 mW and the received intensity is 0.840 mW. Calculate (i) the absorbance, (ii) the path-integrated concentration, (iii) the volumetric methane concentration in ppm, and (iv) the % LEL reading (LEL of CH₄ = 5% vol = 50,000 ppm).**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $\alpha = 0.16\ \text{ppm}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$, $L = 25\ \text{m}$, $I_0 = 1.000\ \text{mW}$, $I = 0.840\ \text{mW}$.

**(i) Absorbance:**
$$A = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1.000}{0.840}\right) = \ln(1.1905) \approx 0.1744$$

**(ii) Path-Integrated Concentration:**
$$C \cdot L = \frac{A}{\alpha} = \frac{0.1744}{0.16} = 1.090\ \text{ppm·m}$$

**(iii) Volumetric Concentration:**
$$C = \frac{C \cdot L}{L} = \frac{1.090}{25} = 0.0436\ \text{ppm} = 43.6\ \text{ppb}$$

**(iv) % LEL Reading:**
$$\%LEL = \frac{C}{C_\text{LEL}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0436}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 8.72 \times 10^{-5}\ \%LEL$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Laser Question):**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Computing $A = I_0 - I = 0.160$ mW (linear difference) instead of $\ln(I_0/I) = 0.1744$. **Always use the natural log of the ratio**, not the linear difference — Beer–Lambert is exponential.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert ppb to ppm or to %LEL. The detector's alarm logic works in %LEL — the student must do all unit conversions.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Reporting $C \cdot L$ and $C$ as the same quantity. The path-integrated value (ppm·m) scales with $L$; the volumetric concentration (ppm) does not.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Read this the night before the exam!**

### Core Concepts
- **Pellistor = Catalytic Combustion** → *burns* the gas on a heated catalyst bead → measures *resistance* change.
- **Laser = Optical Absorption** → *shines light* through the gas → measures *intensity* reduction.
- **LEL of Methane = 5% vol** (i.e., 50,000 ppm). **LEL of Propane ≈ 2.1% vol**. **LEL of Hydrogen = 4% vol**. Always convert to %LEL for alarm logic.
- **Alarm Thresholds:** Warning at **10% LEL**, Danger at **60% LEL** (per NFPA 72 and IS 15683).

### Governing Equations (Must Memorize)
- **Pellistor Bridge Output:** $V_\text{out} = \dfrac{V_\text{cc}\,\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R} \approx \dfrac{V_\text{cc}\,\Delta R}{4R}$
- **Beer–Lambert:** $I = I_0\, e^{-\alpha C L}$ → $C = \dfrac{1}{\alpha L}\ln(I_0/I)$
- **Platinum PTC:** $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$, $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{°C}^{-1}$

### Engineering Trade-offs
- **Pellistor Pros:** Cheap, broad-spectrum, linear. **Cons:** Poisonable, needs O₂, slow.
- **Laser Pros:** Intrinsically safe, selective, ppm sensitivity, works in inert gas. **Cons:** Expensive, line-of-sight, single-gas.

### Numerical Pitfalls
- **Sign of $\Delta R$** in Pellistor: it is *positive* (resistance rises with temperature).
- **Absorbance** uses $\ln(I_0/I)$, **never** the linear difference.
- **%LEL ≠ % vol** — convert using gas-specific LEL values.
- **ppm·m is path-integrated** — divide by $L$ to get volumetric ppm.

### Standards & Codes (Frequently Asked)
- **NFPA 72** — National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code (alarm thresholds).
- **EN 60079-29-2** — Selection, installation, use of flammable gas detectors.
- **IS 15683** — Indian Standard for fixed flammable gas detectors.
- **IEC 60079** — Explosive atmospheres standards (Ex d, Ex i, Ex e).

### KTU-Mandated Keywords (Use These in Answers for Full Marks)
- **Catalytic bead**, **Wheatstone bridge**, **Callendar–Van Dusen**, **reference compensator**, **flame arrestor**, **intrinsic safety (Ex i)**, **poisoning**, **%LEL**, **TDLAS**, **DFB laser**, **Beer–Lambert Law**, **second-harmonic detection (2f)**, **open-path**, **path-integrated concentration**, **absorption cross-section**, **wavelength modulation spectroscopy (WMS)**.

### One-Line Examiner's Insight
> **"If the question is about *heat*, the answer is **Pellistor**. If it is about *light*, the answer is **Laser**. If it asks *why* laser is better, the answer is *no poisoning, no oxygen needed, intrinsically safe, ppm sensitivity, long path*."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Fire Detection Devices as per Relevant Indian Standards Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing of Fire Detection Devices as per Relevant Indian Standards Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Detection Device:** A device that automatically senses the physical and/or chemical phenomena associated with the incipient stage of a fire, such as heat, smoke, flame, gas combustion products, or radiant energy, and provides a signal (visual, audible, electrical, or a combination) to initiate appropriate fire protective action.

**As per IS 2189:2008** (the governing Indian Standard for Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm Systems), a fire detection device is a *component of a fire detection and alarm system which automatically responds to one or more physical or chemical phenomena associated with a fire and provides an indication, alarm, or control signal*. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) has developed a series of specifications that govern the **testing, performance, marking, and certification** of these devices to ensure uniformity, reliability, and life-safety compliance.

### Key Governing Indian Standards (IS Code) — At a Glance

| IS Code | Title | Device Covered |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 2189:2008** | Selection, Installation & Maintenance of Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm Systems — Code of Practice | Entire FDAS |
| **IS 2175:1988** | Specification for Heat Sensitive Fire Detectors | Heat Detectors |
| **IS 11360:1985** | Specification for Point Type Smoke Detectors | Smoke Detectors |
| **IS 14604:1999** | Specification for Carbon Monoxide Fire Detectors | CO Detectors |
| **IS 15909:2012** | Specification for Flame Detectors | Flame Detectors |
| **IS 4643:1984** | Specification for Fire Detectors (General) | General Principles |
| **IS 6360:1971** | Specification for Fire Alarm Bells | Alarm Bells |
| **IS 1476:2001** | Specification for Fire Alarm Devices (Electronic) | Buzzers/Hooters |
| **IS 14610:2018** | Aspirating Smoke Detectors | ASD Systems |
| **IS 15105:2018** | Multi-sensor Fire Detectors | Multi-criteria Detectors |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sensory Nervous System" of a Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a building protected by a fire detection system as the **human body**.
> - **Fire Detectors (Smoke, Heat, Flame, CO)** = Sensory nerve endings (skin, nose, eyes)
> - **Fire Alarm Panel (FACP)** = The brain
> - **Notification Appliances (Bells, Hooters, Strobes)** = The mouth (screams)
> - **Cables & Loops** = The nervous system (spinal cord)
> - **Testing of devices** = A regular neurological health check-up (EEG, MRI, reflex tests)
>
> Just as a doctor runs *standardized tests* (blood pressure ≤ **120/80 mmHg**, reflex response time, etc.) to certify your nervous system is healthy, BIS mandates *standardized tests* (sensitivity, response time, endurance, environmental) to certify that each fire detector is fit for life-safety duty.

**Plain English Explanation:** Fire detection devices must be *tested* before being installed, periodically during their service life, and after any significant modification. Indian Standards (issued by BIS) prescribe **exact test methods, sample sizes, performance thresholds, and pass/fail criteria**. These tests ensure the detector will actually respond when there is a real fire (called "fire sensitivity") and will *not* give false alarms from cooking fumes, dust, or humidity (called "false alarm immunity"). Testing is conducted on **Type**, **Acceptance**, and **Routine** levels.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Detection Testing

- **Standard Test Fire TF1 / TF2 / TF3 / TF4 / TF5 / TF6** (as per EN 54 / adopted in IS) — Controlled reference fires: smoldering cotton, smoldering wood, glowing plastics, liquid heptane, liquid methylated spirit, liquid n-decane.
- **Operating Temperature for Heat Detectors:** Fixed temperature ratings of **57 °C, 70 °C, 90 °C, 140 °C, 180 °C** (most common: **57 °C** and **70 °C** for general premises).
- **Response Time Index (RTI):** A metric in seconds·metres^(1/2) used for rate-of-rise heat detectors; typical threshold ≤ **50 m^(1/2)·s^(1/2)** for ordinary detectors.
- **Optical Density Threshold for Smoke Detectors:** Specified in % obscuration per metre (**%/m**); typical alarm threshold between **2 %/m to 10 %/m** depending on sensitivity class.
- **Wavelength of IR/UV Flame Detector:** IR around **4.4 μm** (CO₂ emission peak), UV around **185–260 nm**.
- **Sound Pressure Level for Notification Appliances:** Minimum **65 dB(A) at 3 m** (occupiable space) and **75 dB(A) at bedhead** for sleeping risk.

## 1.4 The Three Hierarchies of Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Type Tests (Prototype Tests):** Performed once on a representative sample of a new design by an independent test lab (BIS-recognized or NABL-accredited) to qualify the product for the market. **Destructive tests** are permitted.
> 2. **Acceptance Tests (Batch Tests):** Performed on samples drawn from every manufactured batch by the manufacturer or buyer to ensure the batch conforms to the type-approved design. **Statistical sampling (IS 2500)** is used.
> 3. **Routine Tests (Production Tests):** Performed on **every individual unit** leaving the production line (e.g., high-voltage test, functional test, marking check). **Non-destructive** only.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic of Testing — Why Each Test Exists

A fire detector must perform **three simultaneous duties** throughout its life:
1. **Detect** a real fire quickly (Sensitivity).
2. **Reject** nuisance/false stimuli (Discrimination).
3. **Survive** the building environment for ≥ **10 years** (Endurance).

Therefore, the BIS testing framework logically maps each duty to a specific test category:

| Test Family | What it Validates | "Why" Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire Sensitivity Tests** | Detector responds to real fires | Verifies the *purpose* of the device |
| **Response Time Tests** | Speed of detection | Saves lives & property; informs evacuation time |
| **Directional / Orientation Tests** | Performance from any mounting angle | Detectors may be installed tilted in beams |
| **Environmental Tests** | Heat, cold, humidity, vibration, corrosion, dust | The building environment is hostile |
| **Electrical Tests** | Insulation, polarity, surge, EMC | Survives mains disturbances and lightning |
| **Mechanical Tests** | Vibration, shock, drop, impact | Survives handling, earthquakes, vibration |
| **Endurance / Life Tests** | Continuous operation under stress | Confirms 10-year service life |
| **Marking & Documentation Tests** | BIS Standard Mark, lot number, manual | Traceability and code compliance |

## 2.2 Test Categories for Heat Detectors (As per IS 2175)

### 2.2.1 Fixed Temperature Heat Detectors
- **Static Response Temperature Test:** Detector must alarm when its element reaches the rated temperature (e.g., **57 °C ± 5 °C** for a "57 °C" detector). Tested in a temperature-controlled **air oven** with a ramp rate of ≤ **1 °C/min**.
- **Response Time Test:** At a specific over-temperature (typically **30 °C above rated**), the detector must alarm within the maximum permitted time given by the standard curve.
- **Operational / Function Test:** Visual/audible alarm must be generated.
- **Insulation Resistance Test:** ≥ **100 MΩ** at **500 V DC** between live parts and body.
- **High Voltage (Flash) Test:** **1500 V AC rms** for **1 minute** with no breakdown.

### 2.2.2 Rate-of-Rise (ROR) Heat Detectors
- **Response Time Index (RTI) Test:** Calculated using a standard heat source (gas burner) at a defined radial distance.

$$RTI = \tau \cdot \sqrt{u}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = time constant of the detector element (seconds)
- $u$ = air velocity at the detector (m/s)

**Classification (as per IS 2175 / NFPA 72 aligned):**

| Class | RTI Range (m^(1/2)·s^(1/2)) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Ordinary (O) | 0 < RTI ≤ 50 | Low-ceiling, normal environments |
| Intermediate (I) | 50 < RTI ≤ 100 | Medium ambient motion |
| Fast (F) | 100 < RTI ≤ 300 | High-ceiling / warehouse |
| Ultra-Fast (U) | RTI > 300 | Special risk |

### 2.2.3 Environmental Tests
- **Dry Heat Test:** **+55 °C for 16 hours** (operational).
- **Cold Test:** **−10 °C for 16 hours**.
- **Damp Heat Cyclic:** **40 °C, 95 % RH, 6 cycles of 24 h** (as per IS 9000 series / IEC 60068-2-30).
- **Vibration Test:** **10–55 Hz, 0.5 g, 2 hours per axis** (sinusoidal).
- **Impact Test:** **Steel ball of 0.5 kg dropped from 250 mm**.
- **Corrosion Test:** **Salt spray (5 % NaCl) for 96 hours** (as per IS 9000/IEC 60068-2-52).

## 2.3 Test Categories for Smoke Detectors (As per IS 11360)

### 2.3.1 Operational / Fire Sensitivity Tests
The detector is exposed to standard **smoke aerosols** in a tunnel of cross-section ≈ **0.4 m² × 3 m long** with controlled airflow:

$$m = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \dot{m} \, dt \quad \text{(smoke dose reaching the detector, mg)}$$

- **Smoldering Pyrolysis Test (TF2):** Smoldering cotton wick.
- **Smoldering Combustion Test (TF3):** Smoldering wood.
- **Glowing Smolder Test (TF4):** Smoldering PU foam.

**Pass Criterion:** Detector must alarm when the smoke obscuration (m-value) falls within a specified band:

$$m_{\text{lower}} \leq m_{\text{alarm}} \leq m_{\text{upper}}$$

Typically for ionization/optical point detectors: $m$ between **0.5 dB/m and 1.5 dB/m** (≈ **10 %–30 % obscuration/m**).

### 2.3.2 False Alarm Immunity Tests
- **Cooking Fume Test:** Detector is exposed to **toast, frying fat, and cigarette smoke** to verify it does *not* alarm.
- **Aerosol Test:** Exposure to **aerosol sprays** (hairspray, deodorant).
- **Dust Test:** Exposure to **talcum powder / ISO 12103 A2 dust** for ≥ **30 minutes**.

### 2.3.3 Electrical Tests (Same as Heat Detectors)
- Insulation Resistance ≥ **100 MΩ at 500 V DC**.
- High Voltage **1500 V AC** for **1 min**.

### 2.3.4 Sensitivity Drift Test
Long-term sensitivity drift must not exceed ± **50 %** of the original alarm threshold over the **10-year** life.

## 2.4 Test Categories for Flame Detectors (As per IS 15909)

| Test | Method | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectral Response Test** | Exposure to **4.4 μm IR** band & **200–260 nm UV** band | Alarm at specified irradiance |
| **False Alarm Sun Test** | Exposure to direct/modulated sunlight (≤ **50 000 lux**) | No alarm |
| **False Alarm Lamp Test** | Exposure to **fluorescent, halogen, sodium** lamps at 1 m | No alarm |
| **Arc Welding Test** | Welding arc at 3 m | No alarm |
| **Black Body Test** | Heated black body at 700 K at 3 m | No alarm |
| **Sensitivity Test** | Standard heptane fire (1 ft² pan) at **10 m** | Alarm within 30 s |

**Irradiance Threshold:**

$$E_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{P_{\text{received}}}{A_{\text{aperture}}} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

Typical thresholds: IR — **10 W/m² at 4.4 μm**; UV — **1 W/m² at 220 nm**.

## 2.5 Test Categories for Notification Appliances (As per IS 6360 / IS 1476)

| Test | Method | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** | Measured at **3 m on axis** | ≥ **90 dB(A)** for general bells, ≥ **65 dB(A) at bedhead** |
| **Frequency Test** | 1/3-octave analysis | Dominant frequency within **500–3000 Hz** |
| **Polar Dispersion Test** | SPL measured every 15° in horizontal plane | Variation ≤ **±6 dB(A)** |
| **Duty Cycle Test** | Continuous operation **1 h ON / 1 h OFF for 1000 cycles** | No mechanical / electrical failure |
| **Flashing Light Intensity** | Effective candela at specified distance | **15 cd minimum, 75 cd typical** for visual signals |
| **Ingress Protection (IP)** | **IP 33C / IP 54** (as per IS 12063 / IEC 60529) | Outdoor units must pass dust & water jet tests |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table — Detection Testing**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Response Time Index | $RTI = \tau \sqrt{u}$ | $\leq 50$ to $300 \, \text{m}^{1/2}\text{s}^{1/2}$ | Heat detectors |
| 2 | Smoke Obscuration | $m = 10 \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | dB/m or %/m | Smoke detectors |
| 3 | Alarm Threshold Band | $m_L \leq m_a \leq m_U$ | $0.5 \text{ dB/m} \leq m_a \leq 1.5 \text{ dB/m}$ | Smoke detectors |
| 4 | Irradiance Threshold | $E = P/A$ | $10 \text{ W/m}^2$ (IR), $1 \text{ W/m}^2$ (UV) | Flame detectors |
| 5 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ | Bells / Hooters |
| 6 | Attenuation over distance | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | Free-field, point source | SPL at distance |
| 7 | Insulation Resistance | $R_{iso} \geq 100 \, \text{M}\Omega$ | at 500 V DC | All detectors |
| 8 | Withstand Voltage | $V_{\text{test}} = 1500 \, \text{V AC}$ | 1 minute | All detectors |
| 9 | Statistical Sampling Plan | IS 2500 (AQL basis) | AQL 1.0 / 2.5 typical | Acceptance tests |
| 10 | Service Life Expectation | $T_{\text{life}} \geq 10$ years | Continuous duty | All detectors |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of This Testing Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The testing regime prescribed by BIS directly enables:
> - **Insurance Compliance:** Insurers (like New India Assurance) mandate IS-marked devices for premium reduction.
> - **National Building Code (NBC 2016) Compliance:** NBC references IS 2189 for fire alarm systems.
> - **TAC (Tunnel Advisory Council) & MoEFCC Clearances:** For industrial projects, IS-compliant devices are mandatory.
> - **Tariff Advisory Committee (TAC) India:** Certifies fire protection systems in industrial and commercial risks using IS standards as the benchmark.
> - **International Trade:** IS-marked products can use BIS Standard Mark for global tenders aligned with BIS–IEC equivalence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Response Time Index (RTI) from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the formula $RTI = \tau \sqrt{u}$ and apply it to a rate-of-rise heat detector test.

**Step 1 — Set up the thermal energy balance at the detector element.**

The detector sensing element is modelled as a lumped thermal mass. The rate of change of its temperature $T_s$ above ambient $T_\infty$ is governed by convection from the moving hot gas and radiation losses:

$$m_s c_s \frac{dT_s}{dt} = h A_s (T_g - T_s) - \epsilon \sigma A_s (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

**Step 2 — Linearise the radiation term** for small temperature differences (typical detector use case) using $T_s = T_\infty + \theta$, so $T_s^4 - T_\infty^4 \approx 4 T_\infty^3 \theta$. Combining convective and radiative heat transfer into an effective coefficient $h_{\text{eff}} = h + 4 \epsilon \sigma T_\infty^3$:

$$m_s c_s \frac{d\theta}{dt} = h_{\text{eff}} A_s (\theta_g - \theta_s)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the time constant.**

$$\tau = \frac{m_s c_s}{h_{\text{eff}} A_s} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

The governing ODE simplifies to:

$$\tau \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \theta = \theta_g$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the case of a sudden hot gas arrival at velocity $u$.** The forced-convection coefficient scales with the square root of velocity (per standard heat-transfer correlations for cross-flow over a small body):

$$h_{\text{eff}} \propto \sqrt{u} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{u}}$$

Therefore, the *dimensionless product* $\tau \sqrt{u}$ is independent of the test air velocity and is a true property of the detector:

$$RTI = \tau \sqrt{u} \quad (\text{units: } \text{m}^{1/2} \cdot \text{s}^{1/2})$$

**Step 5 — Apply to a real test scenario.** A heat detector with rated temperature **57 °C** is tested in a hot-air stream at **$T_g - T_\infty = 30$ K** (i.e., 30 K over-temperature) and air velocity **$u = 1$ m/s**. The measured time constant is $\tau = 25$ s.

$$RTI = 25 \times \sqrt{1} = 25 \text{ m}^{1/2}\text{s}^{1/2}$$

Since **$RTI \leq 50$**, the detector is classified as **"Ordinary"** class and is suitable for general low-ceiling applications. **PASS.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Smoke Obscuration Threshold and Alarm Limit

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the relationship between smoke concentration and the alarm decision of an optical smoke detector.

**Step 1 — Define light obscuration.** When a beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through smoke of path length $L$, the Beer–Lambert law gives:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-m L}$$

where $m$ is the **extinction coefficient** (per metre). The obscuration in dB/m is:

$$m_{\text{dB/m}} = \frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Converting to percent obscuration per metre** (commonly used in IS 11360 / NFPA 72):

$$\% \text{ obscuration/m} = \left(1 - 10^{-m_{\text{dB/m}} / 10}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Alarm Decision.** A point-type photoelectric detector scatters light into a photodiode. The alarm is triggered when the scattered-light signal $S$ exceeds a reference $S_{\text{ref}}$ set during commissioning. This corresponds to a smoke obscuration threshold:

$$m_{\text{alarm}} = \frac{1}{L} \ln\left(\frac{S_{\text{ref}}}{S_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Pass Criterion per IS 11360.** For a smoldering-wood reference fire (Test Fire TF3), the alarm must trigger within the range:

$$0.5 \text{ dB/m} \leq m_{\text{alarm}} \leq 1.5 \text{ dB/m}$$

Converting these to percent obscuration/m:
- $0.5 \text{ dB/m} \approx 10.7 \% / \text{m}$
- $1.5 \text{ dB/m} \approx 29.2 \% / \text{m}$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical.** A detector under TF3 test triggers alarm at $m = 0.8$ dB/m. Check:

$$0.5 \leq 0.8 \leq 1.5 \quad \checkmark \text{ PASS}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sound Pressure Level of a Notification Appliance

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Compute the SPL at a given distance and verify against IS 6360 / NFPA 72 requirements.

**Step 1 — Inverse-square law in free field for a point source:**

$$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Sample test data:** A fire alarm bell is rated **95 dB(A) at 1 m** on axis. The occupant's bedhead is **4 m** from the nearest bell.

$$L_{p,\text{bed}} = 95 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{1}\right) = 95 - 20 \times 0.602 = 95 - 12.04$$

$$L_{p,\text{bed}} \approx 82.96 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check** (per IS 6360 / NFPA 72): Requirement is **≥ 75 dB(A) at bedhead** for sleeping risk.

$$82.96 \text{ dB(A)} \geq 75 \text{ dB(A)} \quad \checkmark \text{ PASS}$$

**Step 4 — Add ambient noise margin** (NFPA 72 requires ≥ 10 dB(A) above ambient). If ambient bedroom noise is **35 dB(A)**:

$$L_{p,\text{bed}} - L_{\text{ambient}} = 82.96 - 35 = 47.96 \text{ dB(A)} \geq 10 \text{ dB(A)} \quad \checkmark \text{ PASS}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Test Selection by Detector Type

| Test | Heat (IS 2175) | Smoke (IS 11360) | Flame (IS 15909) | CO (IS 14604) | Notification (IS 6360) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fire Sensitivity | ✓ (TF1–TF6 adapted) | ✓ (TF2, TF3, TF4) | ✓ (1 ft² heptane) | ✓ (CO test gas) | N/A |
| Response Time | ✓ (RTI method) | ✓ (smoke tunnel) | ✓ (≤ 30 s) | ✓ (≤ 90 s) | ✓ (≤ 10 s) |
| Insulation Resistance | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| High Voltage | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Dry Heat (+55 °C) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Cold (−10 °C) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Damp Heat Cyclic | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Vibration | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Impact / Shock | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Salt Spray (corrosion) | ✓ (96 h) | ✓ (96 h) | ✓ (96 h) | ✓ (96 h) | ✓ (96 h) |
| EMC / Surge | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| False Alarm Rejection | ✓ (sun, lamp) | ✓ (cooking, dust) | ✓ (sun, arc, lamp) | ✓ (vehicle exhaust) | N/A |
| Sensitivity Drift | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | N/A |
| Marking Check | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| SPL Test | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | ✓ (≥ 90 dB at 1 m) |
| Polar Dispersion | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | ✓ (≤ ±6 dB) |

## 3.5 Sampling Plan Example (Acceptance Test)

As per **IS 2500** (Sampling procedures for inspection by attributes), a batch of **500 heat detectors** is offered for acceptance at **AQL 1.0 %** (Acceptable Quality Level), single sampling plan, normal inspection.

- **Sample size** $n$ = 32 units
- **Acceptance number** $Ac$ = 1
- **Rejection number** $Re$ = 2

If 2 or more detectors fail any test in the sample, the entire batch is **rejected**. If 0 or 1 fail, the batch is **accepted**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Test Sequence for a New Fire Detector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Submitted for Type Test] --> B[Visual & Marking Check]
    B --> C[Electrical Tests]
    C --> C1[Insulation Resistance >= 100 Mohm]
    C --> C2[High Voltage 1500 V AC for 1 min]
    C2 --> D[Fire Sensitivity Tests]
    D --> D1[TF1 Smouldering Cotton]
    D --> D2[TF2 Smouldering Wood]
    D --> D3[TF3 Glowing Plastic]
    D --> D4[TF4 Liquid Heptane]
    D4 --> E[Response Time Test]
    E --> F[Environmental Stress Tests]
    F --> F1[Dry Heat 55 C 16 h]
    F --> F2[Cold minus 10 C 16 h]
    F --> F3[Damp Heat Cyclic 95 pct RH 6 cycles]
    F --> F4[Vibration 10 to 55 Hz]
    F --> F5[Impact 0.5 kg 250 mm]
    F --> F6[Salt Spray 96 h]
    F6 --> G[EMC Surge Tests]
    G --> H[Sensitivity Drift Long Term]
    H --> I{All Tests Pass?}
    I -->|Yes| J[IS Standard Mark Awarded]
    I -->|No| K[Rejection and Design Review]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three-Tier Testing Hierarchy (Type / Acceptance / Routine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Type Tests - Prototype]
    T1A[1 unit or small sample] --> T1B[Destructive allowed]
    T1B --> T1C[BIS recognised lab only]
    T1C --> T1D[IS Standard Mark]
    end
    subgraph T2[Acceptance Tests - Batch]
    T2A[Sampling per IS 2500] --> T2B[Manufacturer or buyer lab]
    T2B --> T2C[Non destructive only]
    T2C --> T2D[Batch release]
    end
    subgraph T3[Routine Tests - Production]
    T3A[Every single unit] --> T3B[Production line only]
    T3B --> T3C[Non destructive only]
    T3C --> T3D[QC stamp]
    end
    T1D --> T2
    T2D --> T3
    T3D --> U[Installed in Building]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Test Architecture for Smoke Detector Sensitivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Smoke Source] --> S1[Smouldering Wood TF3]
    S1 --> ST[Smoke Tunnel 0.4 m2 x 3 m]
    ST --> D[Detector Under Test]
    ST --> M[Photometer Measures m dB per m]
    M --> MC{m in Alarm Band}
    MC -->|Yes 0.5 to 1.5 dB per m| PASS[Pass]
    MC -->|No| FAIL[Fail]
    D --> DA[Alarm Decision Threshold m alarm]
    DA --> MC
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Flame Detector False Alarm Rejection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FD[Flame Detector Under Test] --> SA[IR 4.4 micro metre Channel]
    FD --> SB[UV 200 to 260 nm Channel]
    SA --> AC[AND Logic with flicker analysis]
    SB --> AC
    AC --> AR{Test Stimulus}
    AR -->|Heptane Fire 10 m| P1[Alarm expected PASS]
    AR -->|Direct Sunlight 50 klx| P2[No alarm PASS]
    AR -->|Arc Welding 3 m| P3[No alarm PASS]
    AR -->|Halogen Lamp 1 m| P4[No alarm PASS]
    AR -->|Black Body 700 K| P5[No alarm PASS]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Notification Appliance SPL Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Notification Appliance] --> N1[Fire Alarm Bell]
    N1 --> N2[Mounted on Test Wall 2.5 m]
    N2 --> N3[M1 SPL at 1 m on axis]
    N2 --> N4[M2 SPL at 3 m on axis]
    N2 --> N5[M3 SPL at 15 deg off axis]
    N2 --> N6[M4 SPL at 90 deg off axis]
    N3 --> CHK{Compliance Check}
    N4 --> CHK
    N5 --> CHK
    N6 --> CHK
    CHK -->|SPL >= 90 dB A at 1 m| OK1[PASS]
    CHK -->|Variation <= 6 dB A polar| OK2[PASS]
    CHK -->|Dominant freq in 500 to 3000 Hz| OK3[PASS]
```

## 4.6 Schematic — Test Fail Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Test Result Received] --> E{Result within limits?}
    E -->|Yes| AP[Accept and Record]
    E -->|No| ID{Failure type}
    ID -->|Critical Safety| RJ[Reject entire lot and recall]

    RT --> R
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the three categories of tests prescribed by BIS for fire detection devices. State the purpose of each category in one sentence.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Type Tests:** Conducted once on a prototype to qualify a new design for the market. Purpose: establish that the product meets the standard in every respect (destructive tests allowed).
> 2. **Acceptance Tests:** Conducted on samples drawn from a production batch to confirm the batch conforms to the type-approved design. Purpose: ensure batch-by-batch consistency for the buyer.
> 3. **Routine Tests:** Conducted on every individual unit leaving the production line. Purpose: identify manufacturing defects in each unit before it reaches the market (non-destructive only).

**CO Mapping:** CO3 — *Apply BIS testing protocols for fire detection devices.*
**Bloom's Level:** Remembering (L1)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the BIS code that governs the testing of (a) heat detectors and (b) point-type smoke detectors. Mention two key performance parameters tested under each.**

**Model Answer:**

> - (a) **IS 2175** governs heat detectors. Key parameters tested: (i) **Response Time Index (RTI)** for ROR detectors, and (ii) **Static response temperature** for fixed-temperature detectors.
> - (b) **IS 11360** governs point-type smoke detectors. Key parameters tested: (i) **Smoke obscuration threshold** (m-value in dB/m) and (ii) **False-alarm immunity to cooking fumes and dust**.

**CO Mapping:** CO3 — *Identify and apply relevant IS standards.*
**Bloom's Level:** Remembering (L1)

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Heat Detector RTI

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **Rate-of-Rise (ROR) heat detector** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A heat detector of rated temperature **57 °C** is tested in a hot-air stream at over-temperature **$\Delta T = 30$ K** and air velocity **$u = 2$ m/s**. The measured time constant is **$\tau = 20$ s**. Compute the **Response Time Index (RTI)**, classify the detector as per IS 2175, and state whether it is suitable for a high-ceiling warehouse. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Principle of Operation:**
A Rate-of-Rise (ROR) heat detector responds to a *rapid* increase in ambient air temperature, typically a rate exceeding **8.3 °C/min (≈ 1 °C per 7 s)**. Internally it consists of:

1. A **diaphragm air chamber** (vented to the surrounding air through a calibrated leak orifice).
2. A **snap-action electrical contact** mounted on the diaphragm.
3. The chamber is connected to a small vent (leak).

When the surrounding air temperature rises rapidly, the air inside the chamber expands faster than it can leak out, increasing the chamber pressure, deflecting the diaphragm, and closing the contacts to generate the alarm.

**Sketch:**

```
   +-------------------+   Vent (leak)
   |    Air Chamber    |  O
   | +---------------+ |  |  (calibrated orifice)
   | |   Diaphragm   | |
   | |  with contact | |
   | +---------------+ |
   +-------------------+
            |
            +--- to FACP (Fire Alarm Control Panel)
```

**Limitations:** Slow-rise fires may not trigger the detector; hence IS 2175 mandates a *combined fixed + ROR* detector for general use.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Compute RTI using the standard formula:**

$$RTI = \tau \cdot \sqrt{u}$$

$$RTI = 20 \, \text{s} \times \sqrt{2 \, \text{m/s}} = 20 \times 1.414 = 28.28 \, \text{m}^{1/2}\text{s}^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Classify as per IS 2175 / IS 11360 / NFPA 72-aligned table:**

| Class | RTI Range | Match? |
|---|---|---|
| Ordinary | $0 < RTI \leq 50$ | ✓ |
| Intermediate | $50 < RTI \leq 100$ | ✗ |
| Fast | $100 < RTI \leq 300$ | ✗ |
| Ultra-Fast | $> 300$ | ✗ |

**Result: ORDINARY class detector.**

**Step 3 — Suitability for a high-ceiling warehouse:**

High-ceiling warehouses (ceiling heights **> 9 m**) require either **"Fast" or "Ultra-Fast"** class detectors because the thermal plume from a fire takes longer to reach the ceiling. An "Ordinary" class detector would generate **delayed or no alarm** under such geometry.

**Conclusion:** The detector is **NOT suitable** for a high-ceiling warehouse. A "Fast" or "Ultra-Fast" class detector (RTI > 100) must be used.

**CO Mapping:** CO3, CO4 — *Apply and analyse IS-based testing criteria for detectors.*
**Bloom's Level:** Applying & Analysing (L3, L4)

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Smoke Detector Sensitivity Test

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 3]**
> **(a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, describe the **smoke tunnel test procedure** for evaluating a point-type smoke detector as per **IS 11360**. Mention the reference test fires and the pass criteria. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A photoelectric smoke detector triggers an alarm when the smoke obscuration reaches **$m = 0.8$ dB/m**. Compute the **percent obscuration per metre** at the alarm threshold and verify whether the detector meets the IS 11360 pass band for a smouldering-wood test fire. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Smoke Tunnel Test Procedure (per IS 11360 / IEC 60068-1 / IS 14604):**

**Apparatus:**
- A rectangular smoke tunnel of cross-section approximately **0.4 m² × 3 m long**.
- A smoke source (reference test fire).
- A blower / fan providing uniform airflow.
- A photometer to measure the smoke extinction coefficient $m$ in **dB/m**.
- The detector under test (DUT) mounted on the tunnel ceiling.
- A reference detector (calibrated) co-located with the DUT.

**Test Fires (IS / EN 54-aligned):**
- **TF2:** Smouldering cotton wick.
- **TF3:** Smouldering wood (the most common reference).
- **TF4:** Glowing smouldering PU foam.
- **TF1:** Open cellulosic (smouldering wood variant).

**Procedure:**
1. Mount the DUT in the tunnel.
2. Ignite the reference test fire and introduce smoke at a controlled rate.
3. Measure the **smoke extinction coefficient $m$** using the photometer as smoke concentration builds.
4. Record the **$m$-value at the moment the DUT generates an alarm**, denoted $m_{\text{alarm}}$.
5. Repeat for each test fire.

**Pass Criterion (per IS 11360):**

$$0.5 \, \text{dB/m} \leq m_{\text{alarm}} \leq 1.5 \, \text{dB/m}$$

**Sketch:**

```
+----------------------------------------+
|  PHOTOMETER         D.U.T.             |
|  []<----beam-------->[]<----alarm----> |
+----------------------------------------+
|        SMOKE TUNNEL  (0.4 m² x 3 m)    |
+----------------------------------------+
|     [Smoke Source]   [Blower]          |
+----------------------------------------+
```

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Convert smoke obscuration from dB/m to %/m using:**

$$\%/\text{m} = \left(1 - 10^{-m_{\text{dB/m}}/10}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $m = 0.8$ dB/m:**

$$\%/\text{m} = \left(1 - 10^{-0.8/10}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - 10^{-0.08}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $10^{-0.08}$:**

$$10^{-0.08} = e^{-0.08 \times \ln 10} = e^{-0.08 \times 2.3026} = e^{-0.1842} = 0.8318$$

**Step 4 — Compute the percent obscuration:**

$$\%/\text{m} = (1 - 0.8318) \times 100 = 0.1682 \times 100 = 16.82 \, \% / \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify pass band:**

Lower limit: $0.5 \text{ dB/m} \approx 10.7 \, \%/\text{m}$
Upper limit: $1.5 \text{ dB/m} \approx 29.2 \, \%/\text{m}$

Since **$10.7 \leq 16.82 \leq 29.2$**, the detector **PASSES** the IS 11360 acceptance band for smouldering-wood test fire.

**CO Mapping:** CO3, CO4 — *Apply IS testing protocol to smoke detection equipment.*
**Bloom's Level:** Applying & Evaluating (L3, L5)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make in testing questions (costly marks!):**
> 1. **Confusing IS codes:** Students mix up IS 2175 (heat), IS 11360 (smoke), and IS 2189 (the overall FDAS code). IS 2189 governs *system design and installation*, while IS 2175/11360 govern *device testing*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting units in RTI:** RTI must carry units of **m^(1/2)·s^(1/2)**, not just a number. Omitting units = −0.5 mark.
> 3. **Wrong logarithm base:** In SPL equations, always use **base 10** ($20 \log_{10}$). Using $\ln$ (natural log) is a common error.
> 4. **Misclassifying the detector class:** If the answer says "Ordinary" for an RTI of 280, the student has clearly not memorised the classification table. Memorise: **0–50 O, 50–100 I, 100–300 F, >300 U**.
> 5. **Forgetting pass/fail conclusion:** Every numerical answer must end with a **clear "PASS" or "FAIL" statement** with a justification — examiners specifically look for this.
> 6. **Not converting between dB/m and %/m:** Examiners often ask one form and give the data in the other. Practice the conversion **two-way**.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch:** Even a poor sketch gets 1 mark; no sketch = −1 to −2 marks depending on the part weightage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — Testing of Fire Detection Devices: 10-Point Rapid Revision**

- 🔹 **IS 2189:2008** is the *umbrella* standard for *Fire Detection and Alarm Systems* (FDAS) — it covers selection, installation, and maintenance, but **defers device testing to product-specific standards**.

- 🔹 **IS 2175** → Heat detectors. **Two families:** Fixed-Temperature and Rate-of-Rise. **RTI classification** table: O ≤ 50 < I ≤ 100 < F ≤ 300 < U.

- 🔹 **IS 11360** → Point-type smoke detectors (photoelectric & ionization). **Pass band: 0.5–1.5 dB/m** for TF2/TF3 test fires.

- 🔹 **IS 15909** → Flame detectors. **Two spectral bands:** IR (4.4 μm CO₂ peak) and UV (200–260 nm). **False-alarm rejection** tests against sunlight, arc welding, halogen lamps, and black-body radiators are **mandatory**.

- 🔹 **IS 14604** → CO fire detectors. **Sensitivity in ppm CO**; response time typically ≤ 90 s at alarm level.

- 🔹 **IS 6360 / IS 1476** → Notification appliances (bells/hooters). **SPL ≥ 90 dB(A) at 1 m**, **dominant frequency 500–3000 Hz**, **polar dispersion ≤ ±6 dB(A)**.

- 🔹 **Three test tiers:** **Type (prototype) → Acceptance (batch, IS 2500 sampling) → Routine (every unit)**. Routine tests are *always* non-destructive.

- 🔹 **Universal electrical tests:** Insulation resistance **≥ 100 MΩ at 500 V DC**; High voltage withstand **1500 V AC rms for 1 minute** with no breakdown.

- 🔹 **Environmental tests** as per IS 9000 / IEC 60068: dry heat (+55 °C/16 h), cold (−10 °C/16 h), damp heat cyclic (40 °C, 95 % RH, 6 cycles), vibration (10–55 Hz, 0.5 g), impact (0.5 kg/250 mm), salt spray (96 h).

- 🔹 **Conversion to remember cold:** $m_{\text{dB/m}}$ to $\%/\text{m}$ uses $\%/\text{m} = (1 - 10^{-m/10}) \times 100$. **RTI** uses $\tau \sqrt{u}$. **SPL over distance** uses $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$. **Beer–Lambert** uses $I = I_0 e^{-mL}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristics of Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics of Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Characteristics of Detectors** are the quantitative and qualitative parameters that define the operational efficacy, reliability, and environmental adaptability of a **Fire Detection System (FDS)**. These characteristics determine the detector's ability to accurately sense the byproducts of combustion (smoke, heat, flame, gas) and generate an alarm signal within the prescribed response time while minimizing **False Alarm Rate (FAR)**.

In the context of **NBC 2016 (India)** and **IS 2189:2008**, the performance of a fire detector is judged against standardized test fires (TF1 to TF6 as per **EN 54**, adopted in IS standards) that simulate realistic fire scenarios.

The seven universally accepted performance characteristics of a fire detector are:

1. **Sensitivity** – Ability to respond to low concentrations of fire signatures.
2. **Response Time (Time-to-Alarm)** – Latency between detection threshold crossing and alarm activation.
3. **Stability & Reliability** – Consistency of response under repeated identical stimuli.
4. **Selectivity / Specificity** – Ability to discriminate between fire and non-fire phenomena.
5. **Coverage Area / Field of View** – Spatial volume monitored by the detector.
6. **Environmental Tolerance** – Operability under temperature, humidity, and electromagnetic variation.
7. **False Alarm Resistance (FAR)** – Immunity to non-fire stimuli.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Diabetic Patient's Glucometer**
> Think of a fire detector as a **glucometer** used by a diabetic patient. The glucometer has specific performance characteristics:
> - **Sensitivity** → How small a drop of blood it needs (analogous to how little smoke it needs).
> - **Response Time** → How quickly the reading appears (analogous to alarm delay).
> - **Selectivity** → It must read *glucose* and not be fooled by vitamin C in the juice box.
> - **False Alarm Resistance** → It should not trigger when the patient simply eats a candy.
>
> Just as a doctor trusts a precise glucometer, a fire engineer trusts a detector with high sensitivity, low response time, and high selectivity to safeguard lives in a high-rise building.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Standardized Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Test Fires TF1–TF6** per **EN 54-7 / IS 11360** for smoke detectors.
> - **Operating Voltage:** Typically **24 V DC** (per **IS 2189:2008** for addressable systems) or **12 V / 24 V DC** for conventional detectors.
> - **Quiescent Current:** Typically **50 μA to 500 μA** (varies by type).
> - **Alarm Current:** Typically **20 mA to 100 mA**.
> - **Operating Temperature Range:** **-10°C to +55°C** (per **IS 11360** for ionization smoke detectors).
> - **Relative Humidity Tolerance:** Up to **95% RH (non-condensing)**.
> - **IP Rating:** Typically **IP 43** for indoor, **IP 55 / IP 65** for outdoor detectors.
> - **Threshold Sensitivity (Smoke):** Obscuration levels of **0.05 dB/m to 0.2 dB/m** (light scatter principle).
> - **Threshold Sensitivity (Heat):** Fixed at **57°C, 68°C, or 93°C** (per **IS 2175:1988** Class A1, A2, B).

---
