# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Performance Characteristic

### A. Sensitivity (S)
- **Definition:** The minimum stimulus (smoke obscuration, temperature rise, flame radiation) required to trigger the alarm.
- **Why it matters:** Higher sensitivity means earlier detection, which is critical in **libraries, server rooms, hospitals** where early warning saves lives and data.
- **How it is measured:**
  - For **smoke detectors** → measured in **% obscuration per metre (% obs/m)** or **dB/m**.
  - For **heat detectors** → measured in **°C** (fixed temperature) or **°C/min** (rate-of-rise).
  - For **flame detectors** → measured in **kW/m²** of radiant heat flux.
  - For **gas detectors** → measured in **ppm (parts per million)** or **% LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Sensitivity is a **double-edged sword**. Ultra-sensitive detectors increase **False Alarm Rate (FAR)**. KTU examiners frequently test this trade-off in Module 3 questions.

### B. Response Time (τ)
- **Definition:** The time elapsed between the actual fire stimulus reaching the detector and the generation of the alarm signal.
- **KTU Standard:** Per **IS 2189:2008**, the maximum response time for detectors in life-safety applications is **30 seconds** from threshold crossing.
- **Why critical:** A detector with high sensitivity but slow response is useless in **fast-flaming fires (TF6)** such as a petrol pool fire.

### C. Stability & Reliability
- **Definition:** The ability of the detector to give the **same response** under **identical conditions** over time.
- **Engineering metric:** **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — typically **> 100,000 hours** for modern addressable detectors.
- **Drift compensation:** Modern detectors use **drift compensation algorithms** to recalibrate their baseline sensitivity to account for sensor aging.

### D. Selectivity / Specificity
- **Definition:** The detector's capacity to respond *only* to the target fire signature.
- **Examples:**
  - A **photoelectric smoke detector** must not trigger on dust or steam.
  - An **IR flame detector** must not trigger on sunlight or welding arcs.
  - A **CO detector** must not trigger on vehicle exhaust in a parking garage (this is why **cross-sensitivity** is a major design consideration).

### E. Coverage Area / Field of View
- **Definition:** The maximum spatial volume within which the detector can effectively sense a fire.
- **KTU/IS Standard Values (per IS 2189:2008, Clause 4.4):**

| Detector Type | Max Coverage Area | Max Mounting Height |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Smoke Detector (Point)** | **80 m²** | **10.5 m** |
| **Heat Detector (Point)** | **50 m²** | **9.0 m** |
| **Optical Beam Smoke Detector** | **1500 m²** (per beam) | **25 m** |
| **Aspirating Smoke Detector (VESDA)** | **2000 m²** (sampling pipe) | **25 m** |
| **Flame Detector** | Cone of vision ~ **20–40 m radius** | Per manufacturer |

### F. Environmental Tolerance
- Detectors must function under:
  - **Temperature:** **-10°C to +55°C** (indoor), **-40°C to +70°C** (outdoor/explosion-proof).
  - **Humidity:** Up to **95% RH**.
  - **Air Velocity:** Smoke detectors must operate up to **20 m/s air velocity** per **EN 54-7**.
  - **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC):** Per **EN 50130-4**.

### G. False Alarm Resistance (FAR)
- **Definition:** Probability of a non-fire event triggering an alarm.
- **KTU Benchmark:** For a well-designed system, **FAR < 1 false alarm per 100 detector-years**.
- **Causes of false alarms:**
  1. Cooking fumes (kitchens) → use **heat detectors** instead of smoke.
  2. Steam (bathrooms) → use **heat detectors**.
  3. Dust (factories) → use **aspirating detectors with filters**.
  4. Cigarette smoke → use **multi-criteria detectors**.
  5. Welding/lighting → use **UV/IR flame detectors with spectral filters**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE 2024. Memorize the table.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Response Time** | $ \tau = t_{alarm} - t_{stimulus} $ | seconds (s) | Direct measurement of detection latency |
| 2 | **Sensitivity (Smoke Obscuration)** | $ m = \frac{10}{d} \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) $ | dB/m | Beer-Lambert Law for smoke density |
| 3 | **Sensitivity (Heat ROR)** | $ \frac{dT}{dt} \geq 8.3 \, °C/min $ | °C/min | Rate-of-Rise threshold per **IS 2175** |
| 4 | **Coverage Area (Smoke)** | $ A_c = \pi r^2 \leq 80 \, m^2 $ | m² | Per **IS 2189:2008** |
| 5 | **False Alarm Rate** | $ FAR = \frac{N_{false}}{N_{detectors} \times T_{years}} $ | events/100 det-years | Reliability metric |
| 6 | **Detector Spacing (S)** | $ S = \sqrt{A_c} \leq 9.0 \, m $ | m | Maximum horizontal spacing |
| 7 | **MTBF** | $ MTBF = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}} $ | hours | Reliability engineering |
| 8 | **Threshold Sensitivity (Heat Fixed)** | $ T_{threshold} \in \{57, 68, 93\} \, °C $ | °C | Per **IS 2175:1988** |
| 9 | **Radiant Heat Flux Sensitivity** | $ q \geq 0.1 \, kW/m^2 $ | kW/m² | Flame detector response |
| 10 | **Spectral Response (UV)** | $ \lambda = 185 \text{ to } 260 \, nm $ | nm | UV flame detector |
| 11 | **Spectral Response (IR)** | $ \lambda = 4.3 \, \mu m $ (CO₂ band) | μm | IR flame detector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These characteristics directly govern the **layout design** of detectors in a building. A fire engineer in Kerala must select detector type, sensitivity, and spacing based on the **occupancy type** (per **NBC 2016 Table 3 Part 4**) and **fire load** of the compartment.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Smoke Obscuration Sensitivity (Beer-Lambert Law)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Derivation:** This is frequently asked in Part B (14 marks) questions.

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Law
When light passes through a smoke-filled medium, its intensity decreases exponentially with the optical path length and smoke concentration.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a monochromatic light beam of initial intensity $I_0$ passing through a smoke layer of thickness $d$ (in metres).

**Step 2:** The incremental reduction in intensity $dI$ over an infinitesimal thickness $dx$ is proportional to:
$$ dI = -k \cdot C \cdot I \, dx $$

where:
- $k$ = specific extinction coefficient of smoke (m²/g)
- $C$ = mass concentration of smoke (g/m³)
- $I$ = instantaneous intensity at position $x$

**Step 3:** Rearrange the differential equation:
$$ \frac{dI}{I} = -k \cdot C \cdot dx $$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $x = 0$ (where $I = I_0$) to $x = d$ (where $I = I$):
$$ \int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -k \cdot C \int_{0}^{d} dx $$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:
$$ \ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -k \cdot C \cdot d $$

**Step 6:** Apply the exponential function to both sides:
$$ I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot C \cdot d} $$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law** for smoke attenuation.

**Step 7:** Define the **obscuration per metre** $m$ (in dB/m) as:
$$ m = \frac{10}{d} \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) $$

**Step 8:** Substitute the exponential form from Step 6:
$$ m = \frac{10}{d} \log_{10}\left(e^{k \cdot C \cdot d}\right) $$

**Step 9:** Use the identity $\log_{10}(e^x) = \frac{x}{\ln(10)}$:
$$ m = \frac{10}{d} \cdot \frac{k \cdot C \cdot d}{\ln(10)} $$

**Step 10:** Simplify (d cancels out):
$$ m = \frac{10 \cdot k \cdot C}{\ln(10)} = \frac{10 \cdot k \cdot C}{2.3026} $$

$$ \boxed{m \approx 4.342 \cdot k \cdot C \;\; [dB/m] } $$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A photoelectric smoke detector has a threshold sensitivity of $m = 0.1 \, dB/m$. If the specific extinction coefficient of the smoke is $k = 1.5 \, m^2/g$, calculate the minimum smoke concentration $C$ that will trigger the alarm.

**Step 11:** Use the derived relation:
$$ m = 4.342 \cdot k \cdot C $$

**Step 12:** Substitute the given values:
$$ 0.1 = 4.342 \cdot 1.5 \cdot C $$

**Step 13:** Solve for $C$:
$$ C = \frac{0.1}{4.342 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.1}{6.513} $$

$$ \boxed{C \approx 0.01535 \; g/m^3 = 15.35 \; mg/m^3} $$

This means the detector will alarm when smoke concentration reaches approximately **15.35 mg per cubic metre** — a value consistent with **Test Fire TF2 (smoldering wood)** per EN 54.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Coverage Area and Detector Spacing

### 3.2.1 Concept
For a **point-type smoke detector**, the coverage area is assumed to be a **circle** of radius $r$ on the ceiling. The detector monitors this area for smoke accumulation.

**Step 1:** Area of the circular coverage zone:
$$ A_c = \pi r^2 $$

**Step 2:** Per **IS 2189:2008**, the maximum permissible coverage area for a smoke detector is $A_c = 80 \, m^2$.

**Step 3:** Solve for the maximum radius:
$$ r = \sqrt{\frac{A_c}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{80}{3.1416}} $$

$$ r \approx 5.05 \, m $$

**Step 4:** Convert radius to **diameter** (which is the horizontal detector spacing $S$):
$$ S = 2r = 2 \times 5.05 \approx 10.1 \, m $$

However, per IS 2189, the practical maximum spacing is conservatively limited to:
$$ \boxed{S_{max} = 9.0 \; m \text{ (per IS 2189, Clause 4.4.2)} } $$

This conservative limit accounts for:
- Smoke stratification at high ceilings.
- Air movement near walls.
- Obstructions (beams, ducts).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Detector Selection vs. Performance Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE. Memorize the trade-offs.**

| Performance Characteristic | Ionization Smoke | Photoelectric Smoke | Heat (Fixed) | Heat (ROR) | UV Flame | IR Flame | Aspirating (VESDA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensitivity (Flaming Fire)** | **High** | Medium | Low | Medium | **Very High** | High | **Very High** |
| **Sensitivity (Smoldering Fire)** | Low | **Very High** | Low | Low | None | None | **Very High** |
| **Response Time** | Fast (1–5 s) | Fast (3–10 s) | Slow (30–60 s) | Medium (10–30 s) | Very Fast (<1 s) | Fast (1–5 s) | Very Fast (<1 s) |
| **False Alarm Risk** | High (toast, cooking) | Medium (dust) | **Very Low** | Low | Medium (sunlight) | Medium (heaters) | Low (with filters) |
| **Best Application** | Flaming fires, kitchens | Bedrooms, offices | Garages, kitchens | Living rooms | Petrochemical, aircraft hangars | Industrial, outdoor | Data centres, museums |
| **Cost Index (1–10)** | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 8 | 8 | 10 |
| **MTBF (× 10³ hours)** | 100 | 100 | 150 | 150 | 80 | 80 | 120 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Fire Detector

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the internal functional architecture of a generic fire detector and how each performance characteristic emerges from the signal chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Signature: Smoke, Heat, Flame, Gas] --> B[Sensor Element: Optical, Thermistor, Photodiode, Electrochemical]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning: Amplification, Filtering]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Conversion: ADC]
    D --> E[Threshold Comparator: Sensitivity Setting]
    E --> F{Threshold Crossed?}
    F -- No --> G[Drift Compensation Loop]
    G --> B
    F -- Yes --> H[Response Time Delay Tau]
    H --> I[Alarm Signal Output: 24V DC / 20mA]
    I --> J[False Alarm Validation: Multi-criteria Check]
    J --> K[Final Alarm Decision: FAR Filter]
    K --> L[Fire Alarm Control Panel: FACP]

    M[Environmental Inputs: Temp, Humidity, Air Velocity] --> C
    N[Selectivity Filter: Spectral, Chemical] --> B
    O[Coverage Geometry: Spacing, Height] -.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **drift compensation loop** (G → B) is what gives the detector **stability** over years of service.
> - The **multi-criteria check (J)** is the modern technique to reduce **False Alarm Rate (FAR)**.
> - The **threshold comparator (E)** directly sets the **sensitivity** of the detector.

---

## 4.2 Detector Selection Decision Tree (KTU Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Fire Hazard Identification] --> Q1{Type of Fire Expected?}
    Q1 -- Smoldering, slow --> Photo[Photoelectric Smoke Detector]
    Q1 -- Flaming, fast --> Ion[Ionization Smoke Detector]
    Q1 -- Open flame, high risk --> Flame[UV / IR Flame Detector]
    Q1 -- Heat dominant --> Heat[Heat Detector: Fixed or ROR]
    Q1 -- High sensitivity, clean room --> Asp[Aspirating VESDA]

    Photo --> Q2{Environment Clean?}
    Ion --> Q2
    Heat --> Q2
    Flame --> Q2
    Asp --> Q2

    Q2 -- Yes --> Std[Standard Detector]
    Q2 -- No dusty / humid --> Ind[Industrial IP65 Detector]

    Std --> Sp[Spacing per IS 2189: 9m max]
    Ind --> Sp
```

---

## 4.3 Response Time vs. Sensitivity Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ultra-Sensitive Detector] --> B[Very Low Threshold: 0.05 dB/m]
    B --> C[Fast Response: under 5 s]
    C --> D[High False Alarm Risk]
    D --> E[NOT suitable for kitchens or dusty areas]

    F[Low Sensitivity Detector] --> G[High Threshold: 0.5 dB/m]
    G --> H[Slow Response: 30 to 60 s]
    H --> I[Very Low False Alarm]
    I --> J[Suitable for industrial workshops]

    K[Balanced Multi Criteria Detector] --> L[Adaptive Threshold]
    L --> M[Optimal Response: 10 to 15 s]
    M --> N[Low False Alarm via Voting Logic]
    N --> O[Best for hospitals, malls, offices]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The trade-off curve above is the **core of Module 3**. Examiners love to ask: *"Why can't we use the most sensitive detector everywhere?"* The answer lies in the **inverse relationship** between sensitivity and selectivity — ultra-sensitive detectors cannot discriminate between smoke and steam.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "False Alarm Rate (FAR)" of a fire detector. Mention the accepted KTU benchmark value.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (2 marks):** False Alarm Rate (FAR) is defined as the number of unwanted alarm signals generated by a detector per 100 detector-years of operation, caused by non-fire stimuli such as dust, steam, cooking fumes, or electromagnetic interference.
> - **Formula:** $FAR = \frac{N_{false}}{N_{detectors} \times T_{years}} \times 100$
> - **Benchmark (1 mark):** A well-designed fire detection system per **IS 2189:2008** and **EN 54-1** should achieve **FAR < 1 false alarm per 100 detector-years**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: List any three performance characteristics of a fire detector and state their significance.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Sensitivity (1 mark):** The minimum fire signature (smoke, heat, flame) required to trigger an alarm. Higher sensitivity enables earlier detection but increases false alarms.
> 2. **Response Time (1 mark):** The delay between the fire stimulus and alarm activation. Per **IS 2189**, the maximum is **30 seconds** for life safety.
> 3. **Coverage Area (1 mark):** The maximum floor area monitored by a point detector — **80 m² for smoke detectors** and **50 m² for heat detectors** per IS 2189:2008.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **seven performance characteristics** of a fire detector. For each characteristic, state its measurement unit and one practical example. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A photoelectric smoke detector is installed in a server room of area **400 m²** with a ceiling height of **6 m**. Using **IS 2189:2008**, determine:
   (i) The number of smoke detectors required.
   (ii) The maximum horizontal spacing between detectors.
   (iii) The minimum smoke concentration that will trigger the alarm, given that the threshold sensitivity is **0.12 dB/m** and the specific extinction coefficient of smoke is **k = 1.8 m²/g**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| # | Characteristic | Unit | Practical Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | dB/m, °C, kW/m², ppm | Photoelectric smoke detector senses 0.1 dB/m obscuration |
| 2 | **Response Time** | seconds (s) | Ionization detector alarms in **3 s** on a flaming fire |
| 3 | **Stability & Reliability** | MTBF in hours | Modern addressable detector has MTBF > **10⁵ hours** |
| 4 | **Selectivity** | Cross-sensitivity index | Flame detector rejects sunlight via **4.3 μm IR filter** |
| 5 | **Coverage Area** | m² | Point smoke detector covers **80 m²** per IS 2189 |
| 6 | **Environmental Tolerance** | °C, % RH, m/s | Operates from **-10°C to +55°C** and 95% RH |
| 7 | **False Alarm Resistance** | events/100 det-years | Heat detector in a kitchen has FAR ≈ **0.1 per 100 det-years** |

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Number of detectors (3 marks):**

The maximum coverage area of a point smoke detector per IS 2189:2008 is **80 m²**.

$$ N = \frac{A_{total}}{A_c} = \frac{400}{80} = 5 \text{ detectors} $$

A layout of **2 × 3 minus 1 = 5 detectors** (or **2 × 3 grid with the central one slightly offset**) is typical.

$$\boxed{N = 5 \text{ detectors}}$$

**(ii) Maximum horizontal spacing (2 marks):**

The detectors must be placed on a square grid of side $S$:
$$ S = \sqrt{A_c} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.94 \, m $$

Per IS 2189:2008 (conservative limit):
$$ \boxed{S_{max} = 9.0 \, m } $$

**(iii) Minimum smoke concentration (2 marks):**

Using the Beer-Lambert derived relation from Section 3.1:
$$ m = 4.342 \cdot k \cdot C $$

Substitute $m = 0.12 \, dB/m$ and $k = 1.8 \, m^2/g$:
$$ 0.12 = 4.342 \times 1.8 \times C $$

$$ C = \frac{0.12}{7.8156} $$

$$\boxed{C \approx 0.01536 \; g/m^3 = 15.36 \; mg/m^3 }$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **trade-off between sensitivity and false alarm rate** in fire detector design. With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **photoelectric smoke detector** and identify the performance characteristics it excels at. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A rate-of-rise (ROR) heat detector is calibrated to trigger when the temperature rises at **8.3 °C/min** or above. During a test fire, the room temperature rises from **25°C to 48°C** in **3 minutes**.
   (i) Will the detector trigger?
   (ii) If the detector has a fixed-temperature backup at **57°C**, will it also trigger during this test?
   (iii) Justify which type of heat detector (ROR vs. Fixed) is more suitable for a **kitchen environment**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Trade-off discussion (3 marks):**
- **High sensitivity** → earlier alarm → **saves lives** in sleeping accommodations (hotels, hostels).
- **High sensitivity** → detector also responds to **non-fire aerosols** (steam, dust, cooking vapours) → **false alarms**.
- **False alarms** cause **"alarm fatigue"** → occupants ignore alarms → **defeats the purpose** of life safety.
- **Solution:** Use **multi-criteria detectors** that combine smoke + heat + CO sensing with **voting logic** to verify a real fire before alarming.

**Photoelectric detector working principle (3 marks):**
A **light source (LED)** emits a beam into a sensing chamber at an angle. In normal (clean air) conditions, the light passes straight through without hitting the **photodiode (light receiver)**.
When **smoke particles enter the chamber**, they **scatter (Tyndall effect)** the light, and a portion of the scattered light falls on the photodiode. The photodiode converts this light into a small electrical current, which is amplified and compared against the threshold. If the scatter exceeds the threshold (i.e., obscuration > 0.1 dB/m), the alarm activates.

**Performance characteristics it excels at (1 mark):**
- **High sensitivity to smoldering fires** (large smoke particles, low heat) — best for **bedrooms, offices, server rooms**.
- **Low FAR in non-dusty environments.**

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Will the ROR detector trigger? (3 marks)**

The actual rate of temperature rise is:
$$ \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{48 - 25}{3} = \frac{23}{3} \approx 7.67 \, °C/min $$

The ROR threshold is **8.3 °C/min**.

$$ 7.67 < 8.3 \implies \text{ROR detector will NOT trigger.} $$

**(ii) Will the fixed-temperature detector trigger? (2 marks)**

The final temperature reached is **48°C**.

The fixed-temperature threshold is **57°C**.

$$ 48 < 57 \implies \text{Fixed detector will NOT trigger either.} $$

**(iii) Best choice for a kitchen (2 marks):**

A **Fixed-Temperature Heat Detector** rated at **93°C (Class B per IS 2175)** is most suitable for a kitchen because:
- Kitchens have **routine cooking fumes and moderate heat** (up to 50–60°C) that would false-trigger a smoke detector or a low-rated ROR detector.
- A **57°C fixed** detector is suitable for **normal rooms**, but for kitchens, **93°C** is preferred to avoid false alarms from cooking.
- **ROR detectors are not recommended** in kitchens because sudden bursts of heat (e.g., opening an oven) can cause false alarms.

$$\boxed{\text{Fixed-Temperature Heat Detector (93°C, Class B) is the correct choice for kitchens.}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Performance Characteristics of Detectors" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing obscuration units:** Students often write "% obs/m" and "dB/m" interchangeably. Per **EN 54-7 / IS 11360**, the standard unit is **dB/m**, NOT % obs/m. The relation is: **1% obs/m ≈ 0.0434 dB/m**.
> 2. **Wrong coverage area:** Students often quote **100 m² for smoke detectors**. The correct IS 2189 value is **80 m²**.
> 3. **Confusing ROR threshold:** The threshold is **8.3 °C/min**, not 10 °C/min or 5 °C/min.
> 4. **Misapplying Beer-Lambert:** Forgetting to multiply by **4.342** when converting between log and ln forms.
> 5. **Detector vs. detector-base coverage:** Some students forget that detectors in a corridor must be spaced at **S = d/2 from the wall**, where d is the corridor width.
> 6. **Cost vs. performance:** Examiners often ask *"Why not use VESDA everywhere?"* — answer: **high cost (₹2–5 lakh per zone)** and **over-specification** for ordinary buildings.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall!):**

- [ ] **Seven performance characteristics:** Sensitivity, Response Time, Stability, Selectivity, Coverage Area, Environmental Tolerance, FAR.
- [ ] **Beer-Lambert equation:** $m = \frac{10}{d} \log_{10}(I_0/I) = 4.342 \cdot k \cdot C$ — in **dB/m**.
- [ ] **Smoke detector coverage:** **80 m²**, max spacing **9.0 m**, max height **10.5 m**.
- [ ] **Heat detector coverage:** **50 m²**, max spacing **8.0 m**, max height **9.0 m**.
- [ ] **ROR threshold:** **8.3 °C/min** (per IS 2175 Class A1).
- [ ] **Fixed heat classes:** **57°C (A1), 68°C (A2), 93°C (B)**.
- [ ] **FAR benchmark:** **< 1 per 100 detector-years**.
- [ ] **MTBF:** **> 10⁵ hours** for modern addressable detectors.
- [ ] **Kitchens → use 93°C fixed heat detector** (NEVER smoke or ROR).
- [ ] **Bedrooms → photoelectric smoke detector** (best for smoldering fires).
- [ ] **Data centres / museums → aspirating VESDA** (highest sensitivity).
- [ ] **Petrochemical / hangars → UV + IR dual flame detector** (fastest response).
- [ ] **Always remember:** sensitivity and false alarm rate are **inversely related**.
- [ ] **Reference standards to quote in answers:** **IS 2189:2008**, **IS 2175:1988**, **IS 11360**, **EN 54**, **NBC 2016**.
- [ ] **Sensitivity of ionization vs. photoelectric:** ionization = best for **flaming**, photoelectric = best for **smoldering**.

---

**End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Performance Characteristics of Detectors**

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education Framework*
*Course Code: PCSFT501 | Total Study Effort: ~3 hours | Bloom's Levels Covered: L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)*

topics.title: Lag Time Associated with Fire Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lag Time Associated with Fire Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of performance-based structural fire safety and detection-system design, the **Lag Time Associated with Fire Detection** is defined as the cumulative temporal interval that elapses between the **inception of a fire (ignition)** and the **moment at which the detection system (or a human observer) positively recognizes and confirms the existence of a fire event**, subsequently initiating the alarm or suppression actuation sequence.

Mathematically, the total detection lag time is expressed as:

$$
t_{d,\text{total}} = t_{\text{smoke-transport}} + t_{\text{detector-response}} + t_{\text{recognition/verification}} + t_{\text{alarm-activation}}
$$

Where the individual components carry the following engineering meanings in the KTU 2024 Scheme module framework:

| Symbol | Term | Engineering Meaning |
|--------|------|---------------------|
| $t_{\text{smoke-transport}}$ | Smoke Transport Lag | Time for combustion products (smoke, heat, gases) to reach the detector |
| $t_{\text{detector-response}}$ | Detector Response Lag | Time for the sensing element to react to the smoke/heat/gas stimulus |
| $t_{\text{recognition/verification}}$ | Recognition Lag | Time for human verification (in manual systems) or system logic validation |
| $t_{\text{alarm-activation}}$ | Alarm Activation Lag | Time for the signal processing and notification chain to trigger |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Board Note:** The detection lag time is the **single most critical parameter** in performance-based fire safety design because it directly determines the **Available Safe Egress Time (ASET)** available to occupants and the **Required Fire Resistance Rating (RFR)** of structural elements. A longer lag time compresses the ASET and demands more aggressive structural protection.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Domino Race"**

Imagine you light a campfire in the middle of a field, and there is a smoke detector mounted on a pole **20 feet away** and **15 feet above** the fire. The **lag time** is everything that happens from the moment you strike the match until the moment the detector's red LED starts blinking.

- **Step 1 (Smoke Transport Lag):** The hot, buoyant smoke plume has to rise, hit the ceiling, spread sideways as a ceiling jet, and reach the detector 20 feet away. This is like watching a paper boat float down a stream.
- **Step 2 (Detector Response Lag):** Once the smoke arrives, the sensing element (a thermistor, photoelectric cell, or ionization chamber) must heat up, scatter light, or ionize enough to trip its internal threshold. This is like a metal spoon slowly warming in hot tea.
- **Step 3 (Recognition Lag):** The control panel must interpret the signal, filter out false alarms, and confirm a real fire. This is like a person smelling smoke, then turning around to verify, before shouting "FIRE!"
- **Step 4 (Alarm Activation Lag):** The bell, horn, or sprinkler solenoid must physically energize. This is the final click of the relay.

**Total Lag Time = Step 1 + Step 2 + Step 3 + Step 4.** A KTU examiner will expect you to identify and quantify each of these sub-components.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

Per IS 2189, IS 11360, NFPA 72, and the KTU 2024 Scheme approved syllabus:

- **Standard Detector Ceiling Height:** **3 m (residential)** to **12 m (industrial)** as per NFPA 72.
- **Maximum Allowable Detector Lag Time (Design Criterion):** **30 seconds** for life-safety systems in high-occupancy buildings.
- **Response Time Index (RTI)** for sprinklers: typically **≤ 50 m¹ᐟ²·s¹ᐟ²** for fast-response sprinklers.
- **Activation Temperature** for heat detectors: typically **57 °C, 68 °C, or 79 °C** fixed-temperature thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 3 explicitly expects students to differentiate between **smoke detection lag**, **heat detection lag**, and **human detection lag**, and to understand the engineering implications of each on **tenability-based evacuation modeling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Fire Detection Timeline

The complete fire timeline, from ignition to suppression, is partitioned by KTU into the following sequential phases:

### 2.1.1 Phase A — Incipient (Pre-Flashover) Lag
This is the period between ignition and the first unambiguous detection signal. It is dominated by **smoke transport** and **detector response physics**. The fire is in its **t²-growth phase** here, with HRR given by:

$$
Q(t) = \alpha \, t^{2}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **fire growth coefficient** (kW/s²) and $t$ is measured from ignition. KTU 2024 mandates familiarity with four standard growth categories:

- **Slow:** $\alpha = 0.00293$ kW/s²
- **Medium:** $\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s²
- **Fast:** $\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s²
- **Ultra-Fast:** $\alpha = 0.1876$ kW/s²

### 2.1.2 Phase B — Detector Activation
The ceiling jet temperature rise $\Delta T_{cj}$ at the detector location is governed by the **Heskestad / Alpert Ceiling Jet Correlation**:

$$
\Delta T_{cj} = \frac{5.38 \, \left( \dfrac{Q}{r} \right)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}
$$

valid for **r/H > 0.18** (radiating plume regime), and:

$$
\Delta T_{cj} = \frac{6.85 \, \left( \dfrac{Q}{r} \right)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}
$$

valid for **r/H ≤ 0.18** (weak plume regime).

- $Q$ = convective HRR (kW)
- $r$ = horizontal radial distance from plume centerline to detector (m)
- $H$ = ceiling height above fire (m)

### 2.1.3 Phase C — Recognition and Alarm
Includes human verification (typically 30–60 s for trained staff, up to 5 minutes for untrained occupants) and signal propagation to the FACP (Fire Alarm Control Panel).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below consolidates EVERY formula a KTU 2024 examiner may demand on this topic. Memorize the units and validity ranges.**

| # | Formula / Correlation | Variable Definitions | Validity / Units |
|---|----------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Q(t) = \alpha \, t^{2}$ | $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient; $t$ = time from ignition | kW; seconds |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{cj} = \dfrac{5.38 \, (Q/r)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}$ | $Q$ in kW, $r$ in m, $H$ in m | r/H > 0.18; °C |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{cj} = \dfrac{6.85 \, (Q/r)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}$ | Same as above | r/H ≤ 0.18; °C |
| 4 | $v_{cj} = 0.20 \, \left( \dfrac{Q}{H} \right)^{1/3}$ | Ceiling jet velocity | r/H > 0.42; m/s |
| 5 | $t_{d}^{2} = \dfrac{T_{act} - T_{\infty}}{\alpha \cdot k}$ (approx.) | $T_{act}$ = activation temperature; $T_{\infty}$ = ambient | Simplified detector time |
| 6 | $RTI = \tau \cdot \sqrt{u}$ | $\tau$ = detector time constant; $u$ = gas velocity | m¹ᐟ²·s¹ᐟ² |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{RTI}{\sqrt{u}}$ | Plugg/Heskestad sprinkler activation | seconds |
| 8 | $Q_{d} = \dfrac{T_{act} \cdot H^{5/3} \cdot r^{2/3}}{5.38^{3/2}}$ (inverted) | HRR at detector activation | kW |
| 9 | $t_{\text{alarm}} = t_d + t_{rec}$ | Total alarm lag = detection + recognition | seconds |
| 10 | $t_{\text{ASET}} = t_{d,\text{total}} + t_{\text{evac}} + t_{\text{margin}}$ | Tenability timeline anchor | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In KTU valuation, the use of vertical bars `|x|` inside table cells will break the markdown rendering. The examiner's official answer key will use the inverted form shown above (solving the ceiling jet equation for $Q$).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The detection lag time is the **fundamental input parameter** for:

1. **Performance-Based Egress Design (PBED):** Determines the lead time available before untenable conditions (visibility < 10 m, CO > 1400 ppm, temp > 60 °C) are reached.
2. **Structural Fire Engineering:** Earlier detection → smaller HRR at suppression/evacuation → reduced thermal load on steel/concrete members → lower required fire-resistance rating.
3. **Active System Sizing (NFPA 13 / IS 15105):** Sprinkler spacing and RTI selection depend on acceptable lag time for the worst-case design fire.
4. **Detector Placement Optimization (IS 2189):** The ceiling jet correlation is used to determine the maximum detector spacing so that the lag time remains under 30 s.
5. **Smart Building / IoT Integration:** Modern aspirating smoke detectors (VESDA) reduce transport lag to < 3 s, and this is justified using the same physics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" Question for KTU:** Why does the KTU 2024 Module 3 emphasize lag time so heavily? **Because** lag time is the **bridge** between the **fire science** (t² growth, plume dynamics) and the **human/structural safety outcomes** (egress, fire resistance). Without an accurate lag-time estimate, no performance-based design is valid.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Time to Detector Activation (Q–t relationship)

**Problem Setup:** A t²-medium fire ($\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s²) ignites at the center of a large compartment. A fixed-temperature heat detector is located at a radial distance $r = 4$ m from the plume centerline, mounted on a ceiling of height $H = 5$ m. The detector is rated to activate when the ceiling jet temperature rise reaches $\Delta T_{act} = 30$ °C above ambient ($T_{\infty} = 20$ °C). Determine the **time elapsed from ignition to detector activation**.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Identify which Alpert/Heskestad correlation applies.**

The dimensionless parameter $r/H$ is computed as:

$$
\frac{r}{H} = \frac{4 \text{ m}}{5 \text{ m}} = 0.80
$$

Since $0.80 > 0.18$, the **radiating plume correlation** is valid:

$$
\Delta T_{cj} = \frac{5.38 \, \left( \dfrac{Q}{r} \right)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the activation temperature condition.**

The detector activates when $\Delta T_{cj} = \Delta T_{act} = 30$ °C. Therefore:

$$
30 = \frac{5.38 \, \left( \dfrac{Q_{act}}{4} \right)^{2/3}}{(5)^{5/3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q_{act}$, the HRR at activation.**

First, evaluate $5^{5/3}$:

$$
5^{5/3} = 5^{1.6667} = 5 \cdot 5^{2/3} = 5 \cdot (25)^{1/3} \approx 5 \cdot 2.924 \approx 14.620
$$

Rearrange the equation:

$$
\left( \frac{Q_{act}}{4} \right)^{2/3} = \frac{30 \times 14.620}{5.38}
$$

$$
\left( \frac{Q_{act}}{4} \right)^{2/3} = \frac{438.60}{5.38} \approx 81.52
$$

Raise both sides to the power 3/2:

$$
\frac{Q_{act}}{4} = (81.52)^{3/2}
$$

$$
(81.52)^{3/2} = 81.52 \cdot \sqrt{81.52} = 81.52 \cdot 9.029 \approx 736.0
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{act} = 4 \times 736.0 = 2944 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the t²-fire growth law to find activation time.**

$$
Q(t) = \alpha \, t^{2}
$$

$$
2944 = 0.01172 \cdot t^{2}
$$

$$
t^{2} = \frac{2944}{0.01172} \approx 251,194
$$

$$
t_{d} = \sqrt{251,194} \approx 501.2 \text{ seconds} \approx 8.35 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer statement.**

$$
\boxed{t_{d,\text{detection}} \approx 501 \text{ s} \approx 8.35 \text{ min after ignition}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Effect of Ceiling Height on Detection Lag

**Problem Setup:** Using the same detector activation threshold ($\Delta T_{act} = 30$ °C) and a **t²-fast** fire ($\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s²) at the same radial position $r = 4$ m, but with ceilings of height **H = 3 m** and **H = 8 m**, find the activation HRR and time.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — General formula for $Q_{act}$.**

Inverting the Alpert ceiling jet correlation:

$$
Q_{act} = \left( \frac{\Delta T_{act} \cdot H^{5/3} \cdot r^{2/3}}{5.38} \right)^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2 — Case H = 3 m.**

Compute $3^{5/3}$:

$$
3^{5/3} = 3 \cdot 3^{2/3} = 3 \cdot (9)^{1/3} \approx 3 \cdot 2.0801 \approx 6.240
$$

Compute $4^{2/3}$:

$$
4^{2/3} = (4^{1/3})^{2} = (1.5874)^{2} \approx 2.520
$$

Substitute:

$$
Q_{act} = \left( \frac{30 \times 6.240 \times 2.520}{5.38} \right)^{3/2}
$$

$$
Q_{act} = \left( \frac{471.74}{5.38} \right)^{3/2} = (87.68)^{3/2}
$$

$$
(87.68)^{3/2} = 87.68 \cdot \sqrt{87.68} = 87.68 \cdot 9.364 \approx 821.0 \text{ kW}
$$

Time to activation:

$$
t_{d,3m} = \sqrt{\frac{821.0}{0.0469}} = \sqrt{17,505} \approx 132.3 \text{ s} \approx 2.20 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Case H = 8 m.**

Compute $8^{5/3}$:

$$
8^{5/3} = 8^{1.6667} = 8 \cdot 8^{2/3} = 8 \cdot 4 = 32
$$

Substitute:

$$
Q_{act} = \left( \frac{30 \times 32 \times 2.520}{5.38} \right)^{3/2} = \left( \frac{2419.2}{5.38} \right)^{3/2} = (449.7)^{3/2}
$$

$$
(449.7)^{3/2} = 449.7 \cdot \sqrt{449.7} = 449.7 \cdot 21.21 \approx 9540 \text{ kW}
$$

Time to activation:

$$
t_{d,8m} = \sqrt{\frac{9540}{0.0469}} = \sqrt{203,410} \approx 451.0 \text{ s} \approx 7.52 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparative summary table.**

| Ceiling Height $H$ | Activation HRR $Q_{act}$ | Detection Time $t_d$ |
|-------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| 3 m | ~821 kW | ~132 s (2.2 min) |
| 5 m (from Derivation A) | ~2944 kW | ~501 s (8.4 min) |
| 8 m | ~9540 kW | ~451 s (7.5 min) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As ceiling height increases, the **HRR required for detection rises roughly as $H^{5/2}$** (from inverting the formula), but the **detection time depends on the interaction of $Q_{act}$ with the fire growth rate $\alpha$**. For faster-growing fires, the time penalty for high ceilings is **less severe** in absolute terms, but the **suppression challenge** is much greater because the fire has reached flashover-like HRR before detection.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Sprinkler/Heat Detector Lag Using RTI (Plugg-Heskestad Model)

The first-order thermal response of a sprinkler or fast heat detector is given by:

$$
\frac{dT_{m}}{dt} = \frac{\sqrt{u}}{RTI} \, (T_{g} - T_{m})
$$

where:
- $T_{m}$ = detector link temperature (°C)
- $T_{g}$ = local gas temperature in ceiling jet (°C)
- $u$ = local gas velocity (m/s)
- $RTI$ = Response Time Index (m¹ᐟ²·s¹ᐟ²)

**Step-by-step explicit time integration (constant $T_g$ and $u$):**

$$
T_{m}(t) = T_{g} - (T_{g} - T_{0}) \cdot e^{-t \cdot \sqrt{u} \, / \, RTI}
$$

Solve for the time $\tau$ when $T_{m} = T_{act}$:

$$
\tau = -\frac{RTI}{\sqrt{u}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{T_{g} - T_{act}}{T_{g} - T_{0}} \right)
$$

This is the **exact detector/sprinkler activation lag time**. For a low-RTI sprinkler (e.g., $RTI = 28$ m¹ᐟ²·s¹ᐟ²), $u = 1.5$ m/s, $T_{g} = 100$ °C, $T_{act} = 68$ °C, $T_{0} = 20$ °C:

$$
\tau = -\frac{28}{\sqrt{1.5}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{100 - 68}{100 - 20} \right)
$$

$$
\tau = -\frac{28}{1.225} \cdot \ln(0.40) = -22.86 \cdot (-0.9163) \approx 20.9 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common valuation mistake is to forget the **natural logarithm** step and instead use a linear interpolation. This underestimates the lag by a factor of **3–5×** and will cost **3 marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Detector vs. Expected Lag Time

| Detector Type | Typical Lag (s) | Strengths | Limitations | Typical Application |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Photoelectric Smoke** | 5–30 | Fast for smoldering fires | False alarms from steam, dust | Offices, dormitories |
| **Ionization Smoke** | 10–40 | Fast for flaming fires | Sensitive to humidity | Hotel rooms |
| **Fixed-Temp Heat (68 °C)** | 60–300 | Reliable, low cost | Late for life safety | Garages, kitchens |
| **Rate-of-Rise Heat** | 30–120 | Detects rapid growth | False alarms from HVAC | Atriums |
| **Aspirating (VESDA)** | 1–10 | Very low transport lag | High cost | Server rooms, museums |
| **Linear Heat Cable** | 20–60 | Works in harsh env. | Point-of-failure risk | Tunnels, cable trays |
| **Beam Smoke** | 15–45 | Covers large areas | Struggles in high ceilings | Warehouses, malls |
| **Flame Detector (IR/UV)** | 1–5 | Near-instant for open flames | Line-of-sight only | Petrochemical |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Complete Fire Detection Lag Timeline

This block-level functional architecture flow maps every sub-component of the lag time and its interaction with downstream evacuation and suppression systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Ignition t = 0] --> B[Incipient Growth Phase t squared Fire]
    B --> C{Buoyant Plume Rises to Ceiling}
    C --> D[Ceiling Jet Spreads Radially]
    D --> E{Smoke Heat or Gas Reaches Detector}
    E --> F[Transport Lag t1 complete]
    F --> G[Detector Sensor Element Activates]
    G --> H[Detector Response Lag t2 complete]
    H --> I[Signal to FACP Fire Alarm Control Panel]
    I --> J{FACP Logic Check}
    J --> K[Recognition Lag t3 complete]
    K --> L[Alarm Horn Bells Strobes Energize]
    L --> M[Notification Lag t4 complete]
    M --> N{Occupant Response or Suppression Trigger}
    N --> O[Evacuation Begins ASET starts]
    N --> P[Sprinkler Water Deluge Activates]
    O --> Q[Tenant Tenability Limit Reached RSET fails]
    P --> R[Fire Controlled or Extinguished]
    Q --> S[Structural Collapse Risk]
    R --> T[Safe State Achieved]

    classDef critical fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef routine fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class A,B,C,D,E critical

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Detector Selection Decision Tree Based on Lag Time

```mermaid

    B -->|Medium t2 alpha 0.01172| D[Use Smoke or Heat Detector]
    B -->|Fast t2 alpha 0.0469| E[Use Heat Detector with Low RTI]
    B -->|Ultra Fast t2 alpha 0.1876| F[Use Flame Detector with Suppression]

    C --> G{Ceiling Height H}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G -->|H less than 3 m| H[Standard Spot Detectors OK]
    G -->|H 3 to 6 m| I[Spot or Beam Detectors]
    G -->|H 6 to 12 m| J[Beam or Aspirating Detectors]
    G -->|H greater than 12 m| K[Aspirating VESDA only]

    H --> L[Check Lag Time via Alpert Correlation]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M{total lag less than 30 s}

    M -->|No| O[Reduce Spacing or Upgrade Detector Type]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Ceiling Jet Geometry (Block Architecture)

The physical situation is mapped as a functional block diagram below. The fire plume rises vertically, impinges on the ceiling, and then propagates radially outward as a ceiling jet. Detectors are mounted at distance r and height H above the fire.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fire Source Floor Level] -->|Vertical Plume| B[Ceiling Impingement]
    B -->|Radial Spread r| C[Detector Location at radius r Height H]
    C -->|Ceiling Jet delta T| D[Sensor Thermal Mass]
    D -->|Heat Transfer| E[Activation Threshold T act]
    E -->|Lag Time t d| F[Alarm Signal to FACP]

    classDef source fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef transport fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef sensor fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A source
    class B,C transport
    class D,E sensor
    class F output
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Define the term **"Lag Time Associated with Fire Detection"**. List the **four sub-components** of this lag time and indicate which sub-component typically dominates in **high-ceiling (H > 8 m) warehouse scenarios**.

**Model Answer:**

> **Lag time associated with fire detection** is the total elapsed time between fire ignition and the moment the detection system produces a confirmatory alarm signal.
>
> The four sub-components are:
> 1. **Smoke transport lag** — time for combustion products to physically reach the detector
> 2. **Detector response lag** — time for the sensor element to physically react
> 3. **Recognition/verification lag** — time for system logic or human verification
> 4. **Alarm activation lag** — time for signal processing and notification
>
> In **high-ceiling warehouse scenarios**, the **smoke transport lag** dominates because the hot plume loses thermal energy and dilutes substantially during its long vertical rise and radial ceiling jet travel. The temperature rise at the detector may be too small to trigger a fixed-temperature element until the fire reaches a very large HRR, often in the **MW range**.

**KTU Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Four sub-components (1) + Dominant component with justification (1).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** With reference to the **Alpert/Heskestad ceiling jet correlation**, explain why **detector placement rules in IS 2189 specify a maximum spacing of ~10 m between smoke detectors in standard office ceilings of 3 m height**.

**Model Answer:**

> The Alpert correlation gives the ceiling jet temperature rise at radial distance $r$ from the plume centerline:
>
> $$
> \Delta T_{cj} = \frac{5.38 \, (Q/r)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}} \quad \text{for } r/H > 0.18
> $$
>
> For a 3 m ceiling, the **maximum allowable radial distance** is dictated by the requirement that $\Delta T_{cj}$ exceed the detector activation threshold (typically 8–10 °C for smoke detectors, ~30 °C for fixed-temperature heat detectors) within a **design fire of ~1000 kW HRR**.
>
> Solving the Alpert equation for $r$ at the design HRR gives a **maximum r of approximately 5–7 m**. Allowing for the detector's **coverage area** (typically circular with a search radius of 5–6 m) and requiring **overlap of coverage zones**, IS 2189 specifies **center-to-center spacing of ~10 m** for smoke detectors, ensuring that no point on the ceiling is farther than ~5 m from a detector. This minimizes the **smoke transport lag** below the 30 s design criterion.

**KTU Mark Distribution:** Equation identification (1) + Reasoning (1) + Spacing logic (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions provide an **internal choice** between two alternatives. The student must answer **only one**. Both options are presented below for complete coverage.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q (a)** — **7 Marks:** A t²-medium fire ($\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s²) ignites in the corner of a 10 m × 10 m × 4 m high industrial workshop. A fixed-temperature heat detector (activation temperature 68 °C, ambient 27 °C) is mounted on the ceiling at a horizontal distance of 5 m from the fire plume centerline. Using the **Alpert ceiling jet correlation**, determine the **time from ignition to detector activation**.

**Q (b)** — **7 Marks:** Discuss how **(i)** ceiling height, **(ii)** fire growth rate, and **(iii)** detector activation temperature each influence the **detection lag time**. Support your answer with a **comparative table** and a short sketch of the **ceiling jet geometry**.

### Model Solution for Q (a)

**Step 1 — Identify regime.**

$$
\frac{r}{H} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25 > 0.18
$$

Use the radiating plume correlation:

$$
\Delta T_{cj} = \frac{5.38 \, (Q/r)^{2/3}}{H^{5/3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Set activation temperature rise.**

$$
\Delta T_{act} = 68 - 27 = 41 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $4^{5/3}$:**

$$
4^{5/3} = 4 \cdot 4^{2/3} = 4 \cdot (16)^{1/3} = 4 \cdot 2.520 = 10.080
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q_{act}$:**

$$
41 = \frac{5.38 \, (Q_{act}/5)^{2/3}}{10.080}
$$

$$
(Q_{act}/5)^{2/3} = \frac{41 \times 10.080}{5.38} = \frac{413.28}{5.38} = 76.82
$$

$$
Q_{act}/5 = (76.82)^{3/2} = 76.82 \cdot \sqrt{76.82} = 76.82 \cdot 8.765 = 673.4
$$

$$
Q_{act} = 5 \times 673.4 = 3367 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Time to activation:**

$$
t_d = \sqrt{\frac{Q_{act}}{\alpha}} = \sqrt{\frac{3367}{0.01172}} = \sqrt{287,287} \approx 536.0 \text{ s} \approx 8.93 \text{ min}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{t_d \approx 536 \text{ s} \approx 8.93 \text{ minutes from ignition}}
$$

### Model Solution for Q (b)

**Discussion Points:**

**(i) Ceiling Height Influence:** As $H$ increases, the temperature rise at any fixed $r$ decreases **inversely as $H^{5/3}$**. Higher ceilings therefore require a **larger HRR for detection**, leading to **longer detection lag times** for a given fire growth rate. Detector spacing rules in IS 2189 account for this with **reduced spacing at higher ceilings**.

**(ii) Fire Growth Rate Influence:** A larger $\alpha$ (e.g., ultra-fast $\alpha = 0.1876$ kW/s² vs medium $\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s²) means the fire reaches a given HRR **much faster**. Therefore, for the same detector threshold, the lag time is **shorter in absolute seconds**, but the **physical fire size at detection is similar**. Fast-growth fires stress **suppression capability** more than detection capability.

**(iii) Detector Activation Temperature Influence:** A lower activation temperature (e.g., 57 °C vs 79 °C) reduces $Q_{act}$ required for detection, shortening the lag. However, lower thresholds are more prone to **false alarms** from non-fire sources (HVAC, sunlight, industrial processes).

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Increase Effect on $Q_{act}$ | Effect on $t_d$ (for same $\alpha$) |
|-----------|----------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Ceiling height $H$ | $\uparrow$ (as $H^{5/2}$ from inversion) | $\uparrow$ (longer lag) |
| Fire growth rate $\alpha$ | No direct effect | $\downarrow$ (shorter lag) |
| Activation temperature $T_{act}$ | $\uparrow$ (linearly in $\Delta T_{act}$) | $\uparrow$ (longer lag) |
| Radial distance $r$ | $\uparrow$ (as $r$) | $\uparrow$ (longer lag) |

**Ceiling Jet Geometry Sketch (verbal):** A vertical buoyant plume rises from the fire source on the floor, impinges on the ceiling forming a **ceiling impingement region** of radius $r_0 \approx 0.15H$, and spreads radially as a **ceiling jet** of decreasing temperature and velocity. Detectors are placed at radial distance $r$ and height $H$ above the fire.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**Q (a)** — **7 Marks:** A fast-response sprinkler has $RTI = 50$ m¹ᐟ²·s¹ᐟ² and is rated to activate at $T_{act} = 68$ °C. The ambient temperature is 20 °C and the local gas temperature in the ceiling jet is constant at $T_g = 95$ °C with velocity $u = 2.0$ m/s. Using the **Plugg-Heskestad first-order thermal response model**, compute the **sprinkler activation lag time**.

**Q (b)** — **7 Marks:** Explain the difference between **smoke detection lag** and **heat detection lag**. Under what circumstances would you recommend a **VESDA (aspirating) system** over conventional smoke detection? Justify with a sketch of the **ceiling jet stratification phenomenon** in a high-ceiling atrium.

### Model Solution for Q (a)

**Step 1 — Governing equation:**

$$
\tau = -\frac{RTI}{\sqrt{u}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{T_g - T_{act}}{T_g - T_0} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values:**

$$
\tau = -\frac{50}{\sqrt{2.0}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{95 - 68}{95 - 20} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithm argument:**

$$
\frac{95 - 68}{95 - 20} = \frac{27}{75} = 0.360
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$
\ln(0.360) = -1.0217
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time constant:**

$$
\sqrt{2.0} = 1.414 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}^{1/2}
$$

$$
\tau = -\frac{50}{1.414} \cdot (-1.0217) = 35.36 \cdot 1.0217 \approx 36.1 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\tau \approx 36 \text{ seconds}}
$$

### Model Solution for Q (b)

**Smoke vs Heat Detection Lag:**

- **Smoke detection lag** is dominated by **transport physics** — the time for smoke particles (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ m diameter) to be carried by the buoyant plume and ceiling jet to the detector sensing chamber. For smoldering fires, this can be 5–30 s in standard rooms. Smoke detection has **very low thermal mass** and responds in **~1–2 s** once smoke arrives.
- **Heat detection lag** is dominated by **detector thermal inertia** — the time for the heat to conduct from the gas into the sensing element (thermistor, bimetallic strip, fusible link) and raise its temperature to the activation threshold. This is typically **30–300 s** even in standard rooms.

**VESDA Recommendation:**
A VESDA system is recommended when:
1. **Ceiling height exceeds 12 m** (where smoke stratification prevents smoke from reaching standard ceiling-mounted detectors).
2. **Very early warning is required** (museums, data centers, clean rooms) — VESDA can detect in < 3 s.
3. **High-value or high-sensitivity assets** are present.
4. **Stratification-prone geometries** (atriums, large-volume spaces) exist.

**Ceiling Jet Stratification Sketch (verbal description):** In a high-ceiling atrium, the buoyant plume rises and impinges on the ceiling, but the smoke layer **stratifies** at a level **below the ceiling** if the upward velocity at the plume is insufficient to overcome the buoyancy-driven descent. The smoke forms a descending **layer interface** at height $z_s$ that may be 1–3 m below the actual ceiling. Standard smoke detectors mounted at the ceiling will **miss the stratified layer** entirely, leading to extreme detection lag (often > 5 minutes). VESDA samples air through a **pipe network with multiple holes** at varying heights, allowing detection at the actual smoke layer interface.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Cost Each 1–3 Marks):**
>
> 1. **Using the WRONG Alpert correlation branch:** Students frequently use the $r/H \leq 0.18$ (weak plume) correlation when $r/H > 0.18$, or vice versa. The two coefficients are **5.38 and 6.85**, NOT interchangeable. Always compute $r/H$ first.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 3/2 exponent when inverting the ceiling jet equation:** The ceiling jet formula gives $\Delta T \propto Q^{2/3}$. Inverting requires $Q \propto \Delta T^{3/2}$. Students often write $Q = \Delta T \cdot (\text{stuff})$ linearly, which is dimensionally and mathematically wrong.
>
> 3. **Mixing up t²-fire model:** $Q = \alpha t^2$ means $t = \sqrt{Q/\alpha}$. Students sometimes write $t = Q/\alpha$ or $t = Q \cdot \alpha$, which yields numerically absurd answers. **Always square-root after dividing by $\alpha$.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring the natural logarithm in Plugg-Heskestad RTI model:** Linear interpolation gives ~3–5× shorter lag time. The valuation key specifically looks for the $\ln(\cdot)$ term.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the convective fraction:** The Alpert correlation uses **convective HRR**, not total HRR. For fuel-controlled flaming fires, the convective fraction is typically 60–80%. Strict examiners will deduct 0.5 marks for using total HRR without comment.
>
> 6. **Unit errors:** $r$ in m, $H$ in m, $Q$ in kW. Mixing units (e.g., $Q$ in MW with $H$ in mm) will produce answer errors of 6 orders of magnitude.
>
> 7. **Confusing detection lag with response time index:** RTI is a **detector property**, not a lag time. The actual lag depends on RTI **and** the local ceiling jet velocity via $\tau = RTI/\sqrt{u}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Lag time = ignition → confirmed alarm signal.
- **Four sub-components:** Smoke transport + Detector response + Recognition + Alarm activation.
- **Fire growth model:** $Q = \alpha t^2$ with $\alpha$ = 0.00293 / 0.01172 / 0.0469 / 0.1876 kW/s² for slow / medium / fast / ultra-fast.
- **Alpert radiating plume:** $\Delta T = 5.38 (Q/r)^{2/3} / H^{5/3}$ for $r/H > 0.18$.
- **Alpert weak plume:** $\Delta T = 6.85 (Q/r)^{2/3} / H^{5/3}$ for $r/H \leq 0.18$.
- **Always** check $r/H$ before choosing the coefficient (5.38 or 6.85).
- **Inverted form:** $Q_{act} = (\Delta T_{act} \cdot H^{5/3} \cdot r^{2/3} / 5.38)^{3/2}$ — note the 3/2 power, not 1.
- **Detection time:** $t_d = \sqrt{Q_{act} / \alpha}$ — square root, not linear.
- **Plugg-Heskestad RTI model:** $\tau = -(RTI/\sqrt{u}) \cdot \ln((T_g - T_{act})/(T_g - T_0))$.
- **Convective fraction:** Use 0.6–0.8 of total HRR for Alpert input (be alert to this in advanced problems).
- **High ceilings (H > 8 m):** Smoke transport lag dominates; smoke stratification may prevent detection entirely.
- **Stratification:** Buoyant smoke layer may form **below** the ceiling in atriums → use VESDA.
- **Detector comparison:** Smoke (5–30 s) < Heat (60–300 s) for standard rooms; VESDA (1–10 s) for critical spaces.
- **RTI values:** Fast-response sprinklers RTI ≤ 50; standard sprinklers RTI ≈ 200 m¹ᐟ²·s¹ᐟ².
- **Design criterion (NFPA / IS 2189):** Total detection lag ≤ **30 seconds** for life-safety systems.
- **ASET definition:** $t_{ASET} = t_d + t_{evac} + t_{margin}$ — detection lag is the **first term**.
- **Ventilation effect:** Increased ventilation increases plume cooling and may delay detection in naturally ventilated compartments.
- **Remember units:** kW for Q, m for r and H, °C for temperatures, seconds for time.
- **Examiner's mantra:** "Show all algebra, compute $r/H$ first, square-root after $\alpha$ division, $\ln$ for RTI model."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Detectors, Principles of Fire Extinguishments - Extinction of Premixed Flames, Diffusion Flames and Burning Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Detectors, Principles of Fire Extinguishments - Extinction of Premixed Flames, Diffusion Flames and Burning Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Detectors – Formal Academic Definition

A **fire detector** is a sensor-based device designed to identify one or more characteristic signatures of an incipient fire (heat, smoke, flame radiation, or combustion gas) and to convert that physical/chemical signature into an electrical signal for actuation of an alarm or suppression system. Under **KTU 2024 (PCSFT501)**, detectors are classified as **sensing element**, **decision element**, and **annunciation element** per **IS 2189 / NFPA 72** philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A fire detector is *NOT* the same as a fire alarm. The detector senses; the control and indicating equipment (CIE) processes and annunciates.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of fire detectors as the **human sensory organs** during a fire:
- **Heat detector** = your skin sensing heat (slow but reliable)
- **Smoke detector** = your nose smelling smoke (fast, sensitive)
- **Flame detector** = your eyes seeing flames (instant for visible fires)
- **Gas detector** = your lungs detecting toxic CO (early warning for smouldering fires)

## 1.2 Principles of Fire Extinguishment – Formal Definition

**Fire extinguishment** is the physical and/or chemical act of permanently interrupting the self-sustaining combustion process. The classical **Fire Tetrahedron** (replace the older "Triangle") identifies four legs, all of which must be simultaneously satisfied for continued combustion:

1. **Fuel** – combustible material
2. **Oxidizer** – typically atmospheric O₂ (≥ 16% v/v for most organics)
3. **Heat / Activation Energy** – sustained by exothermic release
4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** – free radical propagation (H•, OH•, O•)

**Extinguishment = removal/suppression of at least ONE leg.**

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a four-legged stool supporting a person (fire). Knock away any one leg (fuel, oxygen, heat, or chain reaction) and the person falls — that's extinguishment. Different agents target different legs:
- **Water** → removes **Heat** (latent heat 2260 kJ/kg)
- **CO₂ / Foam** → removes **Oxidizer**
- **Fuel removal** → removes **Fuel**
- **Dry Chemical / Halon** → disrupts **Chain Reaction**

## 1.3 Three Modes of Flame Extinction (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The mechanism of extinction is fundamentally different for premixed, diffusion, and metal fires. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

| Flame Type | Definition | Real Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Premixed Flame** | Fuel and oxidizer mixed **before** the reaction zone | Bunsen burner, gas stove, vapour cloud explosion |
| **Diffusion Flame** | Fuel and oxidizer mix **at the reaction zone** by molecular/eddy diffusion | Candle, pool fire, jet diffusion flame |
| **Metal Fire (Class D)** | Combustion of the metal itself with O₂; produces solid oxide | Mg, Al, Na, Ti burning |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparison of Fire Detectors (KTU Exam Favourite)

### 2.1.1 Heat Detectors

Two principal sub-types:
- **Fixed Temperature (FT):** Activates at a preset temperature (typically **57 °C, 68 °C, 90 °C**). Uses eutectic alloy, bimetallic strip, or thermistor.
- **Rate-of-Rise (ROR):** Activates when temperature rises faster than **8.3 °C/min (15 °F/min)**. Uses pneumatic diaphragm or thermistor differential.

**Spot-type vs. Line-type:**
- Spot = single point
- Line-type (fibre-optic / pneumatic tube) = continuous along a length

### 2.1.2 Smoke Detectors

| Type | Principle | Best At Detecting |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionization Chamber** | Americium-241 α-source ionizes air; smoke disrupts current | Fast-flaming fires (small smoke particles < 0.3 µm) |
| **Photoelectric (Optical Scattering)** | Light scattering by smoke particles in a chamber | **Smouldering fires** (large particles 0.3–10 µm) |
| **Aspirating (VESDA)** | Active air sampling + laser scatter | Very early warning, clean environments (data centres) |

### 2.1.3 Flame Detectors

Respond to **electromagnetic radiation** emitted by flames.

| Type | Wavelength | Detection Speed | Vulnerable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UV (Ultraviolet)** | 185–260 nm | < 1 second (hydrocarbon) | Arc welding, sunlight, ozone |
| **IR (Infrared)** | 4.4 µm (CO₂ band) | < 1 second | Hot surfaces, sunlight |
| **UV/IR Dual** | Both bands | Very reliable | Reduced false alarms |
| **IR3 (Triple IR)** | Three IR bands (4.4 µm + 2 reference) | Best hydrocarbon | False alarm resistant |
| **Visual Flame (CCTV)** | Pixel-flame analysis | < 10 s | Smoke occlusion |

### 2.1.4 Gas / Multi-Criteria Detectors

Detect **CO, CO₂, HCN, NOₓ, hydrocarbon vapours**. Electrochemical cell, semiconductor, or NDIR sensor. Used in **refineries, parking garages, battery storage (Li-ion off-gassing)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The most common comparison exam question is **"Compare ionization vs. photoelectric smoke detectors"** — the killer difference is *particle size sensitivity*.

## 2.2 Master Comparison Matrix (Detectors)

| Parameter | Heat | Smoke (Ion) | Smoke (Photo) | Flame (UV/IR) | Gas (CO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Phenomenon** | Convection / conduction | Current drop | Light scatter | EM emission | Chemical reaction |
| **Detection Speed** | Slow | Fast | Fast (smoulder) | Very fast | Very early |
| **Best for Fire Type** | Slow-developing | Flaming | Smouldering | Open flame | Pre-combustion |
| **False Alarm Risk** | Low | Moderate (cooking) | Moderate (steam) | High (welding) | Low |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Low–Medium | High | High |
| **Clean Room Suitability** | Poor | Poor | Poor | Good (line-of-sight) | Good (aspirating) |
| **Typical Coverage Area** | ~30–50 m² | ~80–100 m² | ~80–100 m² | Cone of vision | Point/aspirated |

## 2.3 Extinction of Premixed Flames — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Flammability Limits

A premixed flame can only propagate when the fuel-oxidizer ratio lies between:
- **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** — fuel-lean extinction
- **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** — fuel-rich extinction

Methane: LFL = 5.0%, UFL = 15.0% in air.
Propane: LFL = 2.1%, UFL = 9.5% in air.

### 2.3.2 Quenching Distance (d_q)

If a premixed flame enters a channel narrower than a critical dimension, the flame **cannot propagate** because heat is lost to the walls faster than it can be generated.

### 2.3.3 Adiabatic Flame Temperature (T_ad)

The maximum theoretical temperature attained when combustion occurs with no heat loss. Extinction occurs when the gas is cooled below the **adiabatic flame temperature** such that radical recombination exceeds radical production.

## 2.4 Extinction of Diffusion Flames

Unlike premixed flames, diffusion flames extinguish by:
1. **Strain Extinction** – when velocity gradients stretch the flame surface until local reaction rate cannot keep up.
2. **Oxygen Limitation** – at O₂ < ~16%, soot-fuel reactions dominate; further lowering kills the flame.
3. **Blow-off** – when flow velocity exceeds the **blow-off velocity** (similar to jet engine flameholders).

The **critical extinction strain rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ext}$ is the maximum stretch rate the flame can survive.

## 2.5 Extinction of Burning Metals (Class D Fires)

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER use water, foam, or CO₂ on Class D fires.** Water reacts violently with burning Na, K, Mg, Al, producing H₂ and O₂, often causing explosion.

Class D extinguishing agents are **heat-absorbing granular powders** (sodium chloride, copper powder, graphite, sodium carbonate) that:
- **Smother** by forming an oxygen-impermeable crust
- **Cool** via high heat capacity
- **Chemically react** with the metal to form a non-combustible crust (e.g., NaCl + Mg → MgCl₂ + Na, which then oxides to Na₂O)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $d_q \approx \text{const} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L}$ | Quenching distance (Peclet-type) | m |
| 3 | $Pe_{ext} = \dfrac{u \cdot d}{\alpha}$ | Critical Peclet number at extinction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{Y_F \cdot \Delta H_c}{c_p}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K |
| 5 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ext} = \dfrac{S_L \cdot \delta_\ell}{L_f^2}$ | Critical extinction strain (diffusion flame) | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $Ka = \dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon} \cdot \delta_\ell^2}{\alpha}$ | Karlovitz number | dimensionless |
| 7 | $MIE \propto \dfrac{c_p \cdot T_{ad} \cdot d_q^3}{\alpha}$ | Minimum Ignition Energy trend | mJ |
| 8 | $P_{ext} = \dfrac{P_0 \cdot T_{ext}}{T_0}$ | Pressure-temperature relation (ideal gas at extinction) | Pa |
| 9 | $Le = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{th}}$ | Lewis number | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot L_v$ | Heat removal by water (latent heat L_v = 2260 kJ/kg) | kW |

Where:
- $S_L$ = laminar burning velocity (m/s)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $\delta_\ell$ = laminar flame thickness
- $L_f$ = flame length

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quenching Distance (d_q) for Premixed Flame

### Step 1 — Define the heat-release and heat-loss balance
For a premixed flame entering a narrow channel of diameter $d$, the volumetric heat-release rate (per unit flame area) is:

$$
\dot{q}''' = \rho_0 \cdot S_L \cdot Y_{F,0} \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

where $Y_{F,0}$ is the initial fuel mass fraction and $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion (kJ/kg).

### Step 2 — Express conductive heat loss to walls
For a cylinder of diameter $d$ at wall temperature $T_w$:

$$
\dot{q}_{loss} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{k(T_{ad} - T_w)}{d/2} \cdot \text{(geometric factor)}
$$

### Step 3 — Non-dimensionalise via Peclet number
Define the **Peclet number** as the ratio of convective to diffusive transport:

$$
Pe = \dfrac{S_L \cdot d}{\alpha}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the extinction criterion
**Extinction occurs when heat loss > heat generation**, which corresponds to a **critical Peclet number** $Pe_{ext}$ that is a constant for a given mixture (experimentally ~ 65 for methane-air).

### Step 5 — Solve for quenching distance
Equating $Pe = Pe_{ext}$:

$$
\dfrac{S_L \cdot d_q}{\alpha} = Pe_{ext} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_q = \dfrac{Pe_{ext} \cdot \alpha}{S_L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_q = \dfrac{Pe_{ext} \cdot \alpha}{S_L}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Lower burning velocity $S_L$ (e.g., near LFL/UFL or near extinction) → **larger** quenching distance. Faster flames → smaller $d_q$ → can propagate through smaller gaps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering use:** This is why **flame arresters** in pipelines use mesh or crimped ribbon of channel size < $d_q/2$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Extinction Strain Rate for Diffusion Flame

### Step 1 — Flame thickness for a diffusion flame
For a counter-flow or strained diffusion flame:

$$
\delta_\ell = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L}
$$

### Step 2 — Characteristic flow time in a strained field
If the flow is strained at rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ (s⁻¹), the residence time of a fluid parcel in the reaction zone is:

$$
\tau_{res} = \dfrac{1}{\dot{\varepsilon}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with chemical reaction time
The chemical time scale for reaction is:

$$
\tau_{chem} = \dfrac{\delta_\ell}{S_L} = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Damköhler number criterion
The **Damköhler number** $Da$ is the ratio of flow time to chemical time:

$$
Da = \dfrac{\tau_{chem}}{\tau_{res}} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}}{S_L^2}
$$

### Step 5 — Critical extinction condition
Extinction occurs when $Da$ falls below a critical value $Da_{ext}$ (≈ 1 for diffusion flames). Setting $Da = Da_{ext}$:

$$
Da_{ext} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}_{ext}}{S_L^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{\varepsilon}_{ext} = \dfrac{Da_{ext} \cdot S_L^2}{\alpha}\,}
$$

**Physical meaning:** Higher laminar burning velocity → higher extinction strain (harder to blow out). This is why **hydrogen diffusion flames (high $S_L$) are very hard to extinguish**, while **methane pool fires (lower $S_L$) blow off easily** in wind.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Heat Removal for Water Spray

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A water sprinkler discharges 50 L/min onto a 2 m² burning surface. If the water is at 20 °C and reaches 100 °C then boils, calculate (a) sensible heat absorbed, (b) latent heat absorbed, (c) total heat removed, (d) effectiveness compared to a 1 MW fire. Use $c_{p,w} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), $L_v = 2260$ kJ/kg, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³.

#### Step (a) — Mass flow rate
$$
\dot{m} = 50 \,\text{L/min} \times 1 \,\text{kg/L} = 50 \,\text{kg/min} = 0.8333 \,\text{kg/s}
$$

#### Step (b) — Sensible heat (20 → 100 °C)
$$
\dot{Q}_{sen} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.8333 \times 4.18 \times (100-20)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{sen} = 0.8333 \times 4.18 \times 80 = 278.6 \,\text{kW}
$$

#### Step (c) — Latent heat (vaporisation)
$$
\dot{Q}_{lat} = \dot{m} \cdot L_v = 0.8333 \times 2260 = 1883.3 \,\text{kW}
$$

#### Step (d) — Total heat removed
$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{sen} + \dot{Q}_{lat} = 278.6 + 1883.3 = 2162 \,\text{kW}
$$

#### Step (e) — Effectiveness vs 1 MW fire
$$
\text{Effectiveness} = \dfrac{2162}{1000} \approx 2.16
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{The sprinkler removes heat at } 2.16 \times \text{ the fire's HRR.}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **latent heat contributes 87%** of the cooling — this is why water is such an effective extinguisher and why vaporisation (not heating) is the dominant mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Detector Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Fire Hazard Analysis] --> B{What is the dominant fire signature?}
    B -- Fast flaming --> C{Visibility/Line of sight?}
    B -- Smouldering --> D[Photoelectric Smoke Detector]
    B -- Pre-combustion gases --> E[Gas Detector CO/VOC]
    B -- Slow temperature rise --> F[Heat Detector FT or ROR]
    C -- Yes --> G[Flame Detector UV or IR3]
    C -- No --> H[Aspirating Smoke Detector VESDA]
    G --> I[Mount at ceiling or beam orientation]
    H --> J[Sample pipe network]
    D --> K[Mount at ceiling in sleeping areas]
```
