# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Compare ionization and photoelectric smoke detectors with respect to (a) operating principle, (b) type of fire best detected, and (c) typical false alarm sources.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Ionization | Photoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Principle | Uses Am-241 α-source to ionize air; smoke ions reduce current | Uses LED + photodiode; smoke scatters light into detector |
| (b) Best fire | Fast flaming fires (small particles < 0.3 µm) | Smouldering fires (large particles 0.3–10 µm) |
| (c) False alarm | Cooking vapours, steam | Cigarette smoke, dust, insects |

*(1 mark per correct comparison row; concise tabular answer scores full marks.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the principle of "quenching" of a premixed flame in a narrow channel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Quenching is the extinction of a premixed flame when the channel diameter becomes smaller than the **quenching distance** $d_q$. The walls conduct heat away from the flame faster than the reaction can generate it, dropping the gas temperature below the adiabatic flame temperature and starving the chain reaction of radicals. The critical diameter is given by $d_q = Pe_{ext} \cdot \alpha / S_L$ (1 mark). It is the basis of **flame arresters** used in pipelines carrying flammable gases (1 mark). For methane-air, $d_q \approx 2.5$ mm, so industrial arresters use mesh aperture < 1.25 mm (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Detector Comparison + Extinguishment of Premixed Flames
`[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model, Module 3, with internal choice against Question B]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare in detail the four major classes of fire detectors — **heat, smoke (ionization + photoelectric), flame (UV/IR), and gas (CO)** — with reference to: (i) sensing principle, (ii) detection speed, (iii) best-suited fire type, (iv) limitations, and (v) typical applications. Use a comparative matrix.

**(b) [7 marks]** A methane-air premixed flame has a laminar burning velocity $S_L = 0.4$ m/s and thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. The critical Peclet number for extinction is 65. Calculate the (i) quenching distance $d_q$, (ii) the minimum channel diameter a flame arrester must have to safely arrest this flame, and (iii) comment on what would happen if a small quantity of hydrogen (which increases $S_L$) is added.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Comparison Matrix [7 marks]
*(Full credit for complete table; partial credit for each correctly filled row)*

| Class | Principle | Speed | Best for | Limitation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat (FT)** | Eutectic alloy melts at fixed T | Slow (min) | Slow-developing, high HRR | Slow response, ceiling location only | Kitchen, garage |
| **Heat (ROR)** | ΔT/Δt > 8.3 °C/min triggers | Moderate | Rapid temperature rise | False alarm in sun-heated attics | Warehouses |
| **Ionization Smoke** | α-current drop | Fast (s) | Flaming fires | False alarm from cooking | Bedrooms, corridors |
| **Photoelectric Smoke** | Light scatter | Fast for smoulder | Smouldering, low-energy | Less sensitive to clear flames | Living rooms, hotels |
| **UV Flame** | 185–260 nm emission | < 1 s | Open hydrocarbon fires | False alarm from welding, sunlight | Refineries, hangars |
| **IR Flame** | 4.4 µm CO₂ band | < 1 s | Hydrocarbon fires | Hot surface interference | Petrochemical |
| **IR3 (Triple)** | 3 IR bands + ratio logic | < 1 s | Hydrocarbon, false-alarm immune | Cost | Offshore platforms |
| **CO Gas** | Electrochemical cell | Very early | Smouldering, Li-ion | Selectivity, drift | Battery storage, parking |
| **VESDA (Aspirating)** | Active air sample + laser | Earliest | Clean, high-value | Cost, maintenance | Data centres, museums |

*(1.5 marks for sensing principle coverage; 1.5 marks for best fire type mapping; 1.5 marks for limitations; 2.5 marks for overall application relevance.)*

#### Part (b) — Quenching Distance Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $S_L = 0.4$ m/s
- $\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $Pe_{ext} = 65$

**(i) Quenching distance [3 marks]:**

$$
d_q = \dfrac{Pe_{ext} \cdot \alpha}{S_L} = \dfrac{65 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.4}
$$

$$
d_q = \dfrac{1.625 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4} = 4.0625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_q = 4.06 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(ii) Flame arrester design rule [2 marks]:**
A flame arrester must have an aperture **smaller** than $d_q$ to be safe. Common safety factor of 1/2:

$$
d_{aperture} \le \dfrac{d_q}{2} = \dfrac{4.06}{2} = 2.03 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{aperture} \le 2.0 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(iii) Effect of hydrogen addition [2 marks]:**
Hydrogen has a much higher laminar burning velocity (~ 3 m/s in air). Adding even a small fraction to methane increases $S_L$ significantly. From $d_q = Pe_{ext} \cdot \alpha / S_L$, **higher $S_L$ means smaller $d_q$**. This means the flame can propagate through **smaller gaps**, so a flame arrester sized for pure methane may **fail** to arrest a methane–hydrogen blend. Hence, **hydrogen service requires specially certified arresters** with finer mesh.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
`[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **four principles of fire extinguishment** (cooling, smothering, starvation, chain inhibition) with one suitable extinguishing agent example for each. Why is **water the most versatile** agent for Class A fires?

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **extinction of burning metals (Class D fires)**. Why is water **forbidden**? List **three** suitable extinguishing agents and the chemical mechanism by which they extinguish magnesium and aluminium fires.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Four Principles of Fire Extinguishment [7 marks]

1. **Cooling (1.5 marks):** Water absorbs heat via sensible heat (c_p × ΔT) and especially **latent heat of vaporisation (2260 kJ/kg)**. Cooling reduces fuel temperature below pyrolysis temperature. → **Agent: Water spray/sprinkler.**

2. **Smothering (1.5 marks):** Displaces or blocks O₂. Reduces O₂ below the limiting oxygen concentration (LOC ≈ 12–16%). → **Agents: CO₂, foam, dry ice, inert gas (N₂, argon).**

3. **Starvation (1.5 marks):** Removes or isolates the fuel. → **Agents: Shut fuel valve, remove combustibles, foam blanket on liquid pools.**

4. **Chain Inhibition (1.5 marks):** Interrupts the free-radical chain reaction (H•, OH•, O•) by catalytically recombining them. → **Agents: Dry chemical (ABC powder – monoammonium phosphate), Halon, FM-200, Novec 1230.**

**Why water is most versatile for Class A (1 mark):**
- Highest specific heat and latent heat of vaporisation
- Non-toxic, abundant, inexpensive
- Covers the surface, can penetrate porous fuels
- For Class B (liquid fires) → water can be used only as **spray/mist**, not jet (jet causes splashing and steam explosion)

#### Part (b) — Class D Fire Extinction [7 marks]

**Why water is forbidden (3 marks):**
Water reacts violently with burning reactive metals, producing hydrogen gas and large amounts of heat — often leading to **explosive** outcomes:

- **2 Na + 2 H₂O → 2 NaOH + H₂ ↑ + heat**
- **Mg + 2 H₂O → Mg(OH)₂ + H₂ ↑ + heat**
- The liberated H₂ ignites spontaneously, and steam explosions can launch burning metal particles.

**Three suitable Class D agents and mechanisms (4 marks — 1.5 each approx.):**

1. **Sodium Chloride (NaCl) Dry Powder:**
   - Forms an oxygen-impermeable crust over the metal
   - Reaction: $2 NaCl + Mg \rightarrow MgCl_2 + 2 Na$; the Na then oxides to Na₂O
   - Application: Specific agents like **Met-L-X** (sodium chloride + tricalcium phosphate)

2. **Copper Powder (Cu):**
   - Acts as a heat sink and forms a low-melting alloy with the burning metal
   - Forms a dense, oxygen-excluding layer
   - Used in **Cu-X** extinguishers for Li, Li-alloy, and Mg fires

3. **Graphite or Sodium Carbonate (Na₂CO₃):**
   - Applied with a long-handled scoop
   - Graphite has very high thermal conductivity — rapidly conducts heat away
   - Carbonate decomposes endothermically, absorbing heat
   - Forms physical barrier preventing O₂ access

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "water extinguishes burning metals by cooling." This is the **most common error** and will cost 2–3 marks instantly. Water *worsens* Class D fires.
> 2. **Do NOT** mix up "Class B" (liquid fires) and "Class D" (metal fires). The question explicitly asks for metal fires.
> 3. Mentioning *both* the physical (smother) and chemical (crust formation) mechanisms scores the full 7 marks — mentioning only one earns at most 4.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Detector selection** depends on the **dominant fire signature** of the hazard (smouldering → photoelectric, flaming → ionization + flame, smoulder from battery → CO, etc.).
- **Ionization vs Photoelectric** is the most-asked comparison; remember **particle size sensitivity** is the key.
- **Flame detectors (UV/IR)** are the **fastest (< 1 s)** but have highest false-alarm risk.
- The **Fire Tetrahedron** has four legs: Fuel, Oxidizer, Heat, Chain Reaction. **Extinguish by removing any one.**
- **Four extinguishment principles:** Cooling (water), Smothering (CO₂/foam), Starvation (fuel cut), Chain Inhibition (dry chemical/halon).
- **Premixed flame extinction** mechanisms: **Quenching (narrow channel), Flammability limits (LFL/UFL), Cooling below T_ad.**
- **Quenching distance formula:** $d_q = Pe_{ext} \cdot \alpha / S_L$. Lower $S_L$ → larger $d_q$.
- **Diffusion flame extinction** mechanisms: **Strain (velocity gradient), Oxygen limitation (< 16%), Blow-off.**
- **Critical extinction strain rate:** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ext} = Da_{ext} \cdot S_L^2 / \alpha$. Hydrogen flames are hardest to extinguish.
- **Class D (metal) fires** — **NEVER use water**; it produces H₂ + heat → explosion. Use **NaCl, Cu powder, graphite**.
- **Water's primary cooling** is via **latent heat of vaporisation** (2260 kJ/kg), ~ 87% of total heat removed.
- **KTU Frequently Tested Pair:** "Compare Ionization vs Photoelectric" AND "Why is water not used on metal fires" — both are 3-mark guaranteed questions.
- **LFL of methane = 5%, UFL = 15%**; **LFL of propane = 2.1%, UFL = 9.5%** — these numerical values are examiner favourites.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Triangle, Fire Tetrahedron, Basic Concept of Fire Fighting with Water, Carbon Dioxide, Powders, Foams, Inert Gases, Halons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fire Triangle

The **Fire Triangle** (also called the **Combustion Triangle**) is the classical model that represents the three essential elements that must be present simultaneously for a fire to ignite and sustain itself. It was the dominant fire science model until modern research expanded it to the tetrahedron.

The three sides of the fire triangle are:

1. **Fuel** – A combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas) that can undergo oxidation.
2. **Oxygen (Oxidiser)** – Typically atmospheric oxygen at a concentration of **≥ 21 % by volume** in normal air (fire can still be sustained at lower concentrations depending on the fuel).
3. **Heat (Activation Energy / Ignition Temperature)** – The minimum thermal energy required to initiate and sustain the self-sustaining combustion reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Concept):** *"Fire is a rapid oxidation process accompanied by the liberation of heat, light, and flame. For fire to occur, three elements — Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat — must be present simultaneously in appropriate proportions. Removal of any one side of the triangle causes the fire to extinguish."*

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Campfire Stove" Model

Imagine you are trying to boil water on a kerosene stove:
- The **fuel** is the kerosene stored in the tank.
- The **oxygen** is the air that flows in through the air-intake vent.
- The **heat** is the match/spark that first ignites the kerosene, raising it above its flash point.

If you **close the air vent** (cut oxygen), the flame dies. If you **run out of kerosene** (cut fuel), the flame dies. If you **flood the pan with water** (remove heat), the flame dies. This is the exact principle behind every fire-extinguishing agent: it breaks *at least one* side of the triangle.

---

## 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron

Modern fire science recognises that a *self-sustaining* fire requires a fourth element that the triangle neglects — the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** in the flame zone. The **Fire Tetrahedron** adds this fourth face, making it a 3-D pyramid with four faces:

1. **Fuel**
2. **Oxygen (Oxidising Agent)**
3. **Heat (Energy)**
4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction (Self-sustained Chemical Reaction in the vapour phase)**

> [!NOTE]
> The fourth element was added by **Professor Howard Emmons** in the 1970s. The chain reaction consists of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) propagating combustion. Halons and dry chemical powders work specifically by interrupting this fourth face — a mechanism the triangle cannot explain.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Domino Cascade" Model

Think of the chain reaction as a row of standing dominoes:
- The match (heat) tips the first domino (initiation).
- Each falling domino tips the next (propagation).
- The energy released by each falling domino keeps the next one falling (self-sustenance).
- If you **slide a thick book between two dominoes** (chain inhibitor like Halon), the cascade stops — even though fuel, oxygen, and heat are all still present. The fire goes out because the *chain reaction* is broken.

This is why certain fires (e.g., flammable liquid fires) cannot be extinguished by simply removing heat or oxygen — the chain reaction must also be interrupted.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Fire (IS 2190 / IS 15683 & NFPA 10 Reference)

Before discussing extinguishing media, the student must know the **fire classes** because the choice of extinguishing agent depends on the fire class.

| Class | Type of Fuel | Common Examples |
|:------|:-------------|:----------------|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles (solid) | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases | Petrol, kerosene, LPG, solvents, paints |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (under pressure) | Methane, propane, acetylene, hydrogen |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium, titanium |
| **Class E (K)** | Electrical / Cooking oils | Energised equipment, deep-fat fryers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Always identify the fire class *first*. The wrong extinguishing agent can **spread** the fire (e.g., water on a Class D metal fire produces hydrogen gas and may cause an explosion).

---

## 1.4 Why Different Extinguishing Agents Exist — The Master Concept

A single agent cannot efficiently tackle all fire classes. Each agent is engineered to break one or more sides of the **fire tetrahedron** as shown below:

- **Water** → removes **Heat** (cooling)
- **Foam** → removes **Fuel** (smothering) and blocks **Oxygen**
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** → removes **Oxygen** and partially **Heat**
- **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** → breaks the **Chain Reaction**
- **Inert Gases (N₂, Ar, IG-541)** → removes **Oxygen**
- **Halons (Halogenated Hydrocarbons)** → breaks the **Chain Reaction** (now phased out under Montreal Protocol)
- **Wet Chemical** → removes **Heat** and **Fuel** (saponification for Class K)

This "one agent → one face" mapping is the foundation for the design of any fire protection system and is a high-weightage concept in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Each Face of the Fire Tetrahedron

### 2.1.1 Fuel
- A material in solid, liquid, or gaseous state that can release energy through combustion.
- **Pyrolysis** is the thermal decomposition of solid fuel into combustible gases (this is the actual fuel for flaming combustion).
- **Fuels must be in vapour/gaseous phase** to burn — this is why solids like wood must first be heated to release combustible vapours.

### 2.1.2 Oxygen
- Normal air contains **21 % O₂** by volume.
- Fires are generally sustained above **16 % O₂**.
- Below **14 % O₂**, most combustibles will not burn.
- This is the principle behind **inert gas suppression systems** (typically designed to bring O₂ down to 10–12 %).

### 2.1.3 Heat (Activation Energy)
- The **ignition temperature** is the minimum temperature at which a fuel vapour will spontaneously ignite without an external source.
- The **fire point** is the lowest temperature at which a fuel vapour continues to burn after the ignition source is removed.
- Heat sustains the fire by:
  1. **Pyrolyzing** solid fuel into combustible vapours.
  2. **Raising adjacent fuel** to its ignition temperature.
  3. **Convecting and radiating** to spread the fire.

### 2.1.4 Uninhibited Chain Reaction
- Combustion is a free-radical chain reaction: $\text{H}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH}^{\bullet} + \text{O}^{\bullet}$
- The reaction propagates at extremely high rates (sub-millisecond).
- Halons and dry chemical powders donate or capture free radicals, breaking the chain.
- Example for Halon 1301: $\text{CF}_3\text{Br} \rightarrow \text{CF}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Br}^{\bullet}$, where $\text{Br}^{\bullet}$ catalytically recombines $\text{H}^{\bullet}$ and $\text{OH}^{\bullet}$ radicals.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Extinguishing Media — Theory and Application

### 2.2.1 Water as an Extinguishing Agent

**Mechanism (Primary):** **Cooling** (Heat removal).
**Mechanism (Secondary):** **Smothering** through steam generation.

Water has an exceptionally high **latent heat of vaporisation**:

$$L_v = 2256 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad (\text{at } 100 \text{ }^\circ\text{C})$$

This means **1 kg of water absorbs 2256 kJ** to convert into steam, making it the most effective cooling agent per unit cost.

Water also expands **~1700 times** in volume when converted to steam, displacing oxygen.

**Application Matrix for Water:**

| Fire Class | Suitability | Reason |
|:-----------|:------------|:-------|
| Class A | Excellent | Cools solid fuel below ignition temp |
| Class B | Poor–Dangerous | Flammable liquids float on water; may spread fire |
| Class C | Generally NO | Water is conductor → electric shock risk |
| Class D | NEVER | Reacts violently (e.g., Na + H₂O → H₂ ↑ + NaOH) |
| Class K | Limited | Hot cooking oil + water → "flare-up" explosion |

**Forms of Water Application:**
- **Spray / Spray Nozzles** – for cooling.
- **Fog / Mist** – fine droplets with high surface area, effective for radiation shielding.
- **Water Mist Systems** – modern high-pressure systems.
- **Deluge Systems** – all nozzles open simultaneously.

---

### 2.2.2 Foam as an Extinguishing Agent

**Mechanism:** **Smothering** (blankets fuel) + **Cooling** (water content).

Foam is a stable aggregate of **air-filled bubbles** formed by mechanically mixing a **foam concentrate** (Aqueous Film Forming Foam — AFFF, or Protein Foam) with water and air in the correct **proportioning ratio** (typically 1 %, 3 %, or 6 %).

**Proportioning Ratio Formula:**

$$C_{\text{foam}}(\%) = \frac{V_{\text{concentrate}}}{V_{\text{concentrate}} + V_{\text{water}}} \times 100$$

**Expansion Ratio:**

$$E = \frac{V_{\text{foam produced}}}{V_{\text{foam solution (water + concentrate)}}}$$

- **Low Expansion (E = 4 to 12):** For Class B hydrocarbon fires.
- **Medium Expansion (E = 12 to 100):** For flammable liquid storage.
- **High Expansion (E > 200):** For enclosed spaces (e.g., aircraft hangars, ship engine rooms).

**Application Matrix for Foam:**

| Fire Class | Suitability | Reason |
|:-----------|:------------|:-------|
| Class A | Yes (with wet foam) | Penetrates solids |
| Class B | Excellent | Blankets flammable liquids |
| Class C | Limited | For gas fires, only if gas supply can be cut |
| Class D | NO | Incompatible |
| Class K | NO (use wet chemical) | Foam may not withstand high oil temps |

---

### 2.2.3 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) as an Extinguishing Agent

**Mechanism:** **Smothering** (Oxygen reduction) + minor **Cooling**.

CO₂ is stored as a **liquid under high pressure (~57 bar at 21 °C)** in seamless steel cylinders. Upon discharge, it expands rapidly, cooling to approximately **-78 °C**, forming **dry ice (solid CO₂)**.

**Critical Properties of CO₂:**

- **Molecular weight:** $M = 44 \text{ g/mol}$
- **Density (gas at STP):** $\rho \approx 1.87 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (1.5× heavier than air)
- **Design concentration for extinguishment:** 30–50 % by volume for most hazards.
- **Minimum oxygen level tolerated:** ~12–14 %.
- **Oxygen displacement equation:**

$$O_2 (\text{residual}) = 21\% - C_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ (achieved)}}$$

**Application Matrix for CO₂:**

| Fire Class | Suitability | Reason |
|:-----------|:------------|:-------|
| Class B | Excellent | Clean, no residue, non-conductor |
| Class C | Excellent | Non-conductor (electrical fires) |
| Class A (Surface) | Limited | No cooling, reignition possible |
| Class D | Limited | Only for some metals (e.g., magnesium) |
| Class K | NO | Insufficient cooling for hot oil |

> [!WARNING]
> **Toxicity & Asphyxiation Risk:** Concentrations above 4 % CO₂ are hazardous to humans; above 10 % cause unconsciousness. CO₂ systems must have **pre-discharge alarms** and **lock-out** procedures to evacuate personnel.

---

### 2.2.4 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP / ABC Powder)

**Mechanism:** **Chain-Breaking (Inhibition of the chemical chain reaction)** — the most potent action.

Composition: **Monoammonium phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄)** for ABC powder, or **sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃)** for BC powder, micronised to particle size **10–75 μm**.

**Working Principle (Free-Radical Interception):**

$$\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3$$

The phosphoric acid and ammonia decompose in the flame, with intermediate species (PO•, HOPO•, HPO₂•) catalytically recombining the free radicals H• and OH•, breaking the chain reaction.

**Application Matrix for DCP:**

| Fire Class | Suitable Powder |
|:-----------|:----------------|
| Class A | ABC Powder |
| Class B | ABC or BC Powder |
| Class C | ABC or BC Powder |
| Class D | Special DCP (e.g., NaCl for Na, Cu for Li) |
| Class E | BC / ABC (non-conductive) |

---

### 2.2.5 Inert Gases as Extinguishing Agents

**Mechanism:** **Oxygen Displacement** (Smothering) — bring O₂ below the level required to sustain combustion.

Three principal clean-agent gas mixtures (IG systems):

| Agent | Composition | Storage Pressure | Design O₂ Level |
|:------|:------------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| **IG-01 (Argon)** | 100 % Ar | 200 / 300 bar | ≤ 10 % |
| **IG-100 (Nitrogen)** | 100 % N₂ | 200 / 300 bar | ≤ 10 % |
| **IG-541 (Inergen)** | 52 % N₂ + 40 % Ar + 8 % CO₂ | 200 / 300 bar | ≤ 12 % |

**Total Flooding Equation (moles of gas required):**

$$n = \frac{V}{V_m} \left[ \frac{21 - C_{\text{final}}}{C_{\text{final}}} \right]$$

Where $V$ is the protected volume and $V_m$ is the molar volume at design conditions.

**Application:** Data centres, server rooms, archives, museums, telecom rooms, control rooms — wherever clean, residue-free suppression is required.

---

### 2.2.6 Halons (Halogenated Hydrocarbons)

**Mechanism:** **Chain-Breaking** through the release of halogen radicals (Cl•, Br•).

Common halons:
- **Halon 1211** — $\text{CBrClF}_2$ (BCF) — streaming application (portable extinguishers).
- **Halon 1301** — $\text{CBrF}_3$ (BTM) — total flooding systems.

**Chemical Action of Halon 1301:**

$$\text{CBrF}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CF}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Br}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{HBr} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{HBr} + \text{H}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{Br}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(catalytic cycle)}$$

The $\text{Br}^{\bullet}$ is regenerated, hence a single molecule breaks thousands of chain-propagation cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Phased-Out Status:** Halons are **Ozone-Depleting Substances (ODS)** with high ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential). They are banned under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** for new installations. They have been replaced by **Halon alternatives** such as **FM-200 (HFC-227ea), Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12), FE-13, FE-25, and Inert Gas mixtures.** KTU may ask comparison questions.

**Comparison of Halons vs Halon Alternatives:**

| Property | Halon 1301 | FM-200 (HFC-227ea) | Novec 1230 |
|:---------|:-----------|:-------------------|:------------|
| ODP | 12 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | 6900 | 2050 | 1 |
| Atmospheric Lifetime | 65 yrs | 33 yrs | 5 days |
| Extinguishing Mechanism | Chain-break | Chain-break + cooling | Heat removal |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for revision.** These equations are the most commonly tested numerical and theoretical relations in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|:--|:--------|:-------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | Latent heat of vaporisation of water | $L_v = 2256$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ at $100 \text{ }^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 2 | Volumetric expansion (water → steam) | $\Delta V = 1700$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Specific heat of water | $c_p = 4.186$ | $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ |
| 4 | Foam proportioning ratio | $C = V_c / (V_c + V_w) \times 100$ | percentage |
| 5 | Foam expansion ratio | $E = V_{\text{foam}} / V_{\text{solution}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | CO₂ design concentration | $C_{CO_2} = 30\text{ to }50$ | \% by volume |
| 7 | Residual O₂ after CO₂ | $O_{2,res} = 21 - C_{CO_2}$ | \% by volume |
| 8 | Minimum O₂ to sustain fire | $O_{2,min} \approx 14$ | \% by volume |
| 9 | Inert gas design O₂ | $O_{2,final} \le 10\text{ to }12$ | \% by volume |
| 10 | Gas moles (ideal gas) | $n = PV / RT$ | moles |
| 11 | Molar volume at STP | $V_m = 22.414$ | L/mol |
| 12 | ODP of Halon 1301 | $\text{ODP} = 12$ | relative to CFC-11 |
| 13 | GWP of Halon 1301 | $\text{GWP}_{100} = 6900$ | relative to CO₂ |
| 14 | Heat absorbed by water spray | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_v$ | kJ |
| 15 | Discharge time for CO₂ (local) | $t \ge 30$ | seconds (typical) |
| 16 | Discharge time for Halon (flooding) | $t \le 10$ | seconds (typical) |
| 17 | Density of CO₂ (gas) | $\rho_{CO_2} = 1.87$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ at STP |
| 18 | Density of air | $\rho_{air} = 1.204$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ at STP |
| 19 | Argon fraction in IG-541 | $40$ | \% |
| 20 | Critical oxygen index (COI) | $n_{O_2}/(n_{O_2} + n_{N_2})$ | unitless |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Critical Oxygen Index (COI)** is a polymer testing concept (LOI - Limiting Oxygen Index). For inert gas fire suppression design, the same principle applies: a fuel is non-flammable if the surrounding oxygen is below its LOI/COI.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mapping of agent → tetrahedron face is **not just academic**. It is the foundation of every code of practice:

- **NFPA 12** — CO₂ systems.
- **NFPA 17** — DCP systems.
- **NFPA 11** — Low/Medium/High Expansion Foam.
- **NFPA 2001** — Clean Agent (FM-200, Novec, Inert Gas).
- **IS 15493 / 6382 / 2190** — Indian standards for extinguishers and suppression systems.

Designing a fire protection system for a **server room** (Class E hazard) requires:
1. **Inert gas or clean agent** (chain-breaking + oxygen displacement, no residue).
2. **No water** (water damages electronics).
3. **Pre-discharge alarm + 30-second delay** (for human evacuation).

The engineer is essentially **selecting which side(s) of the tetrahedron** to break — guided by hazard type, occupancy, and asset sensitivity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Absorption by a Water Spray System

This is a frequently asked derivation in KTU 2024 ESE. The objective is to compute the total heat absorbed by a water spray nozzle discharging a known mass of water.

**Problem Statement (Model):** A water spray nozzle discharges **m = 5 kg** of water per minute. The water is initially at **T₁ = 20 °C**, and the protected surface reaches thermal equilibrium after complete evaporation. Calculate the total heat absorbed by the water.

### Step 1: Identify the two heat-absorption stages
A water droplet absorbing heat from a flame undergoes:
- **Stage 1 — Sensible heating** from 20 °C to 100 °C.
- **Stage 2 — Latent heat of vaporisation** at 100 °C.

### Step 2: Apply the Sensible Heat Equation

The sensible heat (heating without phase change) is governed by:

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Substitute the values:

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = 5 \text{ kg} \times 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times (100 - 20) \text{ K}$$

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = 5 \times 4.186 \times 80$$

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = 1674.4 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Latent Heat Equation

The latent heat absorbed during vaporisation is:

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_v$$

Substitute the values:

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = 5 \text{ kg} \times 2256 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = 11280 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Total Heat Absorbed

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 1674.4 + 11280$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 12954.4 \text{ kJ} \approx 12.95 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 5: Express in Per-Unit Terms

The total heat absorbed per kg of water is:

$$q_{\text{total}} = c_p \cdot \Delta T + L_v = 4.186 \times 80 + 2256$$

$$q_{\text{total}} = 334.88 + 2256 = 2590.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Latent heat contributes **~87 %** of the total heat absorption. This is why **complete vaporisation** of the water spray is critical — if water is not vaporised, its cooling efficiency drops to roughly one-seventh of the maximum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass of CO₂ Required for Total Flooding of a Room

**Problem Statement (Model):** A computer server room measures **6 m × 5 m × 3 m**. The fire load is Class E. Design the CO₂ system to achieve a concentration of **40 % by volume** at STP.

### Step 1: Calculate the Protected Volume

$$V = L \times B \times H = 6 \times 5 \times 3 = 90 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2: Convert Volume to Litres

$$V = 90 \times 1000 = 90000 \text{ L}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Moles of CO₂ Required

The required concentration of 40 % by volume means CO₂ occupies 40 % of the volume; equivalently, in 1 mole of gas mixture, 0.40 mol is CO₂.

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{V}{V_m} \times \frac{C_{\text{CO}_2}}{100}$$

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{90000}{22.414} \times 0.40$$

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = 4015.35 \times 0.40$$

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = 1606.14 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 4: Convert Moles to Mass of CO₂

Using the molecular mass of CO₂, $M = 44 \text{ g/mol}$:

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = n \times M = 1606.14 \times 44$$

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 70670.16 \text{ g} \approx 70.67 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 5: Account for the Vapour Pressure of Liquid CO₂

The actual liquid CO₂ stored in the cylinder has a density of approximately $\rho_{\text{liq, CO}_2} \approx 0.7 \text{ kg/L}$ at 21 °C. Thus the volume of liquid CO₂ required is:

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}} = \frac{70.67}{0.7}$$

$$V_{\text{liq}} \approx 100.96 \text{ L}$$

### Step 6: Number of 45 L CO₂ Cylinders

Cylinders are typically 45 L water-volume.

$$N_{\text{cylinders}} = \frac{V_{\text{liq}}}{45} = \frac{100.96}{45} \approx 2.24$$

Rounded up to **3 cylinders**, with redundancy and leakage factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** An additional **20–30 % safety factor** is applied for leakage, openings, and pipe losses, leading to a final design mass of approximately **85–92 kg** of CO₂.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Oxygen Concentration for a Fuel

**Problem Statement:** The **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)** of a polymer is **27 %**. The polymer is placed in an environment where N₂ is used to dilute the air. Calculate the mole fraction of N₂ required to suppress combustion.

### Step 1: Recall the LOI Definition

$$\text{LOI} = \frac{V_{O_2}}{V_{O_2} + V_{N_2}} \times 100$$

Rearranging for the mole fraction of O₂:

$$x_{O_2} = \frac{\text{LOI}}{100} = \frac{27}{100} = 0.27$$

### Step 2: Compute the Mole Fraction of N₂

Since the gas mixture is binary (O₂ + N₂):

$$x_{O_2} + x_{N_2} = 1$$

$$x_{N_2} = 1 - 0.27 = 0.73$$

### Step 3: Express as a Percentage

$$x_{N_2} = 73 \text{ \%}$$

### Step 4: Final Concentration of O₂ for Design

A safety margin is added (typically 5 % below LOI):

$$x_{O_2, design} = \text{LOI} - 5 = 22 \text{ \%}$$

Thus the **design N₂ concentration** is **78 %**, providing a safety margin against reignition.

---

This matrix is the **single most important table** for the KTU 2024 ESE. It is frequently asked as a 7-mark question.

| Agent | Fuel | Oxygen | Heat | Chain Reaction | Primary Class | Residue | Conductivity |
|:------|:----:|:------:|:----:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:-------:|:------------:|
| **Water** | – | partial | ✓✓✓ | – | A | Yes (wet) | Conducts |
| **Water Mist** | – | partial | ✓✓✓ | partial | A, B (limited), E | Minimal | Low |
| **Foam (AFFF)** | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | – | A, B | Yes (wet) | Conducts |
| **CO₂** | – | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | – | B, C, E | None | Non-conductor |
| **DCP (ABC)** | – | – | – | ✓✓✓ | A, B, C, E | Yes (powder) | Non-conductor |
| **DCP (BC)** | – | – | – | ✓✓✓ | B, C, E | Yes | Non-conductor |
| **Argon / N₂** | – | ✓✓✓ | – | – | A, B, C | None | Non-conductor |
| **IG-541 (Inergen)** | – | ✓✓✓ | – | partial | A, B, C | None | Non-conductor |
| **Halon 1301** | – | – | – | ✓✓✓ | A, B, C | None | Non-conductor |
| **FM-200** | – | – | ✓ | ✓✓ | A, B, C | None | Non-conductor |
| **Novec 1230** | – | – | ✓✓ | ✓ | A, B, C | None | Non-conductor |
| **Wet Chemical** | ✓ | – | ✓✓ | – | K (F) | Yes (film) | Conducts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** ✓✓✓ = Primary extinguishing mechanism; ✓ = Secondary; – = Not applicable / negligible.

---

## 3.5 Decision Logic Flow for Selecting an Extinguishing Agent

This logic is the basis of the 14-mark KTU design problem. The student should memorise the following order of priority:

1. **Identify fire class** (A, B, C, D, K/E).
2. **Identify presence of electrical hazard** (→ exclude water-based agents).
3. **Identify presence of sensitive equipment** (→ exclude wet/powder agents; prefer clean agents).
4. **Identify human occupancy** (→ exclude toxic agents; require pre-discharge alarm).
5. **Match agent to class using the matrix above.**
6. **Apply code-specified design concentration and discharge time.**
7. **Size the system** (cylinder count, pipe sizing, nozzle count).

This seven-step methodology, when written out clearly, fetches full marks in KTU ESE.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Triangle — 2D Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel]:::fuel -->|Combines with| B[Oxygen]
    B -->|Combined with| C[Heat]
    C -->|Ignites| A
    classDef fuel fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Fire Triangle is a closed loop: each element enables the next. Removing **any one** of the three elements (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat) causes the fire to **extinguish**.

---

## 4.2 Fire Tetrahedron — 3D Pyramid Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel]:::face --> B[Oxygen]:::face
    B --> C[Heat]:::face
    C --> D[Chain Reaction]:::face
    D --> A
    A --> C
    B --> D
    classDef face fill:#F39C12,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Fire Tetrahedron shows the **chain reaction** as a fourth, equally essential face. The four faces are all interdependent — true extinguishment requires breaking **at least one** of the four.

---

```mermaid
    CYL --> PIP[Distribution Piping Network]:::pipe

    FILL --> OUT[Fire Suppressed in Under 60 seconds]:::out

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram is the standard KTU answer expectation for the question *"Explain with a block diagram the operation of a total flooding fire suppression system."* Every block (detection → alarm → evacuation → valve actuation → cylinder discharge → distribution → flooding) carries marks in ESE valuation.

## 4.6 Process Flow — How a Halon/Clean Agent Discharges

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Detection sensors trigger FACP] --> S2[Step 2: Audible and visual pre-discharge alarm]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Personnel evacuation 30 sec]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Solenoid valve opens via FACP signal]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Pressurised agent released from cylinder]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Agent flows through pipework]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Discharge through nozzles into room]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Agent concentration reaches design level within 10 sec]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Chain reaction interrupted and fire suppressed]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Hold time maintained typically 10 to 15 min]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the Fire Triangle and the Fire Tetrahedron. State the significance of the fourth element.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fire Triangle | Fire Tetrahedron |
|:-------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| **Model Year** | Classical (pre-1970s) | Modern (introduced by H.W. Emmons, 1970s) |
| **Number of Elements** | 3 (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat) | 4 (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, Chain Reaction) |
| **Representation** | 2-D triangle | 3-D pyramid (4 faces) |
| **Chain Reaction** | Not represented | Explicit fourth face |
| **Extinguishing Agents Explained** | Water, CO₂, foam | Also explains Halon and DCP mechanisms |

**Significance of the Fourth Element (Chain Reaction):**
- The fourth element is the **self-sustaining chemical chain reaction** in the gas phase.
- It involves free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) propagating combustion.
- **Halons** and **Dry Chemical Powders** extinguish fire primarily by breaking this chain reaction.
- The triangle alone cannot explain how Halons work — hence the tetrahedron was introduced.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Why is water not used on electrical fires and burning oils? Give two reasons for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Why Water is NOT used on Electrical Fires (Class E):**
1. **Conductivity of Water:** Pure water has low conductivity, but ordinary tap water contains dissolved salts and minerals, making it a good conductor. When sprayed on a live electrical conductor, it creates a conductive path, causing **electric shock or electrocution** to the operator.
2. **Short-circuit and Equipment Damage:** Water can cause **short circuits**, damaging expensive electronic equipment. The leakage current can also trip protective devices unpredictably.

**Why Water is NOT used on Burning Oils / Cooking Fats (Class K):**
1. **Flammable Liquid Floatation:** Oil is **less dense** than water, so water sinks below the oil layer. The water rapidly vaporises into steam, ejecting the burning oil upwards in a **"boil-over" or "flare-up" explosion** that can cause severe burns and spread the fire.
2. **Rapid Vapour Expansion:** The 1:1700 expansion of water to steam, combined with the high oil temperature (~300 °C), can cause the burning oil to be projected several metres, escalating the fire dramatically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write *"water conducts electricity"* without mentioning **dissolved salts/impurities** and the **density difference with oil**. The mark-scheme specifically awards credit for these physical causes.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **Fire Triangle** and the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Discuss how the addition of the fourth element changed modern fire-extinguishing theory. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the mechanism, application, advantages, and limitations of **Water** and **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** as fire-extinguishing agents. Tabulate the fire classes on which each is effective. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Part (a)**

**1. Fire Triangle (2 Marks):**
The Fire Triangle is a classical 2-D model with three sides:
- **Fuel** — combustible material.
- **Oxygen** — usually from air (≥ 16 % needed for sustained combustion).
- **Heat** — energy to raise the fuel to its ignition temperature.

Removal of any side stops the fire. This is the basis of traditional suppression: **cooling** (water), **smothering** (foam, CO₂), and **fuel removal** (stop gas supply).

**2. Fire Tetrahedron (2 Marks):**
A 3-D pyramid with four faces. The fourth face is the **self-sustaining chain reaction** of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) in the gas-phase flame zone. Without the chain reaction, combustion is not self-sustaining.

**3. Impact on Modern Theory (3 Marks):**
- Pre-1970s, fire theory could not explain why certain agents (Halon, DCP) were so effective at low concentrations without significantly cooling the fuel or displacing oxygen.
- The tetrahedron introduced by **Howard Emmons** identified that the **chain reaction itself** is a target of extinguishment.
- This led to the development of **chemical inhibition agents** (Halons, ABC powder, FM-200, Novec 1230) that work at the **molecular level**, requiring only **3–7 % by volume** of the agent to extinguish a fire.
- Modern codes (NFPA 2001) and standards (IS 15493) now classify clean agents by their ability to break the chain reaction.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the fire triangle and fire tetrahedron as in Section 4.1 and 4.2 of these notes.

---

### **Model Answer — Part (b)**

**1. Water as Extinguishing Agent (3 Marks):**

**Mechanism:** Primarily **cooling** (heat removal); secondarily **smothering** through steam expansion (1700× volume increase).

**Key Properties:**
- Latent heat of vaporisation: $L_v = 2256 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Specific heat: $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Cheap, abundant, and non-toxic.

**Applications:**
- Class A fires (wood, paper, textiles).
- Class B fires in **spray/fog form** (cooling flammable vapours).
- **Water mist** systems for Class E hazards.

**Limitations:**
- Cannot be used on Class C (electrical) — conducts current.
- Cannot be used on Class D (combustible metals) — reacts violently (e.g., 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂ ↑).
- Cannot be used on Class K (cooking oils) — causes boil-over.
- Frozen pipes can render the system inoperative.

**2. DCP as Extinguishing Agent (3 Marks):**

**Mechanism:** **Chain-breaking** — interrupts the free-radical chain reaction in the flame. Monoammonium phosphate (ABC) decomposes in the flame to form phosphorus-containing radicals that recombine H• and OH• radicals.

**Composition:**
- **ABC Powder** (Ammonium Phosphate-based) — for Class A, B, C, E.
- **BC Powder** (Sodium Bicarbonate-based) — for Class B, C, E.
- **Special DCP** (e.g., NaCl, Cu powder) — for specific Class D metals.

**Applications:**
- Portable extinguishers (most common).
- Fixed DCP systems for engine nacelles, paint shops, chemical plants.

**Limitations:**
- Leaves corrosive / abrasive residue (cannot be used in server rooms, museums, etc.).
- Requires clean-up after discharge.
- Limited cooling effect — reignition risk on hot Class A fuels.
- Visibility in the protected space becomes zero during discharge.

**Tabulation (1 mark):**

| Agent | Class A | Class B | Class C | Class D | Class E | Class K |
|:------|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|
| Water | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗✗ | ✗ | ✗✗ |
| DCP (ABC) | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| DCP (BC) | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the working principles of **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** and **Foam** as fire-fighting media. List the fire classes on which they are used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are **Halons**? Why are they phased out? Discuss any **three Halon alternatives** with their properties and applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Part (a)**

**1. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) (3.5 Marks):**

**Working Principle:** CO₂ is stored under pressure (~57 bar) as a liquid. On discharge, it expands rapidly and **displaces oxygen** in the protected space, reducing O₂ concentration below the ~14 % threshold required to sustain combustion. A small portion absorbs **heat** in the form of **sensible heat** as the gas expands and cools (down to -78 °C, forming dry ice).

**Key Properties:**
- Colourless, odourless, electrically non-conductive.
- Leaves **no residue** — suitable for clean applications.
- Density ≈ 1.87 kg/m³ (1.5× heavier than air — settles below).
- Design concentration: 30–50 % by volume depending on hazard.

**Fire Classes:**
- **Class B** (Flammable liquids) — Excellent.
- **Class C / E** (Electrical fires) — Excellent (non-conductor).
- **Class A** (Surface fires) — Limited (no cooling; reignition risk).

**Limitations:**
- Asphyxiation risk to humans at > 4 % concentration.
- Cannot be used in normally occupied spaces without lock-out.
- Storage pressure makes cylinders heavy.

**2. Foam (3.5 Marks):**

**Working Principle:** Foam is a stable mass of air-filled bubbles formed by mixing a **foam concentrate** (e.g., AFFF — Aqueous Film Forming Foam, or Protein Foam) with water in the ratio **1 %, 3 %, or 6 %**, then aerating. The foam blanket:
- **Smothers** the fuel surface (cuts oxygen).
- **Prevents** vapour release (cuts fuel vapour).
- **Cools** the fuel via the water content.

**Types and Expansion Ratios:**
- **Low Expansion (E = 4 to 12):** Hydrocarbon fires.
- **Medium Expansion (E = 12 to 100):** Bulk flammable liquid storage.
- **High Expansion (E > 200):** Enclosed spaces (engine rooms, hangars).

**Fire Classes:**
- **Class A** (with wet foam) — Yes.
- **Class B** — Excellent (especially AFFF on hydrocarbons).
- **Class C, D, K** — Not recommended.

**Limitations:**
- Foam solution is conductive — cannot be used on live electrical equipment.
- Cannot be used on water-reactive fuels (Class D).
- Polar solvents (alcohol, acetone) require **Alcohol-Resistant AFFF (AR-AFFF)**.

---

### **Model Answer — Part (b)**

**1. What are Halons? (2 Marks):**
Halons are **halogenated hydrocarbon extinguishing agents** in which one or more hydrogen atoms have been replaced by halogen atoms (fluorine, chlorine, bromine). They extinguish fire primarily by **interrupting the chemical chain reaction** of combustion.

Common Halons:
- **Halon 1211** (BCF, CBrClF₂) — for streaming applications (portable extinguishers).
- **Halon 1301** (BTM, CBrF₃) — for total flooding systems (occupied spaces).

**2. Why are Halons Phased Out? (2 Marks):**
- Halons contain **chlorine and bromine**, which catalytically destroy stratospheric ozone.
- **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP):** Halon 1301 has ODP = 12 (twelve times that of CFC-11).
- Atmospheric lifetime: **65 years** (extremely long).
- **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and its amendments ban the production of Halons for new installations.
- Existing Halon systems must be **recovered, recycled, and reused** until decommissioned.

**3. Halon Alternatives (3 Marks):**

**(i) FM-200 (HFC-227ea, C₃HF₇):**
- ODP = 0, GWP₁₀₀ = 2050.
- Extinguishes by **chain interruption** + minor **cooling**.
- Stored as liquefied gas at 25 bar.
- Design concentration: 7–9 % by volume.
- Safe for occupied spaces (NOAEL ≈ 9 %).
- **Application:** Computer rooms, telecommunication centres, control rooms.

**(ii) Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12, C₆F₁₂O):**
- ODP = 0, GWP₁₀₀ = 1 (essentially zero).
- Atmospheric lifetime: **5 days** (cleanest option).
- Extinguishes primarily by **heat absorption** (physical cooling).
- Stored as a liquid at low pressure.
- **Application:** Archives, museums, data centres where environmental impact is critical.

**(iii) Inert Gas Mixtures (IG-01, IG-100, IG-541):**
- **IG-01** (Argon): pure Ar at 200/300 bar.
- **IG-100** (Nitrogen): pure N₂ at 200/300 bar.
- **IG-541 (Inergen)**: 52 % N₂ + 40 % Ar + 8 % CO₂ — 200/300 bar.
- ODP = 0, GWP = 0.
- Extinguish by **oxygen displacement** (bring O₂ from 21 % to ≤ 12 %).
- Ideal for **occupied spaces** and **environmentally sensitive** applications.
- **Application:** Control rooms, switchgear rooms, libraries, archives.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students wrongly state that **FM-200 is "ozone-safe"** without explaining the **GWP of 2050**, which is still significant under the **Kigali Amendment (2016)**. Always mention both ODP and GWP when discussing clean agents. Marks are lost when only one is addressed.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision summary — read this the night before the exam.**

### **Core Definitions**
- **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + Oxygen + Heat (3 elements, classical).
- **Fire Tetrahedron:** Adds **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** as the 4th face (modern).

### **Fire Classes — Quick Map**
- **A** → Solid (water).
- **B** → Liquid (foam, CO₂, DCP).
- **C** → Gas (DCP, CO₂).
- **D** → Metal (special DCP only).
- **E** → Electrical (CO₂, clean agent, DCP).
- **K** → Cooking oil (wet chemical).

### **Mechanism Map (Agent → Face)**
- **Water** → Heat (cooling).
- **Foam** → Fuel + Oxygen (smothering).
- **CO₂** → Oxygen (displacement).
- **DCP** → Chain Reaction.
- **Inert Gas** → Oxygen (displacement).
- **Halon** → Chain Reaction.
- **FM-200 / Novec** → Chain + Heat.
- **Wet Chemical** → Fuel + Heat (saponification).

### **Critical Numbers to Memorise**
- $L_v$ (water) = **2256 kJ/kg**.
- $c_p$ (water) = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- Steam expansion ratio = **1700 ×**.
- Minimum O₂ for combustion = **~14 %**.
- Design O₂ (inert systems) = **≤ 10–12 %**.
- Design CO₂ concentration = **30–50 %**.
- Halon 1301 ODP = **12**, GWP = **6900**.
- FM-200 ODP = **0**, GWP = **2050**.
- Novec 1230 ODP = **0**, GWP = **1**.
- AFFF proportioning: **1 %, 3 %, 6 %**.
- Foam expansion: Low 4–12, Medium 12–100, High > 200.
- CO₂ pre-discharge alarm delay = **30 sec**.
- Halon discharge time = **≤ 10 sec**.
- $V_m$ at STP = **22.414 L/mol**.

### **Forbidden Combinations (Lose Marks if Confused)**
- Water on **electrical fires** → shock risk.
- Water on **metal fires** → hydrogen explosion.
- Water on **cooking oil** → boil-over.
- Water on **flammable liquids** → fuel spread.
- Foam on **electrical fires** → conduction.
- CO₂ on **personnel** without evacuation → asphyxiation.
- Halons on **new installations** → banned.

### **Codes & Standards for Citation**
- **IS 2190** — Fire extinguishers.
- **IS 6382** — Automatic CO₂ systems.
- **IS 15493** — Clean agent systems.
- **NFPA 10** — Portable extinguishers.
- **NFPA 11** — Foam systems.
- **NFPA 12** — CO₂ systems.
- **NFPA 17** — DCP systems.
- **NFPA 2001** — Clean agent systems.
- **Montreal Protocol (1987)** — Halon phase-out.
- **Kigali Amendment (2016)** — HFC phase-down.

### **Key Engineering Formulae (For Derivation Questions)**
- Sensible heat: $Q = m c_p \Delta T$
- Latent heat: $Q = m L_v$
- Total heat: $Q = m c_p \Delta T + m L_v$
- Foam proportioning: $C = V_c / (V_c + V_w) \times 100$
- Foam expansion: $E = V_f / V_s$
- Moles: $n = PV/RT$
- CO₂ mass: $m = (V/V_m) \times C_{CO_2} \times M_{CO_2}$
- LOI definition: $\text{LOI} = [O_2]/([O_2] + [N_2]) \times 100$

### **Top 5 Two-Mark FAQs to Practise**
1. Difference between Fire Triangle and Tetrahedron.
2. Why is CO₂ stored as a liquid under pressure?
3. Why is dry chemical powder effective at low concentrations?
4. What is AFFF? Where is it used?
5. What is the role of bromine in Halon chemistry?

> **END OF TOPIC NOTES — Module 3, PCSFT501 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Halon Replacement and Halon Substitutes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Need for Halon Replacement and Halon Substitutes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Halons** are a class of **brominated fluorocarbons (BFCs)** historically used as highly effective gaseous fire suppression agents. Chemically, they are halocarbons in which one or more of the hydrogen atoms of a hydrocarbon (typically methane or ethane) have been replaced by **bromine (Br)**, along with other halogen atoms (fluorine and/or chlorine).

The two most industrially significant members used in fire protection are:

- **Halon 1211** — Bromochlorodifluoromethane (CF₂ClBr) — primarily used in portable extinguishers.
- **Halon 1301** — Bromotrifluoromethane (CF₃Br) — primarily used in total-flooding systems for high-value assets (server rooms, aircraft, museums, control rooms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Halon Phase-Out Mandate:** Because bromine atoms released in the stratosphere catalytically destroy ozone (O₃), Halons were classified as **Class I Ozone-Depleting Substances (ODS)** under the **Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer (1987)**, with the production freeze initiated in 1994 and complete phase-out (with limited essential-use exemptions) by **January 1, 1994 (Developed Countries)** and **January 1, 2010 (Developing Countries, including India)**.

### 1.2 The Three-Dimensional Damage Profile of Halons

| Dimension | Description | Critical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Ozone Depletion** | Bromine radicals catalytically break O₃ → O₂ | **ODP** (Ozone Depletion Potential) |
| **Global Warming** | Halons are extremely potent greenhouse gases | **GWP** (Global Warming Potential) |
| **Atmospheric Persistence** | Halons resist tropospheric breakdown | **ALT** (Atmospheric Lifetime, in years) |

For reference: **Halon 1301** has an **ODP = 10.0**, **GWP₁₀₀ = 7,140**, and **ALT = 65.0 years** (per IPCC AR6).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lifeguard Who Poisoned the Pool"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners:** Imagine Halon as a brilliant "lifeguard" who saved thousands of "swimmers" (buildings, aircraft, data centers) from "drowning" (fire). The lifeguard worked silently, efficiently, and without leaving residue. However, investigators later discovered that the lifeguard was secretly dumping **bromine-laced chemicals** into the planet's **protective ozone layer** — the Earth's natural "sunscreen." This sunscreen shields all life from harmful **UV-B and UV-C radiation**.
>
> Just as society would not tolerate a lifeguard harming the very pool he protects, the international community under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** decided to **retire Halons** and find replacement "lifeguards" — chemicals that extinguish fire just as effectively but leave the ozone layer (and the climate) **untouched**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized metric values (per **WMO Scientific Assessment of Ozone Depletion, 2018 & 2022**) are central to KTU problems:

- **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** — dimensionless; reference standard is **CFC-11 (CCl₃F) = 1.0**.
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP₁₀₀)** — dimensionless; reference standard is **CO₂ = 1.0** over a 100-year horizon.
- **Atmospheric Lifetime (τ)** — measured in **years**.
- **NOAEL** (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) — % v/v agent in air below which no toxic effect is observed.
- **LOAEL** (Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level) — % v/v above which adverse physiological effects appear.
- **Safety Margin** = $\dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{\text{Design Concentration}}$

> [!NOTE]
> A **safety margin ≥ 1.0** is the absolute KTU / NFPA 2001 minimum. Most authorities (e.g., **NFPA 2001, Annex A**) recommend a margin **≥ 1.3** for occupied spaces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Halons Were Exceptional Fire-Fighters (Historical Context)

The fire-extinguishing efficacy of Halons arises from a **chemical (chain-breaking) mechanism** that interferes with the **fire tetrahedron**:

$$ \text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{R–H} \longrightarrow \text{HBr} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \ (\text{less energetic}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bromine radicals (**Br•**) catalytically scavenge the **free radicals (H•, OH•, O•)** that sustain combustion. This is fundamentally different from inert agents (CO₂, N₂, Ar) that work by **oxygen displacement** or from water/foam that works by **cooling**.

**Why Halons were industry favorites for 30+ years:**

1. **Extremely low extinguishing concentration** (3–5% v/v), so people could still breathe in protected spaces.
2. **Electrically non-conductive** — safe for energized electrical equipment.
3. **Clean** — leaves no residue, no corrosion, no thermal shock damage.
4. **Rapid discharge** — reaches extinguishing concentration in **≤ 10 seconds**.
5. **Compact storage** — liquefied gas at moderate pressure.

### 2.2 The Catalytic Ozone Destruction Cycle by Bromine

The complete bromine-catalyzed ozone destruction occurs in three repeating steps (each cycle destroys **2 O₃** and the Br is regenerated):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Br}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{3} &\longrightarrow \text{BrO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: BrO}^{\bullet} + \text{O} &\longrightarrow \text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Net Reaction: } 2\text{O}_{3} &\longrightarrow 3\text{O}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **"Why Bromine is Worse than Chlorine":** A single bromine atom can destroy **~60,000 ozone molecules** before being sequestered. The corresponding figure for chlorine is **~100,000**, but bromine's stratospheric lifetime and reactivity make it **40–60× more effective per unit mass** at destroying ozone. This is why ODP(Halon 1301) = 10, even though Halon 1301 contains only one Br atom.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the KTU-mandated high-yield formula reference for this Module-3 topic.** Memorize the metric definitions; examiners frequently test the *interpretation* of these values.

| # | Formula / Definition | Mathematical Expression | Standard / Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ozone Depletion Potential** | $\text{ODP}_x = \dfrac{\text{Steady-State O}_3 \text{ destroyed by unit mass of } x}{\text{Steady-State O}_3 \text{ destroyed by unit mass of CFC-11}}$ | WMO 2018/2022 |
| 2 | **Global Warming Potential (100-yr)** | $\text{GWP}_{100}(x) = \dfrac{\int_0^{100} a_x \cdot [x(t)]\, dt}{\int_0^{100} a_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot [\text{CO}_2(t)]\, dt}$ | IPCC AR6 |
| 3 | **Atmospheric Lifetime (1st-order decay)** | $[x](t) = [x]_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | WMO |
| 4 | **Extinguishing Concentration (mole basis)** | $C_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{agent}}}{n_{\text{air}} + n_{\text{agent}}} \times 100\ \% $ | NFPA 2001 |
| 5 | **Safety Margin (occupancy test)** | $\text{SM} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{C_{\text{design}}}$ | NFPA 2001 Annex A |
| 6 | **Mass of Agent Required (kg)** | $m = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot C_{\text{design}} \cdot M_{\text{agent}}}{M_{\text{air}} \cdot 100}$ | ISO 14520 |
| 7 | **Cylinder Fill Density Limit** | $\rho_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{agent}}}{V_{\text{cylinder}}} \leq \rho_{\text{fill, max}}$ | DOT/TC cylinders |
| 8 | **Equivalent Halon 1301 Mass** | $m_{\text{eq}} = m_{\text{Halon 1301}} \times \dfrac{\text{OAC}_{\text{new}}}{\text{OAC}_{\text{Halon 1301}}}$ | NFPA 2001 |
| 9 | **Design Concentration Add-on** | $C_{\text{design}} = C_{\text{ext}} \times \text{Safety Factor (typically 1.2–1.3)}$ | NFPA 2001 |
| 10 | **Boiling Point Criterion** | $T_b \leq -20 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ for total-flooding use at room temperature | ISO 14520 |

Where:
- $V$ = protected volume (m³)
- $\rho_{\text{air}}$ = air density at design temperature ($\approx 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at 20 °C)
- $M_{\text{agent}}, M_{\text{air}}$ = molar masses (kg/kmol)
- OAC = Optimum Application Concentration (% v/v)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The halon-replacement framework taught in this module directly applies to:

- **Data centers and server farms** — protecting uninterrupted mission-critical equipment.
- **Aviation cargo bays** — historical Halon 1301 domain, now transitioning to HFC-227ea / FK-5-1-12.
- **Offshore oil platforms** — unmanned machinery spaces where human toxicity is less critical.
- **Military land vehicles** — Novec 1230 is now standard (U.S. Army STANAG 4561 / NFPA 2001).
- **Telecommunication exchanges** — clean-agent flooding systems.
- **Museums, archives, libraries** — heritage protection, zero residue mandatory.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Skill Linkage:** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO3** — *"Evaluate appropriate fire suppression agents for varied hazards"*, and is examined under **Bloom's Level 4 (Analyze)** in Part A and **Level 5 (Evaluate)** in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence Between a Halon 1301 System and a Substitute System

**Problem Context:** A server room (volume $V = 250\ \text{m}^3$) is currently protected by Halon 1301. Following the Montreal-Protocol phase-out, the operator must replace Halon 1301 with **HFC-227ea (FM-200)**. Determine the **mass of HFC-227ea** required to deliver an equivalent suppression performance, and verify the toxicity safety margin.

#### Given Data (from NFPA 2001 / ISO 14520 tables)

| Parameter | Halon 1301 | HFC-227ea (FM-200) |
|---|---|---|
| **Extinguishing Concentration, $C_{\text{ext}}$** | 3.5 % v/v | 6.7 % v/v |
| **NOAEL** | 5.0 % v/v | 9.0 % v/v |
| **Molar Mass, $M$** | 148.9 g/mol | 170.0 g/mol |
| **Design Safety Factor** | 1.20 | 1.30 |
| **Oxygen, O₂ in protected room** | 21 % | 21 % |
| **Air density at 20 °C, $\rho_{\text{air}}$** | 1.20 kg/m³ | 1.20 kg/m³ |

#### Step 1 — Compute the Design Concentration

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{design}}(\text{Halon 1301}) &= 3.5 \times 1.20 = 4.20\ \% \ \text{v/v} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{design}}(\text{HFC-227ea}) &= 6.7 \times 1.30 = 8.71\ \% \ \text{v/v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Comment:** The design concentration is intentionally raised above the extinguishing concentration to absorb the **fuel-overhang** and **post-discharge leakage** during the **hold time** (typically **≥ 10 min** per NFPA 2001).

#### Step 2 — Verify the Toxicity Safety Margin

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SM}_{\text{Halon 1301}} &= \dfrac{5.0}{4.20} = 1.190 \quad \text{(marginal — barely acceptable)} \\[4pt]
\text{SM}_{\text{HFC-227ea}} &= \dfrac{9.0}{8.71} = 1.033 \quad \text{(very tight — below NFPA recommended 1.3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** The safety margin for HFC-227ea at $1.033$ is **below** the recommended 1.3 for occupied spaces. The examiner may deduct marks if the student does **not** flag this and recommend a redesign (e.g., pre-discharge alarm + evacuation in ≤30 s) to keep it compliant with **NFPA 2001, A.1.3**.

#### Step 3 — Mass of Agent Required (Derivation)

Starting from the standard mass equation (Formula 6 in the cheat sheet):

$$
m = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot C_{\text{design}} \cdot M_{\text{agent}}}{M_{\text{air}} \cdot 100}
$$

> $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97\ \text{g/mol}$ (standard dry air composition)

Substituting for HFC-227ea:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{HFC-227ea}} &= \dfrac{250 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 8.71 \cdot 170}{28.97 \cdot 100} \quad [\text{kg}] \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{250 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 8.71 \cdot 170}{2897} \quad [\text{kg}] \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{444{,}210}{2897} \quad [\text{kg}] \\[6pt]
&\approx 153.33 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Halon 1301 at the same volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Halon 1301}} &= \dfrac{250 \cdot 1.20 \cdot 4.20 \cdot 148.9}{28.97 \cdot 100} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{187{,}534.8}{2897} \\[6pt]
&\approx 64.74 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Mass Equivalence Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{m_{\text{HFC-227ea}}}{m_{\text{Halon 1301}}} &= \dfrac{153.33}{64.74} \approx 2.37
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Replacing Halon 1301 with HFC-227ea in the same hazard requires **~2.37× more agent mass** and roughly the same number of larger cylinders. The fire-suppression **performance** is matched, but the **storage footprint and refill cost** are higher — a key practical consideration KTU examiners love to test.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Halon Substitutes — Side-by-Side Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical matrix consolidates the **NFPA 2001 (2022 edition)**, **ISO 14520**, **WMO 2022**, and **IPCC AR6** data. This is a high-yield reference for KTU 2024 questions and viva.

| Substitute | Class | ODP | GWP₁₀₀ | ALT (yr) | NOAEL (%) | $C_{\text{ext}}$ (%) | Suitability for Occupied Spaces | Storage State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Halon 1301** (baseline, banned) | Brominated CFC | 10.0 | 7,140 | 65.0 | 5.0 | 3.5 | Yes (legacy) | Liquefied gas |
| **HFC-227ea (FM-200)** | Hydrofluorocarbon | 0.0 | 3,220 | 36.5 | 9.0 | 6.7 | Yes (NOAEL tight) | Liquefied gas |
| **HFC-23 (FE-13)** | Hydrofluorocarbon | 0.0 | 14,800 | 228.0 | 50.0 | 16.5 | Yes (high margin) | Gas |
| **HFC-236fa (FE-36)** | Hydrofluorocarbon | 0.0 | 8,610 | 213.0 | 15.0 | 6.9 | Yes | Liquefied gas |
| **FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230)** | Fluoroketone | 0.0 | < 1.0 | 0.014 (16 days) | 10.0 | 4.0–5.0 | Yes (excellent) | Liquid (low pressure) |
| **IG-541 (Inergen: 52% N₂, 40% Ar, 8% CO₂)** | Inert gas blend | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | 43.0 | 35.8–42.8 | Yes (high pressure) | Compressed gas |
| **IG-01 (Argon)** | Inert gas | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | 43.0 | 41.0–47.0 | Yes | Compressed gas |
| **IG-100 (Nitrogen)** | Inert gas | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | 43.0 | 37.0–43.0 | Yes | Compressed gas |
| **IG-55 (50% N₂ / 50% Ar)** | Inert gas | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | 43.0 | 38.0–44.0 | Yes | Compressed gas |
| **CO₂** | Inert / Asphyxiant | 0.0 | 1.0 | N/A | (Toxic at design conc.) | 30.0–75.0 | **NO** (unoccupied) | Liquefied gas |
| **Water Mist** | Physical | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | (No toxicity) | N/A | Yes | Liquid |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Computing ODP from Molecular Structure

**Question:** A hypothetical compound **CF₂Br₂** (dibromodifluoromethane) is being evaluated. The empirical observation is that **1 mole** of CF₂Br₂ destroys the same steady-state ozone as **2.4 moles** of CFC-11. Calculate the ODP.

**Step 1 — Convert molecular ODP (mole basis) to mass basis:**

$$
\text{ODP}_{\text{mass}} = \text{ODP}_{\text{mole}} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{CFC-11}}}{M_{\text{compound}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ODP}_{\text{mass}} &= 2.4 \times \dfrac{137.37}{209.82} \\[4pt]
&= 2.4 \times 0.6546 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.57
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering conclusion:** An ODP of 1.57 means the compound is **not acceptable** under the Montreal Protocol (only ODP = 0 compounds are allowed). This explains why all **C–Br bonds** are now banned in fire-suppression chemistry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
