## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Halons and explain why their production was phased out under the Montreal Protocol.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Halons are brominated fluorocarbons — halocarbons in which hydrogen atoms of a hydrocarbon skeleton are replaced by **bromine** atoms, along with fluorine and sometimes chlorine. The two most significant fire-suppression members are **Halon 1211 (CF₂ClBr)** used in portable extinguishers, and **Halon 1301 (CF₃Br)** used in total-flooding systems.

Their production and consumption were phased out under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** because the bromine atoms released in the stratosphere act as highly efficient **catalysts** for ozone destruction. A single bromine radical can destroy up to **~60,000 ozone molecules** before being sequestered. Halon 1301 has an **ODP = 10.0** (10× more ozone-destructive than CFC-11) and a **GWP₁₀₀ = 7,140**, making it an **ODS Class I substance** banned for routine use.

*(Word limit ~120; key terms underlined for KTU 2024 marking scheme.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. Differentiate between HFC-227ea (FM-200) and FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230) on the basis of GWP and atmospheric lifetime.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HFC-227ea (FM-200) | FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230) |
|---|---|---|
| **GWP₁₀₀** | 3,220 | < 1.0 |
| **Atmospheric Lifetime** | 36.5 years | 16 days (0.014 yr) |
| **ODP** | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| **Class** | Hydrofluorocarbon | Fluoroketone |

HFC-227ea, although ozone-safe, is a **potent greenhouse gas** with a long atmospheric residence time of **36.5 years**, contributing significantly to global warming. FK-5-1-12, in contrast, has a **negligible GWP (< 1)** and an atmospheric lifetime of only **16 days**, decomposing primarily in the troposphere via **photolysis and reaction with hydroxyl radicals (•OH)**. Hence, Novec 1230 is the **environmentally preferred substitute** for long-term sustainability, although FM-200 remains in widespread legacy use.

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of Q3A or Q3B**

---

#### **Question 3A. (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]*

> **(a)** Explain the catalytic mechanism by which bromine atoms released from Halons destroy stratospheric ozone. Derive the net reaction and discuss why bromine is a more efficient ozone-destroyer than chlorine on a per-atom basis. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A telephone exchange of volume **1,200 m³** is to be protected by **IG-541 (Inergen)**. The design concentration is **38% v/v**, NOAEL = 43%, room temperature = 20 °C, $\rho_{\text{air}}$ = 1.2 kg/m³, and $M_{\text{air}}$ = 28.97 g/mol. Calculate: (i) the **mass of IG-541** required, (ii) the **safety margin**, and (iii) comment on its **occupancy suitability**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to 3A(a):**

**The Three-Step Catalytic Cycle:**

**Step 1 — Bromine attacks ozone:**

$$
\text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{3} \longrightarrow \text{BrO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{2}
$$

*Explanatory text:* A free bromine radical collides with an ozone molecule, abstracting an oxygen atom to form bromine monoxide (BrO•) and a stable oxygen molecule (O₂). The BrO• is itself a reactive free radical.

**Step 2 — BrO• regenerates Br• and consumes free oxygen:**

$$
\text{BrO}^{\bullet} + \text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{2}
$$

*Explanatory text:* The bromine monoxide radical collides with a free atomic oxygen (liberated by UV photolysis of O₂ or O₃), producing a second O₂ molecule and **regenerating the Br• catalyst**.

**Step 3 — Net Reaction (sum of Steps 1 and 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Br}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_{3} + \text{O} &\longrightarrow \text{Br}^{\bullet} + 2\text{O}_{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Cancelling Br}^{\bullet} \text{ on both sides: } 2\text{O}_{3} + 2\text{O} &\longrightarrow 4\text{O}_{2} \\
\text{or more simply: } 2\text{O}_{3} &\longrightarrow 3\text{O}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanatory text:* The Br• appears on both sides — it is **not consumed**. Therefore it is a true **catalyst**, and the cycle can repeat **~60,000 times** before the Br is sequestered in reservoir species like HBr or BrONO₂.

**Why Bromine is more efficient per atom than Chlorine:**

- The ClO• intermediate has a stronger **Cl–O bond** (bond dissociation energy ≈ 269 kJ/mol) than Br–O (≈ 230 kJ/mol), so Step 2 for Cl is slower.
- Bromine reservoir species (HBr, BrONO₂) are **photolysed at longer wavelengths**, releasing Br• at lower altitudes and throughout the stratosphere.
- The net result: bromine is ~**40–60× more destructive per unit mass** than chlorine, which is why ODP(Halon 1301) = 10 despite containing only one Br atom.

---

#### **Model Solution to 3A(b):**

**Given:**
- $V = 1{,}200 \ \text{m}^3$
- $C_{\text{design}} = 38\ \% \ \text{v/v}$
- $\text{NOAEL} = 43\ \% \ \text{v/v}$
- $T = 20 \ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97\ \text{g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{IG-541}}$ (52% N₂ + 40% Ar + 8% CO₂) = $0.52(28) + 0.40(40) + 0.08(44) = 33.92\ \text{g/mol}$

**(i) Mass of IG-541:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{IG-541}} &= \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot C_{\text{design}} \cdot M_{\text{agent}}}{M_{\text{air}} \cdot 100} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1{,}200 \times 1.2 \times 38 \times 33.92}{28.97 \times 100} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1{,}855{,}910.4}{2{,}897} \\[6pt]
&\approx 640.66 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Safety Margin:**

$$
\text{SM} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{C_{\text{design}}} = \dfrac{43}{38} = 1.132
$$

**(iii) Occupancy Suitability Comment:**

> [!TIP]
> The calculated SM = **1.132** is **above 1.0** (NFPA 2001 minimum) but **below 1.3** (NFPA 2001 Annex A recommended). Hence the system is **technically acceptable for occupied spaces** but only with a **pre-discharge alarm + evacuation delay of 30 s**, plus an **oxygen self-rescuer (5-min escape BA sets)** for any personnel remaining in the room. Oxygen level in the protected space will fall from 21% to approximately **$21\% \times (1 - 0.38) = 13.0\%$**, which is the threshold for safe human exposure (NFPA 2001).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** compute the agent mass in **kg** using the molar mass of air (28.97) as a divisor only — the **multiplication by $M_{\text{agent}}$** is mandatory and is the most-skipped step.
> 2. **Do not** ignore the 38% v/v being a **design** concentration — do not re-divide by safety factor 1.3 again.
> 3. **Do not** state SM < 1.0 as a disqualification — SM = 1.132 is *acceptable but marginal*. Comment on the engineering remediation; do not just declare "not suitable".
> 4. Many students confuse $M_{\text{air}}$ with **29** (approximation is fine, but do not write 28 without unit; KTU deducts 0.5 mark for unit omission).
> 5. **Common mark-loss area:** failing to compute the **average molar mass of IG-541** from the blend composition (52/40/8) — examiner will award 0/2 marks if a single component's molar mass is substituted.

---

#### **Question 3B. (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]*

> **(a)** Classify and discuss the major categories of **halon substitutes**, citing at least **one example from each category** with its **ODP, GWP₁₀₀, atmospheric lifetime**, and **typical application**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A library archive of **3,000 m³** is to be protected by **FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230)**. The extinguishing concentration is **4.5% v/v**, NOAEL = 10%, design safety factor = 1.30, $M_{\text{Novec}} = 316\ \text{g/mol}$. Calculate: (i) the design concentration, (ii) the safety margin, and (iii) the mass of agent required at standard conditions. Comment on why Novec 1230 is the **preferred** agent for a paper archive. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to 3B(a) — Concise Tabular Answer:**

The major categories of halon substitutes are:

| Category | Example | ODP | GWP₁₀₀ | ALT | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** | HFC-227ea (FM-200) | 0.0 | 3,220 | 36.5 yr | Server rooms, control rooms |
| 2. **Fluoroketones (FKs)** | FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230) | 0.0 | < 1.0 | 16 days | Archives, museums, archives |
| 3. **Inert Gas Blends (IGs)** | IG-541 (Inergen) | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | Switchgear rooms, libraries |
| 4. **Water Mist** | High-pressure water mist | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | Machinery spaces, kitchens |
| 5. **Condensed Aerosols** | Potassium-based | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | Marine engine rooms |
| 6. **Dry Chemical Powders** | ABC powder | 0.0 | 0.0 | N/A | Vehicle engines, flammable-liquid |

> [!NOTE]
> **All halon substitutes have ODP = 0.0** by definition. The differentiation among them is by **GWP, atmospheric lifetime, toxicity (NOAEL), and storage state** — examiners expect the student to know this differentiation cold.

---

#### **Model Solution to 3B(b):**

**Given:**
- $V = 3{,}000\ \text{m}^3$
- $C_{\text{ext}} = 4.5\ \% \ \text{v/v}$
- $\text{NOAEL} = 10\ \% \ \text{v/v}$
- Safety Factor (SF) = 1.30
- $M_{\text{Novec}} = 316\ \text{g/mol}$
- $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97\ \text{g/mol}$

**(i) Design Concentration:**

$$
C_{\text{design}} = C_{\text{ext}} \times \text{SF} = 4.5 \times 1.30 = 5.85\ \%\ \text{v/v}
$$

**(ii) Safety Margin:**

$$
\text{SM} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{C_{\text{design}}} = \dfrac{10}{5.85} \approx 1.709
$$

> [!TIP]
> SM = **1.709** is **excellent** — comfortably above the NFPA 2001 recommended 1.3. Novec 1230 is among the **safest** clean agents for occupied spaces.

**(iii) Mass Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Novec 1230}} &= \dfrac{3{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 5.85 \times 316}{28.97 \times 100} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{6{,}654{,}960}{2{,}897} \\[6pt]
&\approx 2{,}297.3\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Novec 1230 is preferred for a paper archive:**

- **GWP < 1.0** — negligible contribution to climate change; aligns with the **library sector's net-zero commitments**.
- **ALT ≈ 16 days** — does not accumulate in the atmosphere; breaks down in the troposphere before reaching the stratosphere.
- **NOAEL = 10%** with a 5.85% design concentration gives a **very high safety margin (1.71)** — safe for the librarians and visitors present.
- **Liquid storage at low pressure (~3.4 bar at 21 °C)** — unlike inert gases that need **200–300 bar** cylinders, requiring far fewer and smaller cylinders.
- **Zero residue on paper** — Novec 1230 vaporizes completely without leaving moisture, unlike water mist that would destroy priceless documents.
- **No corrosive by-products** — unlike dry chemical or certain aerosols that contaminate documents and require expensive restoration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Question 3B):**
> 1. Always compute $C_{\text{design}} = C_{\text{ext}} \times \text{SF}$ **first** before the safety margin check. A common error is using $C_{\text{ext}}$ directly in the SM formula.
> 2. The **atmospheric lifetime of Novec 1230 (16 days)** is its **signature environmental advantage** — stating "GWP is low" without specifying the ALT fails to address *why* the GWP is so low, and the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 3. For the mass calculation, **do not** confuse $M_{\text{Novec}} = 316\ \text{g/mol}$ with that of HFC-227ea (170 g/mol) — the molar masses are markedly different and so are the required masses.
> 4. The **paper archive justification** is worth **2 of the 7 marks** — it must explicitly mention *zero residue* and *GWP < 1*. A generic "it's environmentally friendly" answer scores 0.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read twice before exam)**

- ✅ **Halons = brominated fluorocarbons (BFCs)**; the two key fire-suppression members are **Halon 1211 (CF₂ClBr)** and **Halon 1301 (CF₃Br)**.
- ✅ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** → Class I ODS → production phase-out complete in developed countries by **1994** and developing countries by **2010**.
- ✅ **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** references **CFC-11 = 1.0**; **Halon 1301 has ODP = 10.0**.
- ✅ **GWP₁₀₀** references **CO₂ = 1.0**; **Halon 1301 = 7,140**; **HFC-227ea = 3,220**; **Novec 1230 < 1.0**.
- ✅ **Atmospheric Lifetime (ALT)** of Halon 1301 = **65 years**; of Novec 1230 = **16 days** — the basis of its negligible GWP.
- ✅ **NOAEL/LOAEL safety margin** must be **≥ 1.0** (mandatory) and preferably **≥ 1.3** (NFPA 2001, Annex A).
- ✅ **Substitute categories** (memorize one example from each): HFC, FK, Inert Gas Blend, Water Mist, Condensed Aerosol, Dry Chemical.
- ✅ **IG-541 (Inergen)** is the only halon substitute with **GWP = 0.0 and ODP = 0.0 AND zero atmospheric lifetime** — it is the **greenest** agent available.
- ✅ **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)** is the **preferred clean agent for occupied spaces and irreplaceable assets** (archives, museums) due to high NOAEL margin + low GWP.
- ✅ **CO₂** is the only common agent that is **NOT safe for occupied spaces** (asphyxiation risk); always design for unoccupied scenarios.
- ✅ **Mass calculation formula** must use both $M_{\text{agent}}$ and $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ g/mol; do not skip either.
- ✅ **Cylinder fill density** is governed by **DOT/TC/ISO 11118/ISO 11439** standards; exceeding $\rho_{\text{fill, max}}$ can lead to **vapor lock** or **cylinder rupture** — never overfill.
- ✅ For the **Montreal Protocol exam question**, the KTU expected answer must include: (a) ODS classification, (b) catalytic ozone destruction mechanism, (c) Br• cycle of 60,000, (d) phase-out date, and (e) substitute names with comparative ODP/GWP.
- ✅ **KTU CO3 mapping:** This topic evaluates your ability to **analyze** (Bloom L4) and **evaluate** (Bloom L5) suppression agents — practice comparing two agents in tabular form every time.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Need for Halon Replacement and Halon Substitutes**
> *These notes are tuned to KTU 2024 Scheme ESE valuation. Revise the formula sheet and the analytical matrix on the day before the exam; practice at least two Part-A and one full Part-B numerical for confidence.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extinguishant Performance - Flame Extinguishing Concentration, Inerting Concentration, Fire Trials, Description, Working Principle, Method of Operation of Different Types of Portable Fire Extinguishers - Water Type, Foam Type, Dry Powder Type, CO2 Type, Vapourizing Liquid Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Extinguishant Performance — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Extinguishant Performance* is the quantitative and qualitative measure of an extinguishing agent's ability to suppress, control, or extinguish a fire. It is evaluated through standardized laboratory and field procedures to determine two critical concentration thresholds: the **Flame Extinguishing Concentration (FEC)** and the **Inerting Concentration (IC)**. These concentrations define the minimum quantity of agent required to extinguish a fire and the quantity required to prevent re-ignition of a flammable mixture, respectively.

### Flame Extinguishing Concentration (FEC)

The **Flame Extinguishing Concentration** is defined as the minimum volume percentage (or mass concentration) of an extinguishing agent, when uniformly mixed with a flammable vapor-air mixture, that will just extinguish a diffusion or pre-mixed flame under specified test conditions (typically per **ISO 14520**, **NFPA 2001**, or **BIS 15493** standards).

**Mathematical representation:**

$$C_{FEC} = \frac{V_{agent}}{V_{agent} + V_{air-fuel\ mixture}} \times 100\ \%$$

### Inerting Concentration (IC)

The **Inerting Concentration** is the minimum concentration of an extinguishing agent required to render a flammable mixture non-ignitable — i.e., the mixture can no longer be ignited by any ignition source, regardless of whether an external flame is present. IC is always numerically **greater than or equal to** FEC.

$$C_{IC} \geq C_{FEC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **FEC** → extinguishes an *existing* flame
> -IC → prevents *future* ignition of a fuel-air mixture
> - IC is the *conservative design value* used in total flooding systems.

### Fire Trials

**Fire Trials** are standardized, reproducible experimental procedures used to evaluate the effectiveness of extinguishing agents and portable extinguishers. They involve controlled combustion of standardized fuel packages (e.g., Class A wood cribs, Class B heptane pans, Class C energized electrical fires) under specified ventilation and ambient conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Fire Test Fuels (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Class A:** Wood crib (12 sticks, 38 mm × 38 mm × 500 mm) — for ordinary combustibles
> - **Class B:** Heptane pan (0.36 m² surface area) — for flammable liquids
> - **Class C:** Live electrical panels — for energized equipment
> - **Class D:** Magnesium / sodium / lithium metal — for combustible metals
> - **Class K (F):** Cooking oil (vegetable oil in deep fryer) — for kitchen fires

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Suffocating Blanket" Concept

Imagine a burning candle on your desk. To put it out, you can either:
1. **Blow it out** (cooling + removing oxygen) — this is what **Water** and **CO₂** do.
2. **Cover it with a glass jar** (starving it of oxygen) — this is **Inerting** (e.g., foam blanket, dry chemical powder cloud).
3. **Sprinkle baking soda on a kitchen grease fire** (chemical chain reaction interruption) — this is how **Dry Powder** and **Vaporizing Liquids (Halons/Clean Agents)** work.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fire Tetrahedron (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams):**
> Modern fire science recognizes **four** elements — not three — necessary for combustion:
> 1. **Fuel** (flammable material)
> 2. **Oxidizer** (typically atmospheric O₂, **20.9 %** by volume)
> 3. **Heat / Activation Energy** (ignition temperature)
> 4. **Sustained Chain Reaction** (free-radical propagation)
>
> Every extinguishing agent attacks at least **one** vertex of the tetrahedron. Water cools (heat removal). Foam smothers (oxidizer exclusion). CO₂ displaces oxygen (oxidizer exclusion). Dry powder and clean agents interrupt the chain reaction.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Value / Standard | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric O₂ concentration | **20.9 %** (by volume) | Baseline for inerting calculations |
| Minimum O₂ for human breathing | **16 %** | Safety threshold for CO₂/clean agent flooding |
| Lethal O₂ concentration | **< 6 %** | Personnel evacuation threshold |
| ISO 14520 | International clean agent standard | FEC and IC determination |
| NFPA 2001 | Standard on Clean Agent Fire Extinguishing Systems | System design |
| IS 15493 / IS 2190 | Indian Standards for portable extinguishers | BIS rating |
| 1 atm | **101.325 kPa** | Standard reference pressure |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Extinguishment — Theoretical Breakdown

Every fire extinguishing agent operates on one or more of the following physical-chemical mechanisms:

### A. Cooling Mechanism (Heat Removal)

The dominant mechanism for **water** and **water-mist** systems. Heat is absorbed during vaporization and heating of the agent.

$$Q_{absorbed} = m_{agent} \times c_{p,agent} \times \Delta T + m_{agent} \times L_{v}$$

Where:
- $Q_{absorbed}$ = total heat absorbed (kJ)
- $m_{agent}$ = mass of agent applied (kg)
- $c_{p,agent}$ = specific heat capacity (kJ/kg·K)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg)

For water: $c_p = 4.186\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ and $L_v = 2260\ \text{kJ/kg}$.

### B. Smothering / Oxygen Displacement Mechanism

The dominant mechanism for **CO₂**, **foam**, and **inert gases** (N₂, Ar). The agent displaces atmospheric oxygen below the combustion-supporting threshold (~15 % for most hydrocarbons).

$$\%O_{2,reduced} = \%O_{2,initial} \times \left(1 - \frac{V_{agent}}{V_{enclosure}}\right)$$

### C. Chain-Breaking (Chemical Inhibition) Mechanism

The dominant mechanism for **dry chemical powders** and **halogenated agents** (Halons, HFCs, FK-5-1-12). The agent decomposes in the flame zone into free radicals (e.g., Br•, Cl•, P•) that recombine with the flame-propagating radicals (H•, OH•, O•), terminating the chain reaction.

$$\text{H•} + \text{Br•} \rightarrow \text{HBr}$$
$$\text{HBr} + \text{OH•} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br•}$$

The regenerated Br• continues to catalytically destroy propagating radicals.

### D. Fuel Isolation Mechanism

The dominant mechanism for **foam** (AFFF, AFFF-AR, FFFP) and **dry powder** blankets. A physical barrier separates fuel vapors from the oxidizer.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Application / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{FEC} = \dfrac{V_{agent}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Flame Extinguishing Concentration | Vol % |
| 2 | $C_{IC} \geq C_{FEC}$ | Inerting concentration is always ≥ FEC | Vol % |
| 3 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_v$ | Total heat absorption by cooling agent | kJ |
| 4 | $m_{water} = \dfrac{Q_{HRR} \times t}{c_p \Delta T + L_v}$ | Mass of water required for HRR over time $t$ | kg |
| 5 | $V_{agent} = \dfrac{C_{FEC} \times V_{room}}{100 - C_{FEC}}$ | Agent volume for total flooding | m³ |
| 6 | $m_{CO_2} = \dfrac{V_{room} \times C_{design}}{\upsilon_{CO_2}}$ | CO₂ mass for total flooding | kg |
| 7 | $V_{CO_2,free\ air} = \dfrac{V_{room}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{100 - C_{design}}{C_{design}}\right)}$ | Free-air volume of CO₂ required | m³ |
| 8 | $A_{foam} = \dfrac{Q_{HRR}}{q_{foam}}$ | Foam application rate (per unit area) | L/min·m² |
| 9 | $t_{discharge,min} = \dfrac{V_{cylinder} \times 1000}{\dot{V}_{nozzle}}$ | Minimum discharge duration | seconds |
| 10 | $P_{burst} > 1.5 \times P_{working}$ | Safety factor for extinguisher pressure vessel | bar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Safety Factor (BIS / IS 2190):**
> Hydraulic test pressure for portable extinguisher cylinders must be **at least 1.5 times** the maximum working pressure. Hydrostatic testing is mandatory every **5 years** for CO₂ and every **2 years** for other types.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Fire Protection:** Knowing FEC and IC allows engineers to size total-flooding suppression systems for server rooms, archives, and control rooms.
- **Portable Extinguisher Selection:** Building codes (NBC 2016, IS 2190) require *specific* extinguisher types for *specific* hazard classes.
- **Industrial Safety:** Refineries, LPG bottling plants, and paint shops use FEC/IC data to design inerting blankets for storage tanks.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Agent Volume for Total Flooding System

> **Problem (KTU Style):**
> A server room of dimensions **6 m × 5 m × 3 m** is to be protected by a clean agent system using FK-5-1-12 (Novec 1230), whose **FEC = 4.5 %** for typical Class A hazards. Compute the quantity of agent required at **sea level (1 atm, 25 °C)** assuming a design concentration of **1.2 × FEC** for safety margin.

### Step 1 — Compute Enclosure Volume

$$V_{room} = L \times B \times H = 6 \times 5 \times 3 = 90\ \text{m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Apply Design Safety Factor

$$C_{design} = 1.2 \times C_{FEC} = 1.2 \times 4.5 = 5.4\ \%$$

### Step 3 — Apply Agent Volume Formula

The agent volume in the room must satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{agent}}{V_{agent} + V_{air}} = \frac{C_{design}}{100}$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{agent} = \frac{C_{design} \times V_{air}}{100 - C_{design}}$$

Since the room volume = $V_{agent} + V_{air}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{agent} \times 100 &= C_{design} \times (V_{room} - V_{agent}) \\
100 V_{agent} &= 5.4 \times (90 - V_{agent}) \\
100 V_{agent} &= 486 - 5.4 V_{agent} \\
105.4 V_{agent} &= 486 \\
V_{agent} &= 4.611\ \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Mass (FK-5-1-12 density at 25 °C ≈ 1.6 kg/L = 1600 kg/m³)

$$m_{agent} = V_{agent} \times \rho = 4.611 \times 1600 = 7377.6\ \text{kg}$$

> **Conclusion:** Approximately **7.38 tonnes** of FK-5-1-12 is required — illustrating why clean agents are used only in **high-value, low-volume** enclosures.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Water Quantity for Cooling a Fire

> **Problem:** A Class A fire releases heat at a rate of **HRR = 1500 kW**. Determine the mass of water required to control (not necessarily extinguish) the fire for **t = 10 minutes**, assuming water enters at 25 °C and must be vaporized to 100 °C steam.

### Step 1 — Total Heat to be Removed

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= \dot{Q}_{HRR} \times t \\
&= 1500 \times (10 \times 60) \\
&= 900{,}000\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Heat Absorbed per kg of Water

Water heats from 25 °C to 100 °C, then vaporizes:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{per\ kg} &= c_p \times \Delta T + L_v \\
&= 4.186 \times (100 - 25) + 2260 \\
&= 313.95 + 2260 \\
&= 2573.95\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Water Required

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{water} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{q_{per\ kg}} \\
&= \frac{900{,}000}{2573.95} \\
&= 349.7\ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Practical implication:** This corresponds to roughly **350 litres** of water — illustrating the **inefficiency of water on large hydrocarbon fires** and justifying dry chemical or foam systems for such hazards.

---

| **Discharge Time** | 60 – 120 s | 30 – 90 s | 8 – 25 s | 8 – 30 s | 6 – 30 s |

## 3.4 Detailed Working Principles

### 3.4.1 Water Type Portable Fire Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- **Cylindrical shell** (mild steel, welded, painted red — IS 5 shade)
- **Siphon tube** reaching the bottom of the cylinder
- **Cap and valve assembly** at the top
- **Pressurizing gas** — either **stored pressure** (N₂ at 7–17 bar) or **separate gas cartridge** (CO₂ at ~80 bar)
- **Discharge hose + nozzle** — straight or spray type

**Working Principle:**
- Water is pressurized by compressed gas (N₂ or CO₂)
- On opening the valve, water is forced up the siphon tube and out through the nozzle
- Cooling effect: water absorbs heat via sensible heating (25 → 100 °C) **AND** latent heat of vaporization (2260 kJ/kg)
- Total heat removed per litre ≈ **2574 kJ**

**Method of Operation (PASS Technique — KTU Mandatory):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **P – A – S – S** (Universal fire extinguisher operating sequence)
> 1. **P**ull the safety pin / break the tamper seal
> 2. **A**im the nozzle at the **base of the fire**, NOT at the flames
> 3. **S**queeze the handle to discharge
> 4. **S**weep from side to side, covering the burning area

**Limitations:**
- ❌ **NOT** suitable for Class B (flammable liquids) — water may splash and spread fire
- ❌ **NOT** suitable for Class C (electrical fires) — water is conductive
- ❌ **NOT** suitable for Class D (combustible metals) — reacts violently
- ❌ **NOT** suitable for Class K (cooking oil) — steam explosion risk
- ❌ Freezes below 0 °C (unless anti-freeze additive used)

---

### 3.4.2 Foam Type Portable Fire Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- Similar shell to water extinguisher
- Inner **polyethylene container** holds the foam concentrate (AFFF, 3 % or 6 %)
- Outer shell holds **pre-mixed water + foam concentrate**
- **Air-aspirating nozzle** (or "inline proportioner") to mix foam solution with air, creating a finished foam blanket

**Working Principle:**
- On discharge, the foam solution passes through an **air-aspirating nozzle**
- Air is entrained, generating **finished foam** with **expansion ratio 6:1 to 10:1** (Low Expansion) or up to 1000:1 (High Expansion)
- Foam floats on the surface of flammable liquids, forming a continuous **aqueous film** (for AFFF) or a **fluoro-protein blanket**
- Three mechanisms operate simultaneously:
  1. **Smothering** — excludes O₂ from fuel surface
  2. **Cooling** — water content evaporates
  3. **Fuel isolation** — prevents vapor release

**Method of Operation:**
- Bounce the foam stream off a nearby wall or surface — never spray directly into the burning liquid (avoids splashing and foam submergence)
- Sweep gently across the surface to allow the foam blanket to form

**Special Variants:**
- **AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam)** — film spreads rapidly, ideal for hydrocarbon fires
- **AFFF-AR (Alcohol Resistant)** — polymeric membrane resists polar solvents (alcohols, ketones)
- **FFFP (Film Forming Fluoro-Protein)** — better heat resistance
- **Class F Foam** — wet chemical for cooking oil (deep fryer) fires

**Limitations:**
- ❌ Not for live electrical fires (foam is conductive)
- ❌ Not for Class D metal fires
- ❌ AFFF environmental concerns (PFAS content — being phased out under new regulations)

---

### 3.4.3 Dry Powder Type Portable Fire Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- **Seamless steel cylinder** (IS 15493)
- Filled with **dry chemical powder** (typically **75 % monoammonium phosphate** for ABC; or **sodium bicarbonate** for BC)
- Pressurized with **N₂** at 10–16 bar (stored pressure type)
- **Discharge hose + applicator nozzle** (sometimes with extension wand)

**Working Principle:**
- On valve opening, N₂ propellant forces powder out through the hose
- Powder cloud enters the flame zone and **thermally decomposes** (e.g., MAP → H₃PO₄ → PO₄³⁻ radicals)
- These radicals **recombine with H• and OH•** propagating radicals in the flame chain reaction
- The reaction is **endothermic**, removing heat
- A **molten powder layer** forms on solid fuels, providing a barrier against reignition

**Method of Operation:**
- Hold the extinguisher **upright**
- **Stand 2–3 m away** from the fire
- Apply powder in **interrupted bursts** (puffs), sweeping side to side
- Continue application **even after flames are extinguished** to prevent reignition

**Advantages:**
- ✅ Effective on **multiple fire classes** (A, B, C, and with special powder on D)
- ✅ Non-conductive — safe on **live electrical up to 35 kV** at 1 m
- ✅ Works at very low temperatures (−20 °C and below)
- ✅ Rapid knockdown (seconds)

**Limitations:**
- ❌ Heavy **corrosive residue** damages electronics, machinery
- ❌ Poor visibility during discharge
- ❌ Powder cakes in humid storage (must be **discharged and recharged every 3 years**)
- ❌ Inhalation hazard in confined spaces

---

### 3.4.4 CO₂ Type Portable Fire Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- **Seamless, high-pressure steel cylinder** (DOT-3AL or IS 7285 specification)
- Working pressure **58 bar at 21 °C**
- **Siphon tube** (tube reaches bottom of cylinder)
- **Reinforced high-pressure hose** (rated ≥ 200 bar burst)
- **Horn (discharge nozzle)** — usually **non-metallic** (to prevent static electricity) and **non-conductive**
- **Safety relief burst disc** set at ~190 bar

**Working Principle:**
- Liquid CO₂ stored at high pressure (~58 bar at 21 °C)
- On valve opening, liquid CO₂ expands through the horn
- Expansion causes **rapid vaporization** and **temperature drop** (Joule-Thomson effect)
- CO₂ solidifies into **dry ice snow** at the nozzle
- Two extinguishing mechanisms:
  1. **Oxygen displacement** — CO₂ dilutes air O₂ from 20.9 % to ~15 % (below combustion threshold for most fuels)
  2. **Cooling** — dry ice snow cools the fuel surface (minor effect)

**Method of Operation:**
- Hold the extinguisher by the **insulated handle** (frostbite hazard on the horn)
- Aim at the **base of the fire**
- Sweep across the burning area
- **Caution:** Do not hold the horn during discharge — extreme cold can burn skin

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **No residue** — ideal for laboratories, server rooms, electrical panels
- ✅ Non-conductive — safe on live electrical
- ✅ Self-propelled — no pressurized gas cartridge

**Limitations:**
- ❌ **Short discharge time** (8 – 30 seconds)
- ❌ **Short throw range** (1.5 – 3 m) — operator must approach fire
- ❌ **Asphyxiation hazard** in confined spaces (O₂ reduced below 16 %)
- ❌ **No reignition protection** — re-ignites when CO₂ dissipates
- ❌ **Frostbite risk** from cold horn
- ❌ Heavy cylinder (22.5 kg CO₂ trolley weighs ~85 kg total)
- ❌ **Not for Class D** metal fires (decomposes MgO etc.)
- ❌ **Not for Class A** deep-seated fires (no cooling effect)

---

### 3.4.5 Vaporizing Liquid Type (Clean Agent) Portable Fire Extinguisher

**Construction:**
- **Seamless steel or aluminium cylinder**
- Charged with **liquefied gas** under its own vapor pressure (~25 bar for HFC-227ea; ~3.5 bar for FK-5-1-12)
- **Stored pressure** design (N₂ not required)
- **Brass valve assembly** with pressure gauge
- **Discharge nozzle** (small bore, high velocity)

**Common Agents:**
- **Halon 1211** (bromochlorodifluoromethane) — *legacy*, **banned** under Montreal Protocol due to ODP = 4
- **HFC-227ea** (FM-200) — ODP = 0, GWP = 3500
- **HFC-236fa** (FE-36) — ODP = 0, GWP = 9400
- **FK-5-1-12** (Novec 1230) — ODP = 0, GWP = 1, atmospheric lifetime = 5 days
- **HCFC Blend B** (Halotron I) — for aviation use

**Working Principle:**
- On valve actuation, the liquid agent discharges through a nozzle and **flash-vaporizes**
- Vapor entrains into the flame zone
- The agent and its decomposition products (e.g., CF₃•, Br•, F•) **interrupt the chain reaction** by recombining with H•, OH•, O• radicals
- Very fast **flame knockdown** (typically < 10 s)
- No residue, no thermal shock, no damage to electronics

**Method of Operation:**
- Hold upright, **pull pin**, squeeze handle
- **Aim at the base of the fire** and sweep
- Suitable for **occupied spaces** (FK-5-1-12 has NOAEL = 10 % — safe for human exposure)
- Discharge time typically **6 – 12 seconds**

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **No residue, electrically non-conductive**
- ✅ **Safe for occupied areas** (NOAEL-based design)
- ✅ **Rapid flame knockdown**
- ✅ **No collateral damage** to electronics, archival material, machinery
- ✅ **Zero ODP** for modern agents (HFC-227ea, FK-5-1-12)
- ✅ Ideal for **server rooms, museums, aircraft cabins, control rooms**

**Limitations:**
- ❌ **Expensive** agent (FK-5-1-12 ~ ₹15,000/kg)
- ❌ Limited cooling effect — not ideal for deep-seated Class A fires
- ❌ Requires **tight enclosure** for total flooding
- ❌ HFC-227ea has GWP concerns (under F-gas regulation phase-down)
- ❌ Discharge in confined spaces — verify NOAEL vs design concentration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOAEL vs LOAEL (Critical for KTU Board):**
> - **NOAEL** (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) = highest concentration with **no** adverse physiological effect
> - **LOAEL** (Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level) = lowest concentration with observable effects
> - Design concentration for occupied spaces **MUST be ≤ NOAEL**

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Flame Extinguishing Concentration (FEC) and Inerting Concentration (IC). Why is IC always greater than or equal to FEC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Flame Extinguishing Concentration (FEC):** The minimum volumetric percentage of an extinguishing agent, when uniformly mixed with a flammable vapor-air mixture, that extinguishes an existing flame under specified test conditions (e.g., cup burner test per ISO 14520).
>
> **Inerting Concentration (IC):** The minimum concentration of extinguishing agent that renders a flammable mixture **non-ignitable** — preventing any flame propagation even with an external ignition source present. **(1 mark for definitions)**
>
> **Why IC ≥ FEC:** Extinguishing an existing flame requires only enough agent to suppress the chain reaction momentarily; however, to **prevent reignition**, the agent must suppress vapor release and cool the fuel below its flash point, which demands a higher concentration. Additionally, IC design must account for agent loss through leakage, decomposition in the flame, and incomplete mixing. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the five classes of fire and state the appropriate portable extinguisher for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Class | Fuel Type | Recommended Extinguisher |
|---|---|---|
| A | Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper) | Water / ABC Powder / Clean Agent |
| B | Flammable liquids (petrol, oil) | Foam (AFFF) / ABC Powder / CO₂ |
| C | Live electrical equipment | CO₂ / ABC Powder / Clean Agent |
| D | Combustible metals (Mg, Na, Li) | Special Dry Powder (NaCl / Cu / graphite) |
| K (or F) | Cooking oils & fats | Wet Chemical (potassium acetate) |

**(1 mark for each correct pairing × 3 marks)** — *Examiner's note: A clean 5-row table earns full marks; partial credit for at least 3 correct pairs.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a) Describe the construction, working principle, and method of operation of a stored-pressure Dry Powder Type portable fire extinguisher. State its suitability for different fire classes and two limitations.** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the Fire Tetrahedron with a neat sketch. How does each vertex correspond to a specific extinguishing mechanism? Provide one example extinguisher for each vertex.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Cylinder body:** Seamless mild steel, red-painted (IS 5 shade), pressure-tested hydrostatically at 1.5 × working pressure
- **Stored propellant:** Dry nitrogen gas at 10–16 bar pre-charged in the same cylinder as the powder
- **Siphon tube:** Reaches the bottom to entrain powder with the gas
- **Valve assembly:** Brass body with squeeze-grip handle, safety pin, tamper seal, pressure gauge
- **Discharge hose:** Reinforced rubber, ~60 cm long, with an **applicator wand or nozzle**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
On squeezing the handle, the valve opens. The pressurized N₂ gas flows through the powder bed, **fluidizing** it into a dust cloud. This dust cloud discharges through the hose and nozzle into the flame zone. Monoammonium phosphate decomposes endothermically:

$$\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3$$

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{PO}_4^{3-}\ \text{radicals} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

These PO₄³⁻ radicals **recombine with H• and OH•** flame-propagating radicals, terminating the chain reaction. A molten phosphate layer also forms on solid fuels, providing reignition protection.

**Method of Operation (1 mark):** PASS technique — Pull, Aim at base, Squeeze, Sweep side to side in **interrupted bursts** from 2–3 m.

**Suitability (0.5 mark):** Class A, B, C fires. Non-conductive (safe up to 35 kV at 1 m).

**Limitations (0.5 mark):** Heavy corrosive residue; poor visibility during discharge; powder caking in humid storage (recharge every 3 years).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Fire Tetrahedron (3 marks):**

The modern model of combustion recognizes **four** essential elements, represented as the four vertices of a tetrahedron:

1. **Fuel** — flammable material (solid, liquid, gas)
2. **Oxidizer** — typically atmospheric O₂ at **20.9 %**
3. **Heat / Activation Energy** — must exceed ignition temperature
4. **Chain Reaction** — free-radical propagation (H•, OH•, O•)

> *"Remove any one vertex → the tetrahedron collapses → the fire is extinguished."*

**Mechanism–Vertex Mapping (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Vertex | Mechanism | Extinguisher Example |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel | Fuel isolation / removal | Foam blanket, dry powder residue |
| Oxidizer | Smothering / oxygen displacement | CO₂, foam, inert gas (N₂, Ar) |
| Heat | Cooling | Water, water-mist |
| Chain Reaction | Chemical inhibition | Dry powder (MAP), Halons, FK-5-1-12 |

**Conclusion:** Modern agents (dry powder, clean agents) are particularly effective because they attack **multiple vertices simultaneously** — chain breaking + slight cooling — leading to extremely fast knockdown.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working principle of a CO₂ Type portable fire extinguisher. State its applications and limitations.** (7 marks)

**(b) What is meant by Fire Trials? Describe the standard fire test fuels used for Class A and Class B fires. Explain how the rating of an extinguisher (e.g., 3A or 21B) is determined.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Construction (2.5 marks):**
Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4.
- High-pressure seamless steel cylinder (DOT-3AL / IS 7285)
- Working pressure 58 bar at 21 °C (saturated liquid CO₂)
- Siphon tube (tube inside the cylinder reaches bottom)
- Brass valve assembly with safety pin, squeeze handle, pressure relief burst disc (~190 bar)
- Reinforced high-pressure hose with **non-metallic, non-conductive horn** (anti-static)

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- CO₂ stored as saturated liquid at 58 bar
- On valve opening, liquid CO₂ flows up the siphon tube through the hose to the horn
- At the horn, sudden pressure drop causes **Joule-Thomson expansion** → temperature falls to **−78.5 °C**, producing CO₂ gas and **dry ice snow**
- The expanding CO₂ gas **displaces atmospheric oxygen**, reducing O₂ from 20.9 % to **below 15 %** (combustion threshold for most hydrocarbons)
- Minor cooling effect from dry ice snow

**Method of Operation (0.5 mark):** PASS technique. Hold the horn with insulated handle — frostbite risk.

**Applications (1 mark):** Electrical panels, transformer rooms, generator rooms, laboratories, computer rooms, paint stores, fuel dispensing areas.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Not for Class A deep-seated fires (no cooling)
- Not for Class D metal fires (Mg, Na react with CO₂)
- Asphyxiation hazard in confined spaces
- Short throw range (1.5–3 m), short discharge time (8–30 s)
- No reignition protection — fire may restart after discharge
- Frostbite risk from horn
- Heavy cylinder

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Fire Trials — Definition (1 mark):**
Fire trials are **standardized, reproducible experimental procedures** specified by national and international codes (IS 2190, IS 15493, NFPA 10, EN 3-7) to evaluate the effectiveness of portable fire extinguishers. They determine the **fire rating** and the agent's ability to extinguish specific fuel classes.

**Standard Test Fuels (3 marks):**

**Class A — Wood Crib Test:**
- Standard crib: 12 sticks of pine wood, each 38 mm × 38 mm × 500 mm, arranged in alternating layers
- The crib is ignited and allowed to burn freely for **6 minutes** before extinguisher discharge
- After extinguishment, the extinguisher is rated by the **maximum crib size** it can extinguish

**Class B — Heptane Pan Test:**
- Standard pan: square steel pan of **0.36 m²** (later expanded to larger sizes)
- Filled with **n-heptane** to a depth of ~50 mm
- Allowed to **pre-burn for 1 minute**
- Extinguisher discharged by an experienced operator
- Pan size is progressively increased until the extinguisher fails — the largest successful size determines the rating

**Extinguisher Rating System (3 marks):**

| Rating | Meaning | Test |
|---|---|---|
| **3A, 13A, 21A, 34A, 55A** | Number = approximate **trisodium phosphate / water equivalence** | Class A wood crib test |
| **5B, 21B, 34B, 55B, 89B, 144B** | Number = **square feet** of flammable-liquid fire extinguished | Class B heptane pan test |
| **1A:10B** | Combination extinguisher rating | Both tests passed |

**Example:** A "3A 55B" extinguisher can extinguish a **Class A wood crib of size 3** and a **Class B heptane fire of 55 ft²** (~5.1 m²).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams (Module 3):**
>
> 1. ❌ **Confusing FEC with IC** — IC ≥ FEC always. State this explicitly.
> 2. ❌ **Writing "Halons are environmentally safe"** — INCORRECT. Halons have **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) > 0** and are banned under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**.
> 3. ❌ **Recommending water on electrical fires** — Water IS conductive and dangerous above 50 V. Always recommend CO₂ / dry powder / clean agent.
> 4. ❌ **Forgetting the safety pin / PASS technique** — Examiners award marks for the operational sequence.
> 5. ❌ **Wrong pressure for CO₂ cylinder** — Memorize: **58 bar at 21 °C** (not 51 bar or 70 bar).
> 6. ❌ **Skipping units in numerical problems** — Always state **units** for every quantity; missing units → −0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 7. ❌ **Confusing AFFF (hydrocarbon) with AFFF-AR (alcohol-resistant)** — Polar solvents (alcohol, acetone) require **AR grade** foam only.
> 8. ❌ **Stating "CO₂ cools the fire"** as the *primary* mechanism — Cooling is **secondary**; **oxygen displacement is primary**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Recall Points for KTU Module 3:**
>
> ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron:** Fuel + Oxidizer + Heat + Chain Reaction — remove any ONE vertex to extinguish
>
> ✅ **Flame Extinguishing Concentration (FEC):** Minimum % agent to extinguish *existing* flame
>
> ✅ **Inerting Concentration (IC):** Minimum % agent to *prevent* ignition — always ≥ FEC
>
> ✅ **Fire Trials:** Standardized tests (Class A wood crib, Class B heptane pan) per IS 2190 / NFPA 10
>
> ✅ **Extinguisher ratings:** 3A (Class A crib), 21B (21 ft² Class B pan)
>
> ✅ **PASS Technique:** Pull → Aim → Squeeze → Sweep
>
> ✅ **Water Type:** Class A only — cooling mechanism — 2574 kJ per litre absorbed
>
> ✅ **Foam Type:** Class A + B — AFFF for hydrocarbons, AFFF-AR for polar solvents — smothering + cooling — air-aspirating nozzle
>
> ✅ **Dry Powder:** Class A + B + C — MAP (ABC) or Sodium bicarbonate (BC) — chain-breaking mechanism — non-conductive — heavy residue
>
> ✅ **CO₂ Type:** Class B + C only — **58 bar at 21 °C** — oxygen displacement — non-conductive, no residue — short throw, short discharge, no reignition protection
>
> ✅ **Vaporizing Liquid / Clean Agent:** Class A + B + C — HFC-227ea or FK-5-1-12 — chain-breaking — NOAEL-safe for occupied spaces — Halon 1211 banned (ODP = 4)
>
> ✅ **Hydrostatic test interval:** CO₂ = every 5 years; Water/Foam = every 2 years; Dry Powder = recharged every 3 years
>
> ✅ **Design safety factor for cylinders:** 1.5 × working pressure (hydrostatic test pressure)
>
> ✅ **Atmospheric O₂:** 20.9 %; combustion threshold ~15 %; human safety threshold 16 %; lethal < 6 %

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and Distribution of Portable Extinguishers for Different Occupancy Classification as per NBC
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selection and Distribution of Portable Extinguishers for Different Occupancy Classification as per NBC

# 🔥 Selection and Distribution of Portable Extinguishers for Different Occupancy Classification as per NBC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Portable Fire Extinguisher:** A portable appliance containing an extinguishing medium (water, foam, dry chemical powder, CO₂, clean agent, or wet chemical) that can be carried by hand and operated manually to suppress or extinguish an incipient-stage fire by discharging its contents under pressure, as defined in **IS 2190:1992** and **NBC 2016 (Part 4)**.

**Selection of Portable Extinguishers** is the engineering process of matching the type, capacity, and quantity of portable extinguishers to the **class of fire** anticipated and the **occupancy hazard** of the building.

**Distribution** refers to the geometric placement pattern of these extinguishers throughout the floor plate such that the **travel distance** from any point in the protected area to the nearest extinguisher does not exceed the statutory limit (typically **15 m** for Class A risks and **10–15 m** for Class B/C risks as per **Table 5 of IS 2190** and **NBC Part 4, Clause 5.7**).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The First-Aid Kit of Buildings

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Building is a Body, and an Extinguisher is its First-Aid Box.**
> Just as a doctor prescribes different medicines (antibiotics, painkillers, antacids) depending on the ailment (bacterial infection, fracture, acidity), a Fire Protection Engineer prescribes different extinguishers (water, foam, CO₂, dry powder) depending on the *type of fuel* the fire is feeding on. You would **never** pour water on a kitchen-oil fire or on a live electrical fire — just as you would never give a sleeping pill for a stomach infection. The **National Building Code (NBC)** acts like a "medical handbook" that tells the engineer exactly which "medicine" (extinguisher) to prescribe for each type of "patient" (occupancy).

### 1.3 NBC Occupancy Classification — The Master Key

The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4 — Fire and Life Safety)** classifies every building into specific occupancy groups. The extinguisher selection directly depends on this classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NBC 2016 — Occupancy Group Classification (Clause 3.1):**
> - **Group A — Residential** (A-1 Lodging, A-2 One/Two Family Dwelling, A-3 Dormitories, A-4 Apartment Houses, A-5 Hotels)
> - **Group B — Educational** (Schools, Colleges, Universities)
> - **Group C — Institutional** (Hospitals, Jails, Mental Institutions)
> - **Group D — Assembly** (D-1 Transit, D-2 Theatres, D-3 Educational Assembly, D-4 Worship, D-5 Recreational)
> - **Group E — Business** (Offices, Banks, Professional Establishments)
> - **Group F — Mercantile** (Shops, Stores, Malls up to 500 m² and above)
> - **Group G — Industrial** (G-1 Low Hazard, G-2 Moderate Hazard, G-3 High Hazard)
> - **Group H — Storage** (Warehouses, Godowns, Cold Storages)
> - **Group I — Hazardous** (Storage/Handling of Hazardous Materials — Explosives, Chemicals, Gases)
> - **Group J — Mixed Use**

### 1.4 Class of Fire — The Universal Fuel Taxonomy

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Real-World Examples | Wrong Extinguisher (Hazard) |
|------------|-----------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics | None (universal) |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids | Petrol, diesel, oil, paint, solvents | Water (causes spread!) |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases | LPG, CNG, methane, acetylene | Water jet (causes explosion) |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium, lithium | Water (reacts violently) |
| **Class E** (or K in India) | Electrical equipment | Live panels, motors, server racks | Water (electrocution) |
| **Class F** (or K in EU) | Cooking oils & fats | Kitchen deep-fryers, vegetable oils | Water (steam explosion) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (India vs. International):** In **India (IS 2190)**, the designation **"Class E"** is used for *Electrical Fires*, and cooking-oil fires are typically grouped with Class B. In **International (IS0/EN)** standards, **"Class F"** or **"Class K"** is reserved for cooking oils. KTU examinations follow the **Indian (IS 2190) convention**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Selection Logic — Five Decisive Parameters

The **Selection of a Portable Extinguisher** is governed by a sequential decision matrix based on:

1. **Class of Fire (A/B/C/D/E/F)** → Determines the extinguishing medium.
2. **Occupancy Hazard Level (Light / Ordinary / Extra)** → Determines the capacity & quantity.
3. **Floor Area & Travel Distance** → Determines the number of extinguishers.
4. **Ambient Conditions** (e.g., freezability of water, electrical risk) → Determines the medium type.
5. **NBC / IS 2190 / Tariff Advisory Committee (TAC) Rules** → Statutory minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard Classification (TAC Rules / IS 2190):**
> - **Light Hazard:** Offices, schools, churches — combustibles are limited, fires are small.
> - **Ordinary Hazard:** Shops, warehouses with limited combustibles, parking garages.
> - **Extra (High) Hazard:** Flammable liquid storage, spray painting, woodworking, etc.

### 2.2 Extinguisher Type → Fire Class Compatibility Matrix

| Extinguisher Type | Medium | Class A | Class B | Class C | Class D | Class E | Class F/K | Colour Band (IS 2190) |
|-------------------|--------|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:---------:|------------------------|
| **Water (Soda-Acid / Stored Pressure)** | $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | Red |
| **Foam (AFFF / Mechanical)** | $\text{AFFF}$ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | Cream |
| **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) — ABC type** | $\text{MAP}$ or $\text{ABC}$ powder | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | Blue |
| **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** | $\text{CO}_2$ gas | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | Black |
| **Wet Chemical** | Potassium acetate | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | Yellow |
| **Clean Agent (HFC-227 / FM-200)** | $\text{HFC}$ gas | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | Green |
| **Special Dry Powder (for metals)** | $\text{NaCl / Cu powder}$ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | (No standard band) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **tested in KTU University Examinations** and are derived from **IS 2190:1992 (Selection, Installation and Maintenance of First-Aid Fire Extinguishers)** and **NBC 2016, Part 4**.

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Typical Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------------|------------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | **Number of Extinguishers (Class A)** | $N = \dfrac{A_{\text{floor}}}{A_{\text{coverage}}}$ | Nos. | $A_{\text{coverage}}$ for 9 L water = **200 m²** (Ordinary Hazard) |
| 2 | **Travel Distance Rule** | $d_{\text{travel}} \le d_{\text{max}}$ | m | $d_{\text{max}} = 15\,\text{m}$ for Class A; $\le 10$–$15$ m for Class B |
| 3 | **Coverage Radius per Extinguisher** | $r_{\text{cov}} = d_{\text{max}}$ | m | 9 L water extinguisher covers a circular zone of radius 15 m |
| 4 | **Minimum Capacity per Extinguisher** | $C \ge C_{\text{min}}(H)$ | L or kg | Depends on Hazard: Light = **2 kg**, Ordinary = **4–9 L water**, Extra = **9 kg DCP** |
| 5 | **Foam Extinguisher Coverage** | $A_{\text{coverage}} = 80$ m² per 9 L | m² | NBC Table for Class B risks |
| 6 | **CO₂ Extinguisher Coverage** | $A_{\text{coverage}} = 25$ m² per 4.5 kg | m² | For electrical risks (Class E) |
| 7 | **DCP (ABC) Extinguisher Coverage** | $A_{\text{coverage}} = 50$ m² per 4 kg | m² | Multi-purpose, high versatility |
| 8 | **Travel Distance for Class B** | $d_{\text{max}} = 15\,\text{m}$ | m | Per IS 2190 |
| 9 | **Floor-Area Rule for Light Hazard** | $1 \times 9\,\text{L water per 600\,\text{m}^2$ | – | TAC Advisory |
| 10 | **Floor-Area Rule for Ordinary Hazard** | $1 \times 9\,\text{L water per 200\,\text{m}^2$ | – | TAC Advisory |
| 11 | **Floor-Area Rule for Extra Hazard** | $2 \times 9\,\text{kg DCP per 100\,\text{m}^2$ | – | TAC Advisory |
| 12 | **Hose-Reel Coverage** | $1 \times \text{Hose Reel per 1000 m²}$ | – | Supplementary to extinguishers |
| 13 | **Discharge Time Threshold** | $t_{\text{discharge}} \ge 30\,\text{s}$ | s | Minimum per IS 2190 |
| 14 | **Extinguisher Spacing on Wall** | $S \le d_{\text{max}}$ between units | m | Uniform grid placement |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulas and selection matrix are not academic abstractions — they form the **basis of every Fire Safety Audit** in India. In actual practice:

- A **Fire Protection Consultant** designs the extinguisher layout using these formulas and submits it for **NOC (No Objection Certificate)** from the local Fire Department (e.g., Kerala Fire & Rescue Services).
- The **Tariff Advisory Committee (TAC)** uses these rules to determine insurance premium discounts — proper placement can reduce a building's fire-insurance premium by up to **15–20%**.
- **NBC Part 4** compliance is a statutory precondition for any building's **Occupancy Certificate (OC)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Extinguishers from Floor Area

> **Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the number of portable extinguishers required for a given floor area under the floor-area method of IS 2190 / TAC.

**Step 1 — Define the input parameters:**

Let:
- $A_f$ = Total floor area to be protected (in m²)
- $A_c$ = Coverage area of one extinguisher (in m²), as per IS 2190
- $N$ = Number of extinguishers required (dimensionless)

**Step 2 — Establish the proportionality:**

The number of extinguishers is directly proportional to the total floor area and inversely proportional to the coverage area of each extinguisher:

$$
N \propto A_f \quad \text{and} \quad N \propto \dfrac{1}{A_c}
$$

Combining:

$$
N = k \cdot \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the proportionality constant $k$:**

For an *ideal* uniformly distributed layout with no overlap and no gap, $k = 1$. However, the **travel-distance constraint** modifies this.

**Step 4 — Incorporate the travel-distance constraint:**

The coverage area of an extinguisher is bounded by the maximum travel distance $d_{\max}$:

$$
A_c = \pi \cdot (d_{\max})^2 \quad \text{(circular coverage model)}
$$

For IS 2190 values: $d_{\max} = 15\,\text{m}$ for Class A risks

$$
A_c = \pi \times (15)^2 = 225\pi \approx 706.86\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with empirical TAC coverage:**

The TAC actually recommends a smaller effective coverage (e.g., **200 m² for 9 L water in Ordinary Hazard**) to account for real-world obstacles, partitions, and partial discharges. This empirical value is the **design coverage**:

$$
A_c^{\text{design}} = 200\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form expression:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_f}{A_c^{\text{design}}} \right\rceil
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** (round up to the next integer — you cannot have a fractional extinguisher!).

**Step 7 — Cross-check with the travel-distance rule:**

The number of extinguishers must *also* satisfy:

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{A_f}{N}} \le 2 \cdot d_{\max}
$$

If this fails, **increase $N$** until both constraints are satisfied.

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Examination Style

> **Question:** A **Class B Mercantile (Group F-1)** shop has a floor area of **600 m²** and is classified as **Ordinary Hazard**. As per IS 2190, recommend the *type, capacity, and number* of portable extinguishers and verify the travel-distance compliance.

**Step 1 — Identify the Fire Class:**

Mercantile (Group F) shops typically stock clothing, packaged goods, cardboard, and limited flammable liquids → **Predominantly Class A** with possible **Class B** at the cosmetics/paints counter → Design for **Class A (primary) + Class B (secondary)**.

**Step 2 — Choose Extinguisher Type (Best Practice):**

For mixed Class A + B risk, use **ABC Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** extinguisher of **4 kg capacity** (versatile, covers both classes).

**Step 3 — Identify Coverage Area:**

For 4 kg DCP extinguisher in Ordinary Hazard (Class A & B):

$$
A_c = 50\,\text{m}^2 \text{ per extinguisher}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Number of Extinguishers:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{600}{50} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 12.0 \right\rceil = 12 \text{ extinguishers}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Travel Distance:**

Assuming uniform square grid placement over 600 m²:

$$
L = \sqrt{A_f} = \sqrt{600} \approx 24.49\,\text{m}
$$

Number along one side: $\sqrt{N} = \sqrt{12} \approx 3.46$ → Use **$4 \times 3 = 12$ grid** for uniform placement.

Spacing between extinguishers:

$$
S = \dfrac{L}{(\sqrt{N} - 1)} = \dfrac{24.49}{(3.46 - 1)} = \dfrac{24.49}{2.46} \approx 9.95\,\text{m}
$$

For corner extinguisher, the maximum diagonal travel distance:

$$
d_{\text{travel}} = \dfrac{S \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{9.95 \times 1.414}{2} \approx 7.04\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with $d_{\max}$:**

$$
d_{\text{travel}} = 7.04\,\text{m} \; < \; d_{\max} = 15\,\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** **12 numbers of 4 kg ABC DCP extinguishers**, uniformly distributed in a $4 \times 3$ grid, are adequate. Additionally, **1 CO₂ extinguisher (4.5 kg)** should be provided near the **billing counter** (electrical/Electronic risk).

### 3.3 Analytical Selection Matrix — Occupancy vs. Extinguisher (NBC Aligned)

| Occupancy (NBC Group) | Sub-Type | Dominant Fire Class | Recommended Extinguisher | Min. Capacity | Min. Nos. & Spacing |
|------------------------|----------|----------------------|--------------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **A — Residential** | A-1 Lodging/Hotel | A, B (kitchen) | ABC DCP + Wet Chemical (kitchen) | 4 kg DCP, 6 L Wet Chem | 1 per 200 m²; $d \le 15$ m |
| **A — Residential** | A-2/A-3 Dwelling | A | Water (Stored Pressure) | 9 L | 1 per 200 m² |
| **B — Educational** | Schools/Colleges | A, E (labs) | ABC DCP + CO₂ (labs) | 4 kg DCP, 4.5 kg CO₂ | 1 per 200 m²; one per floor minimum |
| **C — Institutional** | Hospitals | A, B, E (OTs) | ABC DCP + CO₂ (OTs) | 4 kg DCP, 4.5 kg CO₂ | 1 per 200 m²; $d \le 15$ m |
| **D — Assembly** | Theatres, Halls | A, E | ABC DCP | 4 kg | 1 per 200 m²; extra near stage |
| **E — Business** | Offices | A, E | ABC DCP + CO₂ (server rooms) | 4 kg DCP, 4.5 kg CO₂ | 1 per 200 m² |
| **F — Mercantile** | Shops/Malls | A, B, E | ABC DCP + CO₂ (escalator panel) | 4 kg DCP | 1 per 50 m² (Class A) or 1 per 100 m² (light) |
| **G-1 — Industrial (Low)** | Workshops | A, B | ABC DCP + Foam | 9 kg DCP / 9 L Foam | 1 per 200 m² |
| **G-2 — Industrial (Moderate)** | Manufacturing | A, B, E | ABC DCP + CO₂ | 9 kg DCP | 1 per 100 m²; $d \le 10$ m |
| **G-3 — Industrial (High)** | Petrochemical, Paints | B, C | DCP (BC) + Foam + CO₂ | 9 kg DCP / 9 L Foam | 1 per 50 m²; $d \le 10$ m |
| **H — Storage** | Warehouses | A, B | Foam + DCP | 9 kg | 1 per 100 m² |
| **I — Hazardous** | Explosives, Chemicals | B, C, D | DCP (BC) + Special Metal Powders | 9–25 kg | Per Tariff Advisory; special TAC clearance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Building Occupancy per NBC] --> B[Determine Class of Fire A B C D E F]
    B --> C{Is Electrical Equipment Present?}
    C -- Yes --> D1[Add CO2 or Clean Agent Extinguisher]
    C -- No --> E{Fuel Type?}
    E -- Wood Paper Cloth --> F1[Use Water or ABC DCP]
    E -- Flammable Liquid --> F2[Use Foam or ABC DCP or BC DCP]
    E -- Flammable Gas --> F3[Use BC DCP or CO2]
    E -- Cooking Oil --> F4[Use Wet Chemical]
    E -- Metal --> F5[Use Special Metal Dry Powder]
    F1 --> G[Determine Hazard Level Light Ordinary Extra]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    D1 --> G
    G --> H{Hazard Level?}
    H -- Light --> I1[C min 2 to 4 kg DCP or 6 L Water]
    H -- Ordinary --> I2[C min 4 to 9 kg DCP or 9 L Water]
    H -- Extra --> I3[C min 9 kg DCP or 9 L Foam]
    I1 --> J[Compute Number N equal to Af divided by Ac ceiling]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Verify Travel Distance d travel less or equal 15 m for Class A]
    K --> L{Compliant?}
    L -- No --> M[Increase N and recheck]
    L -- Yes --> N[Final Layout: Grid placement with N units]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Distribution Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Building Floor Plate 600 sqm] --> node2[Divide into Risk Zones per Occupancy]
    node2 --> node3[Class A Zone Office Area 300 sqm]
    node2 --> node4[Class E Zone Server Room 50 sqm]
    node2 --> node5[Class B Zone Pantry 30 sqm]
    node2 --> node6[Class A Zone Lobby 220 sqm]
    node3 --> stepA[Place 6 ABC DCP 4kg units d max 15 m]
    node4 --> stepB[Place 2 CO2 4.5 kg units d max 10 m]
    node5 --> stepC[Place 1 Wet Chemical 6L unit d max 10 m]
    node6 --> stepD[Place 3 ABC DCP 4kg units d max 15 m]
    stepA --> check[Verify Travel Distance Compliance]
    stepB --> check
    stepC --> check
    stepD --> check
    check --> final[Total 12 units Signage and Inspection tags]
```

### 4.3 Visual Layout Concept — Grid Placement (Text Schematic)

```
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|   [E1] ..............................  [E5]  ............  |
|   .      Office Zone A 300 m²        .       Lobby 220 m² |
|   .                                  .                    |
|   [E2] ..........................    [E6]  ...........    |
|   .                                  .                    |
|   [E3] ........................ [E4] ............. [E7]   |
|   .   Server Room 50 m²         .  .  Pantry 30 m²  .    |
|   [CO₂-1]                    [CO₂-2]  [WC-1]               |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
E1–E7 = ABC DCP 4 kg units   |   CO₂-1, CO₂-2 = CO₂ 4.5 kg
Spacing ≈ 10 m   |   Travel distance ≤ 15 m ✓
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the five classes of fire as per IS 2190 and state the corresponding suitable extinguishing medium for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per point + ½ mark for correct medium):**

| Class | Fuel | Suitable Medium |
|-------|------|-----------------|
| A | Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth) | Water, ABC DCP, Foam |
| B | Flammable liquids (oil, petrol, paint) | Foam, BC DCP, CO₂ |
| C | Flammable gases (LPG, CNG) | BC DCP, CO₂ |
| D | Combustible metals (Mg, Na, K) | Special metal dry powder (NaCl/Cu) |
| E | Live electrical equipment | CO₂, Clean Agent, ABC DCP |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*State the travel distance rule for the placement of portable fire extinguishers as per IS 2190. Why is this rule critical?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **travel distance rule** states that the maximum walking distance from *any point* in a protected area to the **nearest extinguisher** shall not exceed **15 metres** for Class A risks and **10–15 metres** for Class B/C risks (as per **IS 2190:1992, Clause 5.2**).

*Criticality (2 marks):*
- During incipient-stage fire, a person has **30–60 seconds** to act before the fire grows uncontrollable.
- Beyond 15 m, evacuation & retrieval time exceeds this window.
- Also ensures that the **discharge time** of the extinguisher (≥ 30 s) is consumed usefully rather than in transit.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* A **Group G-2 (Moderate Hazard Industrial)** factory building has a floor area of **1200 m²** with predominant **Class B (flammable liquid)** fire risk. As per IS 2190 and NBC, determine the **type, capacity, and number** of portable extinguishers required. Verify the travel-distance compliance. (7 marks)

*(b)* A hospital (Group C-1) building has a **central switchgear room (50 m²)** and a **kitchen (30 m²)**. Specify the extinguisher selection for each area with justification based on IS 2190. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Industrial Building (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Fire Class:** Class B (flammable liquids) in G-2 Industrial.

**Step 2 — Select Extinguisher:** **ABC Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** is chosen as it covers Class A, B, C, and E — most versatile.

**Step 3 — Capacity Selection:** For Moderate Hazard, minimum capacity = **9 kg DCP**.

**Step 4 — Coverage Area:** For 9 kg DCP on Class B risk in Moderate Hazard:
$$
A_c = 25\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Extinguishers:**
$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{1200}{25} \right\rceil = \lceil 48 \rceil = 48 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6 — Travel Distance Verification:**

For 48 extinguishers in a square grid:
$$
\text{Grid} = \sqrt{48} \approx 6.93 \Rightarrow \text{use } 7 \times 7 = 49 \text{ units (rounded up)}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{\sqrt{1200}}{7 - 1} = \dfrac{34.64}{6} \approx 5.77\,\text{m}
$$

$$
d_{\text{travel}} = \dfrac{S\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 4.08\,\text{m}
$$

$$
4.08\,\text{m} \;\le\; d_{\max} = 10\,\text{m for Class B in Moderate Hazard} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Recommendation:** **49 units of 9 kg ABC DCP extinguishers**, distributed uniformly; add **2 × 9 L foam units** at the loading bay for additional Class B coverage. (7 marks — distribute 1 for class identification, 1 for selection, 1 for capacity, 2 for calculation, 1 for travel distance, 1 for final recommendation)

---

**Part (b) — Hospital Special Areas (7 marks):**

**Switchgear Room (50 m²) — Class E (Electrical Fire):**
- **Selection:** CO₂ extinguisher, **4.5 kg** capacity, clean & non-conductive.
- **Coverage area per unit:** $A_c = 25\,\text{m}^2$
- **Number required:**
$$
N = \lceil 50/25 \rceil = 2 \text{ units}
$$
- **Justification:** Water is hazardous (electrocution); CO₂ leaves no residue (no damage to electronic equipment). (3.5 marks)

**Kitchen (30 m²) — Class F/K (Cooking Oil):**
- **Selection:** Wet Chemical extinguisher, **6 L** capacity, Potassium Acetate based.
- **Coverage area per unit:** $A_c = 30\,\text{m}^2$
- **Number required:**
$$
N = \lceil 30/30 \rceil = 1 \text{ unit}
$$
- **Justification:** Water reacts violently with hot oil (steam explosion); wet chemical saponifies the oil, sealing the surface. (3.5 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain the **NBC 2016 Occupancy Classification** with at least **six groups** and their associated dominant fire risks. State the appropriate extinguisher for each. (7 marks)

*(b)* A **mercantile (Group F-1)** shopping complex has **three floors**, each of **800 m²**, classified as **Ordinary Hazard** with **mixed Class A and Class B** risk. Calculate the number and type of extinguishers required. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — NBC Occupancy Classification (7 marks):**

| Group | Occupancy | Dominant Fire Risk | Recommended Extinguisher |
|-------|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **A** | Residential | Class A (furniture), Class F (kitchen) | Water + Wet Chemical |
| **B** | Educational | Class A, E (labs) | ABC DCP + CO₂ |
| **C** | Institutional (Hospital) | Class A, B, E | ABC DCP + CO₂ |
| **D** | Assembly | Class A, E (stage lighting) | ABC DCP + CO₂ |
| **E** | Business (Offices) | Class A, E (server rooms) | ABC DCP + CO₂ |
| **F** | Mercantile (Shops/Malls) | Class A, B (cosmetics), E | ABC DCP + CO₂ |
| **G-2** | Industrial (Moderate) | Class A, B, E | ABC DCP + CO₂ |
| **H** | Storage (Warehouse) | Class A, B | Foam + ABC DCP |
| **I** | Hazardous | Class B, C, D | BC DCP + Special Powder |

(1 mark per two correct rows; 7 marks total)

**Part (b) — Mercantile Shopping Complex (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Floor Area per Floor:** $800\,\text{m}^2$; Total = $3 \times 800 = 2400\,\text{m}^2$.

**Step 2 — Extinguisher:** ABC DCP, **4 kg** capacity (handles both Class A and B in ordinary hazard).

**Step 3 — Coverage Area:** For 4 kg DCP in Ordinary Hazard (mixed A/B):
$$
A_c = 50\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Number per Floor:**
$$
N_{\text{floor}} = \lceil 800/50 \rceil = 16 \text{ units per floor}
$$

**Step 5 — Total:**
$$
N_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 16 = 48 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6 — Special Provisions:**
- **1 × 4.5 kg CO₂ extinguisher** near each **escalator electrical panel** (Class E) → 3 additional units.
- **1 × 6 L Wet Chemical** in each floor's food court (Class F) → 3 additional units.

**Final:** 48 ABC DCP + 3 CO₂ + 3 Wet Chemical = **54 units** total.

(1 mark for class identification, 1 for selection, 2 for calculations, 1 for travel distance, 2 for special provisions) — (7 marks total)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing Class E and Class F:** Many students interchange these. Remember: **E = Electrical, F/K = Cooking Oils**. Examiner will deduct **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Ceiling Function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$:** If calculation yields 7.4 extinguishers, answer must be **8** (round up), not 7. Loss of ½ mark.
> 3. **Not Verifying Travel Distance:** IS 2190 mandates *both* the floor-area method *and* the travel-distance method. Submitting only the count without verification = loss of **2 marks**.
> 4. **Wrong Unit Notation:** Writing "metre" instead of "m" or "kg" instead of "kg DCP" — minor formatting deductions of ½ mark each.
> 5. **Forgetting Special Areas:** In hospitals (OT), kitchens, server rooms, switchgear rooms — the generic layout is insufficient. The examiner explicitly tests for *special-area specification*. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Misquoting the Standard:** Always cite **IS 2190:1992** or **NBC 2016 (Part 4)** with the clause number. Vague references like "as per code" lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Five classes of fire (A/B/C/D/E)** and one matching extinguishing medium each.
>
> ✅ **NBC 2016 Part 4** divides buildings into **Groups A through J** (10 groups).
>
> ✅ **Travel distance rule:** Max **15 m** (Class A) and **10–15 m** (Class B/C).
>
> ✅ **9 L water extinguisher** covers **200 m²** in Ordinary Hazard (Class A).
>
> ✅ **4 kg ABC DCP** covers **50 m²** (Ordinary Hazard, mixed A/B).
>
> ✅ **9 kg DCP / Foam** is the **minimum** for **Moderate/Extra Hazard** industrial.
>
> ✅ **CO₂ extinguisher** is the **only** choice for live electrical (Class E) risks.
>
> ✅ **Wet Chemical (Potassium Acetate)** is **mandatory** for cooking-oil (Class F) fires.
>
> ✅ **Special Dry Powder** (NaCl/Cu-based) is required for **Class D (metal) fires** — water is explosive here.
>
> ✅ **Formula:** $N = \lceil A_f / A_c \rceil$ — always round **up**; never provide a fractional extinguisher.
>
> ✅ **Travel-distance verification formula:** $d_{\text{travel}} = (S \sqrt{2})/2 \le d_{\max}$.
>
> ✅ **Standards to cite:** IS 2190:1992, IS 2546, NBC 2016 (Part 4), TAC Rules.
>
> ✅ **Mandatory provisions:** Hospitals need CO₂ in OTs, malls need CO₂ near escalator panels, kitchens need Wet Chemical.
>
> ✅ **Colour codes (IS 2190):** Red = Water; Cream = Foam; Blue = DCP; Black = CO₂; Yellow = Wet Chemical; Green = Clean Agent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Water Sprinkler System - Requirement and Source of Water Supply, Automatic Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Water Sprinkler System - Requirement and Source of Water Supply, Automatic Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automatic Water Sprinkler System** is an integrated, fire-suppression installation comprising a network of heat-responsive sprinkler heads fixed to a pressurized water distribution piping system, designed to detect and extinguish a fire at its incipient stage by discharging water directly onto the heat-affected zone. According to **IS 15105** and **NFPA 13** standards referenced under the KTU 2024 syllabus, the system is considered "automatic" because each individual sprinkler head operates **independently** upon activation of its thermal-sensitive element (glass bulb or fusible link), without requiring manual intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A sprinkler system is "automatic" only when water delivery to individual heads is triggered by fire-induced heat rupture of a thermally-rated seal — *not* by smoke, manual pull, or electrical signal (which describes a deluge or pre-action system instead).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sleeping Firefighter Network"

Imagine a hotel where every square meter of the ceiling is silently watched by a **miniature firefighter** who is asleep, but wears a **heat-sensitive alarm** around his neck. The moment a fire ignites directly beneath him and the room temperature reaches his personal alarm threshold (e.g., **68 °C** or **141 °F** for an ordinary-temperature rating), his alarm breaks and he instantly begins spraying water — *only* on his small patch of fire. Crucially, his neighbors in the corridor or adjacent rooms remain asleep.

This is exactly how a sprinkler system functions:
- **Each head = one sleeping firefighter** with a private thermal trigger.
- **The piping network = the fire station's water mains**, always charged with water.
- **The automatic pump = the fire station's pumper engine**, ready to maintain pressure 24/7.
- **Water supply source = the city's hydrant reservoir** that can never run dry for at least **30–90 minutes** of firefighting demand.

### 1.3 Fundamental Requirements of an Automatic Sprinkler System

A sprinkler system is only as reliable as its weakest component. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the following **four cardinal requirements**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of a Reliable Sprinkler System (TAPPS Framework):**
> 1. **T**rigger Reliability — Every head must operate on its rated temperature.
> 2. **A**dequate Pressure — Minimum **1.4 bar (20 psi)** residual pressure at the most remote head.
> 3. **P**roportional Flow — Design density typically **4.1–12.5 L/min/m²** based on hazard class.
> 4. **P**rovision Duration — Continuous supply for at least **30 minutes (Light Hazard)** to **90 minutes (High Hazard)**.

### 1.4 Source of Water Supply — Hierarchy of Acceptable Sources

Per **IS 15105: 2002** and **NFPA 13**, water supply to a sprinkler system must be drawn from an **approved, reliable, and adequate** source. The recognized hierarchy is:

| Priority | Source Type | Reliability Rating | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Public/Municipal Water Main (with dual feed) | ★★★★★ | Urban buildings |
| 2 | Elevated Gravity Tank | ★★★★☆ | Industrial sheds, warehouses |
| 3 | Pressure Tank (Air-pressurized) | ★★★☆☆ | Small occupancies, basements |
| 4 | Fire Pump + Reservoir | ★★★★☆ | High-rise, large floor areas |
| 5 | Natural Source (Lake, River, Pond) | ★★★☆☆ | When screened and approved |
| 6 | Combination of Two Sources | ★★★★★ | Critical infrastructure (mandatory for Extra High Hazard) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** For **Extra High Hazard** occupancies, the KTU examiner expects the student to state — *"At least two independent, approved sources of water supply, with one capable of supplying the full system demand for the required duration."* Memorize this phrase verbatim.

### 1.5 Automatic Pumps — The Heartbeat of the System

An **Automatic Fire Pump** is a centrifugal pump (single-stage or multi-stage) coupled to either an electric motor or a diesel engine, equipped with automatic starting devices that activate upon a **pressure drop** in the system. The pump assembly typically consists of:

1. **Main Fire Pump** — Supplies the required flow and pressure.
