# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, CO4]**
**"Differentiate between a wet alarm valve and a dry pipe valve in terms of operating principle, pipe content, and typical application. [3 Marks]"**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Wet Alarm Valve | Dry Pipe Valve |
|---|---|---|
| **Pipe content (normal)** | Water under pressure | Pressurized air (30–40 psi) |
| **Operating principle** | Differential clapper, system-side pressure drop opens the valve | Three-chamber differential, air-pressure drop (or rate-of-drop via QOD) trips the valve |
| **Time to water discharge** | Instantaneous (≤ 3 s) | Delayed (≤ 60 s) |
| **Application** | Heated spaces — offices, hospitals, malls | Unheated / cold storage, parking decks, loading docks |
| **Frost risk** | High (cannot use below 4 °C) | None (designed for sub-zero) |
| **Auxiliary equipment** | Retarding chamber | Compressor + QOD + low-air alarm |

(1 mark for pipe content, 1 mark for operating principle, 1 mark for application.)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, CO4]**
**"List the six mandatory components of a control valve trim for a wet sprinkler installation. [3 Marks]"**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Main shut-off valve** — OS&Y gate or butterfly with **tamper switch**.
2. **Retarding chamber** — 30–90 s time delay to prevent false alarms.
3. **Water-motor alarm (gong)** — mechanical fire alarm.
4. **Electric alarm pressure switch** — signal to **FACP**.
5. **Pressure gauges** — supply-side and system-side.
6. **Main drain valve + alarm test valve** — for annual testing per IS 15105 / NFPA 25.

(½ mark each = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, CO4]**

> **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **dry pipe valve** used in automatic sprinkler installations. [**7 Marks**]
>
> **(b)** A dry pipe sprinkler system serves an unheated warehouse. The system has a total water capacity of **800 gallons**, the city supply pressure is **75 psi**, and the differential area ratio of the clapper is **3.5 : 1**. The system is fitted with a **Quick-Opening Device**. Determine: (i) whether the system volume is code-compliant; (ii) the theoretical trip pressure of the dry pipe valve; (iii) the minimum water-motor alarm flow rate required. [**7 Marks**]

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working of a Dry Pipe Valve — 7 Marks**

A dry pipe valve is a **differential-type, hydraulically operated clapper valve** used in dry pipe sprinkler systems where the piping is filled with pressurized air (or nitrogen) instead of water. The construction consists of:

- **Valve Body:** Cast or ductile iron, vertically mounted, with flanged inlet and outlet.
- **Clapper Assembly:** A hinged disc held against a seat by hydraulic differential. The clapper is **larger in area on the system (air) side** than on the supply (water) side — typically a 2.5:1 to 6:1 ratio.
- **Three Chambers:**
  - **Chamber A (Below Clapper):** City water supply at $P_w$ ≈ 60–175 psi.
  - **Chamber B (Intermediate, Above Clapper):** Pressurized air at $P_a$ ≈ 30–40 psi.
  - **Chamber C (System Piping):** Air, connected to Chamber B through a small water seal and bypass.
- **Prime Water:** A small water seal above the clapper (held in the intermediate chamber) prevents air from leaking past the clapper to the supply side.
- **Trim Piping:** Includes the air supply, alarm port, drain, gauges, and QOD connection.

**Working Principle (in set condition):**
- City water pressure $P_w$ acts on the smaller bottom area of the clapper.
- Air pressure $P_a$ acts on the **larger top area** of the clapper.
- Because $P_a \cdot A_{top} > P_w \cdot A_{bot}$, the net force holds the clapper **seated (closed)**, preventing water from entering the system.

**Working Principle (on sprinkler activation):**
1. A sprinkler head fuses and opens.
2. Pressurized air vents from the system.
3. The **rate of pressure drop** in Chamber B is detected by the QOD (if fitted), which **accelerator-pilots** city water into Chamber B to equalize pressure rapidly.
4. As $P_a$ falls below the **trip threshold** $P_{trip} \approx P_w / R$, the clapper **lifts (trips)**.
5. Water enters the system, flows toward the open head, displaces remaining air (vented through the air-relief valve), and discharges.
6. The alarm port opens, sounding the **water-motor alarm** and energizing the **pressure switch** for the FACP.

**Sketch (descriptive):**
- Vertical valve body with flanged inlet (bottom, water) and outlet (top, to system).
- Clapper hinged on a horizontal axis, larger area facing up.
- Intermediate chamber above clapper, with prime water and air supply connection.
- Trim exits: alarm port (right), drain (bottom), gauges (left/right), QOD port.

(Marking: 2 marks sketch/construction, 3 marks working, 2 marks trim & accessories.)

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $V_{sys} = 800$ gal
- $P_w = 75$ psi
- $R = A_{top} / A_{bot} = 3.5$
- Quick-Opening Device **fitted**

**Step (i): Code Compliance Check**

Per NFPA 13 / TAC:
- Without QOD → max $V_{sys} = 750$ gal
- **With QOD → max $V_{sys} = 1000$ gal**

Since $V_{sys} = 800$ gal < 1000 gal and QOD is fitted: ✅ **CODE COMPLIANT**

(2 marks)

**Step (ii): Theoretical Trip Pressure**

$$P_{trip} = \frac{P_w}{R} = \frac{75}{3.5} = 21.43 \text{ psi (gauge)}$$

So the clapper trips when intermediate-chamber air pressure falls below ≈ **21.4 psi**.

(2 marks)

**Step (iii): Minimum Water-Motor Alarm Flow Rate**

Per IS 15105 / NFPA 13: the water-motor alarm must operate at a **minimum flow of 10 GPM** (≈ 38 LPM) at the alarm valve's residual pressure. This is a **listed / approved value** and does not depend on system volume.

Therefore: **$Q_{alarm,min} = 10$ GPM (≈ 38 LPM)**

(2 marks)

**Final Answer:**
- (i) Yes, code-compliant (800 gal < 1000 gal with QOD).
- (ii) $P_{trip} = 21.43$ psi.
- (iii) $Q_{alarm,min} = 10$ GPM.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, CO4]**

> **(a)** Explain the **functions and importance of the retarding chamber** in a wet alarm valve trim. How does it prevent false alarms? [**7 Marks**]
>
> **(b)** A wet pipe sprinkler system has a city supply pressure of **90 psi**. The wet alarm valve's clapper has a bottom area of **1.5 in²** and a top area of **6.0 in²**. Calculate: (i) the net hydraulic force holding the clapper closed in the set condition; (ii) the pressure drop on the system side required to begin valve lift. [**7 Marks**]

#### Model Solution

**(a) Retarding Chamber — 7 Marks**

The **retarding chamber** is a small, restricted-orifice chamber installed in the alarm trim of a wet alarm valve, between the supply side and the system side (above the clapper).

**Functions:**
1. **Delays false alarms** caused by **water-hammer-induced clapper chatter** (the clapper momentarily lifts due to a transient pressure surge, then re-seats — which, without the retard, would sound a false alarm).
2. **Smooths pressure equalization** between the two sides of the clapper during transient events.
3. **Ensures a sustained, real sprinkler flow** triggers the alarm (typically 30–90 s of sustained flow).

**Working Principle:**
- The chamber has a **small orifice** connecting it to the supply side and a **larger outlet** to the system side.
- During a pressure surge (e.g., water hammer), the clapper may lift briefly. The pressure differential is **not sustained**, so the retarding chamber does not have time to equalize.
- During a **real sprinkler activation**, the pressure differential **persists** for more than 30–90 s, allowing the retarding chamber to equalize and the alarm to sound.
- A typical retard time is **30–90 seconds**, adjustable by the chamber's restriction.

**Importance in KTU 2024 context:**
- Without the retarding chamber, **every water-hammer event** in a tall riser could trigger a false evacuation, leading to:
  - Unnecessary business interruption.
  - Loss of confidence in the fire alarm system.
  - Possible "alarm fatigue" where real alarms are ignored.

(Marking: 2 marks function, 3 marks working, 2 marks importance.)

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $P_w = 90$ psi
- $A_{bot} = 1.5 \text{ in}^2$
- $A_{top} = 6.0 \text{ in}^2$

**Step (i): Net hydraulic force on clapper (set condition)**

In the set condition, both sides are at $P_w = 90$ psi.

Force pushing clapper **UP** (city pressure, on bottom):
$$F_{up} = P_w \cdot A_{bot} = 90 \times 1.5 = 135 \text{ lbf}$$

Force pushing clapper **DOWN** (system pressure, on top):
$$F_{down} = P_w \cdot A_{top} = 90 \times 6.0 = 540 \text{ lbf}$$

**Net force = DOWN (seating force):**
$$F_{net} = F_{down} - F_{up} = 540 - 135 = 405 \text{ lbf}$$

(3 marks)

**Step (ii): System-side pressure drop required to begin valve lift**

For the clapper to begin to lift, the **net downward force must drop to zero or reverse**. The condition is:

$$P_w \cdot A_{bot} \ge P_{sys} \cdot A_{top}$$

For the equality (incipient lift):

$$P_{sys} = P_w \cdot \frac{A_{bot}}{A_{top}} = 90 \cdot \frac{1.5}{6.0} = 90 \times 0.25 = 22.5 \text{ psi}$$

So the clapper will begin to lift when **system-side pressure falls to 22.5 psi** or below.

**Pressure drop required:**
$$\Delta P_{drop} = P_w - P_{sys} = 90 - 22.5 = 67.5 \text{ psi}$$

(4 marks)

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $F_{net} = 405$ lbf (seating).
- (ii) $P_{sys,lift} = 22.5$ psi; $\Delta P_{drop} = 67.5$ psi.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "trip pressure" with "set pressure":** The set pressure is the **air pressure in the system at rest** (30–40 psi). The trip pressure is the **air pressure at which the clapper lifts** (typically ≈ $P_w / R$, i.e., much lower). Students mix these up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the code minimum on compressor size:** Even if your calculated $Q_{comp}$ is only 1 CFM, the **NFPA 13 minimum is 5.5 CFM at 40 psi**. The code governs, not your calculation.
>
> 3. **Mixing gauge and absolute pressure in air-system calculations:** Air equations use **absolute pressure** (psia), not gauge (psig). Adding 14.7 psi is **mandatory** for any isothermal/adiabatic calculation.
>
> 4. **Stating "dry systems are safer than wet":** Wrong. **Dry systems are LESS reliable** because of the air-side failure modes (leaks, slow trip, compressor failure). They are used only when **frost protection is mandatory**.
>
> 5. **Omitting the QOD from the volume calculation:** Forgetting to add the QOD-revised limit (1000 gal) when one is fitted.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the retarding chamber in a wet alarm valve trim:** Examiners specifically look for this. A 1-mark deduction if missed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 4 — Control Valves Checklist**

### 🔑 Core Concept
- **Control valve = supervised isolation + alarm-actuating + monitored** device.
- The "VATS-D" mnemonic (Visual, Alarm, Test, Supervision, Drain).

### 💧 Wet Installation Highlights
- Pipe is **water-filled** at all times.
- **Wet Alarm Valve** = single-chamber differential clapper.
- **Retarding Chamber** prevents false alarms (30–90 s).
- Alarm: water-motor gong + electric pressure switch → FACP.
- Used in **heated** spaces only.

### 🌬️ Dry Installation Highlights
- Pipe is **air-filled** at 30–40 psi (or nitrogen).
- **Dry Pipe Valve** = three-chamber (supply / intermediate / system) differential.
- **QOD** speeds up the trip (accelerator or exhauster).
- **Max volume: 750 gal (no QOD) / 1000 gal (with QOD)**.
- **Water delivery time ≤ 60 s** (NFPA / TAC).
- **Air compressor: min 5.5 CFM at 40 psi**; **low-air alarm at 5 psi drop**.
- Used in **unheated / cold** spaces.

### 📐 Formulas to Memorize
- $P_{trip} = P_w / R$ (dry pipe valve trip pressure).
- $V_{sys} \le 750$ gal (no QOD); $\le 1000$ gal (with QOD).
- $t_d \le 60$ s.
- $Q_{comp,min} = 5.5$ CFM @ 40 psi.
- $F_{net} = P \cdot A$ — applied differentially to clapper top and bottom.

### 🛠️ Trim Components (Wet & Dry)
- **Wet:** OS&Y, Retarding Chamber, Water-Motor, Pressure Switch, Gauges, Main Drain, Test Valve, Sight Glass, Tamper.
- **Dry:** All of the above **+** QOD, Compressor, Air Regulator, Low-Air Switch, High-Air Switch, Air-Relief Valve, Drip Check, Prime Water.

### 🚫 Common Pitfalls
- Gauge vs absolute pressure.
- 5.5 CFM code minimum overrides calculation.
- "Safer" = wrong; dry is *less* reliable than wet.
- QOD volume adjustment.

### ✅ Examiner's Mantra
> *"If you forget the retarding chamber in a wet trim sketch, you will lose 1 mark. If you forget the compressor size minimum, you will lose 1 mark. If you confuse trip and set pressures, you will lose 2 marks."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deluge Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deluge Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — DELUGE VALVE: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DELUGE VALVE (NFPA 13 / IS 15105 Compliant Definition):**
> A **Deluge Valve** is a *quick-opening, hydraulically operated, differential diaphragm-actuated control valve* that permits water to flow into a deluge sprinkler or water spray system immediately upon actuation of a fire detection (pilot) system. It remains closed under normal static (non-fire) conditions, maintaining a pressurized water supply on the upstream side, and opens automatically when the detection / release system removes the seal water from the diaphragm chamber.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PCSFT501 — Module 4: Fixed Fire Fighting Installations), the deluge valve is classified as a **Class 1 — Control Valve Assembly** under IS 636:1988 / IS 15105 and **UL Listed Quick Opening Devices (QOD)** under UL 260.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Balanced Door"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a **heavily spring-loaded emergency door** in a high-pressure water tank. The door is held shut on one side by a *small amount of water sealed behind a rubber diaphragm*. As long as that small sealing water is present, the door stays closed — even though the other side has the full force of a city hydrant pushing against it. The instant a smoke detector, heat detector, or manual pull station triggers, the sealing water is **drained away** through a small pilot line. With nothing holding it shut anymore, the door *slams open* and floods the entire protected area in milliseconds. That door, with its diaphragm mechanism, is functionally a **Deluge Valve**.

---

## 1.3 Purpose & Functional Role in a Fire Protection System

| Functional Role | Description |
|---|---|
| **On-Demand Water Release** | Holds pressurized water supply isolated until fire is detected. |
| **System Actuation Trigger** | Acts as the *single point of release* between the water source and open sprinkler/nozzle network. |
| **Anti-False-Spill Barrier** | Prevents accidental flooding by requiring an *external detection event* before opening. |
| **Pressure Boundary Maintenance** | Maintains the *trim pressure* between **2.5 bar to 12 bar** on the diaphragm chamber for reliable sealing. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Operating Pressure Range (as per IS 15105):**
> - Minimum Working Pressure: **2.5 bar (≈ 36 psi)**
> - Maximum Working Pressure: **12 bar (≈ 175 psi)**
> - Differential Pressure Threshold for Actuation: **≤ 0.5 bar**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Deluge Valves by Actuation Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> The four recognized KTU/NFPA actuation categories for Module 4 examination reference:

1. **Pneumatically Actuated Deluge Valve** — triggered by drop in air pressure in the detection network (dry pilot sprinklers).
2. **Hydraulically Actuated Deluge Valve** — triggered by loss of pressure in a hydraulically pressurized pilot line (wet pilot sprinklers).
3. **Electrically Actuated Deluge Valve** — triggered by a signal from an electric detection system (smoke/heat/flame detector).
4. **Manual / Mechanical Release Deluge Valve** — triggered by a manually operated lever or remote hydraulic release station.

---

## 1.5 Standard Symbols & Identifiers Used in Fire Drawings

| Symbol | Representation |
|---|---|
| **DV** | Deluge Valve (general) |
| **DV-E** | Electrically Actuated |
| **DV-P** | Pneumatically Actuated |
| **DV-H** | Hydraulically Actuated |
| **DV-M** | Manual Release |

These symbols follow **NFPA 170 — Standard for Fire Safety and Emergency Symbols** as referenced in KTU Module 4 schematics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4 — DELUGE VALVE: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The deluge valve operates on the principle of **Hydraulic Force Differential Across a Flexible Diaphragm**. The following is the structured logic chain used in KTU valuation for "Explain the working of a deluge valve" (14-mark descriptive questions).

### **A. NORMAL (STANDBY) CONDITION**

- The valve body is installed in the main supply line, **upstream** of the deluge sprinkler network.
- Water from the city main / fire pump fills the **upstream chamber** at full system pressure $P_{u}$.
- A small auxiliary line called the **"Trim Line"** connects the upstream chamber to the **diaphragm chamber** on top of the diaphragm.
- A small **restrictor orifice (typically 3 mm to 6 mm diameter)** in the trim line limits inflow, but a **larger drain orifice (typically 9 mm to 12 mm)** constantly bleeds water from the diaphragm chamber to drain.
- A **solenoid valve / pilot valve / dry pilot sprinkler** in the drain path is **closed** during standby, *so the diaphragm chamber fills up* with water at pressure $P_{c} \approx P_{u}$.
- Because $P_{c} = P_{u}$ on the diaphragm's TOP surface, and $P_{u}$ also pushes on the **underside** of the valve clapper via the water in the main chamber, the **net hydraulic force on the diaphragm is balanced → valve stays CLOSED**.

### **B. ACTUATION (FIRE) CONDITION**

- A fire detection event opens the **pilot / solenoid valve** in the diaphragm drain path.
- Because the drain orifice is **larger** than the trim restrictor, the diaphragm chamber **cannot replenish** fast enough.
- $P_{c}$ drops rapidly toward atmospheric pressure (i.e., $P_{c} \rightarrow 0$).
- The upstream pressure $P_{u}$ now has **no counter-force** on the top of the diaphragm.
- The **clapper assembly is forced open** by the differential pressure $\Delta P = P_{u} - P_{c} \approx P_{u}$.
- Water floods the deluge piping network in **less than 1 second** — all open sprinklers / spray nozzles discharge simultaneously.

### **C. RESET (POST-FIRE) CONDITION**

- The pilot valve is manually closed.
- The diaphragm chamber refills through the trim restrictor until $P_{c} = P_{u}$.
- The valve reseats by hydraulic balance and remains in standby.

---

## 2.2 The Governing Hydraulic Force Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CORE KTU EXAM FORMULA — Diaphragm Force Balance**

The valve remains closed when the **downward hydraulic force on the diaphragm's upper face** is equal to or greater than the **upward hydraulic force on the valve's lower clapper face**:

$$F_{close} \;\geq\; F_{open}$$

$$P_{c} \cdot A_{d} \;\geq\; P_{u} \cdot A_{c} - W_{clapper}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{c} \;\geq\; P_{u} \cdot \dfrac{A_{c}}{A_{d}} - \dfrac{W_{clapper}}{A_{d}}\;}$$

Where:

- $P_{c}$ = Control chamber (diaphragm) pressure, in **bar**
- $P_{u}$ = Upstream main supply pressure, in **bar**
- $A_{d}$ = Effective diaphragm area, in **mm²**
- $A_{c}$ = Effective clapper/seat area, in **mm²**
- $W_{clapper}$ = Weight of clapper assembly, in **N**

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the design ratio $\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}$ is kept in the range **1.3 to 1.8** by valve manufacturers. This is the *designer's hidden lever* — increasing this ratio makes the valve "more sensitive" to a drop in $P_{c}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 4 — Deluge Valve)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{c} \geq P_{u} \cdot \dfrac{A_{c}}{A_{d}} - \dfrac{W_{clapper}}{A_{d}}$ | Diaphragm force balance | bar, mm², N | Valve sensitivity & sizing |
| 2 | $Q = C_{v} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Valve flow coefficient equation | L/min, bar | Water flow through deluge valve |
| 3 | $\Delta P = P_{u} - P_{d}$ | Pressure drop across valve | bar | Sizing for required flow |
| 4 | $t_{trip} = \dfrac{V_{c}}{Q_{d} - Q_{in}}$ | Diaphragm chamber evacuation time | seconds | Actuation speed |
| 5 | $t_{trip} \leq 3 \, \text{s}$ | NFPA maximum allowed trip time | seconds | Compliance check |
| 6 | $P_{f} = P_{u} - \Delta P_{friction}$ | Available pressure at hydraulically remote nozzle | bar | Hydrant/nozzle calculation |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{f} = 4.52 \cdot \dfrac{Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$ | Hazen-Williams friction loss | bar, L/min, m, mm | Deluge pipe network |
| 8 | $A_{orifice} = \dfrac{Q}{C_{d} \cdot \sqrt{2 g \Delta P / \rho}}$ | Trim restrictor orifice area | mm² | Trim line sizing |
| 9 | $N_{d,max} = \dfrac{P_{u,max} \cdot A_{d}}{P_{c,min} \cdot A_{c}}$ | Maximum nozzle design ratio | dimensionless | Reliability check |
| 10 | $V_{req} = Q_{total} \cdot t_{duration}$ | Required water storage volume | m³ | Tank sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Convention:** All instances of $\sqrt{\Delta P}$, $\dfrac{A}{B}$, $\dfrac{P}{A}$, etc. must use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX when embedded inside markdown tables. The pipe character `vert` is reserved in markdown to delimit table columns.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Installations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Deluge valves are deployed in **high-hazard occupancies** where rapid water delivery across a large area is critical:

- **Transformer yards** (oil-fired, with cable galleries) — protected under **IS 15105 / TAC 2021**.
- **Aircraft hangars** — protected under **NFPA 409**.
- **Chemical process plants** — flammable liquid storage, LPG bottling.
- **Power plant cable tunnels & battery rooms**.
- **Offshore oil platforms** (deluge + foam systems).

In each case, the deluge valve is the *single point of failure* that determines whether the system will actually deliver water to the hazard — making its correct design a high-stakes engineering responsibility.

---

| 1 | Valve Body | Ductile Iron / Cast Steel | Houses diaphragm and clapper |
| 2 | Diaphragm | EPDM / NBR Rubber | Pressure-sensitive sealing element |
| 3 | Clapper Assembly | Bronze / SS 316 | Opens to release water |
| 4 | Diaphragm Chamber | Cast Iron | Houses control pressure $P_{c}$ |
| 5 | Trim Restrictor | Brass | 3 mm orifice for slow refill |
| 6 | Drain Orifice | Brass | 9 mm orifice for fast evacuation |
| 7 | Solenoid Valve (24V DC / 110V AC) | SS Body, NBR Seal | Electric release actuator |
| 8 | Pressure Switch | SS Body | Sends signal to fire alarm panel |
| 9 | Manual Release Station | Cast Iron Body | Local manual actuation lever |
| 10 | Water Motor Gong (Alarm) | Bronze | Hydraulic fire alarm bell |

# MODULE 4 — DELUGE VALVE: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Diaphragm Chamber Pressure Required to Hold the Valve Closed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU past papers for Module 4 deluge valve questions.

### **Given:**

- Upstream supply pressure: $P_{u} = 10 \; \text{bar}$
- Effective clapper area: $A_{c} = 3800 \; \text{mm}^{2}$
- Effective diaphragm area: $A_{d} = 5700 \; \text{mm}^{2}$
- Weight of clapper assembly: $W_{clapper} = 35 \; \text{N}$
- $1 \; \text{N/mm}^{2} = 10 \; \text{bar}$

### **Step 1 — Write the Force Balance Equation**

For the valve to remain closed, the downward hydraulic force on the diaphragm must equal or exceed the net upward force on the clapper:

$$P_{c} \cdot A_{d} = P_{u} \cdot A_{c} - W_{clapper}$$

### **Step 2 — Solve for $P_{c}$ (Minimum Required Control Pressure)**

$$P_{c,min} = \dfrac{P_{u} \cdot A_{c} - W_{clapper}}{A_{d}}$$

### **Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$P_{c,min} = \dfrac{(10 \times 3800) - 35}{5700}$$

$$P_{c,min} = \dfrac{38000 - 35}{5700}$$

$$P_{c,min} = \dfrac{37965}{5700}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{c,min} = 6.66 \; \text{bar}\;}$$

### **Step 4 — Interpretation**

Since $P_{c,min} = 6.66 \; \text{bar}$ and the upstream pressure $P_{u} = 10 \; \text{bar}$, the diaphragm chamber can be pressurized to anywhere between **6.66 bar and 10 bar** during standby. The trim line is set to approximately **8 bar** to provide a safety margin of $10 - 8 = 2 \; \text{bar}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Trip Time of the Diaphragm Chamber

### **Given:**

- Volume of diaphragm chamber: $V_{c} = 0.45 \; \text{L}$
- Drain orifice diameter: $d_{d} = 9 \; \text{mm} = 0.009 \; \text{m}$
- Trim restrictor diameter: $d_{in} = 3 \; \text{mm} = 0.003 \; \text{m}$
- Initial control pressure: $P_{c,i} = 8 \; \text{bar} = 8 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$
- Discharge coefficient of drain: $C_{d} = 0.62$
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2}$

### **Step 1 — Drain Flow Rate Through Orifice**

Using the orifice discharge equation:

$$Q_{d} = C_{d} \cdot A_{d,or} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P_{c,i}}{\rho}}$$

Where:

$$A_{d,or} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.009)^{2} = 6.36 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

### **Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$Q_{d} = 0.62 \times 6.36 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8 \times 10^{5}}{1000}}$$

$$Q_{d} = 0.62 \times 6.36 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{1600}$$

$$Q_{d} = 0.62 \times 6.36 \times 10^{-5} \times 40$$

$$Q_{d} = 0.62 \times 6.36 \times 10^{-5} \times 40 = 1.577 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Converting to L/s:

$$Q_{d} = 1.577 \; \text{L/s}$$

### **Step 3 — Trim Inflow Rate Through Restrictor**

$$Q_{in} = C_{d} \cdot A_{in,or} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, (P_{u} - P_{c,i})}{\rho}}$$

$$A_{in,or} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.003)^{2} = 7.07 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$Q_{in} = 0.62 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 10^{5}}{1000}}$$

$$Q_{in} = 0.62 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{400}$$

$$Q_{in} = 0.62 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-6} \times 20$$

$$Q_{in} = 0.62 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 = 8.77 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{in} = 0.0877 \; \text{L/s}$$

### **Step 4 — Net Evacuation Rate**

$$Q_{net} = Q_{d} - Q_{in} = 1.577 - 0.0877 = 1.489 \; \text{L/s}$$

### **Step 5 — Trip Time Calculation**

$$t_{trip} = \dfrac{V_{c}}{Q_{net}}$$

$$t_{trip} = \dfrac{0.45}{1.489}$$

$$\boxed{\;t_{trip} = 0.302 \; \text{seconds}\;}$$

### **Step 6 — Compliance Check**

Since $t_{trip} = 0.302 \; \text{s} < 3 \; \text{s}$ (NFPA limit), the valve **complies** with the actuation speed requirement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sizing the Valve for a Given Deluge System

### **Given:**

- Total design flow rate required: $Q_{total} = 4500 \; \text{L/min}$
- Upstream pressure available: $P_{u} = 8 \; \text{bar}$
- Downstream (deluge network) pressure required: $P_{d} = 4.5 \; \text{bar}$
- Allowed pressure drop across valve: $\Delta P_{allowed} = 1.5 \; \text{bar}$

### **Step 1 — Calculate the Required $C_{v}$ Value**

The valve flow coefficient formula:

$$Q = C_{v} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$$

$$C_{v,req} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\Delta P_{allowed}}}$$

$$C_{v,req} = \dfrac{4500}{\sqrt{1.5}}$$

$$C_{v,req} = \dfrac{4500}{1.225}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_{v,req} = 3675 \; \text{(approx.)}\;}$$

### **Step 2 — Select Standard Valve Size**

From manufacturer's catalog (e.g., Tyco DV-100, Viking E-1, or Gem F302):

- **DN 150 (6 inch) deluge valve** has a $C_{v}$ rating of approximately **380**.
- **DN 200 (8 inch) deluge valve** has a $C_{v}$ rating of approximately **720**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Notice (Important):**
> The above $C_{v}$ values are typical for valves rated in **US GPM at 1 psi**. The formula $Q = C_{v} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ is dimensionally correct **only when $Q$ is in GPM and $\Delta P$ is in psi**. If we re-derive:

Convert $Q_{total}$ to GPM: $4500 \; \text{L/min} = 1189 \; \text{GPM}$.

Convert $\Delta P$ to psi: $1.5 \; \text{bar} = 21.76 \; \text{psi}$.

$$C_{v,req} = \dfrac{1189}{\sqrt{21.76}} = \dfrac{1189}{4.66} \approx 255 \; \text{(GPM-units)}$$

So a **DN 150 valve with $C_{v} \geq 300$** is adequate.

### **Step 3 — Conclusion**

A **DN 150 (6 inch) UL-listed deluge valve** is sufficient for the given design flow of 4500 L/min, with a 1.5 bar pressure drop and an acceptable margin of safety.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Deluge Valve vs. Other Fire Control Valves

| Parameter | Deluge Valve | Wet Alarm Valve | Dry Pipe Valve |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | External detection system | Sprinkler bulb burst | Loss of air pressure |
| **Sprinkler Type** | Open nozzles | Closed (bulb) sprinklers | Closed sprinklers |
| **Response Time** | < 1 second | 1–3 minutes (thermal lag) | 30–60 seconds |
| **Piping Water-Filled?** | No (dry until trigger) | Yes (always) | No (pressurized air) |
| **Hazard Suitability** | High-hazard, fast-spreading fire | Light/Ordinary hazard | Cold storage, unheated buildings |
| **Detection System Required** | Mandatory (electric/pneumatic/hydraulic) | Not required | Not required |
| **KTU Module 4 Emphasis** | **PRIMARY** | Secondary | Secondary |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Numerical — Trim Pressure Setting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** In KTU exams, students often confuse *upstream pressure* with *trim pressure*. They are NOT the same. Trim pressure is set via a pressure-reducing valve.

Given:

- $P_{u} = 12 \; \text{bar}$ (pump discharge)
- Desired trim pressure $P_{c} = 7 \; \text{bar}$
- Pressure reducing valve (PRV) ratio: $R = \dfrac{P_{c}}{P_{u}}$

$$R = \dfrac{7}{12} = 0.583$$

The PRV must be field-adjusted to deliver a downstream (trim line) pressure of $7 \; \text{bar}$ when the upstream pressure fluctuates between $8 \; \text{bar}$ and $12 \; \text{bar}$.
