> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT501 - STRUCTURAL FIRE SAFETY AND DESIGN OF FIRE PROTECTION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic System Components and Design Principles as per Relevant BIS, Design of Water Sprinkler and Spray System as per Relevant BIS - Application to Illustrative Problems, Design of Fixed Foam Type Firefighting Systems for Total Flooding and for Local Application as per Relevant BIS - Application to Typical Hydrocarbon Storage Tank Protection Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Fire Protection System Components

A **fire protection system** is an integrated network of detection, suppression, and control components engineered to detect, contain, or extinguish fire at its incipient stage, thereby protecting life, property, and business continuity. As per KTU 2024 scheme and relevant **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** codes, these systems form the third line of defence after prevention and compartmentalisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relevant BIS Standards Governing this Module**
> - **IS 15105** : Design and Installation of Fixed Automatic Sprinkler Fire Extinguishing Systems
> - **IS 15501** : Design and Installation of Fixed Water Spray Systems
> - **IS 15325** : Design and Installation of Fixed High/Medium Expansion Foam Systems
> - **IS 12796** : Specification for Fixed Foam Fire Extinguishing Systems
> - **IS 4989 (Part 3)** : Foam Concentrates for Fire Fighting – AFFF
> - **IS 15519** : Foam Concentrates – General Requirements
> - **IS 2546** : Specification for Sprinkler Heads
> - **TAC Rules** : Tariff Advisory Committee (now IRDA) guidelines for insurance grading

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Human Immune System

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy**: Think of a fire protection system as the **human immune system**.
> - **Smoke/Heat Detectors** → White blood cells that detect foreign invaders early.
> - **Sprinklers** → Antibodies that neutralise threats locally at the infection site.
> - **Spray Systems** → A localised skin treatment that cools the affected area.
> - **Foam Systems** → A protective vaccine blanket that suffocates hydrocarbon-based "viruses" (flammable liquids).
> - **Pumps & Water Supply** → The heart pumping life-sustaining fluid (water) throughout the body (the building).

## 1.3 Universal System Components (The 5 Pillars)

Every automatic fire protection system, irrespective of the extinguishing medium, is composed of **five fundamental pillars**:

| Pillar | Component Group | Function |
|:------:|:----------------|:---------|
| 1 | **Water/Agent Supply** | Source – city mains, elevated tanks, reservoirs, dedicated fire pumps |
| 2 | **Pressure Booster Assembly** | Fire pumps (Jockey, Main Electric, Diesel), pressure vessels, controllers |
| 3 | **Distribution Network** | Pipes (mains, branch lines, risers), fittings, hangers, valves |
| 4 | **Discharge Devices** | Sprinklers, spray nozzles, foam pourers, foam monitors, water mist nozzles |
| 5 | **Detection & Actuation** | Sprinkler glass bulbs, fusible links, smoke/heat detectors, control valves (OS&Y, butterfly), alarm bell, fire alarm panel |

## 1.4 Classification of Suppression Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Suppression System Families (Module 4 Focus)**
> 1. **Wet Sprinkler Systems** – Pipes always filled with water under pressure; discharges immediately on bulb rupture.
> 2. **Dry Sprinkler Systems** – Pipes filled with pressurised air/N₂; water admitted upon sprinkler activation.
> 3. **Deluge Systems** – All sprinklers/open nozzles are open; a separate detection system actuates a deluge valve to flood the entire protected area.
> 4. **Water Spray (Deluge-type) Systems** – Designed for **special hazard** risks (transformers, oil tanks, cable galleries).
> 5. **Foam Systems** – Fixed foam installations using AFFF, AFFF-AR, Protein, Fluoroprotein, FFFP, or high-expansion foams.

## 1.5 Intuitive Overview of Each System

### (a) Sprinkler System
A **grid of thermally-sensitive discharge devices** connected to a water-filled piping network. The sprinkler closest to the fire operates first, delivering water directly onto the seat of the fire.

### (b) Water Spray System
A **fixed-pipe deluge system** with strategically placed spray nozzles engineered to deliver water at a specified **application density (lpm/m²)** over a specific hazard (transformer, cable tray, tank shell). Unlike sprinklers, nozzles are **always open** and discharge is initiated by a **separate detection-and-actuation system**.

### (c) Foam System
A system in which a **foam concentrate** is proportioned (typically **3 %** or **6 %**) into a water stream, aerated, and discharged as a **tenacious foam blanket** that smothers the fire, separates fuel from oxygen, and cools the surface.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy and Hazard Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **As per IS 15105 (Sprinklers) and IS 15501 (Water Spray)** – Hazard classification dictates the design density, area of operation, water supply duration, and pipe sizing.

### 2.1.1 Hazard Classification (Sprinkler Systems per IS 15105 / TAC)

| Hazard Class | Typical Occupancy | Design Density (mm/min) | Area of Operation (m²) | Duration (min) |
|:-------------|:------------------|:----------------------:|:---------------------:|:--------------:|
| **LH (Light Hazard)** | Offices, Hospitals, Schools | **2.5** | **72** | 30 |
| **OH-1 (Ordinary Hazard Group 1)** | Car parks, light manufacturing | **5.0** | **72** | 60 |
| **OH-2 (Ordinary Hazard Group 2)** | Warehouses (low-piled) | **5.0** | **144** | 60 |
| **OH-3 (Ordinary Hazard Group 3)** | Paper warehouses, woodworking | **5.0** | **216** | 60 |
| **OH-4 (Ordinary Hazard Group 4)** | Chemical warehouses | **5.0** | **360** | 60 |
| **HHP-1 (High Hazard Process 1)** | Aircraft hangars, plastic processing | **12.5** | **260** | 90 |
| **HHP-2 (High Hazard Process 2)** | Flammable liquid handling | **12.5** | **260** | 90 |
| **HHS-1 (High Hazard Storage – Class I–IV)** | Cartoned goods, wood pallets | **12.5 – 25.0** | **260 – 300** | 90 |
| **HHS-2 / HHS-3 / HHS-4** | High rack storage, plastics, aerosols | **25.0 – 50.0** | **260 – 360** | 90 – 120 |

## 2.2 Sprinkler System Design Principles (IS 15105 / IS 2546)

### 2.2.1 Hydraulic Design Approach

The fundamental equation governing discharge from a sprinkler head is derived from the **orifice flow equation**:

$$Q = K \cdot \sqrt{P}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Discharge from a single sprinkler head (lpm)
- $K$ = Discharge coefficient of the sprinkler (typical: **K = 80** for residential/light, **K = 115** for standard, **K = 160, 200, 360** for high-output)
- $P$ = Pressure at the sprinkler head (bar)

### 2.2.2 Sprinkler Temperature Classification

| Classification | Colour of Bulb/Liquid | Operating Temp (°C) | Application |
|:---------------|:---------------------|:-------------------:|:------------|
| Ordinary | Red/Uncoloured | 57–77 | Offices, residences |
| Intermediate | Yellow or Green | 79–107 | Boiler rooms, kitchens |
| High | Blue | 121–149 | Foundries, drying rooms |
| Extra High | Purple | 163–191 | Steel mills, coke ovens |
| Ultra High | Black | 204–260 | Special industrial risks |

### 2.2.3 Sprinkler Spacing Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Maximum coverage per sprinkler head** = **12.0 m²** (LH/OH) and **9.0 m²** (HH)
> - **Maximum spacing sprinkler-to-sprinkler** = **4.0 m** (LH/OH) and **3.5 m** (HH)
> - **Maximum distance from wall to nearest sprinkler** = **2.0 m** (LH/OH) and **1.75 m** (HH)
> - **Minimum distance between sprinklers** = **2.0 m**
> - **Deflector-to-ceiling clearance** = **25 mm to 300 mm**

## 2.3 Water Spray System Design Principles (IS 15501)

### 2.3.1 Application Densities for Special Hazards

| Hazard Protected | Application Density (lpm/m²) | Duration (min) |
|:-----------------|:----------------------------:|:--------------:|
| **Transformers (Oil Cooled)** | 10.2 | 30 – 60 |
| **Oil Filled Circuit Breakers** | 6.1 | 30 |
| **Cable Trays (vertical)** | 6.1 – 10.2 | 30 |
| **Cable Trays (horizontal)** | 6.1 | 30 |
| **LPG / LNG Vessels** | 10.2 | 60 – 90 |
| **Diesel Generator Rooms** | 6.1 – 10.2 | 30 |
| **Belt Conveyors (coal)** | 10.2 | 60 |
| **Turbine Oil Tanks** | 6.1 | 30 |
| **Oil Storage Tank Shells** | 10.2 | 60 – 90 |

### 2.3.2 Nozzle Selection and Discharge Equation

$$Q_{n} = K \cdot \sqrt{P}$$

Where:
- $Q_{n}$ = Discharge per nozzle (lpm)
- $K$ = Nozzle K-factor (varies 25 to 80 typical)
- $P$ = Pressure at nozzle (bar)

### 2.3.3 Pump Sizing Equation

$$P_{pump} = P_{req} + P_{friction} + P_{elevation} + P_{velocity}$$

## 2.4 Foam System Design Principles (IS 15325 / IS 12796)

### 2.4.1 Foam System Classification

| Type | Description | Application |
|:-----|:------------|:------------|
| **Low Expansion (LE)** | Expansion ratio 1:1 to 20:1 | Spill fires, tank rim seal |
| **Medium Expansion (ME)** | Expansion ratio 20:1 to 200:1 | Marine, bund areas |
| **High Expansion (HE)** | Expansion ratio 200:1 to 1000:1 | Total flooding of enclosed spaces |

### 2.4.2 Foam Application Methods

**Method 1 – Fixed Foam Pourers on Cone Roof / Domes:**
- Application rate: **4 lpm/m²** (for hydrocarbon tanks per IS 12796 / NFPA 11)

**Method 2 – Foam Chambers on Rim Seal:**
- Application rate: **6.5 lpm/m²** of rim seal area (for floating roof tanks)

**Method 3 – Sub-surface Foam Injection (Base Injection):**
- Application rate: **4 lpm/m²** of liquid surface
- Foam must be **AFFF** or **Fluoroprotein** to be compatible with sub-surface method.

### 2.4.3 Foam Concentrate Calculation

$$Q_{foam} = A \times R \quad \text{(foam solution rate, lpm)}$$
$$Q_{concentrate} = Q_{foam} \times c \quad \text{(concentrate rate, lpm)}$$
$$V_{concentrate} = Q_{concentrate} \times t \quad \text{(concentrate stock, litres)}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Protected area (m²)
- $R$ = Application rate (lpm/m²)
- $c$ = Concentration ratio (**0.03** for 3 % AFFF, **0.06** for 6 %)
- $t$ = Discharge duration (min) – typically **55 min** for tanks per IS/NFPA

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Reference Table** – memorise these for ESE

| # | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ | Sprinkler / Nozzle discharge (lpm) |
| 2 | $Q_{total} = q \times A_{op}$ | Total water demand (lpm) |
| 3 | $H_{req} = P_{req} + h_f + \Delta z$ | Pump head requirement (m or bar) |
| 4 | $h_f = \frac{6.05 \times Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \times d^{4.87}} \times L$ | Hazen-Williams friction loss |
| 5 | $Q_{spray} = A_{hazard} \times D$ | Water spray system flow (lpm) |
| 6 | $Q_{foam} = A_{tank} \times R$ | Foam solution rate (lpm) |
| 7 | $Q_{conc} = Q_{foam} \times c$ | Foam concentrate rate (lpm) |
| 8 | $V_{stock} = Q_{conc} \times t$ | Foam stock volume (L) |
| 9 | $T_{drain} = \frac{0.001 \, d^2 \, L}{A_{drain}}$ | Drain time (s) |
| 10 | $A_{rim} = \pi \cdot D \cdot w$ | Rim seal area (m²) |
| 11 | $A_{roof} = \pi \cdot r^2 = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$ | Roof/tank surface area (m²) |
| 12 | $N_{heads} = \frac{A_{op}}{A_{covered}}$ | Number of design sprinklers |

## 2.6 Water Supply & Pump Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jockey Pump** – maintains system pressure at 1.0–1.5 bar above static; capacity = **3 %** of main pump.
> **Main Electric Pump** – capacity = **1.25 ×** calculated system demand; starts at pressure drop of 0.5–1.0 bar.
> **Diesel Pump** – capacity = **1.0 ×** calculated system demand; required when electric supply is unreliable.

### 2.6.1 Pipe Sizing (Schedule 40 Mild Steel)

| Pipe Diameter (mm) | Equivalent C-Factor (Hazen-Williams) | Max Velocity (m/s) |
|:------------------:|:------------------------------------:|:------------------:|
| 25 | 120 | 4.5 |
| 50 | 120 | 4.5 |
| 80 | 120 | 4.5 |
| 100 | 120 | 4.5 |
| 150 | 120 | 4.5 |
| 200+ | 120 | 4.5 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Illustrative Problem 1 – Light Hazard Sprinkler System Design (As per IS 15105)

### Problem Statement
A **12 m × 20 m office floor** (240 m²) is to be protected by a wet sprinkler system using K-80 standard spray pendent sprinklers. Determine:
1. The number of design sprinklers
2. Water demand per sprinkler
3. Total system water demand
4. Required minimum pump capacity

### Given Data
- **Hazard class** = LH (Light Hazard)
- **Design density** $q$ = **2.5 mm/min** = **2.5 L/m²/min** (since 1 mm = 1 L/m²)
- **Area of operation** $A_{op}$ = **72 m²** (most demanding 72 m²)
- **K-factor** = **80** (per sprinkler)
- **Minimum residual pressure at most remote head** = **1.0 bar**
- **Duration** = **30 min**
- **Ceiling height** = **3.0 m** (assumed)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Determine the number of design sprinklers**

Each K-80 sprinkler covers a maximum area of **12 m²** in LH hazard.

$$N_{heads} = \frac{A_{op}}{A_{covered}} = \frac{72}{12} = 6 \text{ sprinklers}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate discharge per sprinkler at 1.0 bar**

$$Q_1 = K \cdot \sqrt{P} = 80 \cdot \sqrt{1.0}$$
$$Q_1 = 80 \cdot 1.0 = 80 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 3 – Verify density check (first 5 sprinklers in design area)**

Since design density must be met, we calculate actual density from sprinklers in the remote **36 m²** area (for LH, the most demanding **half of $A_{op}$**):

$$A_{remote} = \frac{72}{2} = 36 \text{ m²}$$
$$N_{remote} = \frac{36}{12} = 3 \text{ sprinklers}$$

Discharge from these 3 sprinklers (assuming equal pressure for first iteration):

$$Q_{3} = 3 \times 80 = 240 \text{ lpm}$$

Average density over the remote area:

$$d_{remote} = \frac{240}{36} = 6.67 \text{ L/m²/min}$$

Wait — for LH the minimum sprinkler pressure must satisfy density. The design density 2.5 mm/min with K=80 requires:

$$P_{min} = \left(\frac{q \times A_{covered}}{N_{remote} \times K}\right)^2$$

Let us compute differently. With 3 sprinklers covering 36 m² at 2.5 L/m²/min total flow needed = 90 lpm. Per head = 30 lpm.

$$P_{min} = \left(\frac{30}{80}\right)^2 = (0.375)^2 = 0.141 \text{ bar}$$

Therefore minimum pressure of **1.0 bar** is well above the 0.141 bar requirement, and is taken as the standard minimum residual pressure per IS 15105.

**Step 4 – Calculate total water demand**

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 \times N_{heads} = 80 \times 6 = 480 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 5 – Convert to m³/hr for pump sizing**

$$Q_{pump} = 480 \times 60 = 28,800 \text{ L/hr} = 28.8 \text{ m³/hr}$$

Adding hose stream demand (typical **500 lpm** for LH per IS):

$$Q_{final} = 480 + 500 = 980 \text{ lpm} \approx 58.8 \text{ m³/hr}$$

**Step 6 – Pump capacity with safety factor (× 1.25 for electric pump)**

$$Q_{pump, electric} = 1.25 \times 980 = 1225 \text{ lpm} \approx 73.5 \text{ m³/hr}$$

**Step 7 – Water storage volume**

$$V_{storage} = 480 \times 30 = 14,400 \text{ L} = 14.4 \text{ m³}$$

Including hose demand:

$$V_{total} = 980 \times 30 = 29,400 \text{ L} = 29.4 \text{ m³}$$

### Final Result Summary
| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Number of design sprinklers | **6** |
| Discharge per sprinkler | **80 lpm** |
| Total system flow | **480 lpm** |
| With hose stream | **980 lpm** |
| Pump capacity (× 1.25) | **1225 lpm ≈ 73.5 m³/hr** |
| Water storage | **29.4 m³** |

---

## 3.2 Illustrative Problem 2 – Water Spray System for Diesel Generator Room (IS 15501)

### Problem Statement
A **diesel generator (DG) room** of dimensions **15 m × 8 m × 5 m (high)** houses a 2.5 MVA diesel generator. The diesel tank capacity is **2000 L**. Design the water spray system.

### Given Data
- **Hazard** = Oil-cooled DG set
- **Application density** $D$ = **10.2 lpm/m²** (per IS 15501 for oil-filled equipment)
- **Discharge duration** $t$ = **30 min**
- **Nozzle type** = Medium velocity spray nozzle, K = **40**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Calculate floor area requiring coverage**

The entire DG room floor area must be covered as a total flooding deluge system:

$$A_{floor} = L \times B = 15 \times 8 = 120 \text{ m²}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate total water flow rate**

$$Q_{total} = A \times D = 120 \times 10.2$$
$$Q_{total} = 1224 \text{ lpm} \approx 20.4 \text{ lps}$$

**Step 3 – Determine number of nozzles and pressure**

Each K-40 nozzle typically covers **6–9 m²** at 3.5 bar. Let us take coverage = **8 m²/nozzle**.

$$N_{nozzles} = \frac{120}{8} = 15 \text{ nozzles}$$

Discharge per nozzle at 3.5 bar:

$$Q_{n} = K\sqrt{P} = 40 \times \sqrt{3.5}$$
$$Q_{n} = 40 \times 1.8708 = 74.83 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 4 – Total flow check**

$$Q_{check} = 15 \times 74.83 = 1122.5 \text{ lpm}$$

This is slightly less than 1224 lpm, so increase pressure to 4.0 bar:

$$Q_{n,4bar} = 40 \times \sqrt{4.0} = 40 \times 2 = 80 \text{ lpm}$$
$$Q_{total,4bar} = 15 \times 80 = 1200 \text{ lpm}$$

Adjust slightly to 4.1 bar:

$$Q_{n,4.1} = 40 \times \sqrt{4.1} = 40 \times 2.0248 = 80.99 \text{ lpm}$$
$$Q_{total,4.1} = 15 \times 80.99 = 1214.9 \text{ lpm} \approx 1224 \text{ lpm} \;\; ✓$$

**Step 5 – Required pump head (assume 20 m pipe length, fittings equivalent 10 m, 5 m elevation)**

Using Hazen-Williams with C = 120, d = 80 mm:

$$h_f = \frac{6.05 \times Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \times d^{4.87}} \times L_{eq}$$

For Q = 1224 lpm ≈ 1224/600 m³/min = 2.04 m³/min ≈ 33.9 lps, in 80 mm pipe:

Velocity check: $v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.0339}{\pi \times 0.08^2/4} = \frac{0.0339}{0.00503} = 6.74 \text{ m/s}$

This exceeds 4.5 m/s, so upgrade to **100 mm** main:

$v = \frac{0.0339}{0.00785} = 4.32 \text{ m/s}$ ✓

Hazen-Williams friction loss per meter (C=120, d=100 mm, Q=33.9 lps ≈ 2.04 m³/min):

$$h_f = \frac{6.05 \times (2.04)^{1.85}}{(120)^{1.85} \times (0.1)^{4.87}} \times 1$$
$$h_f = \frac{6.05 \times 3.39}{4870.6 \times 1.4791 \times 10^{-6}} \text{ m/m}$$

Let us compute step by step:

$(2.04)^{1.85} = e^{1.85 \times \ln(2.04)} = e^{1.85 \times 0.7129} = e^{1.319} = 3.738$

$(120)^{1.85} = e^{1.85 \times \ln(120)} = e^{1.85 \times 4.7875} = e^{8.857} = 7047$

$(0.1)^{4.87} = 10^{-4.87} = 1.349 \times 10^{-5}$

$$h_f = \frac{6.05 \times 3.738}{7047 \times 1.349 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{22.62}{0.09506} = 237.95 \text{ m/m}$$

That seems too high. Let me re-check — this is for a very low flow. The Hazen-Williams formula in metric units:

$$h_f = \frac{6.05 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$$

where Q is in m³/s and d is in m, L in m. Let me re-evaluate:

Q = 33.9 lps = 0.0339 m³/s
d = 0.1 m
C = 120

$Q^{1.85} = (0.0339)^{1.85} = e^{1.85 \ln(0.0339)} = e^{1.85 \times (-3.384)} = e^{-6.260} = 1.90 \times 10^{-3}$

$C^{1.85} = (120)^{1.85} = 7047$

$d^{4.87} = (0.1)^{4.87} = 1.349 \times 10^{-5}$

$$h_f/m = \frac{6.05 \times 1.90 \times 10^{-3}}{7047 \times 1.349 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.0115}{0.0951} = 0.121 \text{ m/m}$$

For total length 30 m (20 m pipe + 10 m equivalent):

$$h_f = 0.121 \times 30 = 3.63 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 – Total pump head**

$$H_{pump} = P_{req} + h_f + \Delta z$$
$$H_{pump} = 41 \text{ m} + 3.63 \text{ m} + 5 \text{ m} = 49.63 \text{ m} \approx 5.0 \text{ bar}$$

(4.1 bar at nozzle = 41 m water column)

**Step 7 – Water storage**

$$V = 1224 \times 30 = 36,720 \text{ L} = 36.72 \text{ m³}$$

### Final Result Summary
| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Floor area | 120 m² |
| Application density | 10.2 lpm/m² |
| Nozzles (K=40) | 15 |
| Nozzle pressure | 4.1 bar |
| Discharge per nozzle | 81 lpm |
| Total system flow | 1224 lpm |
| Pipe main | 100 mm (MS) |
| Pump head | ≈ 5.0 bar |
| Water storage | 36.72 m³ |

---

## 3.3 Illustrative Problem 3 – Fixed Foam System for Hydrocarbon Storage Tank (IS 12796 / NFPA 11)

### Problem Statement
A **fixed cone-roof cylindrical storage tank** contains **naphtha** (Class I hydrocarbon). The tank diameter is **20 m** and it is located in an open tank farm. Design a **fixed foam pourer system** for the tank using **3 % AFFF** concentrate.

### Given Data
- **Tank diameter** $D$ = **20 m**
- **Fuel** = Naphtha (hydrocarbon, flash point < 37.8 °C)
- **Foam concentrate** = **3 % AFFF**
- **Application rate** $R$ = **4 lpm/m²** (for hydrocarbon tank top surface per IS 12796 / NFPA 11)
- **Discharge duration** $t$ = **55 min** (standard for Class I hydrocarbon tanks)
- **Proportioning ratio** = 3 %

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Calculate the liquid surface area**

For a full tank, the protected area equals the circular cross-section of the tank:

$$A_{tank} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} = \frac{400 \pi}{4}$$
$$A_{tank} = 314.16 \text{ m²}$$

**Step 2 – Determine the foam solution rate**

$$Q_{foam} = A_{tank} \times R = 314.16 \times 4$$
$$Q_{foam} = 1256.6 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 3 – Calculate the foam concentrate rate**

For **3 %** AFFF, the proportioning ratio is 0.03:

$$Q_{concentrate} = Q_{foam} \times 0.03$$
$$Q_{concentrate} = 1256.6 \times 0.03 = 37.7 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 4 – Calculate the water flow rate**

$$Q_{water} = Q_{foam} - Q_{concentrate}$$
$$Q_{water} = 1256.6 - 37.7 = 1218.9 \text{ lpm}$$

Or equivalently:

$$Q_{water} = Q_{foam} \times (1 - c) = 1256.6 \times 0.97 = 1218.9 \text{ lpm} \;\; ✓$$

**Step 5 – Calculate the foam concentrate stock volume**

$$V_{concentrate} = Q_{concentrate} \times t$$
$$V_{concentrate} = 37.7 \times 55 = 2073.5 \text{ L} \approx 2.07 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 6 – Calculate the water storage volume**

$$V_{water} = Q_{water} \times t$$
$$V_{water} = 1218.9 \times 55 = 67,040 \text{ L} \approx 67.04 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 7 – Determine the number of foam pourers**

Standard foam pourers discharge **3, 5, 10, or 15 lpm** of foam solution each at **7 bar** pressure. For uniform coverage, we select pourers rated **5 lpm** of foam solution each.

Wait — this is discharge at a specific pressure. The pourer flow is for **foam solution**, not water. Let us reconsider.

Pourer selection criterion: pourers should be placed to deliver foam at the correct rate over the tank surface. For a 20 m diameter tank, pourers are typically installed on the rim at the top of the tank shell, with foam gently cascading onto the fuel surface.

A common pourer discharges **10 lpm of foam solution** at **3.5 bar** inlet pressure. Number of pourers:

$$N_{pourers} = \frac{Q_{foam}}{Q_{pourer}} = \frac{1256.6}{10} = 125.6$$

Round up to **126 pourers**.

**Step 8 – Verify pourer spacing**

Circumference of tank:

$$C = \pi \cdot D = \pi \times 20 = 62.83 \text{ m}$$

Spacing:

$$s = \frac{C}{N_{pourers}} = \frac{62.83}{126} = 0.499 \text{ m} \approx 500 \text{ mm}$$

This is acceptable (typical spacing 0.4 m to 0.6 m around the rim).

**Step 9 – Foam expansion and blanket check**

For 3 % AFFF, expansion ratio ≈ **6:1 to 9:1**. Take **7:1**.

$$Q_{expanded\,foam} = Q_{foam} \times 7 = 1256.6 \times 7 = 8796 \text{ lpm}$$

This expanded foam must cover the tank surface with a layer of at least **100 mm** thickness to be effective. Verify:

$$V_{blanket} = 314.16 \times 0.1 = 31.42 \text{ m³}$$
$$t_{blanket\,formation} = \frac{31416 \text{ L}}{8796 \text{ lpm}} = 3.57 \text{ min}$$

✓ Foam blanket forms within ~4 minutes, which is acceptable (typical 25 % pour time).

**Step 10 – System design summary**

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Tank surface area | 314.16 m² |
| Foam application rate | 4 lpm/m² |
| Foam solution flow | 1256.6 lpm |
| Foam concentrate flow | 37.7 lpm |
| Water flow | 1218.9 lpm |
| Number of pourers (10 lpm each) | 126 |
| Pourer spacing | 0.5 m around rim |
| Discharge duration | 55 min |
| Foam concentrate stock | 2.07 m³ |
| Water storage | 67.04 m³ |
| Pump pressure | 7.0 bar |
| Pump capacity | 1257 lpm × 7 bar |

---

## 3.4 Illustrative Problem 4 – Rim Seal Foam System for Floating Roof Tank

### Problem Statement
A **floating roof tank** of diameter **30 m** storing **diesel** is to be protected by a **foam chamber rim seal system**. Calculate the foam and concentrate quantities.

### Given Data
- **Tank diameter** $D$ = **30 m**
- **Fuel** = Diesel (Class II hydrocarbon)
- **Rim seal width** $w$ = **0.6 m**
- **Application rate** for rim seal = **6.5 lpm/m²** (per IS 12796 / NFPA 11)
- **Discharge duration** $t$ = **20 min** (rim seal fires are typically smaller and require less duration)
- **Concentrate** = 3 % AFFF

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Calculate rim seal area**

$$A_{rim} = \pi \cdot D \cdot w = \pi \times 30 \times 0.6$$
$$A_{rim} = 56.55 \text{ m²}$$

**Step 2 – Foam solution flow**

$$Q_{foam} = A_{rim} \times R = 56.55 \times 6.5$$
$$Q_{foam} = 367.6 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 3 – Foam concentrate flow**

$$Q_{conc} = 367.6 \times 0.03 = 11.03 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 4 – Water flow**

$$Q_{water} = 367.6 \times 0.97 = 356.6 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 5 – Storage volumes for 20 min**

$$V_{conc} = 11.03 \times 20 = 220.6 \text{ L} \approx 0.221 \text{ m³}$$
$$V_{water} = 356.6 \times 20 = 7132 \text{ L} \approx 7.13 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 6 – Number of foam chambers**

Standard foam chambers discharge **2 to 8 lpm** each. Select **5 lpm** chamber:

$$N = \frac{367.6}{5} = 73.5 \rightarrow 74 \text{ chambers}$$

Spacing:

$$s = \frac{\pi \times 30}{74} = 1.27 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rim seal protection is the single most important aspect of floating roof tank safety. Rim seal fires account for ~90 % of all floating roof tank incidents.**

---

## 3.5 Illustrative Problem 5 – High Expansion Foam Total Flooding (IS 15325)

### Problem Statement
Design a **high-expansion foam total flooding system** for a **cable vault** measuring **20 m × 10 m × 6 m (high)**.

### Given Data
- **Volume** $V$ = **20 × 10 × 6 = 1200 m³**
- **Submergence time** $T$ = **4 min** (per IS 15325)
- **Foam expansion ratio** = **500:1** (high expansion)
- **Generator capacity** = **2 m³ of foam/min per kW** (typical)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 – Determine foam solution rate**

For high-expansion foam total flooding, the **rate of foam production** is governed by the volume divided by submergence time:

$$R_{foam} = \frac{V}{T} = \frac{1200}{4} = 300 \text{ m³ foam/min}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate foam solution rate**

$$Q_{sol} = \frac{R_{foam}}{E} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.6 \text{ m³/min} = 600 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 3 – Foam concentrate rate (3 % AFFF)**

$$Q_{conc} = 600 \times 0.03 = 18 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 4 – Water flow**

$$Q_{water} = 600 \times 0.97 = 582 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 5 – Concentrate stock for 25 min** (per IS 15325 minimum duration)

$$V_{conc} = 18 \times 25 = 450 \text{ L}$$

**Step 6 – Water storage**

$$V_{water} = 582 \times 25 = 14,550 \text{ L} \approx 14.55 \text{ m³}$$

### Summary
| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Volume protected | 1200 m³ |
| Foam solution | 600 lpm |
| Water | 582 lpm |
| Concentrate | 18 lpm |
| Stock (25 min) | 450 L concentrate, 14.55 m³ water |

---

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Hazard Type] --> B{What is burning?}
    B -->|Ordinary combustibles Class A| C[Sprinkler System IS 15105]
    B -->|Flammable liquids Class B| D[Tank size and location]
    B -->|Enclosed volume Class A plus B| E[High Expansion Foam IS 15325]
    B -->|Electrical or oil equipment| F[Water Spray System IS 15501]
    D -->|Cone roof tank| G[Foam Pourers Top mounted]
    D -->|Floating roof tank| H[Rim Seal Foam Chambers]
    D -->|Large bulk storage| I[Sub surface Base Injection]
    C --> J[Determine Hazard Class LH, OH, HH]
    J --> K[Select density and area]
    F --> L[Identify hazard surface]
    L --> M[Apply IS 15501 density]
    style A fill:#b3d9ff
    style C fill:#66ff66
    style F fill:#66b3ff
    style G fill:#cc99ff
    style H fill:#cc99ff
    style I fill:#cc99ff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the basic components of a fixed fire protection system. Briefly state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks – pointwise):**

The basic components of a fixed fire protection system (as per relevant BIS) are:

1. **Water/Agent Supply** (1 mark) – Provides the firefighting medium. Examples: city mains, elevated storage tanks, dedicated fire reservoirs.

2. **Pressure Boosting Assembly** (1 mark) – Delivers water/agent at the required pressure and flow. Comprises jockey pump (3 % capacity), main electric pump (1.25× demand), and standby diesel pump (1× demand).

3. **Distribution Network** (½ mark) – Transmits the medium to discharge points via mains, risers, branch lines, and fittings.

4. **Discharge Devices** (½ mark) – Sprinklers, spray nozzles, foam pourers, or water mist nozzles that distribute the medium onto the fire.

5. **Detection & Control Assembly** – (½ mark if covered) – Heat/smoke detectors, control valves (OS&Y), alarm devices, and fire alarm panel that actuate the system.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between a **Wet Sprinkler System** and a **Deluge Sprinkler System**. State one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Wet Sprinkler System | Deluge Sprinkler System |
|:----------|:---------------------|:------------------------|
| **Pipe Content** | Pipes always filled with water under pressure | Pipes empty (dry) until valve opens |
| **Sprinklers** | **Closed** – thermally operated glass bulbs/fusible links | **Open** – nozzles always open |
| **Detection** | Each sprinkler is its own detector | **Separate** detection system actuates the deluge valve |
| **Application** | Offices, hospitals, schools, shopping malls | High-hazard industrial risks, aircraft hangars, transformer protection, cable galleries |
| **Discharge Pattern** | Only sprinklers in fire vicinity open | **All** sprinklers open simultaneously upon actuation |
| **Water Damage** | Limited to fire area | Entire protected area gets wet (justified for high-hazard) |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each question carries 14 marks. Internal choice: answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Sub-parts are typically 7 marks each.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model – Full 14 marks]**

**A light-hazard office building of floor area 800 m² is to be protected by a wet sprinkler system. The system uses K-80 standard spray pendent sprinklers at a spacing of 3.5 m × 3.5 m. The minimum residual pressure at the most remote sprinkler is 1.0 bar.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine (i) the number of design sprinklers, (ii) discharge per sprinkler, (iii) total water demand of the system, and (iv) the design density.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the pump capacity, the minimum residual pressure check, and the water storage tank volume for a duration of 60 minutes. Also include a 500 lpm hose stream allowance.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – [7 marks]**

**Given (1 mark):**
- Hazard class = LH, $q$ = 2.5 mm/min = 2.5 L/m²/min
- $A_{op}$ = 72 m² (per IS 15105)
- K-factor = 80
- Spacing = 3.5 m × 3.5 m = 12.25 m² per head

**Step 1 – Number of design sprinklers (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{A_{op}}{A_{covered}} = \frac{72}{12.25} = 5.88 \rightarrow 6 \text{ sprinklers}$$

**Step 2 – Discharge per sprinkler at 1.0 bar (1 mark):**

$$Q = K\sqrt{P} = 80 \times \sqrt{1.0} = 80 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 3 – Total water demand (1 mark):**

$$Q_{total} = 6 \times 80 = 480 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 4 – Design density check (2 marks):**

Remote 36 m² area contains $N_{remote} = 36/12.25 ≈ 3$ sprinklers.

$$q_{actual} = \frac{3 \times 80}{36} = 6.67 \text{ L/m²/min} = 6.67 \text{ mm/min}$$

Since $q_{actual} = 6.67$ mm/min > $q_{design} = 2.5$ mm/min ✓ **Density satisfied.**

#### Part (b) – [7 marks]

**Step 1 – Total demand including hose stream (2 marks):**

$$Q_{system+hose} = 480 + 500 = 980 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 2 – Pump capacity (2 marks):**

Electric pump (× 1.25):

$$Q_{pump} = 1.25 \times 980 = 1225 \text{ lpm} \approx 73.5 \text{ m³/hr}$$

Jockey pump (3 % of main pump):

$$Q_{jockey} = 0.03 \times 1225 = 36.75 \text{ lpm}$$

Diesel pump: $Q_{diesel} = 1.0 \times 980 = 980$ lpm (excludes 1.25 safety factor, may be taken as 1.0× or 1.25× per IS 15105).

**Step 3 – Water storage for 60 min (3 marks):**

$$V_{water} = 980 \times 60 = 58,800 \text{ L} = 58.8 \text{ m³}$$

Add separate **fire service inlet** connection to allow municipal pumpers to feed system (no extra storage required for that, but BSI/TAC often requires dedicated 60-min dedicated supply).

Final water tank capacity ≈ **60 m³** (rounded up).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**A fixed cone-roof storage tank of 25 m diameter containing gasoline (Class I hydrocarbon) is to be protected by a fixed foam pourer system using 3 % AFFF concentrate. The application rate as per IS 12796 is 4 lpm/m² and the discharge duration is 55 minutes.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate (i) the tank surface area, (ii) the foam solution flow rate, (iii) the foam concentrate flow rate, and (iv) the water flow rate.

**(b) [7 marks]** Determine (i) the number of foam pourers (each rated 12 lpm of foam solution at 7 bar), (ii) the pourer spacing around the tank rim, (iii) the foam concentrate stock volume, and (iv) the water storage volume.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – [7 marks]**

**Step 1 – Tank surface area (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} = \frac{625\pi}{4} = 490.87 \text{ m²}$$

**Step 2 – Foam solution rate (2 marks):**

$$Q_{foam} = A \times R = 490.87 \times 4 = 1963.5 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 3 – Foam concentrate rate (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{conc} = 1963.5 \times 0.03 = 58.9 \text{ lpm}$$

**Step 4 – Water rate (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{water} = 1963.5 \times 0.97 = 1904.6 \text{ lpm}$$

#### Part (b) – [7 marks]

**Step 1 – Number of pourers (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{1963.5}{12} = 163.6 \rightarrow 164 \text{ pourers}$$

**Step 2 – Pourer spacing (1 mark):**

$$s = \frac{\pi D}{N} = \frac{\pi \times 25}{164} = 0.479 \text{ m} \approx 480 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 – Foam concentrate stock (2 marks):**

$$V_{conc} = 58.9 \times 55 = 3239.5 \text{ L} \approx 3.24 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 4 – Water storage (2 marks):**

$$V_{water} = 1904.6 \times 55 = 104,753 \text{ L} \approx 104.75 \text{ m³}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse 4 lpm/m² with 4 mm/min** – the foam application rate of 4 lpm/m² is volumetric, not the depth-based density used in sprinklers.
> 2. **3 % means 0.03, not 0.3** – students frequently make a 10× error. Always confirm the percentage as decimal.
> 3. **Hose stream must be ADDED to sprinkler system demand** (typical 250–500 lpm) but is **not** required in foam system design (covered by foam blanket).
> 4. **In rim seal design, area is $\pi D w$, NOT $\pi D^2/4$** – this is the most common error in KTU papers.
> 5. **Pourer discharge rating is for FOAM SOLUTION, not for water only** – multiplying 12 lpm by 97 % gives water per pourer.
> 6. **Discharge duration for tank foam is 55 min, for rim seal only 20 min, for HHS sprinklers 90–120 min** – memorise this table.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorise Before Exam)**

### Core Formulas
- $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ — **discharge from any sprinkler/nozzle**
- $Q_{total} = q \times A_{op}$ — **water demand**
- $Q_{foam} = A \times R$ — **foam solution rate**
- $Q_{conc} = Q_{foam} \times 0.03 \text{ (or 0.06)}$ — **concentrate rate**
- $V_{storage} = Q \times t$ — **water/concentrate volume**

### Sprinkler System (IS 15105) Key Numbers
- LH: $q$ = 2.5 mm/min, $A_{op}$ = 72 m², $t$ = 30 min
- OH-1: $q$ = 5.0 mm/min, $A_{op}$ = 72 m²
- OH-3: $q$ = 5.0 mm/min, $A_{op}$ = 216 m²
- HHS-3: $q$ = 25 mm/min, $A_{op}$ = 260 m², $t$ = 90 min
- Spacing: max 4 m × 4 m (LH/OH), 3.5 m × 3.5 m (HH)
- Coverage: max 12 m²/head (LH/OH), 9 m²/head (HH)
- Min pressure at remote head: 1.0 bar
- Pump: electric = 1.25×, diesel = 1.0×, jockey = 3 % of main

### Water Spray System (IS 15501) Key Densities
- Transformers (oil): **10.2 lpm/m²**, 30–60 min
- Cable trays (vertical): **6.1–10.2 lpm/m²**
- DG rooms: **6.1–10.2 lpm/m²**
- LPG/LNG vessels: **10.2 lpm/m²**, 60–90 min
- Pressure at nozzle: typically **3.5–7.0 bar**

### Foam System (IS 12796 / 15325) Key Numbers
- **Cone-roof tank top** application: **4 lpm/m²** of tank cross-section
- **Rim seal** application: **6.5 lpm/m²** of $\pi D w$
- **Sub-surface** application: **4 lpm/m²** of tank surface
- Standard proportion: **3 %** AFFF (or 6 %)
- Duration: **55 min** for tank top; **20 min** for rim seal
- Expansion ratio: LE = 1:1–20:1; ME = 20:1–200:1; HE = 200:1–1000:1

### BIS Codes to Remember
- **IS 15105** → Sprinklers
- **IS 15501** → Water Spray
- **IS 15325** → High/Medium Expansion Foam
- **IS 12796** → Fixed Foam Systems (general)
- **IS 2546** → Sprinkler Heads Specification
- **IS 4989** → Foam Concentrates
- **TAC Rules** → Insurance / Tariff

### 5 Most-Asked Topics in KTU Past Papers
1. **LH sprinkler system** numerical (counts, flow, storage)
2. **Foam system for hydrocarbon tank** with concentrate calculations
3. **Rim seal foam system** (floating roof tank)
4. **Water spray system** for transformer / cable tray / DG room
5. **System component identification** with one-line functions

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Tip**
> - Always **state the relevant BIS code** at the start of your design solution – it earns a free ½ mark in every numerical.
> - Always **show the conversion** (e.g., lpm → m³/hr) explicitly.
> - Always **include hose stream** for sprinkler systems (not for foam systems).
> - Always **round UP the number of sprinklers/heads/pourers** – never round down.
> - Always **state the assumption of design density/area** before starting calculations.
